runs.title: I Outsourced an Entire Finance YouTube Channel for $200 — Here's the Truth

recommendations.narration_excerpt: a scriptwriter off Fiverr at thirty dollars per script
recommendations.one_line_description: A visual receipt or line-item card showing vendor name, platform source, unit price, and quantity — like an itemized invoice rendered as motion graphic
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Scenes 4–6 together describe three distinct vendor purchases; a repeatable receipt-style card would let each purchase animate in sequentially, making the budget allocation scannable and concrete instead of relying on a generic AI image
recommendations.schema_sketch: { vendor_name: string, platform: string, unit_cost: number, unit_label: string, icon_slug: string, highlight_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: the niche was too broad to rank for anything
recommendations.one_line_description: An inverted funnel or zoom-in visual that starts wide (e.g. 'personal finance') and narrows through competition tiers to a specific micro-niche, with search volume and difficulty indicators at each tier
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is making a structural argument about niche breadth vs. rankability — a funnel that visually zooms from broad to specific communicates that hierarchy far better than any static image or existing stat component
recommendations.schema_sketch: { tiers: [{ label: string, search_volume: number, difficulty_score: number, is_selected: boolean }], selected_tier_index: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: hook formula, a required call-to-action placement, and a specific forbidden phrases list
recommendations.one_line_description: A document-style card showing a creative brief template with labeled zones (hook section, CTA zone, forbidden list) that animate in with color-coded callout arrows — like a redlined spec sheet
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration describes three structural rules layered into a document; a visual brief with distinct labeled regions makes each rule spatially legible in a way that neither document_reveal nor bullet_list can achieve with the same precision
recommendations.schema_sketch: { sections: [{ zone_label: string, description: string, color: string, position_hint: 'top'|'middle'|'bottom' }], title: string, animate_sequence: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: sixty dollars per video became ninety, after I auditioned three editors with a paid test clip
recommendations.one_line_description: A side-by-side grid of N candidates (thumbnail + score bar + cost label) where losers gray out and the winner pulses — visualizing a competitive selection or A/B/C audition process
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: before_after implies only two states, but the narration describes a three-way audition that converges on one winner; a grid-elimination visual captures the selection process dynamically rather than flattening it into a binary
recommendations.schema_sketch: { candidates: [{ label: string, cost: number, score: number, status: 'winner'|'rejected'|'neutral', thumbnail_url: string }] }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: they pay for volume before they have a working format
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal timeline with two parallel tracks — one showing the 'wrong order' (scale → test) and one showing the 'right order' (test → scale) — with cost markers and outcome icons at each node
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's core insight is about sequencing decisions, not just comparing two outcomes; a dual-track timeline makes the order-of-operations error visually obvious in a way compare_reveal, which is static contrast, cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { tracks: [{ label: string, steps: [{ action: string, cost: number, outcome_icon: string, is_mistake: boolean }] }] }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: I spent one hundred and twelve dollars proving the format was broken before I scaled anything
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated horizontal gauge or fuel-tank bar that fills to a threshold amount, with a 'proof zone' region highlighted and a 'scale zone' region locked behind it — showing deliberate staged spending
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: spotlight_stat just displays the $112 number large; a burn meter contextualizes it as a calculated, bounded experiment — visually distinguishing intentional validation spend from reckless scaling spend, which is the exact lesson being taught
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_budget: number, proof_spend: number, scale_threshold: number, currency_symbol: string, proof_label: string, scale_label: string }

runs.title: How a Faceless Finance Channel Makes $12,000 a Month (No Face, No Voice)

recommendations.narration_excerpt: production stack on this twelve-thousand-dollar channel costs roughly eight hundred dollars a month
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated stacked breakdown ring where each arc segment represents a cost line item, with a total cost label at center and labeled callouts per segment
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is explicitly about proportional cost composition — a segmented visual showing how $800 is carved up is far more informative than a single spotlight number with no structural context
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_label: string, total_value: number, currency: string, segments: [{ label: string, value: number, color: string }], animate_in_sequence: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: four videos a month, that's two hundred dollars in production — plus tools and overhead hitting eight hundred total
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated receipt-style accumulator where line items drop in one by one with running subtotals, culminating in a final bolded total
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration literally performs an additive calculation — a receipt/accumulator animation mirrors that mental math visually in a way no static comparison or chart can replicate
recommendations.schema_sketch: { line_items: [{ label: string, unit_cost: number, quantity: number, line_total: number }], running_total_visible: boolean, final_total: number, currency: string, stagger_delay_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: AdSense CPM in the finance niche runs between fifteen and forty dollars per thousand views
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal gauge bar showing a shaded min-to-max range with a floating marker for the current niche, compared against a faint baseline band for average YouTube CPM
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: CPM is a range, not a single number — a range-band gauge communicates the spread and relative position simultaneously, which a stat_comparison of two discrete numbers completely misses
recommendations.schema_sketch: { metric_label: string, range_min: number, range_max: number, baseline_min: number, baseline_max: number, baseline_label: string, highlight_label: string, unit: string, currency_prefix: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: a single affiliate program can double your effective revenue per viewer without adding one extra video
recommendations.one_line_description: A side-by-side animated bar pair showing revenue-per-viewer with AdSense only versus AdSense plus one affiliate link, with a 'x2' multiplier badge animating between them
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The entire claim is a before/after magnitude comparison on a single metric — a visual multiplier bar makes the doubling effect viscerally concrete rather than relying on a generic AI image to carry a quantitative argument
recommendations.schema_sketch: { metric_label: string, bar_a: { label: string, value: number }, bar_b: { label: string, value: number }, multiplier_badge: string, unit: string, animate_grow: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: niches that bleed views but generate zero revenue: personal finance motivation, money mindset, wealth psychology
recommendations.one_line_description: A minimal two-axis scatter plot plotting niche clusters as labeled dots — high-view/low-revenue niches in one quadrant, low-view/high-revenue niches in another — with quadrant labels
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is making a decoupled-correlation argument (views ≠ revenue) that only a two-dimensional plot can show clearly; stat_comparison can only show two numbers, not the structural relationship across multiple niches simultaneously
recommendations.schema_sketch: { x_axis_label: string, y_axis_label: string, quadrant_labels: { top_left: string, top_right: string, bottom_left: string, bottom_right: string }, points: [{ label: string, x: number, y: number, highlight: boolean, color: string }] }

runs.title: The Strategy That Built a $200K/Year Faceless Finance Channel

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Builders flood into broad finance topics: personal finance, crypto, passive income
recommendations.one_line_description: A particle/bubble cluster visualization where dozens of labeled nodes crowd a central zone, visually communicating oversaturation of a space
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is specifically about mass crowding into the same categories — a cluster of jostling bubbles labeled with niche names communicates competitive density viscerally in a way a static polaroid stack cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { cluster_label: string, nodes: [{ label: string, size: 'large' | 'medium' | 'small', opacity: number }], center_color: string, animation: 'pulse' | 'drift' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: First: niche compression
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated funnel that visually narrows a broad category label at the top into a tight, specific sub-niche label at the bottom with a compression ratio indicator
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The core concept being introduced is literally compression — narrowing a wide topic into a precise one — and a funnel animation makes that spatial metaphor immediate and memorable instead of showing an unrelated number
recommendations.schema_sketch: { top_label: string, bottom_label: string, top_width_label: string, bottom_width_label: string, animation_duration_ms: number, accent_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Sponsorships and affiliate deals pay ten to thirty times the AdSense rate per thousand views
recommendations.one_line_description: A side-by-side animated bar race where one bar (AdSense) stops early and a second bar (sponsorships) extends to 10–30x the length with a live multiplier badge attached to the growing tip
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is making a precise magnitude comparison between two revenue sources — a bar race with a live multiplier badge communicates the relative scale dynamically in a way a single count-up number fundamentally cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { bars: [{ label: string, value: number, color: string }], multiplier_range: { min: number, max: number }, unit_label: string, animation: 'race' | 'simultaneous' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: channels with hundreds of thousands of subscribers and years of authority, your new channel registers as noise
recommendations.one_line_description: A vertical ladder or staircase graphic with established channels plotted high on a subscriber/authority axis and a 'new channel' marker pinned near the bottom, with a visible gap callout between them
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is about a stark authority gap between incumbents and newcomers — a positional ladder with a labeled chasm makes the competitive disadvantage visually legible rather than showing a generic channel page that implies parity
recommendations.schema_sketch: { rungs: [{ label: string, metric_value: number, highlight: boolean }], gap_label: string, y_axis_label: string, new_entry_label: string, accent_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: One mid-roll from a tax software company or a financial advisor network
recommendations.one_line_description: A stylized video scrubber timeline with a sponsor pin dropped at the mid-roll position, a brand logo badge attached to the pin, and an earnings callout bubble floating above it
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is specifically about the placement and value of a single mid-roll deal — a scrubber timeline with a pinned sponsor moment and dollar callout makes the monetization mechanic spatially concrete rather than showing a full video page
recommendations.schema_sketch: { video_duration_seconds: number, sponsor_timestamp_seconds: number, sponsor_label: string, sponsor_logo_url: string, earnings_label: string, pin_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Most people who watch this will reopen TikTok
recommendations.one_line_description: A split-path animation showing a phone with two diverging arrows: one path leads to a TikTok-style scroll loop icon, the other to a channel-build progress icon, with a stark visual contrast between the two outcomes
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is a deliberate behavioral fork moment — the viewer is being challenged to choose a path — and a visual fork with labeled diverging outcomes dramatizes that decision point far better than a static phone screen
recommendations.schema_sketch: { left_path: { label: string, icon: string, color: string, outcome_label: string }, right_path: { label: string, icon: string, color: string, outcome_label: string }, fork_label: string, animation: 'diverge' | 'simultaneous' }

runs.title: How One Trader Turned $10,000 Into Nothing (And What Fixed It)

recommendations.narration_excerpt: averaged down three times on the way. His $10,000 account is now worth $1,340
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated line chart showing account balance declining over time, with labeled drop markers at each averaging-down event
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A static stat split cannot convey the compounding tragedy of three sequential averaging-down decisions the way an animated falling equity curve with annotated inflection points can — it makes the loss feel earned and sequential, not just a number.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { starting_balance: number, events: [{ label: string, date: string, balance_after: number }], end_balance: number, currency_symbol: string, animate_duration_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: actual problem is one behavioral loop: they hold losers and cut winners
recommendations.one_line_description: A circular animated loop graphic showing two opposing arrows — one labeled 'Hold Loser' cycling inward to deeper loss, one labeled 'Cut Winner' cycling outward to missed gain — with icons at each node
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration explicitly describes a loop, which is a structural/relational concept; a static twin image cannot show the self-reinforcing circular causality that makes this behavior destructive, but a loop diagram makes the mechanism immediately legible.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { nodes: [{ id: string, label: string, icon: string, position: 'top'|'right'|'bottom'|'left' }], arrows: [{ from: string, to: string, label: string, color: string }], loop_direction: 'clockwise'|'counterclockwise', animate: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: risk one dollar to make three
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal two-sided bar visual anchored at zero — a short red bar extending left for risk and a longer green bar extending right for reward, with dollar labels and an animated fill reveal
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The 1:3 risk-reward ratio is inherently a proportional, spatial concept; a single big number shows only the ratio abstractly, while a proportional bar makes the asymmetry viscerally obvious at a glance without any reading required.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { risk_label: string, risk_value: number, reward_label: string, reward_value: number, currency_symbol: string, risk_color: string, reward_color: string, animate_fill: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: his maximum loss is pre-defined at two hundred dollars
recommendations.one_line_description: A schematic trade layout showing entry price, a downward stop-loss line (labeled dollar loss), and an upward target line (labeled dollar gain), with zones shaded red and green respectively
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Scenes 16 and 17 together describe a complete trade structure with defined stop and target — a spotlight stat shows only the loss number in isolation, while a trade box diagram shows the full asymmetric structure spatially, which is the entire lesson of the video.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { entry_price: number, stop_price: number, target_price: number, stop_loss_dollars: number, profit_target_dollars: number, ticker: string, loss_zone_color: string, gain_zone_color: string, show_ratio_label: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: same mechanism is destroying 90% of day traders right now
recommendations.one_line_description: A 10×10 grid of trader-icon dots where 90 are colored red (losing) and 10 are colored green (profitable), with an animated sequential fill that groups them visually
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A percent fill bar is a familiar abstraction, but a dot grid makes '90 out of 100 real traders' feel like people rather than a percentage, dramatically increasing the visceral impact of the statistic at a key early hook moment.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_units: number, highlighted_units: number, highlight_color: string, base_color: string, icon: 'person'|'dot'|'trader', highlight_label: string, base_label: string, animate_sequence: boolean, grid_columns: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: The account reads $14,200 and climbing
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated line chart showing account balance over a six-month arc — from the $1,340 low, a flat discipline period, then a steady climb to $14,200 — mirroring the drawdown chart from scene 2 as a visual payoff
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: This scene is the emotional resolution of scene 2's equity collapse; a count_up delivers only the end number, but an equity recovery chart that visually mirrors and reverses the earlier drawdown chart creates a satisfying narrative symmetry that is the whole point of the story.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { events: [{ label: string, date: string, balance: number }], start_balance: number, end_balance: number, currency_symbol: string, highlight_color: string, mirror_component_id: string, animate_duration_ms: number }

runs.title: I Tracked 200 Day Traders For a Year — Here's What Destroyed Them

recommendations.narration_excerpt: takes a loss at ten in the morning and immediately re-enters a bigger position
recommendations.one_line_description: A scrolling timeline of sequential trades for a single session, showing entry time, position size as a bar, and P&L outcome color-coded red/green
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is describing a temporal behavioral loop — a static AI image with a stat cannot show the cause-and-effect chain of loss → bigger re-entry → bigger loss the way a sequential trade replay can
recommendations.schema_sketch: { trades: [{ time: string, direction: 'long'|'short', size_usd: number, outcome_usd: number, label?: string }], highlight_index?: number, session_date?: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: losing three times as much on those trades as they made on winners
recommendations.one_line_description: A mirrored horizontal bar chart with average winner size extending right in green and average loser size extending left in red, with a ratio label in the center
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The core insight is a visual asymmetry between magnitude of wins vs. losses — a mirrored axis makes the 3x imbalance viscerally obvious in a way a generic stat_comparison cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { avg_winner: number, avg_loser: number, ratio_label: string, winner_color?: string, loser_color?: string, currency_symbol?: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: ran eight, ten, twelve positions simultaneously and couldn't track any of them properly
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated grid of ticker tiles that populates rapidly — each tile shows symbol, a tiny sparkline, and a blinking attention dot — visually conveying cognitive overload as tiles multiply
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is about overwhelm from simultaneous positions; a scatter grid that physically fills up and becomes unreadable communicates cognitive overload that a static horizontal bar cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { tickers: [{ symbol: string, sparkline_data: number[], status: 'active'|'ignored' }], max_visible: number, overload_threshold: number, animate_fill_duration_ms?: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: accounts don't bleed slowly. They zero out in weeks.
recommendations.one_line_description: An equity curve animation that starts healthy, accelerates downward with increasing slope, and terminates in a flat zero line with a timestamp marker showing elapsed weeks
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A generic animated_line_chart shows movement but not the psychological shape of account destruction — the accelerating curve slope and hard-zero endpoint are the specific visual payload this narration needs
recommendations.schema_sketch: { starting_balance: number, drawdown_events: [{ week: number, balance: number, trigger?: string }], wipeout_week: number, curve_color?: string, show_trigger_labels?: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: twenty-six traders inside a single quarter
recommendations.one_line_description: A grid of 200 anonymous person icons that animate to a grayed-out or struck-through state one by one, with a counter, until 26 are highlighted as wiped out within a quarter boundary
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Showing 26 out of 200 as a raw number is abstract — watching 26 icons go dark inside a grid of 200 makes the human scale of the loss visceral and concrete in a way big_number cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_cohort: number, eliminated_count: number, eliminated_color?: string, surviving_color?: string, animation_duration_ms?: number, period_label?: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: when they felt confident they went heavy. When they were right, size was still too small
recommendations.one_line_description: A scatter plot where each dot is a trade, X-axis is subjective confidence tier (low/medium/high), Y-axis is position size, and dot color shows win/loss — revealing the inverse correlation between sizing discipline and outcome
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration describes a behavioral pattern across many trades requiring a multi-variable visual — a scatter plot exposes the systematic mismatch between confidence, size, and outcome that a two-panel compare_reveal flattens into abstraction
recommendations.schema_sketch: { trades: [{ confidence_tier: 1|2|3, position_size_pct: number, outcome: 'win'|'loss', pnl?: number }], axis_labels?: { x: string, y: string }, highlight_quadrant?: string }

runs.title: Why Day Trading Destroys 9 Out of 10 Accounts (And How to Be the Other One)

recommendations.narration_excerpt: your brain registers that as a threat. So you force a trade.
recommendations.one_line_description: A dual-gauge visual showing emotional state (calm→panic) on one axis and decision quality (rational→impulsive) on the other, with a live-moving needle animation
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is explicitly about a psychological threat-response loop, not P&L data — a brain/behavior gauge captures the internal escalation that a ledger completely misses.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { emotion_label: string, emotion_level: 0–100, decision_label: string, decision_level: 0–100, trigger_event: string, needle_animation_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Here's the thing about the 90%: they don't fail once.
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated circular diagram showing a repeating behavioral loop (e.g. Loss → Rationalize → Repeat → Loss) with each node pulsing in sequence to emphasize the cyclical, not linear, nature of failure
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The core insight is repetition and cycle, not a before/after split — a looping cycle diagram makes the 'same way repeatedly' pattern viscerally clear where a diagonal split implies a one-time contrast.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { nodes: [{ label: string, icon: string }], loop_color: string, pulse_duration_ms: number, highlight_node_index: number, caption: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: calculated my actual win rate, and realized I had no edge
recommendations.one_line_description: A scatter plot of individual trades plotted by entry-time on the x-axis and outcome (win/loss magnitude) on the y-axis, with a regression line and a highlighted 'perceived edge' vs 'actual edge' divergence band
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration describes auditing 30 real trades to discover edge doesn't exist — a scatter with a regression line shows the absence of a statistical pattern far more honestly than a ledger, which implies organized accounting rather than a brutal self-reckoning.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { trades: [{ date: string, outcome_pct: number, win: boolean }], perceived_edge_line: number, actual_edge_line: number, highlight_band_color: string, annotation_label: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: They treat each trade the way a casino treats each hand
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal frequency distribution showing trade outcomes across a large sample (e.g. 500 trades), with individual outcomes as thin bars forming a bell-curve-like shape and a net-positive expected value arrow overlaid
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The casino metaphor is entirely about law-of-large-numbers probability playing out over repetitions — a distribution bar makes the 'individual outcome doesn't matter, the math does' point visually self-evident in a way a stock photo cannot.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { sample_size: number, win_rate: 0–1, avg_win_r: number, avg_loss_r: number, highlight_single_trade: boolean, ev_arrow_label: string, bar_color_win: string, bar_color_loss: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Behavior doesn't change through information. It changes through systems.
recommendations.one_line_description: A minimal animated flowchart showing a rule-based decision tree (e.g. Setup present? → Size check → Execute / No Setup? → Stand aside) that visually contrasts gut-feel branching with a locked systematic path
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration pivots on the word 'systems' and demands a structural visual — a compare_reveal suggests two static options, but a flowchart shows that a system is a repeatable process architecture, not just a better choice.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { nodes: [{ id: string, label: string, type: 'decision'|'action'|'exit' }], edges: [{ from: string, to: string, label: string }], highlight_path: [string], color_system: string, color_gut: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: size every position on fixed risk, something like one percent of account equity
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated infographic showing an account equity bar being divided, with a highlighted 1% slice expanding to reveal stop-distance and share-count calculation in real time
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: This is a concrete mechanical rule with a specific number — an interactive-style calculator visual makes the math tangible and memorable, whereas a stat split treats it as a talking-head claim rather than a replicable formula.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { account_equity: number, risk_pct: number, stop_distance_pct: number, resulting_shares: number, currency_symbol: string, animate_slice: boolean, label_equity: string, label_risk: string }

runs.title: I Budgeted $2,000 for Bali. Here's What It Actually Cost

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Before you leave the airport, you're already down thirty-five dollars
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated checkpoint/gate visual where each gate you pass through deducts a dollar amount from a running total, shown as physical barriers with price tags
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is literally about being financially drained before clearing the airport — a sequential gate deduction animation makes the trap feel visceral and spatial rather than just displaying a static stat next to an image
recommendations.schema_sketch: { starting_amount: number, gates: [{ label: string, deduction: number, icon?: string }], running_total_color: string, gate_style: 'barrier' | 'turnstile' | 'customs_desk' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: five or six of those days minimum — two hundred and fifty dollars
recommendations.one_line_description: A calendar-strip visual where each day tile fills with a cost amount and a running subtotal climbs visibly across the row as days activate in sequence
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's power is in the compounding of a daily rate across multiple days — a static stat next to an image collapses that compounding into a single number, while an animated day-by-day accumulator shows the trap closing incrementally
recommendations.schema_sketch: { daily_rate_range: { min: number, max: number }, days_active: number, highlight_days: number[], subtotal_display: boolean, currency_symbol: string, label: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: mandatory service charge seventeen percent, twelve dollars per person not included
recommendations.one_line_description: A receipt-style visual where line items stack one by one beneath a base price, each addition visually pushing the total higher with an animated running sum
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is structurally a hidden-fee receipt being read aloud — a stacking receipt animation where each fee lands and bumps the total makes the layering of charges feel exactly as suffocating as the script intends, which checkmark_build cannot convey
recommendations.schema_sketch: { base_price: number, base_label: string, line_items: [{ label: string, amount: number, amount_type: 'flat' | 'percent', percent_base?: number }], final_total: number, currency_symbol: string, animate_delay_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: sixty cents, which means nothing until you're eating out three times a day for ten days
recommendations.one_line_description: A grid of small identical coin or fee icons that populate cell by cell — one per meal — with a growing subtotal shown beneath, making trivial per-unit costs visually accumulate into a surprising total
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The rhetorical move here is showing how an amount that 'means nothing' becomes meaningful through sheer repetition — a line chart implies growth trend, but a filling grid of 30 identical tiny charges communicates the psychological trap of repeated small fees far more precisely
recommendations.schema_sketch: { unit_label: string, unit_cost: number, units_per_day: number, total_days: number, grid_columns: number, unit_icon: string, final_total: number, reveal_speed_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: entirely from costs that existed nowhere on any resource I used
recommendations.one_line_description: A visual matrix of common travel-planning sources (blog, Reddit, booking site, guidebook) on one axis and hidden cost categories on the other, with cells showing empty/absent where coverage should exist
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's claim is specifically that these costs were invisible across all research sources — a matrix that visually proves absence across multiple named source types is far more credible and damning than an evidence board, which typically implies presence of evidence rather than its systematic absence
recommendations.schema_sketch: { sources: [{ name: string, icon?: string }], cost_categories: [{ label: string }], coverage_matrix: [{ source: string, category: string, covered: boolean }], empty_cell_style: 'red_x' | 'blank' | 'ghost', title: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Add at least six hundred and fifty dollars to whatever number you've written down
recommendations.one_line_description: An interactive-feeling slider or dial visual that shows a user-set budget amount, then animates a floor line rising beneath it by the hidden-cost amount, revealing the true minimum with a labeled gap zone
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The call to action is about recalibrating a personal number — a static handwritten note is passive, but a floor-rising animation where the gap between perceived budget and real floor is highlighted makes the adjustment feel urgent and personalized rather than advisory
recommendations.schema_sketch: { perceived_budget_label: string, hidden_cost_addition: number, floor_label: string, gap_zone_color: string, gap_zone_label: string, currency_symbol: string, animate_floor_rise: boolean }

runs.title: 5 Places in Europe That Make Paris Feel Like a Scam

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Hostel bed: €14. Nice hotel: €55.
recommendations.one_line_description: A vertically stacked visual of 2–4 accommodation tiers (icons: bunk bed → single bed → double room → suite) each with a nightly price badge and a city label, animating in from bottom to top
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is delivering a tiered price ladder across multiple cities, not a row-vs-column comparison — a tier stack with iconography communicates the spectrum of options viscerally without requiring the viewer to read a grid
recommendations.schema_sketch: { city: string, tiers: [{ label: string, icon: 'hostel'|'budget'|'midrange'|'luxury', price_per_night: number, currency: string, highlight: boolean }] }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: the azulejo-tiled train station alone would make the Louvre feel like it's trying too hard
recommendations.one_line_description: A split-screen component that places two landmark images face-to-face with a thin divider, each labeled with city name and a single crowd/cost stat underneath, designed for direct visual rivalry
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is explicitly making a head-to-head aesthetic argument between São Bento station and the Louvre — a direct visual duel framing would land the 'trying too hard' punchline far harder than a single zoomed image of one side
recommendations.schema_sketch: { left: { image_prompt: string, label: string, stat_label: string, stat_value: string }, right: { image_prompt: string, label: string, stat_label: string, stat_value: string }, divider_style: 'hard'|'glow'|'versus_badge' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: I stood in a Roman amphitheatre that seats six thousand people with exactly four other tourists
recommendations.one_line_description: A venue silhouette (stadium/amphitheatre shape) filled with a dot-grid of seats where the vast majority are empty and a tiny cluster of 4 colored dots marks the occupied section, with a capacity counter and live-count badge
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The entire comedic and persuasive power of this line is the absurd ratio of 4 people to 6,000 seats — an image of the amphitheatre alone loses that punchline, while a dot-density visualization makes the emptiness the hero of the frame
recommendations.schema_sketch: { venue_shape: 'amphitheatre'|'stadium'|'arena', total_capacity: number, current_crowd: number, occupied_dot_color: string, empty_dot_color: string, capacity_label: string, crowd_label: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Medieval canal city, better preserved than Bruges
recommendations.one_line_description: Two city map pins dropped side by side on a muted European map, each pin expanding into a small card showing a UNESCO/heritage preservation score, tourist volume, and average nightly hotel cost — animating sequentially to dramatize the comparison
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is making a credibility claim about objective quality versus a more famous rival — a data-anchored map-pin card grounds the assertion in geographic and metric reality rather than just a before/after reveal which implies a transformation rather than a rivalry
recommendations.schema_sketch: { pins: [{ city: string, country: string, lat: number, lng: number, preservation_score: number, score_label: string, annual_tourists_millions: number, avg_hotel_cost: number, currency: string, highlight: boolean }], map_style: 'muted'|'vintage' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Porto, Portugal: a bottle of Vinho Verde costs €2 at
recommendations.one_line_description: A passport-style card for a city featuring a hero image, country flag chip, 3–4 iconic cost line items (food, drink, hotel, attraction) displayed as icon + price pairs, and a single 'vs Paris' delta badge in the corner
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Each of the five cities gets introduced with a cluster of price data points spread awkwardly across 3–4 consecutive scenes — a single card component designed to hold an entire city's value proposition would make each city introduction feel like a unified reveal rather than a fragmented stat dump
recommendations.schema_sketch: { city: string, country: string, flag_emoji: string, hero_image_prompt: string, cost_items: [{ icon: string, label: string, price: number, currency: string }], paris_comparison_delta_percent: number, accent_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Paris is still out here pricing itself like a masterpiece and delivering a screensaver
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated XY scatter plot with 'experience quality' on the Y axis and 'average nightly cost' on the X axis, each of the six cities (Paris + the five alternatives) plotted as a labeled dot, with Paris visibly isolated in the high-cost low-value quadrant
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The entire video's thesis is a value-vs-cost argument — a scatter plot at the climax would visually crystallize every data point from the previous 30 scenes into one damning chart that shows Paris's position without needing any more words
recommendations.schema_sketch: { x_axis_label: string, y_axis_label: string, cities: [{ name: string, cost_index: number, experience_index: number, dot_color: string, annotate: boolean }], quadrant_labels: { top_left: string, top_right: string, bottom_left: string, bottom_right: string }, animation: 'sequential'|'simultaneous' }

runs.title: Japan: The Country Where Tourists Leave Their Wallets on the Street

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Assault rates so low that some categories of violent crime barely register statistically
recommendations.one_line_description: A multi-city comparative bar chart where one bar is so dramatically shorter it visually flatlines near the baseline, with city labels and crime-category axis
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's power is in the visual absurdity of near-invisibility — a standard stat_comparison conveys difference, but a bar that barely clears zero creates the visceral 'barely registers' moment the script is describing
recommendations.schema_sketch: { metric_label: string, unit: string, cities: [{ name: string, value: number, highlight: boolean }], baseline_note: string, animation: 'bars_drop_in' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Officers walk the same streets daily until locals know them by name
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated overhead neighborhood map showing a looping officer patrol path with named resident/business nodes lighting up as relationship markers along the route
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is specifically about geographic repetition building personal relationships — a static image cannot show the loop, the familiarity, or the spatial density of coverage that makes kobans structurally powerful
recommendations.schema_sketch: { map_style: 'minimal_urban', route_nodes: [{ label: string, type: 'resident'|'business'|'koban', coords: [x, y] }], path_color: string, loop_animation: boolean, relationship_pulse: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: it punishes their family, their employer, their social network by association
recommendations.one_line_description: A node-and-edge network diagram centered on one individual, with concentric rings radiating outward to family, employer, and community nodes that animate to 'dim' or turn red when the center node is flagged
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Collective shame is fundamentally a network effect — the entire argument collapses without showing that social consequence travels edges, which no static image or existing chart type in the library can depict
recommendations.schema_sketch: { center_node: { label: string }, rings: [{ label: string, nodes: [{ name: string, relationship: string }] }], consequence_animation: 'ripple_dim'|'ripple_red', ring_labels: [string] }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: finders can claim a reward of up to twenty percent of the item's value
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated bar or coin-stack representing a found item's total value, visually splitting into labeled segments for finder reward, owner return, and government portion with percentage callouts
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The percent_fill only shows one fill value in isolation — this scene needs to show the incentive structure as a deliberate division of value, making the reward feel tangible and designed rather than just a number
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_value: number, currency_symbol: string, segments: [{ label: string, percent: number, color: string }], animation: 'stack_split', item_label: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: eighty-three percent of lost wallets are returned — cash included
recommendations.one_line_description: A world map with country-level choropleth shading representing lost wallet return rates, with Japan highlighted and an annotated callout showing its 83% figure against a global average marker
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The 83% figure only lands as extraordinary in geographic context — showing Japan as a visible outlier against global norms transforms a statistic into a spatial, comparative argument that no existing map or chart component can deliver
recommendations.schema_sketch: { metric: string, countries: [{ iso_code: string, value: number | null }], highlight_country: string, global_average: number, callout_text: string, color_scale: [string, string] }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Every few blocks in any Japanese city, a small neighborhood police box — staffed, always open
recommendations.one_line_description: An overhead city-grid map with evenly distributed glowing dots representing koban locations, with a radius-of-coverage overlay showing how every city block falls within walking distance of at least one station
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The architectural argument is about coverage density — 'every few blocks' must be shown spatially to be believed, and no existing component visualizes geographic saturation of a service point across a city grid
recommendations.schema_sketch: { city: string, grid_style: 'minimal'|'satellite', points: [{ coords: [x, y], label: string }], coverage_radius_meters: number, highlight_color: string, animate_reveal: boolean }

runs.title: The Real Dubai: $8,000 Apartments, Visa Traps, and No Safety Net

recommendations.narration_excerpt: ninety-six thousand dollars you need liquid before you move a single box
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated stacking visualization where individual payments (monthly amounts) physically pile up into a single lump-sum total, with each layer labeled
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is specifically about the psychological shock of reframing 12 small monthly numbers into one terrifying upfront wall of cash — a static big number doesn't show the accumulation mechanic that makes this fact land
recommendations.schema_sketch: { unit_label: string, unit_value: number, unit_count: number, total_value: number, stack_color: string, reveal_animation: 'build' | 'slam', total_label: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: your legal right to be in the country is tied to one person's decision
recommendations.one_line_description: A visual chain or tether diagram showing a person node linked by a single fragile line to an employer node, with downstream nodes (visa, housing, income, legal status) hanging below — breaking the chain cascades a failure animation through all nodes
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The entire emotional point is structural dependency — one link controls everything — and no existing component visualizes a single-point-of-failure hierarchy; an image can't show the cascading collapse the way an animated node graph can
recommendations.schema_sketch: { root_node: { label, icon }, dependency_link: { label, fragility: 'single' | 'multi' }, child_nodes: [{ label, icon, loss_label }], break_animation: boolean, chain_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: The grace period to find new work or leave the country is thirty days
recommendations.one_line_description: A calendar grid that animates days crossing out in real time, with a shrinking colored zone representing the remaining viable action window, and parallel task labels (find sponsor, pack, book flight) competing for the same shrinking space
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A countdown_timer shows elapsed time but not the impossible simultaneity of tasks that must fit inside 30 days — the horror is spatial and logistical, not just numerical, and requires showing multiple obligations colliding inside a closing window
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_days: number, window_color: string, critical_threshold_days: number, parallel_tasks: [{ label: string, estimated_days: number, color: string }], start_date_label: string, end_label: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: No unemployment benefits. No public healthcare fallback. No pension system.
recommendations.one_line_description: A side-by-side grid comparing a reference country's social safety net (filled icons: unemployment, healthcare, pension, housing support) against the expat's equivalent grid — all icons present as outlines only, visually hollow or struck through
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's power comes from absence, not presence — three_points renders missing things as positive bullet statements, but a void/hollow comparison grid makes the absence itself the visual subject, which is far more visceral
recommendations.schema_sketch: { columns: [{ label: string, filled: boolean }], rows: [{ benefit_label: string, icon: string, values: [{ filled: boolean, tooltip?: string }] }], empty_state_style: 'hollow' | 'strikethrough' | 'greyed', comparison_label_left: string, comparison_label_right: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: one bad month away from zero with no institutional cushion between you and a flight home
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated cross-section diagram showing a person standing on a platform labeled 'current income,' with labeled floors below (savings buffer, unemployment benefit, healthcare fallback, pension) — the floors are absent, leaving only open space above a baseline labeled 'flight home'
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The metaphor in the narration is explicitly vertical and architectural — cushion, floor, fall — and no existing component renders the absence of protective layers between a financial position and total collapse; an image cannot show the missing floors
recommendations.schema_sketch: { top_platform_label: string, floor_layers: [{ label: string, present: boolean, color: string, height_units: number }], bottom_label: string, fall_animation: boolean, void_color: string, person_icon: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: the number isn't eight thousand dollars a month — it's everything you can't afford to lose
recommendations.one_line_description: An expanding receipt or ledger animation where an initial advertised figure is shown, then line items animate in beneath it — rent upfront, agency fee, service charges, visa costs, zero-net buffer — culminating in a 'true exposure' total that dwarfs the original number
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The entire video's argument is that the visible number conceals a much larger true number, and the closing scene deserves a component that visually performs that unwrapping — a price_tag just restates the surface figure without showing the reveal mechanics that earned this conclusion
recommendations.schema_sketch: { headline_figure: number, headline_label: string, line_items: [{ label: string, value: number, type: 'cost' | 'risk_exposure' | 'missing_cushion' }], true_total_label: string, animate_reveal: boolean, currency: string, color_scheme: 'escalating' | 'flat' }

runs.title: The Tiny Island With No Resources That Beat Every Country in Asia

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Fifty years later, its GDP per capita was higher than the United States
recommendations.one_line_description: A dual animated line chart showing two countries' GDP per capita diverging over decades, with a crossover moment highlighted
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: before_after implies a static two-state flip, but the power of this fact is the trajectory — two lines that start far apart and cross — which only a time-series divergence animation can convey
recommendations.schema_sketch: { series_a: { label, color, data: [{year, value}] }, series_b: { label, color, data: [{year, value}] }, crossover_year, x_label, y_label, highlight_annotation }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Texas Instruments arrived in 1968. Shell and Exxon followed.
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated vertical timeline where company logos visually 'land' on dated nodes with their country of origin and sector tagged
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: polaroid_stack treats these arrivals as aesthetic snapshots, but the narrative weight is the sequential inflow of major multinationals — a logo-drop timeline communicates inevitability and momentum that static stacked images cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { events: [{ year, company_name, logo_url, sector, origin_country }], axis_color, animation_style: 'drop' | 'slide' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: The Strait of Malacca carries forty percent of global trade, and Singapore sat right at its mouth
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated map showing a major shipping corridor as a flowing pulse line with a highlighted chokepoint dot and a stat callout anchored to it
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: a static AI image with a stat cannot show that Singapore is a physical geographic bottleneck — an animated chokepoint map makes the spatial inevitability of the strategy viscerally obvious
recommendations.schema_sketch: { route_coordinates: [[lat, lng]], chokepoint: { lat, lng, label }, stat_callout: { value, unit, description }, pulse_color, map_style: 'minimal' | 'satellite' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: workers put twenty percent of wages into a national fund — so the state never needed to borrow capital
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated Sankey-style flow showing a wage bar splitting into contributions that flow into a pooled fund, then out into infrastructure categories
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: the CPF mechanism is a flow relationship — money moves from workers through the fund into national assets — which a Sankey diagram shows structurally, whereas a split-stat image only asserts the number without showing the causal chain
recommendations.schema_sketch: { source: { label, total_value }, splits: [{ label, percentage, color }], destinations: [{ label, value, icon }], animation_duration_ms, currency_symbol }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: No water it controlled — today it pipes desalinated and recycled water entirely on its own terms
recommendations.one_line_description: A grid of resource categories (water, oil, land, food) each showing an animated status shift from a red 'dependent' state to a green 'sovereign' state with a micro-stat
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: scenes 25–27 repeat the same three-beat reversal pattern across three separate components, but a single animated scorecard grid would land that pattern as one powerful cumulative reveal rather than three disconnected stats
recommendations.schema_sketch: { resources: [{ name, icon, start_status: 'dependent' | 'sovereign', end_status, start_label, end_label, stat: { value, unit } }], reveal_style: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous', color_dependent, color_sovereign }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: By 1972, Singapore was running trade surpluses
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated visual of a trade balance bar crossing from deficit (red, left-weighted) to surplus (green, right-weighted) with the year snapping into place at the crossover
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: a calendar date just marks time, but the narrative beat here is the moment a structural deficit flips to surplus — a balance-crossing animation makes that economic reversal feel earned and dramatic rather than merely noted
recommendations.schema_sketch: { start_year, crossover_year, start_value, crossover_value, deficit_color, surplus_color, currency, annotation_text }

runs.title: Why Scientists Who Visit Antarctica Are Forced to Sign NDAs

recommendations.narration_excerpt: No public map marks them. No official body has acknowledged them.
recommendations.one_line_description: A geographic map where specific regions are obscured by animated black redaction bars, with coordinates visible at the edges but content blacked out
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is explicitly about unmarked geographic zones — a redacted map makes the absence of information spatially legible in a way an evidence board cannot, turning the silence into a visible shape on actual terrain
recommendations.schema_sketch: { base_map: 'antarctica' | 'world', redacted_zones: [{ lat, lon, radius_km, label_visible: boolean }], redaction_style: 'solid' | 'scanline' | 'static_noise', coordinate_bleed: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Samples were recovered. Turns out, the detailed findings were never published in any peer-reviewed journal.
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated citation chain — showing journals, submission dates, and expected publication nodes — where the final node dissolves into nothing or shows a locked/absent state
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's power is in the abrupt stop of an expected institutional process; a visual that shows a research pipeline terminating mid-chain communicates that suppression structurally, not just as text on a document
recommendations.schema_sketch: { steps: [{ label: string, date: string, institution: string, status: 'completed' | 'pending' | 'missing' }], terminal_state: 'dissolve' | 'redacted' | 'locked', highlight_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: does not appear on standard navigational charts used by civilian aircraft
recommendations.one_line_description: Two side-by-side aeronautical chart panels — one official civilian chart with a clean corridor, one alternative rendering with an anomalous restricted zone highlighted — with a swipe or fade toggle between them
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is a direct comparison between what appears on a chart and what does not — a toggleable dual-chart visual makes that specific absence concrete and credible rather than relying on an AI illustration of an aircraft
recommendations.schema_sketch: { chart_a: { label: string, image_src: string, annotation: null }, chart_b: { label: string, image_src: string, annotation: { zone_coords: [lat, lon], highlight_style: 'glow' | 'hatch' | 'redact' } }, toggle_style: 'swipe' | 'fade' | 'split' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Three separate discoveries. Three separate silences.
recommendations.one_line_description: A 3-row grid where each row shows a discovery event on the left (icon + date + label) and its corresponding silence on the right (a flatline signal, a blank press field, or a locked icon), with each row animating in sequentially
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Three_points renders as a generic bullet structure, but the narration's tension lives in the pairing — discovery versus silence — which requires a two-column cause-and-effect visual rhythm to land emotionally
recommendations.schema_sketch: { rows: [{ discovery_label: string, discovery_date: string, discovery_icon: string, silence_type: 'flatline' | 'blank_field' | 'locked' | 'press_release_only', silence_label: string }], animation_delay_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: The Antarctic Treaty comes up for renegotiation in 2048.
recommendations.one_line_description: A circular arc timer showing elapsed years since treaty signing (1959) and remaining years to renegotiation (2048), with labeled milestones plotted along the arc at key events
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A static calendar date flattens what is actually a long institutional arc spanning nearly a century — a circular timeline with milestones gives the 2048 deadline its full weight by showing how much has already passed in silence
recommendations.schema_sketch: { start_year: 1959, end_year: 2048, current_year: 'dynamic', milestones: [{ year: number, label: string, type: 'signed' | 'updated' | 'incident' | 'future' }], arc_style: 'thin' | 'filled', color_elapsed: string, color_remaining: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: researchers often don't know what they've witnessed until they try to describe it out loud
recommendations.one_line_description: An image or scene that begins heavily obscured — frosted, pixelated, or fogged — and partially clears as an audio waveform or typed transcript animates alongside it, simulating the act of describing something into clarity
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration describes the cognitive experience of delayed recognition — a visual that enacts that process of fog lifting as language is produced gives the abstract psychological idea a direct sensory equivalent no static image can provide
recommendations.schema_sketch: { base_image_src: string, obscure_style: 'frost' | 'pixelate' | 'fog', reveal_trigger: 'waveform' | 'typewriter' | 'scroll', reveal_speed_ms: number, transcript_text: string, partial_reveal_percent: number }

runs.title: This Is Why The Universe Is SILENT... (The Zoo Hypothesis)

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Milky Way galaxy alone contains over four hundred billion stars
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated zooming-out visualization that shows nested scales — star to solar system to galaxy — with a particle field that populates to represent a staggering quantity
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A static big number cannot convey the visceral incomprehensibility of 400 billion stars the way a zooming particle field that never stops filling the frame would, making the Fermi paradox feel genuinely overwhelming rather than just large.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { quantity: number, unit_label: string, zoom_levels: [{ label: string, scale_factor: number }], particle_density: 'low' | 'medium' | 'overwhelming', animation_duration_seconds: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: how much human technology has advanced in just the last century. Now imagine a million centuries
recommendations.one_line_description: A logarithmic timeline scrubber where dragging right accelerates through centuries exponentially, with civilization-stage icons appearing at each order-of-magnitude jump
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Before/after only shows two states, but the narration is specifically about the mind-bending compression of exponential time across orders of magnitude — a scrubber that visually accelerates conveys that cognitive vertigo in a way a split panel never could.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { start_year: number, end_year: number, milestones: [{ year: number, icon: string, label: string }], scale: 'linear' | 'logarithmic', scrubber_auto_play: boolean, acceleration_curve: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: kid pressing their face against the glass at a zoo, watching lions pace
recommendations.one_line_description: A split-perspective frame where one side shows a subject from the outside (crisp, lit, observed) and the other shows their blurred, darkened view back through one-way glass, with reflection artifacts
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The zoo hypothesis metaphor is entirely about asymmetric visibility — one party can see clearly while the other cannot — and a one-way glass visual duality would make that power dynamic viscerally immediate in a way a plain fullscreen image cannot.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { observer_side_image: string, subject_side_image: string, glass_opacity: number, reflection_intensity: number, observer_label: string, subject_label: string, glass_tint_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Every major moment in human history — the fall of Rome, the Renaissance, the Industrial Revolution
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal civilization timeline where each era has a pulsing 'eye' surveillance icon above it indicating a silent watcher, with the eye intensity growing closer to the present
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A plain timeline shows events in sequence but misses the entire narrative point — that each event was silently witnessed — and the accumulating surveillance icons would build dread across the timeline in a way no existing component communicates.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { events: [{ year: number | string, label: string, watcher_intensity: number, icon: string }], watcher_icon: string, intensity_gradient_start: string, intensity_gradient_end: string, animate_in_sequence: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: perfect surveillance pattern — let the subjects know something's there, but never let them prove it
recommendations.one_line_description: A circular radar-sweep animation showing blips that appear just inside the outer detection ring then vanish before reaching the confirmed-contact center zone, with a 'proof threshold' ring marked
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration describes a precise and deliberate pattern of staying just below the threshold of provability — a radar visualization with a moving proof-threshold ring makes that calibrated evasion visually concrete in a way an evidence board (which implies accumulating proof) actively contradicts.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { blip_count: number, blip_max_radius_percent: number, proof_threshold_radius_percent: number, sweep_speed_seconds: number, blip_lifetime_ms: number, center_label: string, threshold_label: string, blip_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: certain milestones a species has to reach — technological benchmarks, social developments, ethical evolutions
recommendations.one_line_description: A multi-track progress bar showing parallel civilization readiness dimensions (technology, social cohesion, ethics, survival) each with a fill level and a minimum threshold line for 'contact eligibility'
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Three static bullet points cannot show that these criteria are independent axes being evaluated simultaneously against a threshold — a multi-track meter with a visible contact-eligibility line transforms the abstract graduation test into a tangible, suspenseful readiness dashboard.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { tracks: [{ label: string, current_value: number, threshold_value: number, color: string, icon: string }], contact_unlocked: boolean, threshold_label: string, animate_fill: boolean, max_value: number }

runs.title: We Shouldn’t Be Broadcasting to Space… Here’s Why

recommendations.narration_excerpt: expanding bubble of human noise that grows larger every second
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated concentric-ring pulse radiating outward from a central point (Earth), with a live radius label showing light-years traveled and year markers at each ring
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is fundamentally about an expanding geometric phenomenon with measurable scale — a static AI image cannot convey the relentless, quantified growth that makes this fact viscerally alarming
recommendations.schema_sketch: { origin_label: string, start_year: number, current_year: number, speed_label: string, ring_intervals_ly: number[], milestone_labels: [{ year: number, radius_ly: number, label: string }], animate: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: earliest radio transmissions have reached over ten thousand star systems
recommendations.one_line_description: A top-down stylized galactic map with Earth at center, a shaded radial reach zone drawn outward, and dot-clusters representing star systems inside the exposure radius
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Ten thousand star systems is a spatial, cartographic fact — a galactic map with a drawn exposure boundary makes the existential scale immediately legible in a way a split-stat layout with a number cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { center_label: string, reach_radius_ly: number, reach_label: string, star_count_in_zone: number, zone_color: string, highlight_named_systems: [{ name: string, distance_ly: number }], galactic_context: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: if a hostile civilization received our broadcasts and immediately launched an attack, we wouldn't know
recommendations.one_line_description: Two parallel horizontal timelines — one showing Earth's signal traveling outward, one showing a response/vessel traveling back — with a collision or intercept point marked, and elapsed years labeled on both tracks
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration hinges on a precise temporal paradox between two simultaneous journeys across space; a dual animated timeline communicates this cause-and-effect race in a way no static image or single stat can
recommendations.schema_sketch: { timeline_a: { label: string, start_year: number, event_label: string, travel_years: number }, timeline_b: { label: string, start_year: number, event_label: string, travel_years: number }, intercept_year: number, intercept_label: string, units: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: some of those signals have been traveling through space for over seventy years now
recommendations.one_line_description: A rolling odometer-style display showing light-years traveled, ticking upward in real time, with a secondary readout showing the corresponding year the signal was originally broadcast
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A count_up shows a static number arriving at a destination; an odometer physically rolling conveys relentless, unstoppable forward travel — matching the horror that these signals cannot be recalled
recommendations.schema_sketch: { signal_name: string, launch_year: number, speed: string, current_ly_traveled: number, tick_rate_ms: number, secondary_label: string, show_broadcast_year: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: exactly where we live, what we look like, and how advanced our technology was
recommendations.one_line_description: A pixelated binary grid that animates column-by-column to progressively reveal simple iconic glyphs — a human figure, a solar system diagram, a DNA strand — as if being decoded in real time
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The Arecibo Message is a famous visual artifact and the narration is explicitly about its decoded contents; an animated pixel-reveal of the actual message structure is irreplaceable for making the 'cosmic address label' metaphor concrete
recommendations.schema_sketch: { grid_cols: number, grid_rows: number, binary_data: string, reveal_segments: [{ label: string, glyph_type: 'figure'|'solar_system'|'dna'|'telescope'|'custom', cell_range: { row_start: number, col_start: number, row_end: number, col_end: number } }], reveal_speed_ms: number, highlight_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: silence isn't because there's no one out there — it's because everyone's hiding
recommendations.one_line_description: A dark field of faint, semi-transparent civilization icons scattered across a star-map canvas — most are dim and silent, one (Earth) pulses with a visible broadcast ring, illustrating asymmetric visibility
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The Dark Forest Theory is a comparative, spatial argument about relative silence versus exposure; a scatter visualization that literally shows many hidden nodes and one glowing beacon makes the theory's logic visually self-evident in a way a full-screen AI image cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { background: 'star_field', total_civilizations: number, silent_icon_opacity: number, broadcasting_icon: { label: string, pulse_color: string, pulse_radius: number }, silent_icons: [{ x_pct: number, y_pct: number }], animate_pulse: boolean, label_broadcasting: string }

runs.title: The Missing Page of Think and Grow Rich — What the 1937 Edition Was Really Hiding

recommendations.narration_excerpt: wealthy people decide fast and reverse slowly. Poor people decide slowly
recommendations.one_line_description: A dual horizontal slider showing two opposing behavioral patterns on a spectrum, with labeled anchor points and animated position markers for each persona
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The contrast here is not a table of facts but a directional behavioral axis — a spectrum slider viscerally shows the speed/reversal tradeoff as opposing positions on a single continuum rather than static rows
recommendations.schema_sketch: { axis_label: string, left_anchor: string, right_anchor: string, persona_a: { label: string, position: number }, persona_b: { label: string, position: number }, highlight_color_a: string, highlight_color_b: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Month three excited. Month six determined. Month nine tired. Month twelve fatigue
recommendations.one_line_description: A curved timeline arc with labeled emotional waypoints, each node showing a month marker, an emotion label, and an icon — the arc visually droops as energy declines across stages
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: An animated line chart implies numeric data and upward/downward performance; this scene tracks emotional state across time, which needs a drooping arc with mood labels at each node, not a quantitative Y-axis
recommendations.schema_sketch: { waypoints: [{ month: string, emotion: string, icon: string, arc_height: number }], curve_color: string, endpoint_label: string, droop_animation: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: ninety percent of wealth-building attempts die. Not in failure. In fatigue
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated funnel or container that visually empties from a full cohort down to a surviving ten percent, with cause-of-exit labels appearing as segments drain away
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's power is in showing WHERE the ninety percent exit — not failure, but fatigue — a draining container with annotated exit points makes that distinction viscerally visible rather than just stating a percentage
recommendations.schema_sketch: { start_count: number, segments: [{ label: string, exit_percent: number, exit_reason: string, color: string }], survivor_label: string, animation_duration_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: You don't become rich by learning rich behaviors. You learn rich behaviors by becoming
recommendations.one_line_description: A two-panel animated diagram showing two arrow sequences side by side — the wrong order (behavior → identity) with a red X, and the correct order (identity → behavior) with a green checkmark, labels animating in sequence
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: This scene's entire argument is about reversing a causal chain — a visual that explicitly flips two labeled arrow sequences makes the cognitive reversal immediate and memorable, which no existing text or image component achieves
recommendations.schema_sketch: { wrong_sequence: [string], correct_sequence: [string], wrong_label: string, correct_label: string, animation_style: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous', accent_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: If daily routines are scattered results scattered. Decisions emotional money emotional. Actions inconsistent
recommendations.one_line_description: A three-row mirrored grid where each row shows an internal state on the left and its corresponding external outcome on the right, connected by a visual bridge or equals sign, all rows building in sequence
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration delivers three explicit cause-effect pairs in parallel structure — a mirrored grid that builds row by row makes each pairing land with visual weight that a static image with a stat cannot replicate
recommendations.schema_sketch: { pairs: [{ internal: string, external: string, icon_left: string, icon_right: string }], bridge_style: 'arrow' | 'equals' | 'mirror_line', build_animation: boolean, accent_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Hidden in the gap between knowing and doing. Not hidden in some vault
recommendations.one_line_description: A visual chasm diagram with two labeled cliffs — KNOWING on the left, DOING on the right — separated by an animated gap, with a dotted bridge outline representing the missing behavioral link
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The entire video thesis culminates in the word 'gap' — a literal visual chasm with labeled cliffs and an absent bridge makes the metaphor concrete and payoff-worthy at the climax, which a hero image completely fails to deliver
recommendations.schema_sketch: { left_cliff_label: string, right_cliff_label: string, gap_label: string, bridge_style: 'absent' | 'dotted' | 'building', gap_width_percent: number, background_style: 'dark' | 'cinematic', animation: 'zoom_in' | 'pan_across' }

runs.title: Build Your First budget in 10  Minutes (even if you failed before)

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Fifty percent fixed bills, thirty percent wants, twenty percent savings
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated pie/donut that draws itself in labeled wedges sequentially, with each segment pulsing and dollar amounts populating as the slice appears
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A static donut_chart renders all segments simultaneously, but the narration introduces each percentage one at a time — a sequentially-animated draw that reveals each wedge on cue would mirror the spoken reveal and make the rule viscerally land rather than just appear
recommendations.schema_sketch: { segments: [{ label: string, percent: number, color: string, dollar_amount: number }], currency_symbol: string, base_income: number, animation_order: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous', center_label: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: fifty thousand a year, take-home is usually around thirty-seven thousand — three thousand not four
recommendations.one_line_description: A vertical waterfall bar that starts at gross salary and shows labeled deductions (taxes, health insurance, 401k) as descending steps, landing on a highlighted take-home bar at the bottom
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A before_after panel shows only two states, but this narration is specifically about the stepwise erosion of a number through deductions — a waterfall communicates the mechanism of shrinkage, not just the outcome, which is the entire psychological point of the scene
recommendations.schema_sketch: { gross: number, deductions: [{ label: string, amount: number, color: string }], take_home: number, currency_symbol: string, highlight_final: boolean, animate_steps: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: eighteen percent of Group one still budgeting versus seventy-four percent of Group two after six months
recommendations.one_line_description: A dual-line chart showing two cohorts' adherence percentage declining over six monthly intervals, with one line staying high and the other dropping sharply, and endpoint callout labels
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: stat_comparison shows only the final numbers side by side, erasing the drama of the decay story — the whole persuasive power of this data is that Group 1 fell off a cliff over time while Group 2 stayed flat, which only a time-series chart can show
recommendations.schema_sketch: { x_axis: string[], series: [{ label: string, color: string, data: number[], end_callout: string }], y_axis_label: string, y_min: number, y_max: number, highlight_gap_at_index: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: nine hundred a month for variable spending — that's about two-ten a week
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated envelope or wallet visual that shows a monthly budget dividing into four weekly 'envelopes,' each labeled with the weekly amount, with a depletion fill-down animation
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is introducing the key behavioral mechanic of the whole system — translating monthly to weekly mental accounting — and a static stat image completely misses this conceptual transformation; a visual that literally breaks one container into four would make the cognitive trick stick
recommendations.schema_sketch: { monthly_amount: number, weekly_amount: number, weeks: number, currency_symbol: string, envelope_color: string, fill_direction: 'top_down' | 'bottom_up', label_format: 'currency' | 'currency_with_label' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: decision fatigue kicks in after about seven choices — by category twelve you're just guessing
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal capacity bar representing mental bandwidth that fills progressively as budget category icons appear one by one, turning red and cracking/breaking at a threshold count
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A gauge_meter shows a single static reading, but the narration's power is in the progressive accumulation that tips into overwhelm — an animated bar that visibly breaks at category 7–8 would viscerally demonstrate why complexity kills budgets in a way a static dial cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { categories: [{ icon: string, label: string }], overload_threshold: number, bar_colors: { safe: string, warning: string, overload: string }, break_animation: boolean, label_above: string, label_at_break: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: fourteen hundred plus nine hundred plus six hundred equals three thousand — perfect
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated arithmetic display showing three labeled dollar amounts sliding in from left, a plus sign between each, an equals sign animating in, and the total populating with a green confirmation flash
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A before_after panel implies contrast between two states, but this moment is a satisfying proof-of-balance — the math checking out is the emotional payoff, and an equation-style animation that builds left to right and resolves with a checkmark would deliver that closure visually rather than obscuring it behind a comparison frame
