recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The half-life story requires showing exponential decay across specific clock times with threshold crossings — a generic line chart cannot encode the biological threshold concept or let the viewer internalize the personal dose-time relationship the narration is building
recommendations.schema_sketch: { substance_name, ingestion_time, half_life_hours, starting_amount_mg, threshold_markers: [{ label, mg_level, color }], time_axis_start, time_axis_end, curve_color }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: metabolism slowed twelve percent but your eating schedule hasn't changed
recommendations.one_line_description: A split analog clock face where one half shows the body's internal biological pace (slowed, color-coded) and the other shows the habit/schedule clock still running at the old rate, with a visible gap angle between them
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The core insight is a mismatch between two timelines running at different speeds — a visual metaphor of two clocks diverging communicates this biological drift far more viscerally than a text-based stat comparison
recommendations.schema_sketch: { label_left, label_right, pace_left_percent, pace_right_percent, event_marker: { label, time_string }, gap_highlight_color, annotation }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: front-loading hydration instead of spreading it out across the day
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal 24-hour timeline bar where fluid (or nutrient) intake volumes are shown as stacked blocks across the day, with two side-by-side rows comparing the 'old pattern' versus the 'optimized front-loaded pattern', color-coded by a danger/safe zone after a cutoff time
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's entire point is about redistribution of the same total quantity across time — a temporal volume distribution visual makes the before/after shift immediately legible in a way no checklist or bar chart can achieve
recommendations.schema_sketch: { row_a_label, row_b_label, time_blocks: [{ hour, volume_oz, row, color }], cutoff_hour, cutoff_label, unit_label }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: core body temperature drops two degrees — that's the trigger for sleep
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated sine-wave style curve mapped to a 24-hour clock showing core body temperature rising and falling, with a highlighted descent zone labeled as the sleep-trigger window and meal-spike overlays showing how eating disrupts the natural dip
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A gauge shows a single static value, but the narration is describing a dynamic biological rhythm with a critical directional drop — only a time-mapped wave can show both the natural curve and the meal-caused disruption on the same visual axis
recommendations.schema_sketch: { base_temp_f, sleep_trigger_drop_degrees, wave_data: [{ hour, temp_offset }], disruption_events: [{ hour, spike_degrees, label }], highlight_zone: { start_hour, end_hour, label }, color_scheme }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: optimal melatonin window exactly four hours before target sleep time
recommendations.one_line_description: A visual reverse-countdown stack anchored to a user-set target bedtime, showing each intervention (magnesium, melatonin, last meal, last fluid, last caffeine) as labeled bands counting backward from that anchor with color-coded urgency indicating how far each deadline falls from now
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is building a precise multi-step backward-scheduled protocol — a stacked reverse-timeline anchored to bedtime makes all the interdependencies and relative spacings visible simultaneously, which no linear forward timeline or text list achieves
recommendations.schema_sketch: { target_bedtime, interventions: [{ label, hours_before_bed, color, icon }], current_time_indicator: boolean, timezone_aware: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: tryptophan only works if not competing with other amino acids from a big meal
recommendations.one_line_description: A simplified lane-race diagram showing amino acid molecules as labeled icons competing to cross a barrier (blood-brain barrier), with tryptophan winning when lanes are clear (small meal) versus getting blocked when lanes are crowded (large meal)
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The mechanism being explained is literal biological competition for a transport pathway — a visual metaphor of crowded versus clear lanes at a crossing makes the abstract biochemistry intuitively graspable without any text, which is precisely what the narration needs at this turning-point moment
recommendations.schema_sketch: { barrier_label, competitor_molecules: [{ name, icon, count_small_meal, count_large_meal }], hero_molecule: { name, color }, lane_count, scenario_a_label, scenario_b_label }

runs.title: grip strength exercises-why they're important,

recommendations.narration_excerpt: arteries getting stiffer, blood pressure failing, heart working harder
recommendations.one_line_description: A vertical chain of connected body-system nodes that visually 'degrade' in sequence — each node dims and cracks as an animated signal passes downward through the chain
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is explicitly describing a domino cascade of systemic failure, which a static three-point list flattens into parallel items — an animated chain diagram would make the sequential degradation viscerally readable at a glance
recommendations.schema_sketch: { nodes: [{ label: string, icon: string, severity_color: string }], animation_direction: 'top_down' | 'bottom_up', pulse_speed_ms: number, degradation_style: 'crack' | 'dim' | 'redline' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: weakest grip showed cognitive decline two and a half times faster
recommendations.one_line_description: Two horizontal race-track lanes with animated dots moving at different speeds, labeled with cohort names and a speed-multiplier badge — visually communicating relative rates rather than static endpoint values
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is specifically about the RATE of decline (2.5× faster), not a snapshot difference — a speed-differential animation communicates 'faster' intuitively in a way a static comparison card cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { lane_a: { label: string, speed_multiplier: number, color: string }, lane_b: { label: string, speed_multiplier: number, color: string }, metric_label: string, duration_seconds: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: every two-pound increase in grip strength reduces mortality risk by seven percent
recommendations.one_line_description: A vertical ladder or staircase where each rung represents a fixed unit increase (e.g. +2 lbs grip) and the corresponding risk percentage visibly drops on a live counter as the camera climbs the rungs
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration presents a precise dose-response relationship — every unit of input yields a proportional output — which a static spotlight stat ignores entirely; the ladder makes the compounding, actionable nature of that relationship visually obvious
recommendations.schema_sketch: { unit_label: string, unit_increment: number, effect_per_unit: number, effect_direction: 'decrease' | 'increase', effect_metric: string, rungs_visible: number, animate_climb: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: lose six to twelve percent of grip strength every decade after age thirty
recommendations.one_line_description: A timeline chart with age decades on the x-axis where each decade column is shaded as a gradient band — the band width representing the min/max range (6–12%) and an animated fill showing cumulative loss accumulating across a lifetime
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration gives a range (6–12%), not a single line — an animated_line_chart plots one trajectory and loses the uncertainty band that makes the stat feel personally threatening across a spectrum of outcomes
recommendations.schema_sketch: { start_age: number, end_age: number, decade_loss_min_pct: number, decade_loss_max_pct: number, baseline_value: number, unit_label: string, highlight_age: number, animate_fill: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: neural pathways that power your grip also stabilize your spine and coordinate walking
recommendations.one_line_description: A simplified human silhouette with animated signal-pulse lines tracing from the hands up through the spine and down to the legs, color-coded by pathway type (grip → core → legs), with labeled waypoints that light up in sequence
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is making a specific anatomical connectivity argument — shared neural infrastructure — which an AI image of two people cannot convey; an animated pathway diagram makes the biological claim self-evident without any text
recommendations.schema_sketch: { pathways: [{ id: string, label: string, color: string, waypoints: [{ body_region: string, x_pct: number, y_pct: number }] }], silhouette_style: 'outline' | 'filled', pulse_loop: boolean, animation_delay_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: strong grip strength at sixty predicts independence at eighty
recommendations.one_line_description: A split timeline showing two projected futures from a shared present age — one path (strong grip) annotated with independence milestones, one path (weak grip) annotated with dependency milestones — rendered as a forking road diagram with age markers
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A before_after component implies a transformation of one thing, but this narration is about two diverging futures from the same starting point — a forking projection diagram captures the choice-and-consequence framing that makes the longevity argument emotionally land
recommendations.schema_sketch: { fork_age: number, path_a: { label: string, color: string, milestones: [{ age: number, event: string }] }, path_b: { label: string, color: string, milestones: [{ age: number, event: string }] }, end_age: number }

runs.title: 10 Cars Insurance has blacklisted

recommendations.narration_excerpt: for every dollar they collect, they pay out one dollar and forty-seven cents
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated side-by-side cash flow visual showing dollars IN versus dollars OUT with a running deficit ticker that bleeds red as outflows exceed inflows
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A static balance metaphor misses the visceral arithmetic here — animated cash physically flowing in one column and hemorrhaging out a larger column turns an abstract ratio into a felt financial loss
recommendations.schema_sketch: { inflow_label: string, inflow_amount: number, outflow_label: string, outflow_amount: number, currency_symbol: string, deficit_highlight_color: string, animate_duration_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: You wait for Tesla to ship them. Average wait time? Fourteen weeks.
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal Gantt-style bar broken into labeled phases — diagnosis, parts order, shipping, repair, QA — each phase filling in sequentially with a running total-days counter and a rental cost accumulator ticking alongside it
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is fundamentally about compounding waiting costs across distinct phases, not a single rising number — a phased timeline makes the insurance company's pain tangible in a way count_up cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { phases: [{ label: string, duration_days: number, color: string }], daily_rental_cost: number, currency_symbol: string, show_cost_accumulator: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: There are forty-seven McLaren-authorized service centers in the entire United States
recommendations.one_line_description: A filled US map where authorized service locations appear as glowing dots — the vast empty geography between dots auto-shading darker to visualize coverage deserts, with a tow-radius ring around each dot
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: map_callout highlights a single location; the actual story is the terrifying emptiness between 47 dots across a continent, which only a density-aware choropleth map communicates viscerally
recommendations.schema_sketch: { locations: [{ lat: number, lng: number, label: string }], coverage_radius_miles: number, empty_zone_color: string, dot_color: string, show_tow_radius: boolean, country_bounds: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: owners total them on purpose because the insurance payout exceeds what they owe on financing
recommendations.one_line_description: A dual-line animated chart plotting vehicle market value decay against outstanding loan balance over time, with a highlighted crossover zone where payout exceeds debt — the fraud incentive window — shaded in red
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The fraud motive is entirely a crossover-point story between two financial curves; no existing chart component visualizes the intersection of depreciation and loan balance that creates the incentive window
recommendations.schema_sketch: { vehicle_start_value: number, loan_start_balance: number, depreciation_curve: [{ month: number, value: number }], loan_balance_curve: [{ month: number, balance: number }], highlight_crossover: boolean, fraud_zone_color: string, x_axis_label: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Claims paid out divided by premiums collected. Anything above ninety percent makes executives nervous.
recommendations.one_line_description: A segmented radial gauge with color-coded threshold bands — green for healthy, yellow for nervous, red for blacklist territory — where an animated needle sweeps to a specific loss ratio value with threshold labels at 90% and 120%
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: percent_fill shows a single fill level with no concept of danger thresholds; the narration explicitly defines multiple meaningful breakpoints that a banded gauge communicates in one glance
recommendations.schema_sketch: { current_value: number, bands: [{ min: number, max: number, color: string, label: string }], needle_animate: boolean, show_threshold_labels: boolean, unit_suffix: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: The M8 Competition has the highest theft rate of any BMW model
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal ranked bar chart comparing theft rates across BMW model lineup, with the M8 Competition bar animating last and overshooting all others, its bar rendered in alarm red with a theft-ring icon and absolute theft-per-10k-vehicles figure
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A static image with a stat gives one data point in isolation; the claim that it has the HIGHEST rate among a lineup is inherently a comparative story that only lands when you see all the other bars dwarfed beside it
recommendations.schema_sketch: { models: [{ name: string, theft_rate_per_10k: number, highlight: boolean }], bar_color_default: string, bar_color_highlight: string, animate_sequence: string, icon: string, source_label: string }

runs.title: What's trending in Cruises for 2026

recommendations.narration_excerpt: thirty passengers arrive and stay for three days instead of twenty-five hundred
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated dot-grid where each dot represents a person, transitioning between two crowd states (e.g. 2500 dots collapsing to 30) with duration labels beneath each state
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The sheer visual impact of 2,500 dots dissolving into 30 makes the scale contrast viscerally felt in a way no number comparison can match — this is the single most dramatic data point in the entire video
recommendations.schema_sketch: { crowd_a: { count: 2500, label: 'Traditional cruise', duration: '6 hours' }, crowd_b: { count: 30, label: 'Immersion itinerary', duration: '3 days' }, dot_color_a: string, dot_color_b: string, animation_style: 'collapse' | 'scatter' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: average person talks to three strangers during their entire week-long cruise
recommendations.one_line_description: A node-graph animation showing one central passenger node with connection lines attempting to reach a field of 4,000 faded nodes, with only 3 lines fully illuminating
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The isolation of '3 out of 4,000' is a social geometry story, not a photograph story — a network graph makes the loneliness of mass cruising instantly readable without a word of text
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_nodes: 4000, active_connections: 3, center_label: 'You', connected_label: 'Talked to', unconnected_opacity: 0.08, animation: 'pulse_active_nodes', layout: 'radial' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: seven countries in seven days versus three destinations three to four days each
recommendations.one_line_description: A split animated map showing two cruise routes simultaneously — left side traces rapid multi-stop pins across many countries, right side shows fewer pins with expanding radius circles indicating depth of stay
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: This is fundamentally a geographic and temporal argument that lives on a map; a before/after static frame cannot show the frantic breadth vs. rooted depth contrast the way animated route overlays on actual geography can
recommendations.schema_sketch: { route_a: { label: 'Traditional', stops: [{ location, country, days: 1 }], line_style: 'dashed-fast' }, route_b: { label: 'Immersion', stops: [{ location, country, days: 3 }], depth_radius: number, line_style: 'solid-slow' }, map_region: string, split_mode: 'side-by-side' | 'overlay-toggle' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: blood work, genetic testing, fitness evaluations, sleep studies before you board
recommendations.one_line_description: A stylized pre-boarding health profile card that animates in biometric data rings (sleep, genetics, fitness, nutrition) filling progressively, mimicking a real medical onboarding UI
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A checkmark list makes this feel like a hotel amenity menu; an animated biometric dashboard makes it feel like the premium medical product it actually is, selling the credibility of wellness cruising visually
recommendations.schema_sketch: { metrics: [{ label: 'Blood work', icon: string, fill_percent: number, color: string }], card_style: 'medical' | 'premium', animation: 'sequential_fill', passenger_name_placeholder: boolean, subtitle: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Royal Caribbean twenty-billion-dollar decision selling off floating cities building floating neighborhoods
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated horizontal timeline showing ship silhouettes shrinking in size across years, with fleet count and average passenger capacity displayed as live-updating numbers below each year marker
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A $20B strategic fleet pivot is a multi-year physical transformation story — silhouettes of ships visibly downsizing across a timeline communicates industrial scale shift in a way no single spotlight statistic can
recommendations.schema_sketch: { years: [{ year: number, avg_ship_capacity: number, fleet_size: number, silhouette_scale: number }], investment_label: '$20B', highlight_year: number, animation: 'scrub' | 'auto-play', color_scheme: 'brand' | 'neutral' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: improved cardiovascular health by measurable metrics better sleep patterns six months later
recommendations.one_line_description: A dual-panel animated line chart showing the same passenger's biometric curves (weight, cardiovascular score, sleep quality) at embarkation vs. six months post-cruise, with delta callouts
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Health outcomes are longitudinal data — they need a before/after chart with time on the x-axis to show permanence, not a static stat split, which is precisely what makes the 'not vacation weight loss' claim believable
recommendations.schema_sketch: { metrics: [{ name: string, unit: string, before_value: number, after_value: number, delta_label: string }], time_labels: { t0: 'Embarkation', t1: '6 months later' }, chart_style: 'line' | 'area', highlight_color: string, annotation: string }

runs.title: Mediterranean Cruises

recommendations.narration_excerpt: ships dock in twelve different countries across seventeen days
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated dotted ship-path tracing port stops sequentially across a geographic map, with port name labels pulsing on arrival and a running day counter
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: map_highlight statically shades regions, but the narration is about movement and accumulation across a multi-stop journey — an animated sequential route makes the 'twelve countries, seventeen days' feel viscerally large and operationally complex, which is what makes it threatening
recommendations.schema_sketch: { ports: [{ name, lat, lng, day_number, country }], ship_icon, path_color, stop_pulse_color, day_counter_visible: boolean, loop: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: six passengers assigned to that specific cabin have disappeared or died
recommendations.one_line_description: A stylized overhead floor-plan of a single ship cabin with dated incident markers appearing one by one as pins, each pin expanding to a one-line fate label
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: a number statistic alone abstracts the horror — anchoring each incident to the physical space of one room makes the pattern feel concrete and inescapable, which is the entire narrative point of the 'suicide room' section
recommendations.schema_sketch: { room_label, incidents: [{ date, victim_name_or_anon, fate_label, pin_color }], reveal_mode: 'sequential' | 'all_at_once', room_outline_style: 'blueprint' | 'realistic' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: thirty-one passengers haven't returned by seven thirty PM departure
recommendations.one_line_description: A grid of uniform human-silhouette icons representing total disembarked passengers, with silhouettes animating to 'gone' state (fading to red outlines) one cluster at a time until thirty-one remain hollow
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: big_number renders 31 as an abstraction, but a silhouette grid makes viewers feel the mass of individual people vanishing — it visualizes the proportion and the humanity simultaneously, which directly serves the 'they can't come back' reveal
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_disembarked, missing_count, icon_style: 'silhouette' | 'person', missing_color, present_color, animation_speed_ms, grid_columns }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: spend more money on legal settlements than they do on passenger safety
recommendations.one_line_description: A single horizontal bar that animates splitting into two labeled segments — settlements vs. safety spend — with dollar amounts counting up inside each segment as they grow
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: scale_balance is iconographic and static; this narration is specifically about proportional budget allocation across two competing line items, which demands a quantified proportional visual that makes the imbalance legible at a glance rather than metaphorical
recommendations.schema_sketch: { segments: [{ label, amount, color }], bar_height, currency_symbol, animation_duration_ms, show_percentage: boolean, emphasis_segment_index }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: keycards inactive, last known locations captured on malfunctioning security cameras
recommendations.one_line_description: A simulated CCTV monitor UI showing a timestamped corridor feed that cuts to static mid-playback, with a blinking 'SIGNAL LOST' overlay and a redacted incident log beneath
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: evidence_board shows collected documents pinned together, but this moment is specifically about surveillance footage failing at a critical instant — a CCTV simulation that visually 'loses' the subject creates the exact dread of the cover-up in motion rather than its aftermath
recommendations.schema_sketch: { camera_id, location_label, timestamp_start, timestamp_cutout, static_duration_ms, signal_lost_text, incident_log_lines: [{ time, entry, redacted: boolean }] }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: passengers who fit specific profiles get marked before they even disembark
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated passenger manifest row that scrolls through profile attributes — age, gender, travel-party size, payment method — with a targeting reticle locking onto rows that match a hidden filter criteria, flagging them red
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: evidence_board is static and document-like, but the narration describes an active selection process — a scanning/filtering animation makes the algorithmic, predatory nature of 'profiling before disembarkation' viscerally legible rather than merely alleged
recommendations.schema_sketch: { manifest_rows: [{ passenger_id, age, gender, party_size, payment_method, flagged: boolean }], filter_criteria_visible: boolean, reticle_color, flag_color, scan_speed_ms, redact_names: boolean }

runs.title: Alaskan cruises-name and rate cruise line pros & cons

recommendations.narration_excerpt: The ship carries three thousand. Do the math.
recommendations.one_line_description: A silhouette crowd grid that fills proportionally to show what fraction of a total population fits a condition — like 200 of 3000 people highlighted
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The gut-punch of 200 vs 3000 is visceral and spatial — you need to see the tiny lit cluster drowning in a sea of grey figures, which no existing ratio or stat component renders as a human-scale crowd
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_count: number, highlighted_count: number, highlight_label: string, total_label: string, icon_shape: 'person' | 'dot', highlight_color: string, grid_columns: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: caught in Alaska, frozen, shipped to Seattle, processed, then shipped back
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated geographic loop map tracing a product's multi-stop journey with labeled waypoints, directional arrows, and irony-highlighting at the origin/end match
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The absurdity of salmon traveling Alaska→Seattle→Alaska is inherently a looping geographic animation — roadmap_journey is linear and non-geographic, so it cannot show the circular waste or actual map distance that makes this narration land
recommendations.schema_sketch: { stops: [{ location: string, lat: number, lng: number, action_label: string }], loop_closes: boolean, highlight_stop_index: number, line_color: string, animation_duration_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Misty Fjords. Tracy Arm. Places where the water's so narrow you can reach out and touch
recommendations.one_line_description: A top-down or cross-section diagram placing a to-scale ship silhouette inside a waterway outline, showing clearance margins with labeled widths for both vessel and passage
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The entire point is physical scale — how narrow the waterway is relative to the ship — which is a spatial comparison that a photograph cannot convey but a dimensioned diagram communicates immediately and memorably
recommendations.schema_sketch: { waterway_name: string, waterway_width_meters: number, ship_name: string, ship_beam_meters: number, clearance_label: string, view_mode: 'top_down' | 'cross_section', highlight_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Ketchikan gets fourteen hours instead of eight. Juneau gets overnight stays.
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal bar chart where each row is a port, and color-coded bars extend to show hours docked per cruise line, animating in line by line for dramatic comparison
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Shore time is the entire competitive differentiator being argued here — a table renders it as text, but an animated duration bar makes the extra hours feel tangible and lets viewers instantly see which line wins each port at a glance
recommendations.schema_sketch: { ports: [{ name: string, lines: [{ cruise_line: string, hours_docked: number, includes_overnight: boolean, color: string }] }], max_hours_axis: number, animate_stagger_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Ten out of ten for service. Five out of ten for authenticity.
recommendations.one_line_description: A split scorecard that rates two opposing dimensions simultaneously with independent scales, visual icons per axis, and a tension-gap indicator between the two scores
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Every cruise line in this video earns two separate verdicts on different axes — comfort vs wilderness, service vs authenticity — but review_stars collapses them into a single number, losing the exact tension that is the video's central thesis
recommendations.schema_sketch: { line_name: string, axes: [{ label: string, score: number, max: number, icon: string, color: string }], tension_label: string, show_gap_indicator: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: The Alaska you can visit today won't exist in twenty years.
recommendations.one_line_description: A side-scrolling timeline with a glacier silhouette that visually shrinks at labeled year markers, ending with a projected outline versus current outline overlaid
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: This is the emotional climax and the only data-backed urgency claim in the entire video — rendering it as a text spotlight wastes the one moment that deserves a visceral animated visual showing actual disappearance over time
recommendations.schema_sketch: { glacier_name: string, waypoints: [{ year: number, volume_percent_remaining: number, label: string }], projection_year: number, current_year: number, recession_color: string, baseline_color: string }

runs.title: Alaskan videos-WHAT TO KNOW

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Alaska has more federal land than most countries have total territory
recommendations.one_line_description: Side-by-side silhouette map overlays scaling one region against another with labeled area figures beneath each shape
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A raw number like '600 million acres' is cognitively abstract, but seeing Alaska's federal land mass rendered as a silhouette dwarfing recognizable countries makes the scale viscerally obvious in a way a big_number cannot.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { primary_region: { label, silhouette_svg, area_acres }, comparison_regions: [{ label, silhouette_svg, area_acres }], highlight_color, annotation_text }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: land that looks public on maps might be owned by one of more than two hundred village corporations
recommendations.one_line_description: An interactive-style map of Alaska that animates through stacked land-ownership layers — federal, state, Native corporation, private — each appearing as a distinct color wash with a legend
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The entire point of this narration is that invisible ownership boundaries exist beneath a deceptively uniform landscape; a layered map visualization makes that invisible complexity literally visible in a way a full-screen AI image cannot.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { base_map_region, layers: [{ label, color, opacity, area_label }], animation_sequence: 'stack' | 'reveal', legend_position }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: summer temperatures can hit ninety degrees while winter drops to negative sixty
recommendations.one_line_description: A vertical thermometer-style bar spanning the full recorded temperature range for a location, with labeled markers at key thresholds and a sliding highlight showing the human-survivable zone
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The 150-degree swing is the dramatic hook, and a thermometer range bar communicates that extreme span as a single continuous visual object — the sheer distance between the two endpoints lands harder than two static numbers side by side.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { location_label, temp_min_f, temp_max_f, highlight_zones: [{ label, min, max, color }], unit: 'F' | 'C', animate_sweep: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Alaska has more than three million square miles — fewer paved roads than Rhode Island
recommendations.one_line_description: Two scaled map outlines side by side — Alaska and Rhode Island — with their actual paved road networks drawn as white lines on dark fill, making the density contrast immediately legible
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: This is a pure visual-density argument; rendering the actual sparseness of Alaska's road lines against Rhode Island's dense web communicates the absurdity of the comparison in one glance rather than requiring the viewer to process a text reveal.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { regions: [{ label, outline_svg, road_network_svg, area_sq_miles, paved_road_miles }], background_color, road_color, scale_to: 'equal_area' | 'equal_size' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: two hundred and twenty-nine federally recognized tribes speaking twenty different languages
recommendations.one_line_description: A map of Alaska with each of the 229 tribal nations represented as a color-coded dot or region polygon, grouped by language family, with a language-family legend and count badges
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is making a diversity-of-jurisdiction argument that is spatial at its core; showing 229 distinct entities distributed across the map makes the 'cultural minefield' metaphor concrete rather than leaving it as an abstract statistic.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { map_region, tribes: [{ name, lat, lon, language_family, corporation_affiliation }], color_by: 'language_family' | 'corporation', show_legend: boolean, dot_size_px }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: eight hundred dollars per flight each way — transportation costs alone can hit five thousand dollars
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal waterfall/cascade bar chart that builds a total production cost by stacking labeled cost segments — permits, flights, gear shipping, accommodation — with a running total that climbs to a final figure
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narrative through scenes 66–67 is fundamentally about cumulative cost shock; a waterfall chart that animates each cost segment snapping into place and visibly growing the total drives that 'costs compound fast in Alaska' message far better than isolated price tags.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { currency, segments: [{ label, amount, color }], animate: boolean, show_running_total: boolean, highlight_segment: string }

runs.title: The future of scuba diving.

recommendations.narration_excerpt: No air bubbles. No nitrogen absorption. No decompression sickness.
recommendations.one_line_description: A semi-transparent human silhouette with animated overlays highlighting specific organs, vessels, or systems with labeled callouts
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is mechanically about what happens inside the lungs and blood during liquid breathing — a body diagram animating oxygen flow through lung tissue would make the physiology viscerally clear in a way a simple checkmark list cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { body_region: string, highlight_zones: [{ label: string, color: string, pulse_animation: boolean }], overlay_mode: 'skeletal' | 'organs' | 'vascular', caption: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: He's about to descend to eight hundred feet.
recommendations.one_line_description: A vertical animated depth ruler showing ocean zones with a diver marker descending in real time, labeled with depth milestones and danger thresholds
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Scenes 33–39 are entirely structured around depth milestones — 100ft, 300ft, 600ft, 800ft — and a single animated descent ruler would unify all of them into a coherent visual journey that a static stat number completely fails to convey
recommendations.schema_sketch: { max_depth_ft: number, milestones: [{ depth_ft: number, label: string, event: string, danger_level: 'safe' | 'warning' | 'lethal' }], animated_marker_label: string, ocean_zone_bands: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: memories, her training, her instincts — all uploaded and enhanced with processing power
recommendations.one_line_description: A split graphic showing a human brain on one side and a circuit/processor schematic on the other, with animated data-stream particles flowing between them
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The core concept here is the transfer of human consciousness into a machine substrate — a static stat block communicates nothing about that transformation, while an animated brain-to-chip transfer graphic makes the conceptual leap tangible
recommendations.schema_sketch: { source_label: string, destination_label: string, transfer_items: [{ name: string, icon: string }], particle_speed: 'slow' | 'fast', color_theme: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Massive transparent domes create neighborhoods connected by pressurized tunnels.
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated architectural cross-section cutaway of a multi-level underwater structure showing labeled zones at different depths, with ambient marine life and light rays
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Scenes 74–90 repeatedly reference layered depth zones, dome structures, and tunnels — a cross-section cutaway is the canonical visual language for explaining layered architecture and would serve five consecutive scenes that AI imagery cannot anchor precisely
recommendations.schema_sketch: { city_name: string, depth_zones: [{ depth_ft: number, zone_label: string, purpose: string, color_band: string }], connectors: [{ from_zone: string, to_zone: string, type: 'tunnel' | 'shaft' }], ambient_elements: ['fish' | 'vents' | 'light_rays'] }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Similar cities are under construction off the coasts of Australia, Chile, and Iceland.
recommendations.one_line_description: A 3D-tilted globe or flat world map with animated location pins showing construction status, depth, and projected completion date per site
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: map_highlight is a static region shader — this scene needs multiple discrete point-locations with per-site metadata (status, depth, population target) conveying a global rollout, which is a fundamentally different spatial-data problem
recommendations.schema_sketch: { projection: 'globe' | 'mercator', sites: [{ name: string, lat: number, lon: number, status: 'planned' | 'under_construction' | 'operational', depth_ft: number, capacity: number, completion_year: number }], animate_pins: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: The ocean contains ninety-nine percent of Earth's livable space.
recommendations.one_line_description: Two side-by-side 3D volumetric blocks — land-habitable space vs. ocean volume — scaled accurately to each other with animated fill and labeled percentages
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The 99% claim is about three-dimensional livable volume, not a simple proportion — a percent_fill bar flattens this into 2D and loses the entire conceptual punch; volumetric blocks make the ocean's spatial dominance immediately shocking
recommendations.schema_sketch: { items: [{ label: string, volume_relative: number, color: string, icon: string }], animate_fill: boolean, display_mode: '3d_blocks' | 'spheres', highlight_winner: boolean }

runs.title: Build Your First budget in 10  Minutes (even if you failed before)

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Fixed bills. Variable spending. Savings.
recommendations.one_line_description: Three side-by-side animated bucket or jar illustrations that visually fill with color as dollar amounts are assigned to each category
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is literally introducing three money containers — an animated filling metaphor makes the categorization visceral and memorable in a way a text list never can
recommendations.schema_sketch: { buckets: [{ label: string, color: string, fill_percent: number, amount: number }], animation_sequence: 'simultaneous' | 'staggered' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Fifty percent needs. Thirty percent wants. Twenty percent savings.
recommendations.one_line_description: A single horizontal bar that animates left-to-right, carving itself into three labeled color segments with percentage callouts that pop in as each segment completes
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A donut chart shows static proportions, but an animated sequential split communicates the rule as a decision process — need first, then want, then save — matching the instructional cadence of the narration
recommendations.schema_sketch: { segments: [{ label: string, percent: number, color: string, dollar_amount: number }], animation_style: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous', show_dollar_labels: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Open Google Sheets and create a new document. Title it My Budget 2026.
recommendations.one_line_description: A screen-recorded-style animated spreadsheet that types in rows, column headers, and values one step at a time in sync with narration cues
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Scenes 51–64 walk through building a real spreadsheet row by row — google_docs is a static screenshot, but the entire instructional value of this section depends on watching the budget assemble itself live
recommendations.schema_sketch: { rows: [{ row_number: number, label: string, value: string | number, style: 'header' | 'item' | 'total', reveal_delay_ms: number }], highlight_color: string, animate_typing: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Tight, but doable if you're intentional about where the money goes.
recommendations.one_line_description: An interactive-looking slider UI showing a take-home income amount, with the three budget category bars below it dynamically rescaling as the income value changes across displayed scenarios
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Scenes 67–73 show multiple income levels to prove the system is universal — a slider visualization communicates scalability far more powerfully than a static side-by-side table
recommendations.schema_sketch: { scenarios: [{ income: number, fixed: number, variable: number, savings: number }], active_scenario_index: number, bar_colors: { fixed: string, variable: string, savings: string }, animate_transition: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Every month you stick to the system, even imperfectly, builds the muscle.
recommendations.one_line_description: A six-month mini calendar grid where each month cell fills in or checks off progressively, with an accumulating streak counter, visualizing compounding consistency over time
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Scenes 82–90 are entirely about the value of imperfect repetition over time — a streak calendar makes the compounding nature of monthly consistency visible in a way a single gauge reading cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { months: [{ label: string, status: 'complete' | 'partial' | 'empty', savings_amount?: number }], streak_count: number, highlight_color: string, show_amounts: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: You make sixty thousand but take home around forty-two thousand after taxes.
recommendations.one_line_description: A waterfall-style bar that starts at gross income and shows sequential downward deduction blocks — taxes, health insurance, retirement — landing on the net take-home amount at the bottom
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's entire point is showing money disappearing through deductions layer by layer — a waterfall decomposition makes each deduction a visible subtraction, which before_after's binary framing completely misses
recommendations.schema_sketch: { gross_income: number, deductions: [{ label: string, amount: number, color: string }], net_income: number, animate_sequential: boolean, currency_symbol: string }

runs.title: Your budget is broken - Here is how to actually fix it

recommendations.narration_excerpt: after just forty-eight hours, people forget thirty-seven percent of their purchases
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated curve showing retention percentage dropping over time, with labeled drop-off points at key intervals (24h, 48h, 1 week)
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration describes a time-based decay process with two specific data points — a curve over time communicates the forgetting slope far more viscerally than a static fill bar that only shows a single percentage
recommendations.schema_sketch: { x_axis_label: string, y_axis_label: string, data_points: [{ time_label: string, retention_percent: number, highlight: boolean }], curve_color: string, annotation_labels: string[] }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: you've already blown past your dining out category by a hundred and twelve dollars
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal budget bar showing the planned limit, a fill representing actual spending that visually bursts past the limit line with an overflow amount labeled in red
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's emotional punch is the overage — spending going beyond a boundary — which requires a single bar with a hard limit line and a visible overflow zone, something a before/after two-panel layout cannot show as a continuous transgression
recommendations.schema_sketch: { category_label: string, budget_amount: number, actual_amount: number, currency_symbol: string, overflow_color: string, limit_line_label: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: two hundred and thirty-seven dollars a month is two thousand eight hundred and forty-four dollars a year
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated split display that shows a monthly figure on the left transforming into annual, 3-year, and 5-year totals on the right with a rolling ticker, optionally labeling each total with a real-world equivalent (e.g., 'a vacation')
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's entire power is the compounding revelation across time horizons — a single rolling money counter only lands one number, while this component would animate the multiplication across multiple timeframes to make the scale feel progressively more shocking
recommendations.schema_sketch: { base_amount: number, base_label: string, projections: [{ period_label: string, total: number, real_world_equivalent: string }], currency_symbol: string, animation_duration_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Marcus found out he was paying for six different streaming services. Six. He thought he was paying for two.
recommendations.one_line_description: A grid of app/service logo tiles that animate in one by one, with a running total cost ticker at the bottom and tiles color-coded between 'known' (e.g., white) and 'forgotten' (e.g., red)
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The visual shock of six logos appearing when you expected two — especially with forgotten ones highlighted differently — is a far stronger concrete reveal than a stat beside an image, and directly mirrors the discovery experience being described
recommendations.schema_sketch: { services: [{ name: string, logo_url: string, monthly_cost: number, was_forgotten: boolean }], total_label: string, known_color: string, forgotten_color: string, animate_in_sequence: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: you've got a hundred dollars left, and there are still ten days in the month
recommendations.one_line_description: A dual-axis visual showing remaining budget dollars on one axis and remaining days in the month on the other, with a diagonal 'safe zone' band and a plotted current position dot indicating whether spending pace is sustainable
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration explicitly juxtaposes two dimensions — money remaining vs. time remaining — which is a spatial tension that no single-axis meter or alert notification can express; this component would make the collision between those two countdowns immediately readable
recommendations.schema_sketch: { budget_remaining: number, days_remaining: number, total_budget: number, total_days: number, safe_zone_color: string, danger_zone_color: string, current_dot_label: string, daily_burn_rate: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: sixty percent of what she bought at Target wasn't food
recommendations.one_line_description: A stylized receipt visual where line items are color-coded by category, with a live-tallying footer showing each category subtotal as items animate in, making misclassification visually obvious
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is specifically about hidden category mixing inside a single merchant transaction — a receipt metaphor with color-coded line items makes the 'groceries vs. not groceries' split tangible in a way a percent fill bar completely loses
recommendations.schema_sketch: { merchant_name: string, items: [{ description: string, amount: number, category: string, category_color: string }], category_totals: [{ category: string, total: number, percent_of_receipt: number }], animate_sequential: boolean }

runs.title: The Budget that actually builds Wealth- Plan 6 months ahead

recommendations.narration_excerpt: put fifty-four dollars aside every month — by December, six hundred and fifty dollars waiting
recommendations.one_line_description: A jar or container that visually fills month-by-month as fixed contributions stack toward a labeled target amount, with each month's deposit animating in as a distinct colored layer
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A line chart implies volatility and trend — this narration is about predictable, mechanical accumulation toward a fixed goal, which a filling vessel communicates instantly and emotionally without axes or gridlines
recommendations.schema_sketch: { monthly_contribution: number, target_amount: number, months: number, container_label: string, currency_symbol: string, fill_color: string, target_label: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: months that look totally normal and months that look terrifying
recommendations.one_line_description: A six-month calendar grid where each month cell is color-coded by total irregular expense load — cool for light months, hot/red for heavy months — with dollar totals displayed inside each cell
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is explicitly about scanning across a six-month horizon to spot dangerous months at a glance; a heatmap calendar makes that danger visible spatially in a way no split-image or stat card can replicate
recommendations.schema_sketch: { months: [{ label: string, total_irregular: number, expenses: [{ name: string, amount: number }] }], low_color: string, high_color: string, currency_symbol: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: five thousand dollars by December thirty-first — four hundred and seventeen dollars monthly starting now
recommendations.one_line_description: A goal amount at the top with an animated backward-calculation cascade showing deadline → months remaining → required monthly amount → required weekly amount, each value revealing in sequence
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The entire point of this segment is the backwards calculation logic — the cognitive 'working backwards' move — and no existing component visualizes a multi-step reverse derivation; a static stat split only shows the answer, not the method that makes the strategy credible
recommendations.schema_sketch: { goal_amount: number, deadline_label: string, months_remaining: number, monthly_required: number, weekly_required: number, currency_symbol: string, accent_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: less than one thousand saved spends eight percent — more than five thousand saved spends less than two percent
recommendations.one_line_description: A two-cluster dot plot showing households plotted by savings level on the X-axis against percent of income lost to fees and emergencies on the Y-axis, with a visible cliff-edge threshold line at the five-thousand-dollar savings mark
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is making a threshold argument — that crossing a specific savings level produces a dramatic, non-linear drop in financial friction costs — which a scatter with a threshold line communicates as a structural insight rather than just two numbers side by side
recommendations.schema_sketch: { x_label: string, y_label: string, threshold_value: number, threshold_label: string, clusters: [{ label: string, savings_range: string, avg_pct_lost: number, dot_color: string, dot_count: number }], currency_symbol: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Year one stop overdrafting. Year two real emergency fund. Year three investing. Year five making money on money
recommendations.one_line_description: A vertical ladder or staircase where each rung represents a year, with the rung height scaling non-linearly to reflect compounding momentum — early rungs close together, later rungs visibly further apart — and each step labeled with its milestone and a net-worth delta indicator
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A standard timeline treats all years as equal intervals, but the narration's power is the compounding acceleration — later years are qualitatively larger leaps — and a ladder with non-linear spacing visually encodes that the payoff is back-weighted, which is the emotional core of the argument
recommendations.schema_sketch: { steps: [{ year: number, milestone_label: string, delta_label: string, rung_weight: number }], accent_color: string, show_compound_curve: boolean, currency_symbol: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: three thousand in checking but none labeled for car repairs — feels like you can't afford it
recommendations.one_line_description: A row of visual containers (buckets or envelopes) each with a label and fill level, showing how the same total balance looks either as one unlabeled pool versus multiple purpose-assigned buckets that cover known expenses
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is making a purely visual argument about money organization — same dollars, different psychological reality depending on whether they are partitioned — and no existing component shows parallel allocation states of an identical balance
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_balance: number, currency_symbol: string, views: [{ view_label: string, buckets: [{ label: string, amount: number, color: string, is_deficit: boolean }] }] }

runs.title: Buy These 5 Cryptos Before 2026 Ends (Last Chance?)

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Accumulation phase. Markup phase. Distribution phase. Markdown phase. Repeat.
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated circular dial divided into four named arc segments that highlights the current active phase and rotates to show progression through a repeating cycle
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is explicitly about a repeating circular structure — a linear roadmap metaphor fights the geometry of the idea, while a rotating wheel makes the 'repeat' nature viscerally obvious
recommendations.schema_sketch: { phases: [{ label, duration_months, color }], current_phase_index: number, cycle_count: number, animation: 'clockwise_sweep' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: ten thousand dollars worth of Solana would've had four point two million dollars
recommendations.one_line_description: A side-by-side animated panel showing a starting dollar amount on the left inflating in real time into a final value on the right, anchored to a dated price chart underneath both figures
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A simple counter ticking up loses the causal story — pairing the growing dollar figure directly above the actual Solana price curve for that 12-month window makes the gain feel earned rather than abstract
recommendations.schema_sketch: { asset: string, start_date: string, end_date: string, start_value_usd: number, end_value_usd: number, price_series: [{ date, price }], animation_duration_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Bank of America can send ten million dollars to Tokyo in three seconds for twelve cents
recommendations.one_line_description: A dual-lane animated race graphic where two labeled transaction bars travel from a source city to a destination city at visually proportional speeds, with cost and time stamps appearing on arrival
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The comparison_table presents this as static rows, but the entire emotional punch of the XRP argument is kinetic — one transfer is instantaneous and cheap, the other is glacially slow and expensive, which only lands if the viewer watches the contrast happen
recommendations.schema_sketch: { route: { from: string, to: string }, options: [{ label, transfer_time_seconds, cost_usd, color }], animation: 'simultaneous_race' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: two billion people who own smartphones but don't have bank accounts
recommendations.one_line_description: A dense grid of small human-figure icons representing a total population, where a highlighted subset fills in progressively to represent the target segment with a live counter
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A raw number like '2 billion' is cognitively flat, but watching two billion dots out of a global population grid illuminate makes the addressable market feel physically massive rather than rhetorically large
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_population: number, highlighted_segment: number, segment_label: string, icon_shape: 'person' | 'dot', highlight_color: string, fill_animation: 'sweep' | 'random' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Fifty percent of your crypto allocation goes to Bitcoin and Ethereum
recommendations.one_line_description: An interactive-style animated allocation bar that fills segment by segment as each asset is named, with risk-level color coding and a live percentage label snapping into place per segment
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A static donut renders all allocations simultaneously, collapsing the narrative build across scenes 98–107 into a single glance; an allocation bar that constructs itself one asset at a time as the voiceover names each coin holds attention across the full sequence
recommendations.schema_sketch: { segments: [{ asset_name, allocation_percent, risk_level: 'low'|'medium'|'high'|'speculative', color }], reveal_mode: 'sequential', show_risk_label: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: we're in month thirty-seven of the current cycle. Thirteen months left before the next markdown phase
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal progress bar representing the full 48-month cycle with a scrubber marker at the current month, elapsed portion filled, remaining portion color-coded by phase, and a ticking months-remaining counter in the corner
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A gauge meter implies a pressure or intensity reading, but the narration is about elapsed vs. remaining time within a fixed-length window — a segmented timeline bar with a live countdown communicates both position and urgency simultaneously
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_months: number, current_month: number, phases: [{ label, start_month, end_month, color }], show_countdown: boolean, urgency_threshold_months: number }

runs.title: Why Big Money Is Quietly Buying Crypto Right Now

recommendations.narration_excerpt: spend six to eighteen months building positions, then retail investors notice
recommendations.one_line_description: A dual-layer animated timeline showing two actor groups moving through the same phases at offset intervals, with a visible lag gap between them
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The core visual message is the temporal gap between institutional and retail entry — a standard roadmap shows sequence but cannot show two parallel actors moving through identical phases with a deliberate delay, which is the entire point of the narration
recommendations.schema_sketch: { phases: [{ label, duration_weeks }], actors: [{ name, color, start_phase_offset }], gap_highlight_label, x_axis_unit }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Institutions control roughly eighty-seven percent of all trading volume
recommendations.one_line_description: A field of animated dots where each dot represents a unit of trading volume, dynamically clustering into two color-coded groups to show proportional control
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: An 87/13 split needs visceral scale to land emotionally — a percent_fill bar is abstract, but watching hundreds of particles magnetically pull toward the institutional cluster makes the dominance feel overwhelming and real in a way a single fill bar cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_particles: number, segments: [{ label, percent, color }], animation_style: 'cluster' | 'sweep', duration_ms }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: institutional buying increased by two hundred and thirty percent while retail selling hit record highs
recommendations.one_line_description: A split animated chart where two opposing trend lines move in real time in opposite vertical directions from a shared center axis, with magnitude labels on each arm
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration describes simultaneous opposite behaviors — one group aggressively buying while the other panic-sells — which is a divergence story that needs two lines moving away from each other, not a side-by-side static stat card
recommendations.schema_sketch: { event_label, axis_zero_label, series: [{ name, direction: 'up' | 'down', magnitude_percent, color }], time_period }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: buying it for their private wealth clients, their institutional accounts, and their own balance sheet
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated org-chart style visual showing a single entity at the top distributing flows downward into labeled destination buckets with proportional flow-width pipes
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration reveals a hidden multi-channel accumulation structure — one buyer, three distinct destinations — which is an allocation flow diagram, not a bullet list; the visual hierarchy and pipe widths would communicate scale and strategy that three_points completely flattens
recommendations.schema_sketch: { source: { name, logo_slug }, destinations: [{ label, allocation_percent, color }], animation: 'flow' | 'reveal', currency_unit }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Bitcoin's volatility has dropped forty-three percent since institutional ETFs launched
recommendations.one_line_description: A price chart overlaid with a dynamically shrinking shaded band representing volatility range, showing the envelope visibly compressing over time as a key second data layer
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A plain line chart shows price but cannot show volatility compression — the entire argument depends on visualizing the narrowing range around price movement, which requires a dedicated band/envelope layer that animated_line_chart does not support
recommendations.schema_sketch: { asset_name, data_points: [{ date, price, volatility_band_width }], milestone_marker: { date, label }, band_color, reduction_callout_percent }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: consistent buying pressure from large wallets over multiple weeks, that's institutional accumulation
recommendations.one_line_description: A calendar-grid heatmap of on-chain wallet activity where each cell represents one day, color intensity encodes transaction volume from large wallets, and clusters of intensity are circled and labeled
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is literally teaching the viewer to read accumulation patterns over time from on-chain data — this is a temporal pattern-recognition visual that cannot be conveyed by a static AI image, and no existing chart type in the library handles the calendar-grid heat encoding needed
recommendations.schema_sketch: { asset, weeks_shown, cells: [{ date, large_wallet_volume, intensity_score }], threshold_label, highlight_clusters: [{ start_date, end_date, annotation }] }

runs.title: How Money Works (Why No One Teach You This)

recommendations.narration_excerpt: average American pays 42% of their income in taxes
recommendations.one_line_description: A stacked bar or layered block diagram where each tax type visually consumes a slice of a single income dollar, with labels and percentages animating in layer by layer
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's entire power comes from the accumulation of hidden tax layers — a single big number flattens that discovery arc, while a layered visual builds the dread incrementally as each tax type lands on top of the last
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_income: number, layers: [{ label: string, percent: number, color: string }], final_total_percent: number, animation_style: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: invested that money and earned 10% annually, worth $155,000 by 65
recommendations.one_line_description: A single starting dollar amount that visually forks into two animated growth curves — one exponential (invested) and one flat (savings account) — with a dramatic gap that widens over a timeline marked in decades
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is explicitly comparing two diverging futures from one identical starting point; a standard line chart lacks the visual drama of the fork moment and the yawning gap that makes the opportunity cost feel visceral
recommendations.schema_sketch: { start_amount: number, start_age: number, end_age: number, path_a: { label: string, annual_rate: number, color: string }, path_b: { label: string, annual_rate: number, color: string }, milestone_ages: number[] }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Warren Buffett pays effective tax rate of 17.4%, less than half what you pay
recommendations.one_line_description: A side-by-side portrait comparison panel — one labeled 'You' and one labeled a named public figure — each with a fill-meter animating up to their respective effective tax rate, making the disparity feel personal and immediate
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The stat comparison component shows numbers side by side but lacks the identity contrast the narration is weaponizing; the face-off format makes the viewer feel the injustice against themselves rather than between two abstract percentages
recommendations.schema_sketch: { entity_a: { label: string, avatar_type: 'generic_silhouette' | 'icon', effective_rate: number, color: string }, entity_b: { label: string, avatar_type: 'generic_silhouette' | 'icon', effective_rate: number, color: string }, annotation: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: average managed mutual fund charges 1.3% annually, reduces your lifetime returns
recommendations.one_line_description: A waterfall chart showing a projected portfolio balance being stepped down by recurring fee extractions year by year, with the cumulative amount lost to fees rendered as a filled red zone beneath the 'what you kept' bar
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is making a compounding-loss argument — fees aren't a one-time cut but a continuous drain that compounds in reverse — and a static before/after hides that temporal erosion, while a waterfall makes the bleeding visible across time
recommendations.schema_sketch: { starting_balance: number, annual_return_gross: number, annual_fee_percent: number, years: number, bar_color_kept: string, bar_color_lost: string, final_loss_label: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: average person loses two to three million dollars in potential wealth over their lifetime
recommendations.one_line_description: A real-time animated counter that ticks upward showing 'wealth lost so far today' based on a lifetime loss figure divided into a per-second rate, with a secondary display showing the cumulative loss since age 25
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A money_counter shows a static or simple count-up to a number, but the narration's emotional hook is that the loss is happening right now, constantly, in real time — a clock framing converts an abstract lifetime stat into an immediate, personal urgency
recommendations.schema_sketch: { lifetime_loss: number, working_years: number, per_second_rate: number, viewer_assumed_age: number, display_modes: ['per_second', 'cumulative_from_age'], accent_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: trading time for money like employee you get punished, building assets like owner you get rewarded
recommendations.one_line_description: A 2x2 grid visual where axes are 'income source' (earned vs. capital) and 'tax treatment' (punished vs. rewarded), with icons populating each quadrant and color-coded penalty/reward indicators
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is describing a structural rule — a coded system with two distinct behavioral categories and two distinct outcomes — which is a matrix logic that a tilting balance scale cannot convey; the matrix makes the system's design feel deliberate and learnable
recommendations.schema_sketch: { quadrants: [{ x_label: string, y_label: string, examples: string[], tax_rate_range: string, sentiment: 'punished' | 'rewarded', icon: string, color: string }], axis_x_label: string, axis_y_label: string }

runs.title: The Missing Page of Think and Grow Rich — What the 1937 Edition Was Really Hiding

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Vague desire creates vague effort. Vague effort creates vague results.
recommendations.one_line_description: A three-row visual cascade where each row shows a labeled input dissolving into a progressively blurrier or smaller output, illustrating compounding vagueness
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is explicitly about a chain reaction of diminishing specificity — a static bullet list cannot show the compounding nature of that degradation the way a visual cascade can.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { rows: [{ label: string, clarity_level: 0-100, output_label: string }], animation: 'sequential_fade', arrow_style: 'degrading_dotted' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: The wealthy made decisions fast and changed them slowly. Everyone else made decisions slowly and changed them fast.
recommendations.one_line_description: Two parallel horizontal timelines side by side — one showing a fast decision point followed by a long stable hold, the other showing a slow crawling decision followed by rapid reversals — with labeled inflection markers
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: This is a speed-and-pattern contrast that lives in time, not in static values — two timelines with distinct velocity signatures would make the behavioral gap viscerally obvious where compare_reveal only handles a before/after snapshot.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { tracks: [{ label: string, decision_speed_ms: number, reversal_count: number, color: string, events: [{ t: number, type: 'decide'|'reverse', label: string }] }] }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: The steel worker saving for his machine shop quit twice.
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal savings/progress bar filling over a timeline with two visible break points — each marked with a cause label and a restart marker — ending at goal completion
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The story is structurally about sustained progress punctuated by two specific collapses and restarts; a progress bar with labeled interruptions makes the emotional arc of persistence legible at a glance in a way no stat or image can.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { goal_label: string, total_duration_months: number, segments: [{ start: number, end: number, status: 'active'|'quit'|'restart' }], break_labels: [{ month: number, reason: string }], goal_reached_month: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Most people love the thinking part. They'll spend hours visualizing success. Days planning. Weeks researching.
recommendations.one_line_description: Two side-by-side donut charts — one labeled 'Most People' showing dominant slices for thinking/planning/researching with a tiny action slice, one labeled 'Hill's Wealthy' showing the inverse — with animated slice reveals
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is explicitly about proportional time allocation, which is the native language of a donut chart; a fullscreen image leaves the quantitative contrast invisible and forces the viewer to infer what the visual could directly show.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { panels: [{ label: string, slices: [{ activity: string, percent: number, color: string }] }], animation: 'sequential_arc_draw', highlight_slice: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: The problem isn't access to information. It's the gap between knowing and doing.
recommendations.one_line_description: A split-platform diagram showing two cliffs labeled 'KNOWING' and 'DOING' with a visible gap between them, an animated figure standing at the edge of the first cliff, and a bridge rendered as a dotted outline labeled 'Application'
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The word 'gap' is doing heavy lifting in this narration — the behavioral chasm between knowing and doing is the entire thesis of the video, and a literal visual chasm with a missing bridge externalizes the abstract concept far more powerfully than a quote card.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { left_label: string, right_label: string, gap_label: string, bridge_label: string, bridge_state: 'missing'|'partial'|'complete', figure_position: 'left_edge'|'mid_bridge'|'right_edge', color_scheme: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: The wealthy people Hill studied had one thing in common.
recommendations.one_line_description: Multiple labeled nodes representing different wealthy individuals radiating inward with animated arrows converging on a single central trait node, visualizing 'one thing in common' across diverse people
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is structurally a convergence story — many different people, one shared behavior — and a radial convergence diagram makes that pattern legible instantly, whereas a vintage film image simply illustrates the era rather than the analytical finding.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { central_trait: string, nodes: [{ name: string, descriptor: string, icon_style: 'portrait'|'initials' }], animation: 'staggered_arrow_draw', color_scheme: string, center_emphasis: 'pulse'|'glow' }

runs.title: Everything We Know About Our Closest Solar System (Alpha Centauri)

recommendations.narration_excerpt: orbit each other every eighty years, sometimes coming as close as Saturn
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated top-down diagram of two or more bodies tracing elliptical orbits around a shared barycenter, with distance markers and period labels
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is fundamentally about orbital geometry and the range of separation between two stars — a spatial, animated diagram conveys the eccentric ellipse and distance extremes far more precisely than a static image with a stat overlay
recommendations.schema_sketch: { bodies: [{ name, size_relative, color }], orbit_type: 'elliptical' | 'circular', period_years, min_distance: { value, unit, reference_body }, max_distance: { value, unit, reference_body }, animate: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: cut the journey time to Alpha Centauri down to centuries instead of millennia
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal bar chart where each bar represents a propulsion method, scaled to show journey duration from years to millennia, with a glowing highlight on the shortest option
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is explicitly comparing multiple propulsion technologies by travel time across orders of magnitude — a logarithmic duration bar gives that compression visceral impact in a way a bullet list completely fails to
recommendations.schema_sketch: { entries: [{ label, duration_years, category: 'current' | 'near_future' | 'theoretical', highlight: boolean }], scale: 'linear' | 'log', unit_display: 'years' | 'auto' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: accelerate these micro-probes to twenty percent the speed of light
recommendations.one_line_description: A radial or linear gauge that fills from 0% to a marked percentage of the speed of light, with reference tick marks for notable human-made speeds like Voyager and New Horizons
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Twenty percent of light speed is meaningless without context — a gauge anchored to real reference speeds (Voyager at 0.006%, New Horizons at 0.005%) transforms an abstract number into a viscerally comprehensible leap
recommendations.schema_sketch: { target_percent_c: number, reference_marks: [{ label, percent_c }], animate_fill: boolean, display_mph: boolean, color_ramp: [string] }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: right now, something on Proxima b is looking back at us
recommendations.one_line_description: A split-space visual showing two planets facing each other across a starfield with a faint dotted sightline beam connecting them, and a light-travel-time label on the beam
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's entire emotional payload is the mutual, symmetrical act of two civilizations looking at each other across an impossible gap — a sightline diagram with a labeled delay makes that reciprocal longing concrete rather than abstract
recommendations.schema_sketch: { planet_a: { name, appearance_hint }, planet_b: { name, appearance_hint }, distance_light_years: number, beam_label: string, animate_pulse: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: how common life might be, and how utterly impossible it is to reach it
recommendations.one_line_description: A two-axis scatter plot where X is distance from Earth and Y is estimated habitability likelihood, with named exoplanets plotted as labeled dots and a shaded unreachable zone
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The scene crystallizes the video's central tension — proximity of hope versus impossibility of contact — and a scatter plot with an explicit unreachable zone boundary makes that structural irony visually undeniable rather than philosophical
recommendations.schema_sketch: { planets: [{ name, distance_ly, habitability_score, highlight: boolean }], unreachable_threshold_ly: number, axes: { x_label, y_label }, shaded_zone_label: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: four hours to snap photos and beam data back to Earth before disappearing
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal mission arc timeline showing launch, transit years, arrival window, and data-return delay as distinct labeled phases, with a tiny highlighted sliver representing the active window
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The emotional gut-punch is the grotesque ratio of a 20-year journey to a 4-hour window followed by a 4-year return signal delay — a proportional timeline makes that absurdity visible in a single glance where a stat overlay cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { phases: [{ label, duration_value, duration_unit, color, highlight: boolean }], total_mission_years: number, show_proportional: boolean, active_window_label: string }

runs.title: This Is Why The Universe Is SILENT... (The Zoo Hypothesis)

recommendations.narration_excerpt: expanding civilization should fill the entire galaxy over time
recommendations.one_line_description: A top-down galaxy or grid map where a glowing origin point pulses outward in concentric waves, each ring labeled with a time epoch (1M yrs, 10M yrs, etc.), visually filling the space
