recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is explicitly spatial — a civilization radiating outward across star systems — which a line chart cannot convey; a radial expansion map makes the inevitability of galactic colonization viscerally obvious
recommendations.schema_sketch: { origin_label: string, wave_intervals: [{ years: number, label: string, color: string }], galaxy_background: boolean, animation_duration_seconds: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Not just technological, but social. Emotional. Spiritual.
recommendations.one_line_description: A radar/spider chart with 5–7 labeled axes (Technology, Social Cohesion, Emotional Maturity, Environmental Stewardship, etc.) showing humanity's current profile as a partially-filled polygon against a 'threshold' outline
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration explicitly frames civilizational readiness as a multi-dimensional scorecard, not a list — a radar chart visualizes the gap between where humanity is and where the threshold lies across all dimensions simultaneously
recommendations.schema_sketch: { axes: [{ label: string, current_value: number, threshold_value: number }], current_label: string, threshold_label: string, color_current: string, color_threshold: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: A probe might malfunction and become visible. A monitoring device might be detected.
recommendations.one_line_description: A simulated radar or sensor-screen display showing a blip or anomalous contact appearing briefly, with scan lines, signal-strength bars, and a classified-style timestamp overlay — then fading out
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration describes instruments detecting something fleeting and anomalous; a radar/sensor aesthetic communicates the accidental detection narrative far more viscerally than a static evidence board
recommendations.schema_sketch: { screen_type: 'radar' | 'spectrograph' | 'thermal', blip_label: string, coordinates: { x: number, y: number }, signal_strength: number, timestamp: string, fade_after_seconds: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: a message broadcast simultaneously to every radio, television, and internet connection
recommendations.one_line_description: A world map where simultaneous transmission rings emanate from a single orbital point, with small device icons (TV, radio, phone, monitor) lighting up across continents in real time as the signal reaches them
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's power is in the word 'simultaneously' — a cascade animation across a globe showing every medium lighting up at once captures the overwhelming, inescapable nature of the contact moment in a way a notification stack cannot
recommendations.schema_sketch: { origin: 'orbit', targets: [{ region: string, device_icons: string[], delay_ms: number }], wave_color: string, map_style: 'dark' | 'satellite', animation_duration_seconds: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Earth sits in the middle of a cosmic zoo where exhibits don't know they're being watched
recommendations.one_line_description: A cutaway schematic diagram showing Earth at center inside a transparent containment sphere, with observer nodes positioned around the perimeter at various distances, connected by dotted sight-lines to the planet
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The Zoo Hypothesis is fundamentally a structural/spatial concept — something is enclosing and observing something else — and a schematic diagram externalizes that containment geometry in a way no photographic or text component can
recommendations.schema_sketch: { subject_label: string, subject_icon: 'earth' | 'custom', containment_layers: [{ radius: number, label: string, opacity: number }], observer_nodes: [{ angle_degrees: number, label: string, sight_line: boolean }], style: 'blueprint' | 'clean' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: They've studied our psychology, our social structures, our capacity for change
recommendations.one_line_description: A classified-file-style data card displaying a species profile with redacted fields, behavioral metrics shown as small bar indicators, observation duration counter, and a case-file number — styled as alien bureaucratic documentation
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration frames humanity as a documented research subject with accumulated behavioral data; a dossier card concretizes the chilling idea that we are already categorized and filed by an external intelligence
recommendations.schema_sketch: { case_id: string, subject_name: string, observation_start: string, metrics: [{ label: string, value: number, max: number }], redacted_fields: string[], classification_level: string, status: 'active' | 'pending' | 'concluded' }

runs.title: Terrifying Things Astronauts Saw in Space That They Couldn’t Explain

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Debris from their own rocket couldn't maintain formation at that range
recommendations.one_line_description: An interactive scale visualization showing two objects in space with a dynamic distance readout between them, animated to illustrate how debris dispersal increases exponentially over distance
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is making a precise spatial-physics argument about why debris cannot maintain formation at millions of miles — a scaled distance diagram with labeled separation vectors would make that argument visually self-evident rather than leaving it abstract
recommendations.schema_sketch: { object_a_label: string, object_b_label: string, distance_value: number, distance_unit: string, dispersion_rate_km_per_hour: number, formation_broken_threshold: number, animate_divergence: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: The longer humans spend in deep space, the more these flashes intensify
recommendations.one_line_description: A mission-duration timeline bar with a radiation dose accumulation overlay that pulses brighter at each flash-event marker, showing the compounding neurological exposure over days in deep space
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: animated_line_chart renders generic x/y data; this narration needs a human-body-in-time visualization where the dose accumulates against a silhouette of a mission arc, making the biological threat visceral rather than statistical
recommendations.schema_sketch: { mission_name: string, duration_days: number, flash_events: [{ day: number, intensity: number, description: string }], exposure_unit: string, danger_threshold: number, body_region_highlight: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Keeping pace with the station. For seven minutes and thirty-three seconds
recommendations.one_line_description: A side-by-side overhead orbital path diagram showing two trajectory lines — the station's and the unknown object's — with timestamps marking where the paths stay parallel versus where debris would have diverged
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The entire evidentiary weight of this scene rests on the claim that the object's path was anomalously stable; a dual-trajectory diagram with a 'where debris should have been' ghost line makes the physics argument visually undeniable rather than asking the viewer to take it on faith
recommendations.schema_sketch: { track_a: { label: string, color: string, path_points: [{ time_s: number, altitude_km: number }] }, track_b: { label: string, color: string, path_points: [{ time_s: number, altitude_km: number }] }, ghost_track: { label: string, diverge_at_second: number }, duration_seconds: number, anomaly_window: { start: number, end: number } }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Some reported seeing geometric patterns so complex they sketched them in their mission logs
recommendations.one_line_description: A aged paper mission-log page rendered with handwritten grid coordinates, a date stamp, and a central area where a geometric sketch animates into existence stroke by stroke, as if being drawn in real time
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: handwritten_note exists but is generic; this narration needs a specifically space-mission-document aesthetic — graph-paper grid, mission date header, and the sketch appearing progressively — to sell the authenticity of astronauts documenting impossible visions
recommendations.schema_sketch: { mission_id: string, date_stamp: string, astronaut_initials: string, sketch_svg_path: string, annotation_text: string, draw_duration_ms: number, paper_texture: 'aged' | 'clean', stamp_label: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: A figure. Humanoid. Walking on the outside of the station
recommendations.one_line_description: A schematic side-elevation diagram of a space station hull with a small human silhouette figure positioned on the exterior surface, no suit indicator, with a pulsing 'vacuum pressure' environmental readout showing conditions incompatible with survival
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: An AI image will render this as a dramatic visual scene, but what makes the moment horrifying is the physics impossibility — a clinical schematic showing exactly WHERE on the hull the figure appeared alongside lethal environmental data creates dread through information rather than through imagery
recommendations.schema_sketch: { station_model: string, figure_position: { x_percent: number, y_percent: number }, figure_label: string, environmental_readouts: [{ metric: string, value: string, status: 'lethal' | 'warning' | 'normal' }], highlight_module: string, animate_figure_disappear: boolean, disappear_after_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: We've sent maybe six hundred people beyond Earth's atmosphere in the entire history
recommendations.one_line_description: A grid of six hundred small human-figure icons representing every person ever sent to space, with the grid dwarfed beside a scale representation of Earth's total population, animating in one dot at a time before zooming out to show the overwhelming ratio
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: spotlight_stat shows a number; this narration is making a profound argument about how vanishingly small our observational sample is — a dot-grid that builds person by person and then zooms out against 8 billion makes the epistemological humility visceral and is a genuinely missing isotype-style component
recommendations.schema_sketch: { space_travelers_count: number, total_population_reference: number, icon_shape: 'human' | 'dot', build_animation: boolean, build_duration_ms: number, zoom_out_to_population: boolean, highlight_color: string, background_color: string, label_space: string, label_population: string }

runs.title: The 5 Strangest Planets in Our Universe

recommendations.narration_excerpt: day side reaches two thousand degrees. Night side drops to nearly absolute zero.
recommendations.one_line_description: A rotating planet graphic split into two visible hemispheres with a continuous color-temperature gradient from deep red/white on one side to deep blue/black on the other, with temperature labels anchored to each pole.
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is fundamentally about a spatial temperature contrast across a single object — a gradient-mapped sphere communicates that extreme divide in one glance where two stacked text numbers cannot.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { hot_side_label: string, hot_side_temp: string, cold_side_label: string, cold_side_temp: string, gradient_colors: [string, string], planet_name: string, rotation_speed_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: planet is hemorrhaging mass at a rate of ten billion tons per second.
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated planet silhouette with a visible stream of particles bleeding off one edge into a star glow, alongside a real-time depleting fill-bar representing remaining planetary mass with a projected extinction timestamp.
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The horror of WASP-12b's story is the relentless, ongoing loss — an animated drain visualization makes that continuous hemorrhaging visceral in a way a static large number cannot.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { planet_name: string, loss_rate_label: string, loss_rate_unit: string, current_mass_percent: number, years_remaining: number, particle_color: string, star_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: reflects less than one percent of the light that hits it.
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal row of sphere icons representing different objects (Earth, Jupiter, coal, asphalt, TrES-2b) each rendered with its actual reflectivity as a visible brightness level, with albedo percentage labels beneath each.
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's power comes from the ranked comparison of reflectivities across familiar objects — a brightness-scaled lineup makes the darkness of TrES-2b immediately and viscerally legible where a single percent-fill bar loses all context.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { items: [{ label: string, albedo_percent: number, icon: string }], highlight_item: string, background_color: string, label_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: strip away Earth's entire atmosphere, then its oceans, then its crust, leaving only the molten iron core.
recommendations.one_line_description: A cross-section sphere animation that sequentially strips away labeled concentric layers (atmosphere, ocean, crust, mantle) one by one until only a glowing core remains, with each removed layer fading outward.
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is explicitly a sequential stripping metaphor — an animated layer-removal diagram maps directly onto that logic and turns an abstract description into a concrete, memorable visual process.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { layers: [{ name: string, color: string, thickness_relative: number }], strip_interval_ms: number, core_color: string, core_label: string, final_hold_ms: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: winds carry clouds of vaporized silicate from day side to night side, where temperature drop turns vapor back into glass.
recommendations.one_line_description: A looping animated diagram showing a substance cycling through labeled phase states (liquid → vapor → solid) as it travels between two temperature zones, with arrows indicating directional flow and phase-change labels at transition points.
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration describes a planetary-scale chemical phase cycle — a directional flow diagram between two named temperature zones with phase-change callouts captures the scientific mechanism far better than an atmospheric image alone.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { zone_a: { label: string, temp: string, color: string }, zone_b: { label: string, temp: string, color: string }, substance_name: string, phases: [{ zone: string, state: string, label: string }], arrow_color: string, loop_duration_ms: number }

runs.title: How ACTUALLY Big Is Our Universe?

recommendations.narration_excerpt: somewhere between two hundred billion and four hundred billion stars
recommendations.one_line_description: A field of thousands of tiny glowing dots that floods the screen in waves, with a live counter, conveying quantity so large individual units become texture
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A count_up gives you a number, but the narrative center demands the previous frame of reference become meaningless — watching discrete particles lose individuality as they overwhelm the screen physically enacts that collapse in a way a counter cannot.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { particle_count_target: number, particle_color: hex, wave_count: number, counter_label: string, overflow_behavior: 'blur' | 'compress' | 'fade', duration_seconds: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: chances of any two individual stars actually hitting each other is virtually zero
recommendations.one_line_description: A vast grid of empty space with two labeled objects placed so far apart that the emptiness is the overwhelming visual — objects occupy maybe 0.001% of visible area
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: percent_fill communicates a statistic, but this moment requires the viewer to viscerally feel the absurdity of emptiness — the component needs to make the void the protagonist, not a labeled bar.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { grid_width_units: number, grid_height_units: number, objects: [{ label: string, size_px: number, position: { x: number, y: number } }], background_color: hex, label_probability: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: largest structure we've ever discovered is called the Hercules-Corona Borealis Great Wall
recommendations.one_line_description: A horizontal ruler that zooms through nested scale ticks — Earth, Solar System, Milky Way, Local Group, Observable Universe, Great Wall — each prior label shrinking to invisibility as the next is revealed
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narrative center is explicitly about each new scale making the previous one meaningless — a definition card names the structure but a nested scale ruler performs that annihilation visually in real time.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { scales: [{ label: string, size_ly: number, color: hex }], animation: 'sequential_zoom' | 'simultaneous', highlight_index: number, unit_label: string, duration_seconds: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: cosmic horizon — regions of space are moving away from us faster than light can travel
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated radial diagram with an observer at center, concentric rings showing light travel zones, and an outer boundary ring visibly expanding faster than an inward-moving light pulse can close the gap
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: This is one of the hardest concepts in the video to grasp — the horizon is not a place but a dynamic relationship between expansion speed and light speed — a definition card gives words where a diagram showing the light pulse perpetually falling behind gives genuine intuition.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { observer_label: string, rings: [{ label: string, radius_relative: number, color: hex }], light_pulse_speed: number, expansion_speed: number, horizon_ring_index: number, show_speed_labels: boolean, duration_seconds: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: total universe could be at least ten to the twenty-three times larger than what we can observe
recommendations.one_line_description: A full-bleed field representing the total universe at some scale, with a single near-invisible labeled dot marking the entire observable universe — the dot is the point, not the field
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: big_number renders 10²³ as typography, which the brain instantly discards as incomprehensible — rendering the observable universe as a literally invisible dot inside a vast field forces the eye to do the cognitive work the narration is demanding.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { total_field_label: string, dot_label: string, dot_size_px: number, dot_color: hex, dot_position: 'center' | 'random', field_color: hex, scale_ratio_display: string, pulse_animation: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: one bubble in an endless multiverse where new universes are constantly being born
recommendations.one_line_description: An infinite dark field where labeled iridescent bubbles continuously nucleate, expand, and occasionally overlap at edges, with one bubble highlighted as 'our universe'
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: An AI image can render something beautiful but static — the multiverse claim is inherently about ongoing, endless process, and the continuous nucleation animation makes the 'constantly being born' language land as direct observation rather than metaphor.
recommendations.schema_sketch: { bubble_spawn_rate_per_second: number, bubble_growth_speed: number, highlight_bubble_label: string, highlight_bubble_color: hex, default_bubble_color: hex, field_color: hex, physics_label_display: string[], loop: boolean }

runs.title: 8 Terrifying Space Mysteries That Keep Me Up At Night

recommendations.narration_excerpt: dark matter makes up eighty-five percent of all matter in the universe
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated 3D-style sphere that fractures and reveals proportional interior layers for dark energy, dark matter, and visible matter with glowing labels
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The three-way cosmic composition (68% dark energy, 27% dark matter, 5% visible matter) is a nested relationship across three invisible categories that a flat percent_fill bar fundamentally misrepresents — a layered orb makes the 'invisible wrapping the visible' concept viscerally clear
recommendations.schema_sketch: { segments: [{ label: string, percent: number, color: string, glow_color: string }], reveal_order: 'outside_in' | 'inside_out', animation_style: 'fracture' | 'peel' | 'expand' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: expansion isn't slowing down at all — it's speeding up, every second faster
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated grid of glowing dots representing galaxies that visibly accelerates its outward drift in real-time, with a live velocity counter climbing in the corner
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A static trend arrow cannot convey accelerating expansion — the horror of dark energy is that the rate itself is increasing, which requires an animation where the dots not only move apart but visibly lurch faster as you watch, something no existing component delivers
recommendations.schema_sketch: { dot_count: number, initial_velocity: number, acceleration_factor: number, duration_seconds: number, show_velocity_counter: boolean, background_color: string, dot_color: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: our galaxy alone contains four hundred billion stars, many of them have planets
recommendations.one_line_description: A top-down spiral galaxy renders from a single dot outward, with a zoom-to-highlight that isolates one tiny marked region and labels it 'You Are Here' alongside a particle count readout
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The Fermi Paradox gut-punch depends on the viewer viscerally feeling their own insignificance inside 400 billion stars — a big_number is just typography, while a rendered galaxy that zooms to show how cosmically lost Earth is makes the silence feel genuinely terrifying
recommendations.schema_sketch: { star_count_label: string, particle_density: number, highlight_position: { arm: number, radius_percent: number }, highlight_label: string, zoom_sequence: boolean, arm_count: number }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: they arrive without warning and disappear just as quickly, some happen only once
recommendations.one_line_description: An oscilloscope-style dark screen with a flat baseline that suddenly erupts in a sharp spike of light, then returns to silence — repeatable and configurable for single-burst vs. repeating-pattern variants
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Fast Radio Bursts are defined by their millisecond brevity and the contrast between silence and violent energy — an oscilloscope blip that fires and dies is the only visual that literally enacts the phenomenon rather than describing it, and nothing in the library animates raw signal data
recommendations.schema_sketch: { burst_count: number, burst_interval_ms: number, burst_duration_ms: number, spike_height: number, baseline_noise_level: number, color_scheme: 'radio_green' | 'plasma_white' | 'warning_red', show_energy_label: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: a planet floating in darkness between stars, surface covered in ice kilometers thick
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated layered cross-section of a rogue planet cutting from frozen surface crust through ice mantle down to a glowing liquid ocean, with depth and temperature labels at each layer boundary
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The rogue planet's entire conceptual tension — frozen death outside, possible life inside — lives in the vertical layer structure that no AI image can clearly label and annotate; a cross-section diagram makes the hidden ocean's existence feel scientifically concrete rather than speculative
recommendations.schema_sketch: { layers: [{ name: string, depth_km: number, temperature_celsius: number, color: string, texture: 'solid' | 'liquid' | 'gas' }], planet_radius_km: number, show_depth_labels: boolean, animate_heat_glow: boolean, glow_source: 'core' | 'radioactive_decay' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: supermassive black hole processing more information every second than every computer ever existed
recommendations.one_line_description: A side-by-side animated comparison of two throughput meters — one representing all human computing history as a finite bar, one representing the black hole's information processing as an overflowing, crashing meter that shatters its scale
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The awe of this claim depends on the ratio being incomprehensible — a static stat on a split image doesn't convey overflow and scale-breaking, but a visual meter that literally cannot contain the black hole's value makes the consciousness-emergence argument feel earned rather than asserted
recommendations.schema_sketch: { left: { label: string, value: number, unit: string, color: string }, right: { label: string, value: number, unit: string, color: string, overflow_behavior: 'shatter' | 'flood' | 'infinite_scroll' }, animation_duration_seconds: number, show_ratio_label: boolean }

runs.title: Is Light Speed Actually The Limit?

recommendations.narration_excerpt: energy required to push that object faster approaches infinity
recommendations.one_line_description: A smooth curve that visibly rockets upward and flattens toward a vertical asymptote, with a labeled threshold line and axis annotations
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A generic line chart implies finite growth, but the visual drama of a curve screaming toward infinity and never crossing a vertical wall is the entire physical intuition the narration is building — that distinction is lost without an asymptote-specific component
recommendations.schema_sketch: { x_label: string, y_label: string, asymptote_value: number, curve_data: [{x, y}], threshold_label: string, highlight_color: string, animation_speed: 'slow'|'fast' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: contracts space in front of the ship and expands space behind it, creating a bubble
recommendations.one_line_description: An animated cross-section diagram showing a ship at center, with grid lines compressing ahead and stretching behind, and a labeled bubble boundary ring
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: This is the single most important conceptual diagram in the entire video — the Alcubierre bubble — and a static AI image cannot animate the directional compression/expansion that makes the physics legible; a dedicated diagram component would make the loophole viscerally understandable
recommendations.schema_sketch: { ship_label: string, front_compression_factor: number, rear_expansion_factor: number, bubble_radius_display: string, grid_color: string, bubble_color: string, animate: boolean, speed_label: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: mass-energy equivalent of Jupiter converted entirely into exotic matter just
recommendations.one_line_description: A side-by-side proportional size comparison of two or more celestial or physical objects with labeled mass/energy values beneath each, rendered as stylized silhouettes to scale
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration's entire emotional punch is the absurd scale mismatch between Jupiter's mass and a car-sized ship — a split-stat image cannot render that proportional horror the way a true to-scale silhouette comparison could
recommendations.schema_sketch: { items: [{ label: string, silhouette_type: 'planet'|'star'|'ship'|'human'|'building', relative_scale: number, stat_label: string, stat_value: string }], background_color: string, unit_note: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Quantum entanglement lets particles communicate instantly across any distance
recommendations.one_line_description: Two particle nodes connected by an animated pulsing line across an arbitrary distance, with an instantaneous-signal indicator and a light-travel-time counter shown for contrast
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Entanglement's key visual concept is the instantaneous correlated link across space — a twin image suggests similarity but not connection, whereas an animated bidirectional link with a simultaneous-flash makes the physics immediately graspable
recommendations.schema_sketch: { particle_a_label: string, particle_b_label: string, distance_display: string, signal_type: 'instant'|'light_speed', light_travel_time_label: string, pulse_color: string, animate: boolean }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Dark energy is stretching space faster than light on cosmic scales
recommendations.one_line_description: A radial diagram of observable universe boundaries with animated outward arrows whose speed is labeled at different distance bands, showing recession velocity exceeding c beyond a threshold ring
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: The narration is making a precise and counterintuitive claim — that space itself already exceeds light speed at large distances — which needs a labeled radial velocity map to feel like evidence rather than assertion
recommendations.schema_sketch: { center_label: string, rings: [{ distance_label: string, recession_velocity: string, exceeds_c: boolean }], arrow_color: string, threshold_ring_color: string, animate_expansion: boolean, hubble_constant_note: string }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: GPS satellites have to account for time dilation effects predicted by Einstein's theories
recommendations.one_line_description: Two synchronized analog or digital clocks side by side — one labeled 'GPS Satellite' and one labeled 'Earth Surface' — drifting apart in real time with a running microseconds-per-day drift counter beneath each

runs.title: Grownsy vs Momcozy vs Frida Baby - Best Baby Nasal Aspirator Comparison!!

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Grownsy produces forty-five kilopascals — equivalent to a small shop vacuum
recommendations.one_line_description: A side-by-side animated pressure visualization showing three devices as vertical liquid columns or piston-force bars, with a highlighted 'safe zone' band overlaid and real-world analogy icons (shop vac, dishwasher, etc.) anchored to their respective pressure levels
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: A single gauge only shows one device at a time, but the narrative payoff here is the simultaneous visual relationship between all three pressure values AND the pediatric safe-zone band — that three-column pressure comparison with a shaded optimal range is fundamentally a different component than a lone radial gauge
recommendations.schema_sketch: { devices: [{ label, value_kpa, color }], safe_zone: { min_kpa, max_kpa, label }, analogy_anchors: [{ value_kpa, icon, label }], animation: 'fill_up' | 'piston' }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Level three worked best for newborns to three months; level four for three to twelve months

recommendations.schema_sketch: { devices: [{ label, grid: [{ age_band, suction_level, rating: 'optimal'|'acceptable'|'too_strong'|'too_weak' }] }], age_bands: string[], suction_levels: number[], color_map: { optimal, acceptable, too_strong, too_weak } }

recommendations.narration_excerpt: Momcozy hits fifty-one decibels — noticeably louder and more startling for infants
recommendations.one_line_description: Three stacked animated sound-wave bars (one per device) whose waveform amplitude and color intensity scale with measured dB value, with a labeled threshold line for 'typical infant startle response' and real-world environment callouts pinned at matching dB points along a shared axis
recommendations.why_this_fits_better: Sound is inherently a waveform concept — static numbers in a comparison block don't viscerally communicate 'this will scare your baby' the way an amplitude-animated waveform does, and the shared environmental-reference axis (dishwasher, vacuum) gives parents an immediate sensory anchor that no text-based stat component can replicate
recommendations.schema_sketch: { devices: [{ label, db_value, color }], startle_threshold_db: number, environment_anchors: [{ db_value, label, icon }], animation: 'oscillate' | 'pulse', waveform_style: 'sine' | 'jagged' }

annotated_brief_card.schema_sketch: { sections: [{ zone_label: string, description: string, color: string, position_hint: 'top'|'middle'|'bottom' }], title: string, animate_sequence: boolean }

examples.narration: hook formula, a required call-to-action placement, and a specific forbidden phrases list
examples.why: The narration describes three structural rules layered into a document; a visual brief with distinct labeled regions makes each rule spatially legible in a way that neither document_reveal nor bullet_list can achieve with the same precision

candidate_audition_grid.description: A side-by-side grid of N candidates (thumbnail + score bar + cost label) where losers gray out and the winner pulses — visualizing a competitive selection or A/B/C audition process
candidate_audition_grid.schema_sketch: { candidates: [{ label: string, cost: number, score: number, status: 'winner'|'rejected'|'neutral', thumbnail_url: string }] }

examples.narration: sixty dollars per video became ninety, after I auditioned three editors with a paid test clip
examples.why: before_after implies only two states, but the narration describes a three-way audition that converges on one winner; a grid-elimination visual captures the selection process dynamically rather than flattening it into a binary

spend_sequence_timeline.description: A horizontal timeline with two parallel tracks — one showing the 'wrong order' (scale → test) and one showing the 'right order' (test → scale) — with cost markers and outcome icons at each node
spend_sequence_timeline.schema_sketch: { tracks: [{ label: string, steps: [{ action: string, cost: number, outcome_icon: string, is_mistake: boolean }] }] }

examples.narration: they pay for volume before they have a working format
examples.why: The narration's core insight is about sequencing decisions, not just comparing two outcomes; a dual-track timeline makes the order-of-operations error visually obvious in a way compare_reveal, which is static contrast, cannot

budget_burn_meter.description: An animated horizontal gauge or fuel-tank bar that fills to a threshold amount, with a 'proof zone' region highlighted and a 'scale zone' region locked behind it — showing deliberate staged spending
budget_burn_meter.schema_sketch: { total_budget: number, proof_spend: number, scale_threshold: number, currency_symbol: string, proof_label: string, scale_label: string }

examples.narration: I spent one hundred and twelve dollars proving the format was broken before I scaled anything
examples.why: spotlight_stat just displays the $112 number large; a burn meter contextualizes it as a calculated, bounded experiment — visually distinguishing intentional validation spend from reckless scaling spend, which is the exact lesson being taught

cost_breakdown_donut_stack.description: An animated stacked breakdown ring where each arc segment represents a cost line item, with a total cost label at center and labeled callouts per segment
cost_breakdown_donut_stack.schema_sketch: { total_label: string, total_value: number, currency: string, segments: [{ label: string, value: number, color: string }], animate_in_sequence: boolean }

examples.narration: production stack on this twelve-thousand-dollar channel costs roughly eight hundred dollars a month
examples.why: The narration is explicitly about proportional cost composition — a segmented visual showing how $800 is carved up is far more informative than a single spotlight number with no structural context

running_cost_adder.description: An animated receipt-style accumulator where line items drop in one by one with running subtotals, culminating in a final bolded total
running_cost_adder.schema_sketch: { line_items: [{ label: string, unit_cost: number, quantity: number, line_total: number }], running_total_visible: boolean, final_total: number, currency: string, stagger_delay_ms: number }

examples.narration: four videos a month, that's two hundred dollars in production — plus tools and overhead hitting eight hundred total
examples.why: The narration literally performs an additive calculation — a receipt/accumulator animation mirrors that mental math visually in a way no static comparison or chart can replicate

range_band_gauge.description: A horizontal gauge bar showing a shaded min-to-max range with a floating marker for the current niche, compared against a faint baseline band for average YouTube CPM
range_band_gauge.schema_sketch: { metric_label: string, range_min: number, range_max: number, baseline_min: number, baseline_max: number, baseline_label: string, highlight_label: string, unit: string, currency_prefix: string }

examples.narration: AdSense CPM in the finance niche runs between fifteen and forty dollars per thousand views
examples.why: CPM is a range, not a single number — a range-band gauge communicates the spread and relative position simultaneously, which a stat_comparison of two discrete numbers completely misses

revenue_multiplier_split.description: A side-by-side animated bar pair showing revenue-per-viewer with AdSense only versus AdSense plus one affiliate link, with a 'x2' multiplier badge animating between them
revenue_multiplier_split.schema_sketch: { metric_label: string, bar_a: { label: string, value: number }, bar_b: { label: string, value: number }, multiplier_badge: string, unit: string, animate_grow: boolean }

examples.narration: a single affiliate program can double your effective revenue per viewer without adding one extra video
examples.why: The entire claim is a before/after magnitude comparison on a single metric — a visual multiplier bar makes the doubling effect viscerally concrete rather than relying on a generic AI image to carry a quantitative argument

views_vs_revenue_scatter.description: A minimal two-axis scatter plot plotting niche clusters as labeled dots — high-view/low-revenue niches in one quadrant, low-view/high-revenue niches in another — with quadrant labels
views_vs_revenue_scatter.schema_sketch: { x_axis_label: string, y_axis_label: string, quadrant_labels: { top_left: string, top_right: string, bottom_left: string, bottom_right: string }, points: [{ label: string, x: number, y: number, highlight: boolean, color: string }] }

examples.narration: niches that bleed views but generate zero revenue: personal finance motivation, money mindset, wealth psychology
examples.why: The narration is making a decoupled-correlation argument (views ≠ revenue) that only a two-dimensional plot can show clearly; stat_comparison can only show two numbers, not the structural relationship across multiple niches simultaneously

crowded_field_cluster.description: A particle/bubble cluster visualization where dozens of labeled nodes crowd a central zone, visually communicating oversaturation of a space
crowded_field_cluster.schema_sketch: { cluster_label: string, nodes: [{ label: string, size: 'large' | 'medium' | 'small', opacity: number }], center_color: string, animation: 'pulse' | 'drift' }

examples.narration: Builders flood into broad finance topics: personal finance, crypto, passive income
examples.why: The narration is specifically about mass crowding into the same categories — a cluster of jostling bubbles labeled with niche names communicates competitive density viscerally in a way a static polaroid stack cannot

funnel_compress.description: An animated funnel that visually narrows a broad category label at the top into a tight, specific sub-niche label at the bottom with a compression ratio indicator
funnel_compress.schema_sketch: { top_label: string, bottom_label: string, top_width_label: string, bottom_width_label: string, animation_duration_ms: number, accent_color: string }

examples.narration: First: niche compression
examples.why: The core concept being introduced is literally compression — narrowing a wide topic into a precise one — and a funnel animation makes that spatial metaphor immediate and memorable instead of showing an unrelated number

multiplier_bar_race.description: A side-by-side animated bar race where one bar (AdSense) stops early and a second bar (sponsorships) extends to 10–30x the length with a live multiplier badge attached to the growing tip
multiplier_bar_race.schema_sketch: { bars: [{ label: string, value: number, color: string }], multiplier_range: { min: number, max: number }, unit_label: string, animation: 'race' | 'simultaneous' }

examples.narration: Sponsorships and affiliate deals pay ten to thirty times the AdSense rate per thousand views
examples.why: The narration is making a precise magnitude comparison between two revenue sources — a bar race with a live multiplier badge communicates the relative scale dynamically in a way a single count-up number fundamentally cannot

authority_gap_ladder.description: A vertical ladder or staircase graphic with established channels plotted high on a subscriber/authority axis and a 'new channel' marker pinned near the bottom, with a visible gap callout between them
authority_gap_ladder.schema_sketch: { rungs: [{ label: string, metric_value: number, highlight: boolean }], gap_label: string, y_axis_label: string, new_entry_label: string, accent_color: string }

examples.narration: channels with hundreds of thousands of subscribers and years of authority, your new channel registers as noise
examples.why: The narration is about a stark authority gap between incumbents and newcomers — a positional ladder with a labeled chasm makes the competitive disadvantage visually legible rather than showing a generic channel page that implies parity

video_timeline_deal_pin.description: A stylized video scrubber timeline with a sponsor pin dropped at the mid-roll position, a brand logo badge attached to the pin, and an earnings callout bubble floating above it
video_timeline_deal_pin.schema_sketch: { video_duration_seconds: number, sponsor_timestamp_seconds: number, sponsor_label: string, sponsor_logo_url: string, earnings_label: string, pin_color: string }

examples.narration: One mid-roll from a tax software company or a financial advisor network
examples.why: The narration is specifically about the placement and value of a single mid-roll deal — a scrubber timeline with a pinned sponsor moment and dollar callout makes the monetization mechanic spatially concrete rather than showing a full video page

attention_fork.description: A split-path animation showing a phone with two diverging arrows: one path leads to a TikTok-style scroll loop icon, the other to a channel-build progress icon, with a stark visual contrast between the two outcomes
attention_fork.schema_sketch: { left_path: { label: string, icon: string, color: string, outcome_label: string }, right_path: { label: string, icon: string, color: string, outcome_label: string }, fork_label: string, animation: 'diverge' | 'simultaneous' }

examples.narration: Most people who watch this will reopen TikTok
examples.why: The narration is a deliberate behavioral fork moment — the viewer is being challenged to choose a path — and a visual fork with labeled diverging outcomes dramatizes that decision point far better than a static phone screen

equity_drawdown_chart.description: An animated line chart showing account balance declining over time, with labeled drop markers at each averaging-down event
equity_drawdown_chart.schema_sketch: { starting_balance: number, events: [{ label: string, date: string, balance_after: number }], end_balance: number, currency_symbol: string, animate_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: averaged down three times on the way. His $10,000 account is now worth $1,340
examples.why: A static stat split cannot convey the compounding tragedy of three sequential averaging-down decisions the way an animated falling equity curve with annotated inflection points can — it makes the loss feel earned and sequential, not just a number.

behavior_loop_diagram.description: A circular animated loop graphic showing two opposing arrows — one labeled 'Hold Loser' cycling inward to deeper loss, one labeled 'Cut Winner' cycling outward to missed gain — with icons at each node
behavior_loop_diagram.schema_sketch: { nodes: [{ id: string, label: string, icon: string, position: 'top'|'right'|'bottom'|'left' }], arrows: [{ from: string, to: string, label: string, color: string }], loop_direction: 'clockwise'|'counterclockwise', animate: boolean }

examples.narration: actual problem is one behavioral loop: they hold losers and cut winners
examples.why: The narration explicitly describes a loop, which is a structural/relational concept; a static twin image cannot show the self-reinforcing circular causality that makes this behavior destructive, but a loop diagram makes the mechanism immediately legible.

risk_reward_bar.description: A horizontal two-sided bar visual anchored at zero — a short red bar extending left for risk and a longer green bar extending right for reward, with dollar labels and an animated fill reveal
risk_reward_bar.schema_sketch: { risk_label: string, risk_value: number, reward_label: string, reward_value: number, currency_symbol: string, risk_color: string, reward_color: string, animate_fill: boolean }

examples.narration: risk one dollar to make three
examples.why: The 1:3 risk-reward ratio is inherently a proportional, spatial concept; a single big number shows only the ratio abstractly, while a proportional bar makes the asymmetry viscerally obvious at a glance without any reading required.

trade_box_diagram.description: A schematic trade layout showing entry price, a downward stop-loss line (labeled dollar loss), and an upward target line (labeled dollar gain), with zones shaded red and green respectively
trade_box_diagram.schema_sketch: { entry_price: number, stop_price: number, target_price: number, stop_loss_dollars: number, profit_target_dollars: number, ticker: string, loss_zone_color: string, gain_zone_color: string, show_ratio_label: boolean }

examples.narration: his maximum loss is pre-defined at two hundred dollars
examples.why: Scenes 16 and 17 together describe a complete trade structure with defined stop and target — a spotlight stat shows only the loss number in isolation, while a trade box diagram shows the full asymmetric structure spatially, which is the entire lesson of the video.

population_dot_grid.description: A 10×10 grid of trader-icon dots where 90 are colored red (losing) and 10 are colored green (profitable), with an animated sequential fill that groups them visually
population_dot_grid.schema_sketch: { total_units: number, highlighted_units: number, highlight_color: string, base_color: string, icon: 'person'|'dot'|'trader', highlight_label: string, base_label: string, animate_sequence: boolean, grid_columns: number }

examples.narration: same mechanism is destroying 90% of day traders right now
examples.why: A percent fill bar is a familiar abstraction, but a dot grid makes '90 out of 100 real traders' feel like people rather than a percentage, dramatically increasing the visceral impact of the statistic at a key early hook moment.

examples.narration: saved ninety-nine percent of Denmark's Jewish population
examples.why: A percent_fill bar conveys a number but a dot-grid of human figures makes the 99% survival viscerally human rather than abstract — each dot is a person, and watching 7,900 of 8,000 figures turn to 'safe' while a handful remain is the emotional punch this climactic statistic deserves

examples.narration: two billion people who own smartphones but don't have bank accounts
examples.why: A raw number like '2 billion' is cognitively flat, but watching two billion dots out of a global population grid illuminate makes the addressable market feel physically massive rather than rhetorically large

equity_recovery_chart.description: An animated line chart showing account balance over a six-month arc — from the $1,340 low, a flat discipline period, then a steady climb to $14,200 — mirroring the drawdown chart from scene 2 as a visual payoff
equity_recovery_chart.schema_sketch: { events: [{ label: string, date: string, balance: number }], start_balance: number, end_balance: number, currency_symbol: string, highlight_color: string, mirror_component_id: string, animate_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: The account reads $14,200 and climbing
examples.why: This scene is the emotional resolution of scene 2's equity collapse; a count_up delivers only the end number, but an equity recovery chart that visually mirrors and reverses the earlier drawdown chart creates a satisfying narrative symmetry that is the whole point of the story.

trade_sequence_replay.description: A scrolling timeline of sequential trades for a single session, showing entry time, position size as a bar, and P&L outcome color-coded red/green
trade_sequence_replay.schema_sketch: { trades: [{ time: string, direction: 'long'|'short', size_usd: number, outcome_usd: number, label?: string }], highlight_index?: number, session_date?: string }

examples.narration: takes a loss at ten in the morning and immediately re-enters a bigger position
examples.why: The narration is describing a temporal behavioral loop — a static AI image with a stat cannot show the cause-and-effect chain of loss → bigger re-entry → bigger loss the way a sequential trade replay can

win_loss_asymmetry_bar.description: A mirrored horizontal bar chart with average winner size extending right in green and average loser size extending left in red, with a ratio label in the center
win_loss_asymmetry_bar.schema_sketch: { avg_winner: number, avg_loser: number, ratio_label: string, winner_color?: string, loser_color?: string, currency_symbol?: string }

examples.narration: losing three times as much on those trades as they made on winners
examples.why: The core insight is a visual asymmetry between magnitude of wins vs. losses — a mirrored axis makes the 3x imbalance viscerally obvious in a way a generic stat_comparison cannot

position_scatter_grid.description: An animated grid of ticker tiles that populates rapidly — each tile shows symbol, a tiny sparkline, and a blinking attention dot — visually conveying cognitive overload as tiles multiply
position_scatter_grid.schema_sketch: { tickers: [{ symbol: string, sparkline_data: number[], status: 'active'|'ignored' }], max_visible: number, overload_threshold: number, animate_fill_duration_ms?: number }

examples.narration: ran eight, ten, twelve positions simultaneously and couldn't track any of them properly
examples.why: The narration is about overwhelm from simultaneous positions; a scatter grid that physically fills up and becomes unreadable communicates cognitive overload that a static horizontal bar cannot

account_drawdown_wipeout.description: An equity curve animation that starts healthy, accelerates downward with increasing slope, and terminates in a flat zero line with a timestamp marker showing elapsed weeks
account_drawdown_wipeout.schema_sketch: { starting_balance: number, drawdown_events: [{ week: number, balance: number, trigger?: string }], wipeout_week: number, curve_color?: string, show_trigger_labels?: boolean }

examples.narration: accounts don't bleed slowly. They zero out in weeks.
examples.why: A generic animated_line_chart shows movement but not the psychological shape of account destruction — the accelerating curve slope and hard-zero endpoint are the specific visual payload this narration needs

cohort_elimination_grid.description: A grid of 200 anonymous person icons that animate to a grayed-out or struck-through state one by one, with a counter, until 26 are highlighted as wiped out within a quarter boundary
cohort_elimination_grid.schema_sketch: { total_cohort: number, eliminated_count: number, eliminated_color?: string, surviving_color?: string, animation_duration_ms?: number, period_label?: string }

examples.narration: twenty-six traders inside a single quarter
examples.why: Showing 26 out of 200 as a raw number is abstract — watching 26 icons go dark inside a grid of 200 makes the human scale of the loss visceral and concrete in a way big_number cannot

confidence_size_scatter.description: A scatter plot where each dot is a trade, X-axis is subjective confidence tier (low/medium/high), Y-axis is position size, and dot color shows win/loss — revealing the inverse correlation between sizing discipline and outcome
confidence_size_scatter.schema_sketch: { trades: [{ confidence_tier: 1|2|3, position_size_pct: number, outcome: 'win'|'loss', pnl?: number }], axis_labels?: { x: string, y: string }, highlight_quadrant?: string }

examples.narration: when they felt confident they went heavy. When they were right, size was still too small
examples.why: The narration describes a behavioral pattern across many trades requiring a multi-variable visual — a scatter plot exposes the systematic mismatch between confidence, size, and outcome that a two-panel compare_reveal flattens into abstraction

brain_state_meter.description: A dual-gauge visual showing emotional state (calm→panic) on one axis and decision quality (rational→impulsive) on the other, with a live-moving needle animation
brain_state_meter.schema_sketch: { emotion_label: string, emotion_level: 0–100, decision_label: string, decision_level: 0–100, trigger_event: string, needle_animation_ms: number }

examples.narration: your brain registers that as a threat. So you force a trade.
examples.why: The narration is explicitly about a psychological threat-response loop, not P&L data — a brain/behavior gauge captures the internal escalation that a ledger completely misses.

failure_loop_cycle.description: An animated circular diagram showing a repeating behavioral loop (e.g. Loss → Rationalize → Repeat → Loss) with each node pulsing in sequence to emphasize the cyclical, not linear, nature of failure
failure_loop_cycle.schema_sketch: { nodes: [{ label: string, icon: string }], loop_color: string, pulse_duration_ms: number, highlight_node_index: number, caption: string }

examples.narration: Here's the thing about the 90%: they don't fail once.
examples.why: The core insight is repetition and cycle, not a before/after split — a looping cycle diagram makes the 'same way repeatedly' pattern viscerally clear where a diagonal split implies a one-time contrast.

trade_audit_scatter.description: A scatter plot of individual trades plotted by entry-time on the x-axis and outcome (win/loss magnitude) on the y-axis, with a regression line and a highlighted 'perceived edge' vs 'actual edge' divergence band
trade_audit_scatter.schema_sketch: { trades: [{ date: string, outcome_pct: number, win: boolean }], perceived_edge_line: number, actual_edge_line: number, highlight_band_color: string, annotation_label: string }

examples.narration: calculated my actual win rate, and realized I had no edge
examples.why: The narration describes auditing 30 real trades to discover edge doesn't exist — a scatter with a regression line shows the absence of a statistical pattern far more honestly than a ledger, which implies organized accounting rather than a brutal self-reckoning.

probability_distribution_bar.description: A horizontal frequency distribution showing trade outcomes across a large sample (e.g. 500 trades), with individual outcomes as thin bars forming a bell-curve-like shape and a net-positive expected value arrow overlaid
probability_distribution_bar.schema_sketch: { sample_size: number, win_rate: 0–1, avg_win_r: number, avg_loss_r: number, highlight_single_trade: boolean, ev_arrow_label: string, bar_color_win: string, bar_color_loss: string }

examples.narration: They treat each trade the way a casino treats each hand
examples.why: The casino metaphor is entirely about law-of-large-numbers probability playing out over repetitions — a distribution bar makes the 'individual outcome doesn't matter, the math does' point visually self-evident in a way a stock photo cannot.

system_flowchart.description: A minimal animated flowchart showing a rule-based decision tree (e.g. Setup present? → Size check → Execute / No Setup? → Stand aside) that visually contrasts gut-feel branching with a locked systematic path
system_flowchart.schema_sketch: { nodes: [{ id: string, label: string, type: 'decision'|'action'|'exit' }], edges: [{ from: string, to: string, label: string }], highlight_path: [string], color_system: string, color_gut: string }

examples.narration: Behavior doesn't change through information. It changes through systems.
examples.why: The narration pivots on the word 'systems' and demands a structural visual — a compare_reveal suggests two static options, but a flowchart shows that a system is a repeatable process architecture, not just a better choice.

position_size_calculator_visual.description: An animated infographic showing an account equity bar being divided, with a highlighted 1% slice expanding to reveal stop-distance and share-count calculation in real time
position_size_calculator_visual.schema_sketch: { account_equity: number, risk_pct: number, stop_distance_pct: number, resulting_shares: number, currency_symbol: string, animate_slice: boolean, label_equity: string, label_risk: string }

examples.narration: size every position on fixed risk, something like one percent of account equity
examples.why: This is a concrete mechanical rule with a specific number — an interactive-style calculator visual makes the math tangible and memorable, whereas a stat split treats it as a talking-head claim rather than a replicable formula.

cost_gate_sequence.description: An animated checkpoint/gate visual where each gate you pass through deducts a dollar amount from a running total, shown as physical barriers with price tags
cost_gate_sequence.schema_sketch: { starting_amount: number, gates: [{ label: string, deduction: number, icon?: string }], running_total_color: string, gate_style: 'barrier' | 'turnstile' | 'customs_desk' }

examples.narration: Before you leave the airport, you're already down thirty-five dollars
examples.why: The narration is literally about being financially drained before clearing the airport — a sequential gate deduction animation makes the trap feel visceral and spatial rather than just displaying a static stat next to an image

day_rate_accumulator.description: A calendar-strip visual where each day tile fills with a cost amount and a running subtotal climbs visibly across the row as days activate in sequence
day_rate_accumulator.schema_sketch: { daily_rate_range: { min: number, max: number }, days_active: number, highlight_days: number[], subtotal_display: boolean, currency_symbol: string, label: string }

examples.narration: five or six of those days minimum — two hundred and fifty dollars
examples.why: The narration's power is in the compounding of a daily rate across multiple days — a static stat next to an image collapses that compounding into a single number, while an animated day-by-day accumulator shows the trap closing incrementally

price_stack_builder.description: A receipt-style visual where line items stack one by one beneath a base price, each addition visually pushing the total higher with an animated running sum
price_stack_builder.schema_sketch: { base_price: number, base_label: string, line_items: [{ label: string, amount: number, amount_type: 'flat' | 'percent', percent_base?: number }], final_total: number, currency_symbol: string, animate_delay_ms: number }

examples.narration: mandatory service charge seventeen percent, twelve dollars per person not included
examples.why: The narration is structurally a hidden-fee receipt being read aloud — a stacking receipt animation where each fee lands and bumps the total makes the layering of charges feel exactly as suffocating as the script intends, which checkmark_build cannot convey

micro_fee_compounding_grid.description: A grid of small identical coin or fee icons that populate cell by cell — one per meal — with a growing subtotal shown beneath, making trivial per-unit costs visually accumulate into a surprising total
micro_fee_compounding_grid.schema_sketch: { unit_label: string, unit_cost: number, units_per_day: number, total_days: number, grid_columns: number, unit_icon: string, final_total: number, reveal_speed_ms: number }

examples.narration: sixty cents, which means nothing until you're eating out three times a day for ten days
examples.why: The rhetorical move here is showing how an amount that 'means nothing' becomes meaningful through sheer repetition — a line chart implies growth trend, but a filling grid of 30 identical tiny charges communicates the psychological trap of repeated small fees far more precisely

source_gap_map.description: A visual matrix of common travel-planning sources (blog, Reddit, booking site, guidebook) on one axis and hidden cost categories on the other, with cells showing empty/absent where coverage should exist
source_gap_map.schema_sketch: { sources: [{ name: string, icon?: string }], cost_categories: [{ label: string }], coverage_matrix: [{ source: string, category: string, covered: boolean }], empty_cell_style: 'red_x' | 'blank' | 'ghost', title: string }

examples.narration: entirely from costs that existed nowhere on any resource I used
examples.why: The narration's claim is specifically that these costs were invisible across all research sources — a matrix that visually proves absence across multiple named source types is far more credible and damning than an evidence board, which typically implies presence of evidence rather than its systematic absence

budget_floor_adjuster.description: An interactive-feeling slider or dial visual that shows a user-set budget amount, then animates a floor line rising beneath it by the hidden-cost amount, revealing the true minimum with a labeled gap zone
budget_floor_adjuster.schema_sketch: { perceived_budget_label: string, hidden_cost_addition: number, floor_label: string, gap_zone_color: string, gap_zone_label: string, currency_symbol: string, animate_floor_rise: boolean }

examples.narration: Add at least six hundred and fifty dollars to whatever number you've written down
examples.why: The call to action is about recalibrating a personal number — a static handwritten note is passive, but a floor-rising animation where the gap between perceived budget and real floor is highlighted makes the adjustment feel urgent and personalized rather than advisory

accommodation_tier_stack.description: A vertically stacked visual of 2–4 accommodation tiers (icons: bunk bed → single bed → double room → suite) each with a nightly price badge and a city label, animating in from bottom to top
accommodation_tier_stack.schema_sketch: { city: string, tiers: [{ label: string, icon: 'hostel'|'budget'|'midrange'|'luxury', price_per_night: number, currency: string, highlight: boolean }] }

examples.narration: Hostel bed: €14. Nice hotel: €55.
examples.why: The narration is delivering a tiered price ladder across multiple cities, not a row-vs-column comparison — a tier stack with iconography communicates the spectrum of options viscerally without requiring the viewer to read a grid

side_by_side_landmark_duel.description: A split-screen component that places two landmark images face-to-face with a thin divider, each labeled with city name and a single crowd/cost stat underneath, designed for direct visual rivalry
side_by_side_landmark_duel.schema_sketch: { left: { image_prompt: string, label: string, stat_label: string, stat_value: string }, right: { image_prompt: string, label: string, stat_label: string, stat_value: string }, divider_style: 'hard'|'glow'|'versus_badge' }

examples.narration: the azulejo-tiled train station alone would make the Louvre feel like it's trying too hard
examples.why: The narration is explicitly making a head-to-head aesthetic argument between São Bento station and the Louvre — a direct visual duel framing would land the 'trying too hard' punchline far harder than a single zoomed image of one side

crowd_density_overlay.description: A venue silhouette (stadium/amphitheatre shape) filled with a dot-grid of seats where the vast majority are empty and a tiny cluster of 4 colored dots marks the occupied section, with a capacity counter and live-count badge
crowd_density_overlay.schema_sketch: { venue_shape: 'amphitheatre'|'stadium'|'arena', total_capacity: number, current_crowd: number, occupied_dot_color: string, empty_dot_color: string, capacity_label: string, crowd_label: string }

examples.narration: I stood in a Roman amphitheatre that seats six thousand people with exactly four other tourists
examples.why: The entire comedic and persuasive power of this line is the absurd ratio of 4 people to 6,000 seats — an image of the amphitheatre alone loses that punchline, while a dot-density visualization makes the emptiness the hero of the frame

preservation_score_map_pin.description: Two city map pins dropped side by side on a muted European map, each pin expanding into a small card showing a UNESCO/heritage preservation score, tourist volume, and average nightly hotel cost — animating sequentially to dramatize the comparison
preservation_score_map_pin.schema_sketch: { pins: [{ city: string, country: string, lat: number, lng: number, preservation_score: number, score_label: string, annual_tourists_millions: number, avg_hotel_cost: number, currency: string, highlight: boolean }], map_style: 'muted'|'vintage' }

examples.narration: Medieval canal city, better preserved than Bruges
examples.why: The narration is making a credibility claim about objective quality versus a more famous rival — a data-anchored map-pin card grounds the assertion in geographic and metric reality rather than just a before/after reveal which implies a transformation rather than a rivalry

city_cost_passport_card.description: A passport-style card for a city featuring a hero image, country flag chip, 3–4 iconic cost line items (food, drink, hotel, attraction) displayed as icon + price pairs, and a single 'vs Paris' delta badge in the corner
city_cost_passport_card.schema_sketch: { city: string, country: string, flag_emoji: string, hero_image_prompt: string, cost_items: [{ icon: string, label: string, price: number, currency: string }], paris_comparison_delta_percent: number, accent_color: string }

examples.narration: Porto, Portugal: a bottle of Vinho Verde costs €2 at
examples.why: Each of the five cities gets introduced with a cluster of price data points spread awkwardly across 3–4 consecutive scenes — a single card component designed to hold an entire city's value proposition would make each city introduction feel like a unified reveal rather than a fragmented stat dump

value_scatter_plot.description: An animated XY scatter plot with 'experience quality' on the Y axis and 'average nightly cost' on the X axis, each of the six cities (Paris + the five alternatives) plotted as a labeled dot, with Paris visibly isolated in the high-cost low-value quadrant
value_scatter_plot.schema_sketch: { x_axis_label: string, y_axis_label: string, cities: [{ name: string, cost_index: number, experience_index: number, dot_color: string, annotate: boolean }], quadrant_labels: { top_left: string, top_right: string, bottom_left: string, bottom_right: string }, animation: 'sequential'|'simultaneous' }

examples.narration: Paris is still out here pricing itself like a masterpiece and delivering a screensaver
examples.why: The entire video's thesis is a value-vs-cost argument — a scatter plot at the climax would visually crystallize every data point from the previous 30 scenes into one damning chart that shows Paris's position without needing any more words

near_zero_bar_chart.description: A multi-city comparative bar chart where one bar is so dramatically shorter it visually flatlines near the baseline, with city labels and crime-category axis
near_zero_bar_chart.schema_sketch: { metric_label: string, unit: string, cities: [{ name: string, value: number, highlight: boolean }], baseline_note: string, animation: 'bars_drop_in' }

examples.narration: Assault rates so low that some categories of violent crime barely register statistically
examples.why: The narration's power is in the visual absurdity of near-invisibility — a standard stat_comparison conveys difference, but a bar that barely clears zero creates the visceral 'barely registers' moment the script is describing

patrol_route_map.description: An animated overhead neighborhood map showing a looping officer patrol path with named resident/business nodes lighting up as relationship markers along the route
patrol_route_map.schema_sketch: { map_style: 'minimal_urban', route_nodes: [{ label: string, type: 'resident'|'business'|'koban', coords: [x, y] }], path_color: string, loop_animation: boolean, relationship_pulse: boolean }

examples.narration: Officers walk the same streets daily until locals know them by name
examples.why: The narration is specifically about geographic repetition building personal relationships — a static image cannot show the loop, the familiarity, or the spatial density of coverage that makes kobans structurally powerful

social_ripple_graph.description: A node-and-edge network diagram centered on one individual, with concentric rings radiating outward to family, employer, and community nodes that animate to 'dim' or turn red when the center node is flagged
social_ripple_graph.schema_sketch: { center_node: { label: string }, rings: [{ label: string, nodes: [{ name: string, relationship: string }] }], consequence_animation: 'ripple_dim'|'ripple_red', ring_labels: [string] }

examples.narration: it punishes their family, their employer, their social network by association
examples.why: Collective shame is fundamentally a network effect — the entire argument collapses without showing that social consequence travels edges, which no static image or existing chart type in the library can depict

value_split_breakdown.description: An animated bar or coin-stack representing a found item's total value, visually splitting into labeled segments for finder reward, owner return, and government portion with percentage callouts
value_split_breakdown.schema_sketch: { total_value: number, currency_symbol: string, segments: [{ label: string, percent: number, color: string }], animation: 'stack_split', item_label: string }

examples.narration: finders can claim a reward of up to twenty percent of the item's value
examples.why: The percent_fill only shows one fill value in isolation — this scene needs to show the incentive structure as a deliberate division of value, making the reward feel tangible and designed rather than just a number

country_return_rate_map.description: A world map with country-level choropleth shading representing lost wallet return rates, with Japan highlighted and an annotated callout showing its 83% figure against a global average marker
country_return_rate_map.schema_sketch: { metric: string, countries: [{ iso_code: string, value: number | null }], highlight_country: string, global_average: number, callout_text: string, color_scale: [string, string] }

examples.narration: eighty-three percent of lost wallets are returned — cash included
examples.why: The 83% figure only lands as extraordinary in geographic context — showing Japan as a visible outlier against global norms transforms a statistic into a spatial, comparative argument that no existing map or chart component can deliver

density_dot_map.description: An overhead city-grid map with evenly distributed glowing dots representing koban locations, with a radius-of-coverage overlay showing how every city block falls within walking distance of at least one station
density_dot_map.schema_sketch: { city: string, grid_style: 'minimal'|'satellite', points: [{ coords: [x, y], label: string }], coverage_radius_meters: number, highlight_color: string, animate_reveal: boolean }

examples.narration: Every few blocks in any Japanese city, a small neighborhood police box — staffed, always open
examples.why: The architectural argument is about coverage density — 'every few blocks' must be shown spatially to be believed, and no existing component visualizes geographic saturation of a service point across a city grid

cash_stack_builder.description: An animated stacking visualization where individual payments (monthly amounts) physically pile up into a single lump-sum total, with each layer labeled
cash_stack_builder.schema_sketch: { unit_label: string, unit_value: number, unit_count: number, total_value: number, stack_color: string, reveal_animation: 'build' | 'slam', total_label: string }

examples.narration: ninety-six thousand dollars you need liquid before you move a single box
examples.why: The narration is specifically about the psychological shock of reframing 12 small monthly numbers into one terrifying upfront wall of cash — a static big number doesn't show the accumulation mechanic that makes this fact land

dependency_chain.description: A visual chain or tether diagram showing a person node linked by a single fragile line to an employer node, with downstream nodes (visa, housing, income, legal status) hanging below — breaking the chain cascades a failure animation through all nodes
dependency_chain.schema_sketch: { root_node: { label, icon }, dependency_link: { label, fragility: 'single' | 'multi' }, child_nodes: [{ label, icon, loss_label }], break_animation: boolean, chain_color: string }

examples.narration: your legal right to be in the country is tied to one person's decision
examples.why: The entire emotional point is structural dependency — one link controls everything — and no existing component visualizes a single-point-of-failure hierarchy; an image can't show the cascading collapse the way an animated node graph can

shrinking_window.description: A calendar grid that animates days crossing out in real time, with a shrinking colored zone representing the remaining viable action window, and parallel task labels (find sponsor, pack, book flight) competing for the same shrinking space
shrinking_window.schema_sketch: { total_days: number, window_color: string, critical_threshold_days: number, parallel_tasks: [{ label: string, estimated_days: number, color: string }], start_date_label: string, end_label: string }

examples.narration: The grace period to find new work or leave the country is thirty days
examples.why: A countdown_timer shows elapsed time but not the impossible simultaneity of tasks that must fit inside 30 days — the horror is spatial and logistical, not just numerical, and requires showing multiple obligations colliding inside a closing window

safety_net_void.description: A side-by-side grid comparing a reference country's social safety net (filled icons: unemployment, healthcare, pension, housing support) against the expat's equivalent grid — all icons present as outlines only, visually hollow or struck through
safety_net_void.schema_sketch: { columns: [{ label: string, filled: boolean }], rows: [{ benefit_label: string, icon: string, values: [{ filled: boolean, tooltip?: string }] }], empty_state_style: 'hollow' | 'strikethrough' | 'greyed', comparison_label_left: string, comparison_label_right: string }

examples.narration: No unemployment benefits. No public healthcare fallback. No pension system.
examples.why: The narration's power comes from absence, not presence — three_points renders missing things as positive bullet statements, but a void/hollow comparison grid makes the absence itself the visual subject, which is far more visceral

financial_floor_drop.description: An animated cross-section diagram showing a person standing on a platform labeled 'current income,' with labeled floors below (savings buffer, unemployment benefit, healthcare fallback, pension) — the floors are absent, leaving only open space above a baseline labeled 'flight home'
financial_floor_drop.schema_sketch: { top_platform_label: string, floor_layers: [{ label: string, present: boolean, color: string, height_units: number }], bottom_label: string, fall_animation: boolean, void_color: string, person_icon: boolean }

examples.narration: one bad month away from zero with no institutional cushion between you and a flight home
examples.why: The metaphor in the narration is explicitly vertical and architectural — cushion, floor, fall — and no existing component renders the absence of protective layers between a financial position and total collapse; an image cannot show the missing floors

true_cost_unpack.description: An expanding receipt or ledger animation where an initial advertised figure is shown, then line items animate in beneath it — rent upfront, agency fee, service charges, visa costs, zero-net buffer — culminating in a 'true exposure' total that dwarfs the original number
true_cost_unpack.schema_sketch: { headline_figure: number, headline_label: string, line_items: [{ label: string, value: number, type: 'cost' | 'risk_exposure' | 'missing_cushion' }], true_total_label: string, animate_reveal: boolean, currency: string, color_scheme: 'escalating' | 'flat' }

examples.narration: the number isn't eight thousand dollars a month — it's everything you can't afford to lose
examples.why: The entire video's argument is that the visible number conceals a much larger true number, and the closing scene deserves a component that visually performs that unwrapping — a price_tag just restates the surface figure without showing the reveal mechanics that earned this conclusion

diverging_growth_chart.description: A dual animated line chart showing two countries' GDP per capita diverging over decades, with a crossover moment highlighted
diverging_growth_chart.schema_sketch: { series_a: { label, color, data: [{year, value}] }, series_b: { label, color, data: [{year, value}] }, crossover_year, x_label, y_label, highlight_annotation }

examples.narration: Fifty years later, its GDP per capita was higher than the United States
examples.why: before_after implies a static two-state flip, but the power of this fact is the trajectory — two lines that start far apart and cross — which only a time-series divergence animation can convey

foreign_investment_timeline_drop.description: An animated vertical timeline where company logos visually 'land' on dated nodes with their country of origin and sector tagged
foreign_investment_timeline_drop.schema_sketch: { events: [{ year, company_name, logo_url, sector, origin_country }], axis_color, animation_style: 'drop' | 'slide' }

examples.narration: Texas Instruments arrived in 1968. Shell and Exxon followed.
examples.why: polaroid_stack treats these arrivals as aesthetic snapshots, but the narrative weight is the sequential inflow of major multinationals — a logo-drop timeline communicates inevitability and momentum that static stacked images cannot

trade_route_map_pulse.description: An animated map showing a major shipping corridor as a flowing pulse line with a highlighted chokepoint dot and a stat callout anchored to it
trade_route_map_pulse.schema_sketch: { route_coordinates: [[lat, lng]], chokepoint: { lat, lng, label }, stat_callout: { value, unit, description }, pulse_color, map_style: 'minimal' | 'satellite' }

examples.narration: The Strait of Malacca carries forty percent of global trade, and Singapore sat right at its mouth
