examples.why: a static AI image with a stat cannot show that Singapore is a physical geographic bottleneck — an animated chokepoint map makes the spatial inevitability of the strategy viscerally obvious

flow_allocation_diagram.description: An animated Sankey-style flow showing a wage bar splitting into contributions that flow into a pooled fund, then out into infrastructure categories
flow_allocation_diagram.schema_sketch: { source: { label, total_value }, splits: [{ label, percentage, color }], destinations: [{ label, value, icon }], animation_duration_ms, currency_symbol }

examples.narration: workers put twenty percent of wages into a national fund — so the state never needed to borrow capital
examples.why: the CPF mechanism is a flow relationship — money moves from workers through the fund into national assets — which a Sankey diagram shows structurally, whereas a split-stat image only asserts the number without showing the causal chain

resource_sovereignty_scorecard.description: A grid of resource categories (water, oil, land, food) each showing an animated status shift from a red 'dependent' state to a green 'sovereign' state with a micro-stat
resource_sovereignty_scorecard.schema_sketch: { resources: [{ name, icon, start_status: 'dependent' | 'sovereign', end_status, start_label, end_label, stat: { value, unit } }], reveal_style: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous', color_dependent, color_sovereign }

examples.narration: No water it controlled — today it pipes desalinated and recycled water entirely on its own terms
examples.why: scenes 25–27 repeat the same three-beat reversal pattern across three separate components, but a single animated scorecard grid would land that pattern as one powerful cumulative reveal rather than three disconnected stats

balance_cross_indicator.description: An animated visual of a trade balance bar crossing from deficit (red, left-weighted) to surplus (green, right-weighted) with the year snapping into place at the crossover
balance_cross_indicator.schema_sketch: { start_year, crossover_year, start_value, crossover_value, deficit_color, surplus_color, currency, annotation_text }

examples.narration: By 1972, Singapore was running trade surpluses
examples.why: a calendar date just marks time, but the narrative beat here is the moment a structural deficit flips to surplus — a balance-crossing animation makes that economic reversal feel earned and dramatic rather than merely noted

redacted_map_overlay.description: A geographic map where specific regions are obscured by animated black redaction bars, with coordinates visible at the edges but content blacked out
redacted_map_overlay.schema_sketch: { base_map: 'antarctica' | 'world', redacted_zones: [{ lat, lon, radius_km, label_visible: boolean }], redaction_style: 'solid' | 'scanline' | 'static_noise', coordinate_bleed: boolean }

examples.narration: No public map marks them. No official body has acknowledged them.
examples.why: The narration is explicitly about unmarked geographic zones — a redacted map makes the absence of information spatially legible in a way an evidence board cannot, turning the silence into a visible shape on actual terrain

publication_trail_dead_end.description: An animated citation chain — showing journals, submission dates, and expected publication nodes — where the final node dissolves into nothing or shows a locked/absent state
publication_trail_dead_end.schema_sketch: { steps: [{ label: string, date: string, institution: string, status: 'completed' | 'pending' | 'missing' }], terminal_state: 'dissolve' | 'redacted' | 'locked', highlight_color: string }

examples.narration: Samples were recovered. Turns out, the detailed findings were never published in any peer-reviewed journal.
examples.why: The narration's power is in the abrupt stop of an expected institutional process; a visual that shows a research pipeline terminating mid-chain communicates that suppression structurally, not just as text on a document

chart_comparison_overlay.description: Two side-by-side aeronautical chart panels — one official civilian chart with a clean corridor, one alternative rendering with an anomalous restricted zone highlighted — with a swipe or fade toggle between them
chart_comparison_overlay.schema_sketch: { chart_a: { label: string, image_src: string, annotation: null }, chart_b: { label: string, image_src: string, annotation: { zone_coords: [lat, lon], highlight_style: 'glow' | 'hatch' | 'redact' } }, toggle_style: 'swipe' | 'fade' | 'split' }

examples.narration: does not appear on standard navigational charts used by civilian aircraft
examples.why: The narration is a direct comparison between what appears on a chart and what does not — a toggleable dual-chart visual makes that specific absence concrete and credible rather than relying on an AI illustration of an aircraft

discovery_silence_grid.description: A 3-row grid where each row shows a discovery event on the left (icon + date + label) and its corresponding silence on the right (a flatline signal, a blank press field, or a locked icon), with each row animating in sequentially
discovery_silence_grid.schema_sketch: { rows: [{ discovery_label: string, discovery_date: string, discovery_icon: string, silence_type: 'flatline' | 'blank_field' | 'locked' | 'press_release_only', silence_label: string }], animation_delay_ms: number }

examples.narration: Three separate discoveries. Three separate silences.
examples.why: Three_points renders as a generic bullet structure, but the narration's tension lives in the pairing — discovery versus silence — which requires a two-column cause-and-effect visual rhythm to land emotionally

treaty_countdown_arc.description: A circular arc timer showing elapsed years since treaty signing (1959) and remaining years to renegotiation (2048), with labeled milestones plotted along the arc at key events
treaty_countdown_arc.schema_sketch: { start_year: 1959, end_year: 2048, current_year: 'dynamic', milestones: [{ year: number, label: string, type: 'signed' | 'updated' | 'incident' | 'future' }], arc_style: 'thin' | 'filled', color_elapsed: string, color_remaining: string }

examples.narration: The Antarctic Treaty comes up for renegotiation in 2048.
examples.why: A static calendar date flattens what is actually a long institutional arc spanning nearly a century — a circular timeline with milestones gives the 2048 deadline its full weight by showing how much has already passed in silence

memory_fog_reveal.description: An image or scene that begins heavily obscured — frosted, pixelated, or fogged — and partially clears as an audio waveform or typed transcript animates alongside it, simulating the act of describing something into clarity
memory_fog_reveal.schema_sketch: { base_image_src: string, obscure_style: 'frost' | 'pixelate' | 'fog', reveal_trigger: 'waveform' | 'typewriter' | 'scroll', reveal_speed_ms: number, transcript_text: string, partial_reveal_percent: number }

examples.narration: researchers often don't know what they've witnessed until they try to describe it out loud
examples.why: The narration describes the cognitive experience of delayed recognition — a visual that enacts that process of fog lifting as language is produced gives the abstract psychological idea a direct sensory equivalent no static image can provide

cosmic_scale_visualizer.description: An animated zooming-out visualization that shows nested scales — star to solar system to galaxy — with a particle field that populates to represent a staggering quantity
cosmic_scale_visualizer.schema_sketch: { quantity: number, unit_label: string, zoom_levels: [{ label: string, scale_factor: number }], particle_density: 'low' | 'medium' | 'overwhelming', animation_duration_seconds: number }

examples.narration: Milky Way galaxy alone contains over four hundred billion stars
examples.why: A static big number cannot convey the visceral incomprehensibility of 400 billion stars the way a zooming particle field that never stops filling the frame would, making the Fermi paradox feel genuinely overwhelming rather than just large.

exponential_time_slider.description: A logarithmic timeline scrubber where dragging right accelerates through centuries exponentially, with civilization-stage icons appearing at each order-of-magnitude jump
exponential_time_slider.schema_sketch: { start_year: number, end_year: number, milestones: [{ year: number, icon: string, label: string }], scale: 'linear' | 'logarithmic', scrubber_auto_play: boolean, acceleration_curve: string }

examples.narration: how much human technology has advanced in just the last century. Now imagine a million centuries
examples.why: Before/after only shows two states, but the narration is specifically about the mind-bending compression of exponential time across orders of magnitude — a scrubber that visually accelerates conveys that cognitive vertigo in a way a split panel never could.

one_way_glass_overlay.description: A split-perspective frame where one side shows a subject from the outside (crisp, lit, observed) and the other shows their blurred, darkened view back through one-way glass, with reflection artifacts
one_way_glass_overlay.schema_sketch: { observer_side_image: string, subject_side_image: string, glass_opacity: number, reflection_intensity: number, observer_label: string, subject_label: string, glass_tint_color: string }

examples.narration: kid pressing their face against the glass at a zoo, watching lions pace
examples.why: The zoo hypothesis metaphor is entirely about asymmetric visibility — one party can see clearly while the other cannot — and a one-way glass visual duality would make that power dynamic viscerally immediate in a way a plain fullscreen image cannot.

observed_history_timeline.description: A horizontal civilization timeline where each era has a pulsing 'eye' surveillance icon above it indicating a silent watcher, with the eye intensity growing closer to the present
observed_history_timeline.schema_sketch: { events: [{ year: number | string, label: string, watcher_intensity: number, icon: string }], watcher_icon: string, intensity_gradient_start: string, intensity_gradient_end: string, animate_in_sequence: boolean }

examples.narration: Every major moment in human history — the fall of Rome, the Renaissance, the Industrial Revolution
examples.why: A plain timeline shows events in sequence but misses the entire narrative point — that each event was silently witnessed — and the accumulating surveillance icons would build dread across the timeline in a way no existing component communicates.

detection_threshold_radar.description: A circular radar-sweep animation showing blips that appear just inside the outer detection ring then vanish before reaching the confirmed-contact center zone, with a 'proof threshold' ring marked
detection_threshold_radar.schema_sketch: { blip_count: number, blip_max_radius_percent: number, proof_threshold_radius_percent: number, sweep_speed_seconds: number, blip_lifetime_ms: number, center_label: string, threshold_label: string, blip_color: string }

examples.narration: perfect surveillance pattern — let the subjects know something's there, but never let them prove it
examples.why: The narration describes a precise and deliberate pattern of staying just below the threshold of provability — a radar visualization with a moving proof-threshold ring makes that calibrated evasion visually concrete in a way an evidence board (which implies accumulating proof) actively contradicts.

species_graduation_meter.description: A multi-track progress bar showing parallel civilization readiness dimensions (technology, social cohesion, ethics, survival) each with a fill level and a minimum threshold line for 'contact eligibility'
species_graduation_meter.schema_sketch: { tracks: [{ label: string, current_value: number, threshold_value: number, color: string, icon: string }], contact_unlocked: boolean, threshold_label: string, animate_fill: boolean, max_value: number }

examples.narration: certain milestones a species has to reach — technological benchmarks, social developments, ethical evolutions
examples.why: Three static bullet points cannot show that these criteria are independent axes being evaluated simultaneously against a threshold — a multi-track meter with a visible contact-eligibility line transforms the abstract graduation test into a tangible, suspenseful readiness dashboard.

expanding_signal_sphere.description: An animated concentric-ring pulse radiating outward from a central point (Earth), with a live radius label showing light-years traveled and year markers at each ring
expanding_signal_sphere.schema_sketch: { origin_label: string, start_year: number, current_year: number, speed_label: string, ring_intervals_ly: number[], milestone_labels: [{ year: number, radius_ly: number, label: string }], animate: boolean }

examples.narration: expanding bubble of human noise that grows larger every second
examples.why: The narration is fundamentally about an expanding geometric phenomenon with measurable scale — a static AI image cannot convey the relentless, quantified growth that makes this fact viscerally alarming

star_map_reach.description: A top-down stylized galactic map with Earth at center, a shaded radial reach zone drawn outward, and dot-clusters representing star systems inside the exposure radius
star_map_reach.schema_sketch: { center_label: string, reach_radius_ly: number, reach_label: string, star_count_in_zone: number, zone_color: string, highlight_named_systems: [{ name: string, distance_ly: number }], galactic_context: boolean }

examples.narration: earliest radio transmissions have reached over ten thousand star systems
examples.why: Ten thousand star systems is a spatial, cartographic fact — a galactic map with a drawn exposure boundary makes the existential scale immediately legible in a way a split-stat layout with a number cannot

dual_timeline_race.description: Two parallel horizontal timelines — one showing Earth's signal traveling outward, one showing a response/vessel traveling back — with a collision or intercept point marked, and elapsed years labeled on both tracks
dual_timeline_race.schema_sketch: { timeline_a: { label: string, start_year: number, event_label: string, travel_years: number }, timeline_b: { label: string, start_year: number, event_label: string, travel_years: number }, intercept_year: number, intercept_label: string, units: string }

examples.narration: if a hostile civilization received our broadcasts and immediately launched an attack, we wouldn't know
examples.why: The narration hinges on a precise temporal paradox between two simultaneous journeys across space; a dual animated timeline communicates this cause-and-effect race in a way no static image or single stat can

signal_age_odometer.description: A rolling odometer-style display showing light-years traveled, ticking upward in real time, with a secondary readout showing the corresponding year the signal was originally broadcast
signal_age_odometer.schema_sketch: { signal_name: string, launch_year: number, speed: string, current_ly_traveled: number, tick_rate_ms: number, secondary_label: string, show_broadcast_year: boolean }

examples.narration: some of those signals have been traveling through space for over seventy years now
examples.why: A count_up shows a static number arriving at a destination; an odometer physically rolling conveys relentless, unstoppable forward travel — matching the horror that these signals cannot be recalled

decoded_message_reveal.description: A pixelated binary grid that animates column-by-column to progressively reveal simple iconic glyphs — a human figure, a solar system diagram, a DNA strand — as if being decoded in real time
decoded_message_reveal.schema_sketch: { grid_cols: number, grid_rows: number, binary_data: string, reveal_segments: [{ label: string, glyph_type: 'figure'|'solar_system'|'dna'|'telescope'|'custom', cell_range: { row_start: number, col_start: number, row_end: number, col_end: number } }], reveal_speed_ms: number, highlight_color: string }

examples.narration: exactly where we live, what we look like, and how advanced our technology was
examples.why: The Arecibo Message is a famous visual artifact and the narration is explicitly about its decoded contents; an animated pixel-reveal of the actual message structure is irreplaceable for making the 'cosmic address label' metaphor concrete

dark_forest_scatter.description: A dark field of faint, semi-transparent civilization icons scattered across a star-map canvas — most are dim and silent, one (Earth) pulses with a visible broadcast ring, illustrating asymmetric visibility
dark_forest_scatter.schema_sketch: { background: 'star_field', total_civilizations: number, silent_icon_opacity: number, broadcasting_icon: { label: string, pulse_color: string, pulse_radius: number }, silent_icons: [{ x_pct: number, y_pct: number }], animate_pulse: boolean, label_broadcasting: string }

examples.narration: silence isn't because there's no one out there — it's because everyone's hiding
examples.why: The Dark Forest Theory is a comparative, spatial argument about relative silence versus exposure; a scatter visualization that literally shows many hidden nodes and one glowing beacon makes the theory's logic visually self-evident in a way a full-screen AI image cannot

behavioral_spectrum_slider.description: A dual horizontal slider showing two opposing behavioral patterns on a spectrum, with labeled anchor points and animated position markers for each persona
behavioral_spectrum_slider.schema_sketch: { axis_label: string, left_anchor: string, right_anchor: string, persona_a: { label: string, position: number }, persona_b: { label: string, position: number }, highlight_color_a: string, highlight_color_b: string }

examples.narration: wealthy people decide fast and reverse slowly. Poor people decide slowly
examples.why: The contrast here is not a table of facts but a directional behavioral axis — a spectrum slider viscerally shows the speed/reversal tradeoff as opposing positions on a single continuum rather than static rows

emotional_journey_arc.description: A curved timeline arc with labeled emotional waypoints, each node showing a month marker, an emotion label, and an icon — the arc visually droops as energy declines across stages
emotional_journey_arc.schema_sketch: { waypoints: [{ month: string, emotion: string, icon: string, arc_height: number }], curve_color: string, endpoint_label: string, droop_animation: boolean }

examples.narration: Month three excited. Month six determined. Month nine tired. Month twelve fatigue
examples.why: An animated line chart implies numeric data and upward/downward performance; this scene tracks emotional state across time, which needs a drooping arc with mood labels at each node, not a quantitative Y-axis

attrition_drain_visual.description: An animated funnel or container that visually empties from a full cohort down to a surviving ten percent, with cause-of-exit labels appearing as segments drain away
attrition_drain_visual.schema_sketch: { start_count: number, segments: [{ label: string, exit_percent: number, exit_reason: string, color: string }], survivor_label: string, animation_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: ninety percent of wealth-building attempts die. Not in failure. In fatigue
examples.why: The narration's power is in showing WHERE the ninety percent exit — not failure, but fatigue — a draining container with annotated exit points makes that distinction viscerally visible rather than just stating a percentage

causality_reversal_diagram.description: A two-panel animated diagram showing two arrow sequences side by side — the wrong order (behavior → identity) with a red X, and the correct order (identity → behavior) with a green checkmark, labels animating in sequence
causality_reversal_diagram.schema_sketch: { wrong_sequence: [string], correct_sequence: [string], wrong_label: string, correct_label: string, animation_style: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous', accent_color: string }

examples.narration: You don't become rich by learning rich behaviors. You learn rich behaviors by becoming
examples.why: This scene's entire argument is about reversing a causal chain — a visual that explicitly flips two labeled arrow sequences makes the cognitive reversal immediate and memorable, which no existing text or image component achieves

parallel_mirror_grid.description: A three-row mirrored grid where each row shows an internal state on the left and its corresponding external outcome on the right, connected by a visual bridge or equals sign, all rows building in sequence
parallel_mirror_grid.schema_sketch: { pairs: [{ internal: string, external: string, icon_left: string, icon_right: string }], bridge_style: 'arrow' | 'equals' | 'mirror_line', build_animation: boolean, accent_color: string }

examples.narration: If daily routines are scattered results scattered. Decisions emotional money emotional. Actions inconsistent
examples.why: The narration delivers three explicit cause-effect pairs in parallel structure — a mirrored grid that builds row by row makes each pairing land with visual weight that a static image with a stat cannot replicate

gap_bridge_diagram.description: A visual chasm diagram with two labeled cliffs — KNOWING on the left, DOING on the right — separated by an animated gap, with a dotted bridge outline representing the missing behavioral link
gap_bridge_diagram.schema_sketch: { left_cliff_label: string, right_cliff_label: string, gap_label: string, bridge_style: 'absent' | 'dotted' | 'building', gap_width_percent: number, background_style: 'dark' | 'cinematic', animation: 'zoom_in' | 'pan_across' }

examples.narration: Hidden in the gap between knowing and doing. Not hidden in some vault
examples.why: The entire video thesis culminates in the word 'gap' — a literal visual chasm with labeled cliffs and an absent bridge makes the metaphor concrete and payoff-worthy at the climax, which a hero image completely fails to deliver

budget_split_pie_animated.description: An animated pie/donut that draws itself in labeled wedges sequentially, with each segment pulsing and dollar amounts populating as the slice appears
budget_split_pie_animated.schema_sketch: { segments: [{ label: string, percent: number, color: string, dollar_amount: number }], currency_symbol: string, base_income: number, animation_order: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous', center_label: string }

examples.narration: Fifty percent fixed bills, thirty percent wants, twenty percent savings
examples.why: A static donut_chart renders all segments simultaneously, but the narration introduces each percentage one at a time — a sequentially-animated draw that reveals each wedge on cue would mirror the spoken reveal and make the rule viscerally land rather than just appear

salary_waterfall_chart.description: A vertical waterfall bar that starts at gross salary and shows labeled deductions (taxes, health insurance, 401k) as descending steps, landing on a highlighted take-home bar at the bottom
salary_waterfall_chart.schema_sketch: { gross: number, deductions: [{ label: string, amount: number, color: string }], take_home: number, currency_symbol: string, highlight_final: boolean, animate_steps: boolean }

examples.narration: fifty thousand a year, take-home is usually around thirty-seven thousand — three thousand not four
examples.why: A before_after panel shows only two states, but this narration is specifically about the stepwise erosion of a number through deductions — a waterfall communicates the mechanism of shrinkage, not just the outcome, which is the entire psychological point of the scene

retention_decay_chart.description: A dual-line chart showing two cohorts' adherence percentage declining over six monthly intervals, with one line staying high and the other dropping sharply, and endpoint callout labels
retention_decay_chart.schema_sketch: { x_axis: string[], series: [{ label: string, color: string, data: number[], end_callout: string }], y_axis_label: string, y_min: number, y_max: number, highlight_gap_at_index: number }

examples.narration: eighteen percent of Group one still budgeting versus seventy-four percent of Group two after six months
examples.why: stat_comparison shows only the final numbers side by side, erasing the drama of the decay story — the whole persuasive power of this data is that Group 1 fell off a cliff over time while Group 2 stayed flat, which only a time-series chart can show

weekly_spend_envelope.description: An animated envelope or wallet visual that shows a monthly budget dividing into four weekly 'envelopes,' each labeled with the weekly amount, with a depletion fill-down animation
weekly_spend_envelope.schema_sketch: { monthly_amount: number, weekly_amount: number, weeks: number, currency_symbol: string, envelope_color: string, fill_direction: 'top_down' | 'bottom_up', label_format: 'currency' | 'currency_with_label' }

examples.narration: nine hundred a month for variable spending — that's about two-ten a week
examples.why: The narration is introducing the key behavioral mechanic of the whole system — translating monthly to weekly mental accounting — and a static stat image completely misses this conceptual transformation; a visual that literally breaks one container into four would make the cognitive trick stick

cognitive_overload_meter.description: A horizontal capacity bar representing mental bandwidth that fills progressively as budget category icons appear one by one, turning red and cracking/breaking at a threshold count
cognitive_overload_meter.schema_sketch: { categories: [{ icon: string, label: string }], overload_threshold: number, bar_colors: { safe: string, warning: string, overload: string }, break_animation: boolean, label_above: string, label_at_break: string }

examples.narration: decision fatigue kicks in after about seven choices — by category twelve you're just guessing
examples.why: A gauge_meter shows a single static reading, but the narration's power is in the progressive accumulation that tips into overwhelm — an animated bar that visibly breaks at category 7–8 would viscerally demonstrate why complexity kills budgets in a way a static dial cannot

budget_balance_equation.description: An animated arithmetic display showing three labeled dollar amounts sliding in from left, a plus sign between each, an equals sign animating in, and the total populating with a green confirmation flash
budget_balance_equation.schema_sketch: { terms: [{ label: string, amount: number, color: string }], total: number, currency_symbol: string, operator: '+', confirmation_style: 'green_flash' | 'checkmark' | 'both', animate_sequential: boolean }

examples.narration: fourteen hundred plus nine hundred plus six hundred equals three thousand — perfect
examples.why: A before_after panel implies contrast between two states, but this moment is a satisfying proof-of-balance — the math checking out is the emotional payoff, and an equation-style animation that builds left to right and resolves with a checkmark would deliver that closure visually rather than obscuring it behind a comparison frame

feedback_loop_diagram.description: An animated circular causality diagram where 3–5 labeled nodes connect with arrows that pulse in sequence, visually reinforcing a self-reinforcing loop
feedback_loop_diagram.schema_sketch: { nodes: [{ id, label, icon_key }], edges: [{ from, to, label }], loop_direction: 'clockwise' | 'counterclockwise', pulse_speed_ms: number, danger_highlight_node_id: string }

examples.narration: Walk slower, lose muscle faster, walk even slower — downward spiral
examples.why: A line chart implies linear time-series data, but this narration describes a closed causal loop — the feedback_loop_diagram would show the spiral feeding back on itself, which is the exact cognitive model the narration is trying to implant

age_decay_slope.description: A human silhouette overlaid on a declining curve where the figure's posture gradually shifts from upright to stooped as the line descends across age brackets
age_decay_slope.schema_sketch: { x_axis_label: string, y_axis_label: string, data_points: [{ age: number, speed_percent: number }], highlight_ages: [number], posture_keyframes: [{ age: number, silhouette_variant: 'upright' | 'slight_lean' | 'stoop' | 'cane' }] }

examples.narration: at forty, walking speed starts declining one to two percent every year
examples.why: A bare animated line chart communicates the numbers but loses the embodied human reality — fusing the silhouette posture change with the curve makes the biological cost visceral and instantly readable without any text

effort_reserve_bar.description: A dual-layer horizontal bar showing total available strength capacity on the bottom layer and required effort per step on the top layer, with a danger zone that animates when the gap between the two closes
effort_reserve_bar.schema_sketch: { capacity_percent: number, demand_percent: number, capacity_label: string, demand_label: string, danger_threshold_percent: number, animate_gap_close: boolean, age_scenario_label: string }

examples.narration: leg muscles must generate at least forty percent of maximum strength with every step
examples.why: percent_fill shows one number in isolation, but the entire rhetorical force of scenes 52–54 depends on visualizing the shrinking buffer between capacity and demand simultaneously — a single fill bar cannot show two competing quantities converging

brain_activation_compare.description: Side-by-side top-down brain outline diagrams where color-coded activation regions fill in dynamically — left panel shows minimal motor cortex glow for young adults, right panel shows broad frontal lobe saturation for older adults
brain_activation_compare.schema_sketch: { panels: [{ label: string, age_group: string, activated_regions: [{ region_name: string, intensity: 0–1, color_hex: string }] }], brain_view: 'top' | 'lateral' | 'frontal', animation_style: 'fill_on_scroll' | 'pulse' }

examples.narration: older adults entire frontal lobe lit up like a Christmas tree just to walk normally
examples.why: The narration is explicitly comparing two brain activation patterns, which is the most visually specific claim in the entire video — an ai_image with a stat overlay leaves this as abstract text, whereas an anatomically-anchored compare diagram makes the neurological argument self-evident

rep_counter_test.description: An animated on-screen physical test widget showing a chair icon, a rising rep count (1→5), a pass/fail threshold indicator, and a linked risk-outcome label that reveals once the count is complete
rep_counter_test.schema_sketch: { test_name: string, target_reps: number, chair_icon_style: string, pass_label: string, fail_label: string, fail_consequence_text: string, animation_duration_per_rep_ms: number, threshold_rep: number }

examples.narration: stand up from a standard height chair five times in a row without using hands
examples.why: This scene is the video's single actionable self-assessment moment — it invites the viewer to test themselves right now, which calls for an interactive-feeling widget that counts reps and delivers a verdict, not a zoomed photograph that keeps them passive

survival_population_grid.description: A 10×10 grid of 100 human icons where icons are progressively dimmed or crossed out to represent mortality probability across two speed cohorts shown side by side
survival_population_grid.schema_sketch: { cohorts: [{ label: string, speed_threshold: string, mortality_percent: number, icon_color_active: string, icon_color_inactive: string }], grid_size: number, animation_style: 'fade_out' | 'cross_out', comparison_layout: 'side_by_side' | 'stacked' }

examples.narration: slower than point eight meters per second, ninety-one percent chance of dying within next decade
examples.why: A percent_fill bar abstracts mortality into a geometric shape, but 91 out of 100 human figures going dark is the kind of visceral icon-array visualization proven to make probabilistic risk emotionally legible — it respects the gravity of the statistic

resource_allocation_diagram.description: A brain silhouette with animated budget bars showing how cognitive resources shift between competing functions over time
resource_allocation_diagram.schema_sketch: { organ_label: string, resource_pools: [{ label: string, color: string, age_40_pct: number, age_60_pct: number }], animation_duration_ms: number, highlight_shift_arrow: boolean }

examples.narration: brain begins prioritizing balance over speed. And this shift is measurable.
examples.why: A gauge implies a single scalar value, but this narration is fundamentally about reallocation — two functions trading share — which requires a split/flow visual that shows balance stealing budget from speed simultaneously

body_depletion_fill.description: A human silhouette that visually empties or fades in layers as age milestones tick forward, with percentage labels appearing at each stage
body_depletion_fill.schema_sketch: { silhouette_type: 'male' | 'female' | 'neutral', stages: [{ age: number, depletion_pct: number, label: string }], fill_color: string, depleted_color: string, animation_style: 'drain' | 'fade' }

examples.narration: By seventy, you've lost ten percent. By eighty, twenty percent.
examples.why: Muscle loss is viscerally about the body itself diminishing — a silhouette draining communicates the stakes in a way a line chart cannot, turning an abstract percentage into a felt physical reality

muscle_sequence_animator.description: A transparent leg/torso anatomy overlay where labeled muscle groups light up in firing order during a single animated walking stride
muscle_sequence_animator.schema_sketch: { anatomy_view: 'lateral' | 'posterior', muscles: [{ name: string, highlight_color: string, fire_order: number, delay_ms: number, position_xy: [number, number] }], loop: boolean, stride_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: muscles that fire in precise sequences to keep you upright and moving forward
examples.why: The narration is explicitly about sequential muscle activation — a static bullet list destroys the entire point; only an animated sequence diagram communicates the choreography and shows why any single weak link breaks the chain

context_speed_drop_map.description: A top-down floor-plan path showing a walking route with color-coded speed zones that visually dim and narrow at labeled trigger points — unfamiliar surface, obstacle, distraction
context_speed_drop_map.schema_sketch: { path_length_units: number, zones: [{ label: string, trigger_type: 'surface' | 'obstacle' | 'cognitive', speed_reduction_pct: number, path_position_pct: number, icon: string }], baseline_color: string, reduced_color: string }

examples.narration: Twenty-five percent around obstacles. And thirty percent when they're distracted.
examples.why: Three horizontal bars compare magnitudes but lose the spatial and situational context entirely; a path map makes the viewer feel the real-world conditions that cause each drop, matching the environmental framing of the narration

feedback_loop_spiral.description: An animated downward spiral diagram where each loop is labeled with a cause-and-effect step, visually tightening toward a collapse point at the center
feedback_loop_spiral.schema_sketch: { loop_steps: [{ label: string, sublabel: string, icon: string }], spiral_direction: 'inward' | 'outward', collapse_label: string, color_gradient_start: string, color_gradient_end: string, animation_speed: 'slow' | 'medium' }

examples.narration: moving slower actually makes you more likely to fall because you lose dynamic balance
examples.why: The narration explicitly describes a self-reinforcing downward spiral — this is one of the most recognized causal structures in health communication and no existing component in the library renders a closed feedback loop with compounding visual momentum

side_by_side_timer_race.description: Two synchronized countdown timers running side by side over identical path graphics, one finishing three seconds earlier, with a mortality-risk delta badge appearing on completion
side_by_side_timer_race.schema_sketch: { left: { label: string, duration_seconds: number, color: string }, right: { label: string, duration_seconds: number, color: string }, path_icon: string, completion_badge: { label: string, delta_value: string, delta_unit: string }, autoplay: boolean }

examples.narration: difference between taking thirty-five seconds to walk to your mailbox versus thirty-two seconds
examples.why: The narration's power comes from making a 3-second gap feel consequential — a before/after static frame cannot create the tension of watching two timers race; the animation turns an invisible abstraction into a viscerally small-but-meaningful gap

reaction_timer_race.description: Two side-by-side animated stopwatch bars that fill at different speeds, labeled with age group and time value, showing the same event completing at dramatically different rates
reaction_timer_race.schema_sketch: { label_a: string, time_a: number, label_b: string, time_b: number, event_label: string, unit: 'seconds' | 'ms', animate: boolean }

examples.narration: point-zero-eight seconds. At seventy? Point-three-two seconds. Four times longer
examples.why: A static before/after panel cannot convey the kinetic reality of elapsed time — an animated dual-timer visually enacts the four-fold delay so the viewer feels the sluggishness rather than just reading a number

cell_depletion_grid.description: A grid of uniform dot-icons representing a population of sensor cells, with cells progressively greying out or disappearing across three labeled age columns (50, 65, 75) in a single animated sweep
cell_depletion_grid.schema_sketch: { total_cells: number, stages: [{ age_label: string, percent_remaining: number }], cell_shape: 'dot' | 'hexagon', active_color: string, lost_color: string }

examples.narration: lost thirty percent of the cells. By seventy-five? Fifty percent
examples.why: The narration is fundamentally about physical cell loss at scale — a dot-grid that visibly empties makes the depletion visceral and countable in a way a number comparison card never achieves

visual_field_shrink.description: A first-person circular field-of-view diagram that animates inward at the edges across three age snapshots, with degree-of-loss arcs labeled on each side
visual_field_shrink.schema_sketch: { stages: [{ age_label: string, degrees_lost_per_side: number }], base_fov_degrees: number, highlight_lost_region_color: string, animate: boolean }

examples.narration: lost fifteen degrees of visual field on each side. By eighty? Twenty-five degrees
examples.why: Peripheral vision loss is a spatial, geometric concept — an animated FOV cone that literally shrinks lets the viewer experience tunnel narrowing rather than compare two abstract numbers

sensor_signal_map.description: An anatomical foot-and-ankle silhouette with numbered sensor nodes that light up on trigger; two states shown side-by-side — one with 16 nodes firing, one with 9 — with a tilt-angle indicator at the ankle
sensor_signal_map.schema_sketch: { body_region: string, tilt_angle_degrees: number, state_a: { label: string, active_nodes: number, total_nodes: number }, state_b: { label: string, active_nodes: number, total_nodes: number }, node_active_color: string, node_dead_color: string }

examples.narration: ankle tilts two degrees. At forty, sixteen sensors fire. Now only nine sensors work
examples.why: The narration gives exact sensor counts and a specific tilt angle, begging for a labeled anatomical diagram that shows the gap between full and partial sensor firing rather than a generic full-screen photo

conflict_signal_dashboard.description: A three-panel instrument-cluster graphic showing Ear, Eyes, and Feet as labeled gauges or signal indicators — each broadcasting a contradictory status simultaneously, with a central 'Brain Decision' output showing a flashing conflict state
conflict_signal_dashboard.schema_sketch: { systems: [{ name: string, icon: string, signal_label: string, status: 'ok' | 'conflict' | 'dead' }], central_output_label: string, conflict_indicator: 'flashing' | 'warning_color', animate: boolean }

examples.narration: Ear says falling left. Eyes say steady. Feet say nothing because sensors are dead
examples.why: The core metaphor of the whole video is three systems arguing; a multi-channel conflict dashboard makes that invisible internal war tangible and visually scannable in one glance, which no single full-screen image can do

cascade_consequence_flow.description: A downward-branching flow diagram starting from a single 'Fall / Hip Fracture' node that splits into labeled consequence branches (bedridden → pneumonia, immobility → blood clots, hospital → infection) with mortality percentage anchored at the bottom
cascade_consequence_flow.schema_sketch: { root_event: string, branches: [{ cause: string, effect: string, icon?: string }], terminal_stat: { label: string, value: string }, direction: 'top-down' | 'left-right' }

examples.narration: twenty percent die within a year. Not from the fall itself, but from cascade of complications
examples.why: The word 'cascade' is used explicitly and the narration lists three distinct causal chains — a branching flow diagram is the only component that honestly represents sequential causation rather than forcing three outcomes into a flat bullet list

subscription_web.description: An animated radial web showing subscription logos/icons orbiting a central wallet, with total monthly cost accumulating as each node lights up
subscription_web.schema_sketch: { items: [{ label: string, icon_slug: string, monthly_cost: number }], total_label: string, reveal_speed_ms: number, highlight_color: string }

examples.narration: seventeen different monthly subscriptions — most people can only name half
examples.why: The radial reveal of 17 distinct nodes materializes the 'invisible sprawl' of forgotten subscriptions far more viscerally than a title-card accent can, letting viewers mentally match their own services as each appears

leak_drain_meter.description: A visual container (wallet or tank) that slowly drains as labeled micro-expense drips animate out from the bottom, with a running total of lost dollars beside each drip
leak_drain_meter.schema_sketch: { leaks: [{ label: string, annual_cost: number, drip_color: string }], container_label: string, total_lost: number, drain_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: small bleeds add up to over a thousand dollars a year for most people
examples.why: The narrative center of the entire video is 'unconscious bleed' — a physical draining animation makes that metaphor literal and emotionally resonant in a way a simple number count-up cannot

debt_drag_timeline.description: A horizontal scrolling timeline showing a single purchase date on the left, then a long shaded drag zone with compounding interest markers, ending at final payoff date on the far right
debt_drag_timeline.schema_sketch: { purchase_label: string, purchase_amount: number, interest_rate: number, monthly_payment: number, payoff_months: number, total_paid: number, highlight_overpaid: number }

examples.narration: minimum payment on your credit card means paying for that dinner for seven years
examples.why: A generic timeline component doesn't communicate the oppressive length and compounding cost of minimum payments — this specialized variant needs a visual weight or 'drag' effect that grows heavier as years pass, making the abstraction of 7 years feel real

cost_per_use_duel.description: Side-by-side animated product silhouettes where each degrades or holds up over a simulated time scrubber, with a cost-per-month figure updating dynamically beneath each option
cost_per_use_duel.schema_sketch: { option_a: { label: string, upfront_cost: number, lifespan_months: number, icon_slug: string }, option_b: { label: string, upfront_cost: number, lifespan_months: number, icon_slug: string }, scrubber_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: forty-dollar shoes that fall apart in three months versus hundred-twenty-dollar pair lasting two years
examples.why: A static comparison table lists numbers but doesn't show the core insight — that cheap goods cost more per unit of time — whereas an animated degradation visual with a live cost-per-month counter makes the 'false economy' concept immediately intuitive without reading

savings_brick_builder.description: A grid of 20 weekly 'bricks' that fill in one by one as the narration plays, with each brick labeled with its week number and a running total that lands on $500 at completion
savings_brick_builder.schema_sketch: { weekly_amount: number, total_weeks: number, target_total: number, brick_fill_color: string, fill_animation_ms: number, show_running_total: boolean }

examples.narration: save twenty-five dollars a week for twenty weeks — less than four dollars a day
examples.why: A line chart implies continuous growth data, but this is a discrete, achievable 20-step plan — a filling grid makes the finish line feel close and countable rather than abstract, directly countering the 'feels hopeless' emotional barrier the narration names

perception_reality_gauge.description: A dual-needle gauge or shadow-overlay bar where a 'perceived' marker sits visibly short of where the 'actual' needle lands, with the gap zone shaded and labeled as the blind spot
perception_reality_gauge.schema_sketch: { perceived_value: number, actual_value: number, unit_label: string, gap_label: string, gauge_min: number, gauge_max: number, perceived_color: string, actual_color: string }

examples.narration: average American underestimates their spending by thirty-seven percent — like thinking you weigh one-fifty when you actually weigh two hundred
examples.why: The narration's entire point is the gap between mental model and reality — a split-stat image just shows a number, while a dual-needle gauge viscerally encodes the distance between what people believe and what is true, which is the narrative center of the whole video

emotion_mask_reveal.description: An animated split that peels back a surface emotion label to expose the underlying emotion beneath it, like lifting a mask
emotion_mask_reveal.schema_sketch: { surface_emotion: string, hidden_emotion: string, surface_color: hex, hidden_color: hex, surface_icon: string, hidden_icon: string, reveal_trigger: 'auto' | 'tap', tagline: string }

examples.narration: The anger is fear wearing a disguise
examples.why: The narration is literally about one emotion disguising another — a peeling/reveal animation between two named emotional states would make this metaphor visceral in a way compare_reveal (designed for side-by-side data) cannot

parallel_behavior_tracker.description: A two-column animated log that fills in rows simultaneously — child behavior on the left, mirrored parent behavior on the right — revealing the pattern as entries appear
parallel_behavior_tracker.schema_sketch: { rows: [{ child_action: string, parent_mirror: string, fear_label: string }], reveal_speed_ms: number, left_header: string, right_header: string, accent_color: hex }

examples.narration: she wrote down three things: what they did, what she was afraid it meant, and what she'd done that same day
examples.why: The entire power of Secret Two is the visual shock of seeing parent and child behaviors logged side-by-side in real time; no existing component shows a filling parallel log that makes the mirror dynamic emotionally land

breath_cycle_animator.description: A looping circular visual that expands and contracts in real time with labeled phase arcs (inhale / hold / exhale / hold) and a count display at the center
breath_cycle_animator.schema_sketch: { inhale_counts: number, hold_in_counts: number, exhale_counts: number, hold_out_counts: number, cycles: number, ring_color: hex, bg_color: hex, phase_labels: { inhale: string, hold: string, exhale: string }, center_label: string }

examples.narration: Four counts in. Hold for four. Four counts out. Hold for four.
examples.why: The narration is describing a precise physiological breathing protocol that is the mechanical heart of Secret Three — an animated breath pacer lets viewers actually feel the rhythm rather than just read counts on a static note

nervous_system_gauge_live.description: A body-silhouette with an animated internal 'activation' meter that rises in response to a stressor stimulus shown on screen, then visibly drops as a breathing animation overlays it
nervous_system_gauge_live.schema_sketch: { stressor_label: string, peak_activation_pct: number, recovery_duration_ms: number, body_silhouette_color: hex, activation_color: hex, calm_color: hex, breath_overlay: boolean, annotation_peak: string, annotation_calm: string }

examples.narration: She'd picture it clearly. Feel her body start to react. Then breathe through it until her nervous system stayed calm.
examples.why: This scene is the conceptual climax of the nervous-system training explanation — showing arousal literally rising then falling inside a body outline makes the invisible biological process concrete in a way a fullscreen image of someone breathing cannot

family_timeline_arc.description: A curved chronological arc spanning a defined period with labeled milestone nodes for the family — incidents, practice starts, behavioral shifts — each node expandable with a short descriptor
family_timeline_arc.schema_sketch: { start_label: string, end_label: string, duration_display: string, milestones: [{ date_label: string, event: string, sentiment: 'negative' | 'neutral' | 'positive', icon: string }], arc_color: hex, negative_color: hex, positive_color: hex }

examples.narration: Three years later, Sarah's house looks completely different. Not because her children became perfect.
examples.why: The narrative has been building through a three-year transformation journey with discrete turning points; a visual arc with plotted milestones would give the payoff scene the earned weight of showing elapsed time and progress that a zoom image entirely misses

behavioral_mirror_animation.description: Two facing silhouettes where an animated emotional signal (color wave, gesture ripple) originates from the adult figure and propagates to the child figure with a short delay, looping continuously
behavioral_mirror_animation.schema_sketch: { adult_label: string, child_label: string, signal_color: hex, signal_label: string, propagation_delay_ms: number, wave_style: 'ripple' | 'pulse' | 'arc', loop: boolean, bg_color: hex }

examples.narration: hardwired to copy the emotional patterns of the adults around them
examples.why: The word 'hardwired' and the mirroring concept are purely visual and kinetic — a propagating-signal animation between parent and child silhouettes demonstrates the transmission mechanism in a way pull_quote (text on screen) cannot approach

compound_accumulator.description: An animated ticker that multiplies a daily micro-event across years, showing the staggering cumulative total with a running visual pile-up effect
compound_accumulator.schema_sketch: { daily_count: number, unit_label: string, compounding_period_years: number, final_total: number, accumulation_visual: 'dots' | 'bars' | 'pile', milestone_markers: [{ year: number, label: string }] }

examples.narration: dismissed emotions an average of four times per day repeated over eighteen years
examples.why: The entire narrative center is 'invisible compounding' — a component that visually transforms '4 times/day' into '26,280 dismissals over 18 years' makes the core thesis visceral rather than just numerical

interrupted_flow_diagram.description: A two-path flow animation showing the healthy path (Event → Feel → Process → Act) versus the avoidant path (Event → ~~Feel~~ → Act), with the middle node visibly bypassed or crossed out
interrupted_flow_diagram.schema_sketch: { paths: [{ label: string, nodes: [{ id: string, label: string, skipped: boolean }], outcome_label: string, outcome_sentiment: 'healthy' | 'avoidant' }], animation_style: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous' }

examples.narration: learned to skip the feeling and go straight to the fixing
examples.why: This scene describes a specific psychological short-circuit — a branching flow diagram that literally shows the skipped node communicates the mechanism far more precisely than any split image

generational_mirror.description: A side-by-side silhouette animation of parent and child where behavioral traits visually transfer as glowing particles or matching color fills flowing downward between the two figures
generational_mirror.schema_sketch: { parent_traits: [string], child_traits: [string], match_percentage: number, transfer_animation: 'particles' | 'color_fill' | 'outline_trace', generation_labels: { parent: string, child: string } }

examples.narration: ninety-three percent of children developed the exact same emotional coping strategies as their parents
examples.why: The word 'exact same' across generations is inherently visual — a mirroring/transfer animation between parent and child silhouettes makes intergenerational transmission emotionally tangible in a way a fill bar cannot

emotional_closeness_thermometer.description: Two side-by-side thermometer-style gauges filling upward to different levels, labeled with parent behavior type, with the temperature metaphor reinforcing warmth/coldness of emotional connection
emotional_closeness_thermometer.schema_sketch: { gauges: [{ label: string, value: number, behavior_descriptor: string, color: string }], unit_label: string, title: string, animation: 'fill_up' | 'simultaneous' | 'sequential' }

examples.narration: seventy-nine percent for kids whose emotions were consistently validated even when irrational
examples.why: Closeness to a parent is inherently a warmth/temperature concept — a thermometer gauge encodes both the numerical gap (31% vs 79%) AND the emotional quality of the relationship in a single glanceable visual

daily_moment_grid.description: A 24-hour day represented as a grid of icon-sized moment slots, where 8 slots highlight as 'emotional priority moments' and a second version dims all but 2, showing the real-world deficit
daily_moment_grid.schema_sketch: { total_slots: number, needed_count: number, actual_count: number, slot_icon: string, needed_color: string, actual_color: string, deficit_color: string, label_needed: string, label_actual: string, animation: 'fill_then_dim' | 'side_by_side' }

examples.narration: children need an average of eight moments per day where emotional needs take priority — most kids getting less than two
examples.why: The contrast between needed (8) and actual (2) is about scarcity within a finite day — a visual grid of all daily moments makes the shortfall feel concrete and personally recognizable rather than abstract

emotion_suppression_iceberg.description: An iceberg diagram where labeled emotions sit above the waterline (expressed) and suppressed versions sink below with a growing submerged mass over time, animated across age stages
emotion_suppression_iceberg.schema_sketch: { age_stages: [{ age: number, expressed_emotions: [string], suppressed_emotions: [string], suppression_ratio: number }], waterline_label: string, above_color: string, below_color: string, animation: 'timeline_scrub' | 'staged_reveal' }

examples.narration: learned that certain feelings weren't acceptable so they stopped having them where we could see
examples.why: The narration explicitly describes emotions going 'underground' — an iceberg is the canonical visual metaphor for hidden emotional mass, and animating the above/below ratio shifting over childhood directly illustrates the suppression dynamic

asymptotic_curve_chart.description: An animated line chart where the curve climbs steeply and visibly flattens toward a vertical asymptote labeled with a limit value, never crossing it
asymptotic_curve_chart.schema_sketch: { x_label: string, y_label: string, asymptote_value: number, asymptote_label: string, curve_data_points: number[], animation_duration_ms: number, color_scheme: string }

examples.narration: energy required to make it go faster approaches infinity
examples.why: A simple trend arrow implies linear growth, but the entire dramatic point is the curve bending toward infinity and never arriving — that asymptotic shape IS the concept, and no existing chart component captures a hard ceiling that cannot be crossed

spacetime_warp_diagram.description: A side-view animated diagram of a ship centered in a bubble, with grid lines visibly compressing ahead and stretching behind it in real time
spacetime_warp_diagram.schema_sketch: { ship_label: string, bubble_radius: number, compression_direction: 'front' | 'rear' | 'both', grid_line_count: number, animation_speed: 'slow' | 'medium' | 'fast', compression_color: string, expansion_color: string, bubble_color: string }

examples.narration: shrink the space in front of the ship and expand the space behind it
examples.why: This is the single most technical and counterintuitive visual concept in the entire video — a static AI image cannot show the directional asymmetry of space compression vs expansion that makes the Alcubierre mechanism comprehensible

entanglement_pulse_diagram.description: Two labeled particles shown at opposite ends of a wide gap with a simultaneous animated pulse firing between them, annotated with distance and a 'time elapsed: 0' callout
entanglement_pulse_diagram.schema_sketch: { particle_a_label: string, particle_b_label: string, distance_label: string, time_elapsed_label: string, pulse_color: string, pulse_animation_style: 'instant' | 'simultaneous_bidirectional', background: 'dark_space' | 'neutral' }

examples.narration: particles that seem to influence each other instantly across vast distances
examples.why: Quantum entanglement's core visual drama is simultaneity across distance — a fullscreen zoom image cannot encode the zero-time linkage or the spatial separation that makes the concept startling; an animated connection diagram makes both elements visible at once

cosmic_expansion_ruler.description: A horizontal animated ruler showing two galaxy icons drifting apart, with a live speed readout above the gap that ticks past 'c' while a 'no information transferred' disclaimer anchors below
cosmic_expansion_ruler.schema_sketch: { object_a_label: string, object_b_label: string, expansion_speed_value: string, speed_of_light_marker_label: string, caveat_text: string, ruler_unit: string, animation_loop: boolean }

examples.narration: space between distant galaxies is already expanding faster than light right now
examples.why: The narration makes a precise factual claim that expansion already exceeds light speed without violating relativity — this requires a visual that simultaneously shows superluminal separation AND the caveat, which no static image or existing data component can encode together

relativistic_effects_stack.description: Three side-by-side animated bars — Time Dilation, Length Contraction, Relativistic Mass — all growing simultaneously as a shared velocity slider increases toward c
relativistic_effects_stack.schema_sketch: { velocity_slider_max: 'c', bars: [{ label: string, unit: string, baseline_value: number }], velocity_steps: number[], highlight_color_near_limit: string, shared_slider: boolean }

examples.narration: faster you go, the more extreme these effects become. But there's a point
examples.why: Einstein's three linked effects are explicitly named together in this passage and their simultaneous scaling with velocity is the key insight — a single gauge meter shows only one dimension, missing the correlated multi-variable drama that makes special relativity visually striking

universe_composition_burst.description: A radial burst or layered ring diagram showing the universe's energy-matter composition — dark energy, dark matter, ordinary matter — with animated segment labels and percentages
universe_composition_burst.schema_sketch: { segments: [{ label: string, percent: number, color: string }], center_label: string, animation_style: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous', highlight_segment: string }

examples.narration: dark energy already fills seventy percent of the universe and causes space itself to expand
examples.why: A percent_fill component shows a single value filling toward 100%, but the narration's point only lands if the 70% dark energy figure is contrasted against what else exists — the composition context is what makes the scale feel extraordinary

memory_depth_stack.description: A vertical cross-section visualization showing layered memory tiers — surface, mid-layer, and deep storage — with items visually sinking or rising between layers
memory_depth_stack.schema_sketch: { layers: [{ label: string, depth_label: string, fill_color: string, items: string[] }], active_layer: number, animation: 'drain' | 'surface_only' }

examples.narration: burning through obvious choices, low-hanging fruit, names at the surface of your memory
examples.why: The narration is literally describing a tiered depth model of memory, which a balance scale cannot convey — a layered depth stack would make the 'surface vs. deep storage' metaphor visually concrete and reinforce the video's central thesis

letter_pressure_display.description: A large bold letter centered on screen with a radial countdown ring shrinking around it and a live response field that flickers or pulses to simulate cognitive scrambling
letter_pressure_display.schema_sketch: { letter: string, timer_seconds: number, response_text: string | null, flicker_mode: boolean, ring_color_start: string, ring_color_end: string, background_color: string }

examples.narration: Letter Q fills your screen, timer starts, five four three, mind goes blank
examples.why: A plain countdown_timer shows elapsed time but misses the full dramatic unit — the letter, the pressure ring, and the blank-brain flicker together — which is the precise visual moment this narration is trying to recreate for the viewer

recall_fog_grid.description: A grid of name slots where some cells are clearly visible and others are obscured by a frosted or blurred overlay, visualizing partial recall and mental blanks
recall_fog_grid.schema_sketch: { slots: [{ text: string, visibility: 'clear' | 'foggy' | 'blank' }], grid_columns: number, fog_opacity: number, animate_reveal: boolean }

examples.narration: Xavier, the only X name most people know, what if your brain freezes and can't even remember Xavier
examples.why: The narration is describing the exact experience of knowing that answers exist but being unable to access them — a fog/blur grid makes that invisible cognitive state visually tangible in a way a bullet list of clearly readable names actively contradicts

alphabet_confidence_heatmap.description: A horizontal strip of all 26 alphabet letters color-coded from cool/green to hot/red reflecting difficulty or confidence level, with a sweeping highlight showing progression through the challenge
alphabet_confidence_heatmap.schema_sketch: { letters: [{ char: string, difficulty_score: number, label?: string }], color_scale: { low: string, mid: string, high: string }, highlight_current: string | null, animate_sweep: boolean }

examples.narration: somewhere around J or K you felt that first hint of panic, by Q you were scrambling
examples.why: This scene is explicitly recapping the emotional arc across all 26 letters — a heatmap across the full alphabet is the only component that could show that entire journey in a single glance, which is exactly what the narration is summarizing

expectation_vs_reality_meter.description: A dual horizontal gauge showing a user's self-assessed score alongside their actual measured score, with the gap between the two bars visually highlighted and labeled
expectation_vs_reality_meter.schema_sketch: { metric_label: string, expected_value: number, actual_value: number, max_value: number, gap_label: string, expected_color: string, actual_color: string, gap_color: string }

examples.narration: most people think they know more names than they actually do, mental speed isn't as fast as they believed
examples.why: Before/after implies a time sequence but this narration is about simultaneous contrast between self-perception and actual performance — a split gauge makes the gap quantifiable and visually striking in a way a before/after panel cannot

cognitive_load_pulse.description: An animated radial pulse or heartbeat-style waveform that visibly increases in frequency and intensity to represent rising mental effort, with a live 'effort level' label
cognitive_load_pulse.schema_sketch: { effort_level: number, max_level: number, pulse_frequency_hz: number, waveform_style: 'heartbeat' | 'radial' | 'bar_bounce', color_ramp: string[], label: string }

examples.narration: Letter K flashes up, Kevin first to mind, then Kyle, brain is working harder now
examples.why: A static gauge meter shows a fixed level but the narration describes a dynamic, escalating feeling of effort in real time — a pulsing waveform that visibly speeds up would make the viewer feel the cognitive strain rather than simply read a dial

stacked_income_streams.description: An animated layered bar that builds upward stream by stream, each layer labeled with source name and live dollar contribution
stacked_income_streams.schema_sketch: { streams: [{ label: string, monthly_value: number, color: string }], animation_mode: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous', total_label: string }

examples.narration: business value plus real estate appreciation plus ongoing rental income
examples.why: The narration is explicitly about simultaneous, additive wealth layers growing at once — a static bullet list kills the compounding momentum, whereas a stacked animated build would make the multiplication feel visceral and real

level_unlock_ladder.description: A vertical game-style progression ladder with distinct levels, each showing the income mechanism, ceiling cap, and a lock/unlock state
level_unlock_ladder.schema_sketch: { levels: [{ level_number: number, label: string, mechanism: string, ceiling_description: string, is_unlocked: boolean, highlight: boolean }] }

examples.narration: ceiling is how many hours you can work and how much each hour is worth
examples.why: The narration introduces a literal 'levels of wealth' framework spanning three scenes — a gauge only shows one value, while a level ladder visualizes the entire game structure the video is built around and makes the ceiling concept concrete

deal_flow_splitter.description: A branching diagram showing one starting value splitting into two paths — sell vs. borrow — with each path animating its costs, retained assets, and final net position
deal_flow_splitter.schema_sketch: { starting_value: number, paths: [{ label: string, steps: [{ description: string, delta: number }], final_net: number, retained_assets: string[] }] }

examples.narration: pay capital gains taxes and walk away with about two million after everything
examples.why: The narration presents a fork-in-the-road financial decision where the outcome difference is the entire point; a static table shows the numbers but not the structural logic of why keeping the asset while borrowing against it beats selling outright

equity_scatter_map.description: A city map overlay with building markers sized by ownership percentage, animating each acquisition in sequence with a running total equity counter
equity_scatter_map.schema_sketch: { city: string, acquisitions: [{ building_id: string, lat: number, lng: number, equity_pct: number, acquisition_date: string, value: number }], animate_sequence: boolean }

examples.narration: small ownership stakes — five percent here, ten percent there
examples.why: The narration is about geographic, incremental equity accumulation across real physical buildings — a donut chart collapses all spatial and sequential meaning into a static slice, losing the sense of a growing empire being assembled piece by piece

deal_structure_diagram.description: A simple animated flow diagram showing two parties, the asset in the center, arrows indicating capital flow in and income flow out, with percentage labels on each side
deal_structure_diagram.schema_sketch: { parties: [{ label: string, role: 'lender' | 'operator', contribution: string, return_pct: number, return_duration_years: number }], asset_label: string, income_label: string }

examples.narration: convinced his uncle to lend the down payment in exchange for fifty percent ownership
examples.why: This is the video's first and most critical explanation of 'other people's money' — the mechanism only clicks when you see capital moving between parties visually, which a portrait-style twin image cannot communicate at all

empire_growth_timeline.description: A dual-axis animated timeline where one track counts buildings with the technology installed and a second track shows equity positions acquired, both animating across years with milestone callouts
empire_growth_timeline.schema_sketch: { timeline_start: string, timeline_end: string, tracks: [{ label: string, unit: string, color: string, milestones: [{ date: string, value: number, callout?: string }] }] }

examples.narration: parking technology operating in forty-seven buildings, significant equity in twelve properties
examples.why: The narration is the payoff moment summarizing a multi-year build — two separate metrics growing on parallel tracks across real time needs a temporal visualization, not a side-by-side static number comparison that strips out the journey

kinetic_chain_cascade.description: An animated silhouette of a human body where joints light up sequentially from bottom to top, each with a brief dysfunction label and a ripple/shockwave effect between joints
kinetic_chain_cascade.schema_sketch: { joints: [{ name: string, position_y_percent: number, label: string, delay_ms: number, color: string }], body_silhouette_style: 'front' | 'side', wave_color: string, loop: boolean }

examples.narration: ankle compensates, knee twists inward, hip shifts forward, pelvis tilts
examples.why: The cascade of compensations is inherently spatial and sequential — a body-map animation showing each joint firing in dysfunctional order makes the chain reaction viscerally clear in a way a text checklist cannot

spring_compression_meter.description: A side-view anatomical foot diagram with an animated spring embedded in the arch that visibly compresses on impact and releases on push-off, with a millimeter displacement readout
spring_compression_meter.schema_sketch: { compression_mm: number, compression_label: string, phase_labels: { impact: string, load: string, release: string }, healthy_color: string, dysfunctional_color: string, cycle_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: arch of your foot compresses by eight millimeters with every step, storing energy like a spring
examples.why: The spring metaphor and the precise 8mm compression figure demand a mechanical animation — a static image with a stat overlay cannot convey the dynamic energy-storage concept the narration is built around

age_decay_curve.description: A dual-line animated chart plotting foot mobility percentage (y-axis) against age (x-axis) for elite athletes vs. average adults, with the gap between curves shaded and key age milestones marked
age_decay_curve.schema_sketch: { x_axis: { label: string, range: [number, number] }, y_axis: { label: string, unit: string }, series: [{ label: string, data: [{ age: number, value: number }], color: string }], milestones: [{ age: number, annotation: string }], shade_gap: boolean }

examples.narration: average adults lose four percent of foot mobility every single year after age thirty
examples.why: Two diverging trajectories over decades of age is a chart story, not an arrow — the visual drama of the widening gap between elite and average is completely lost with a single trend arrow

pain_source_distance_map.description: A body-outline diagram with two distinct highlighted zones — a 'felt pain' zone in red and a 'actual dysfunction' zone in amber — connected by a dotted path line with a joint-count distance label
pain_source_distance_map.schema_sketch: { body_view: 'front' | 'side' | 'back', pain_zone: { region: string, color: string, label: string }, source_zone: { region: string, color: string, label: string }, distance_label: string, path_style: 'dotted' | 'dashed', annotation: string }

examples.narration: actual dysfunction was at least one joint away from where the person felt pain
examples.why: Regional interdependence is a spatial concept about anatomical distance between cause and symptom — it needs a body map showing two separate locations simultaneously, which no existing component can render

joint_grid_unlock.description: A grid of 33 small joint-dot icons where 8 animate to 'active' (bright, pulsing) and the remaining 25 fade to 'locked' (grey, static) with a running counter below
joint_grid_unlock.schema_sketch: { total_units: number, active_units: number, active_color: string, inactive_color: string, active_label: string, inactive_label: string, icon_shape: 'dot' | 'circle' | 'joint_node', animate_sequence: boolean, counter_visible: boolean }

examples.narration: average adult can only move eight of those joints properly — eight out of thirty-three
examples.why: The 8-of-33 ratio is a part-to-whole story that needs individual unit representation — seeing 25 greyed-out dots alongside 8 glowing ones makes the dysfunction visually devastating in a way a bar comparison never could

balance_timer_challenge.description: An interactive-style countdown display showing a silhouette balancing on one foot with a 30-second arc timer, benchmark zones (0-10s: poor, 10-30s: average, 30s+: elite) color-coded on the arc
balance_timer_challenge.schema_sketch: { duration_seconds: number, zones: [{ label: string, range: [number, number], color: string }], silhouette_style: 'one_leg', animate: boolean, benchmark_labels: { poor: string, average: string, elite: string }, cta_text: string }

examples.narration: stand on one foot with eyes closed — if you can't hold thirty seconds, your feet have lost ability
examples.why: This is a direct viewer challenge with a measurable threshold — a visual timer with performance benchmarks turns a passive instruction into an engaging self-assessment graphic that anchors the proprioception concept concretely

child_schedule_grid.description: A weekly calendar grid styled like a child's planner, filling up with color-coded activity blocks in real time to visualize over-scheduling
child_schedule_grid.schema_sketch: { child_age: number, activities: [{ name: string, days: string[], color: string, icon: string }], free_slots_remaining: number, overflow_animation: boolean }

examples.narration: Piano lessons at four. Advanced math at six. Every weekend packed with activities
examples.why: An animated calendar filling to overflow makes the 'packed with activities' overload viscerally legible in a way a split image cannot — the viewer watches free time disappear block by block

body_stress_heatmap.description: A silhouette of a child and an adult side by side with color-mapped stress zones (cortisol heat) that animate to match each other, showing physiological equivalence
body_stress_heatmap.schema_sketch: { left_label: string, right_label: string, stress_zones: [{ region: string, intensity: 0-100 }], match_animation: boolean, metric_label: string }

examples.narration: Kids in more than three structured activities show cortisol levels comparable to adults in high-stress jobs
examples.why: The entire rhetorical punch of this stat is the shocking equivalence between a child's body and a stressed adult's body — a visual overlay of matching heat signatures makes that comparison land physically, not just numerically

split_reality_mirror.description: A vertical split screen showing the same scene twice — left side styled as a polished social media post (saturated, posed), right side as raw unfiltered reality (muted, candid) — with a sliding reveal divider
split_reality_mirror.schema_sketch: { left_label: string, right_label: string, left_style: 'instagram' | 'magazine', right_style: 'raw' | 'candid', divider_animation: 'slide' | 'static', caption_left: string, caption_right: string }

examples.narration: They start performing their lives instead of living them
examples.why: The narration is literally about performance vs. reality — a draggable or animated split between curated and unfiltered versions of the same moment is the exact visual metaphor the script is describing

resilience_cycle_wheel.description: An animated circular loop diagram showing the repeating cycle of Challenge → Struggle → Cope → Grow, with each stage lighting up sequentially like a life-skill feedback loop
resilience_cycle_wheel.schema_sketch: { stages: [{ label: string, icon: string, description: string }], loop_speed_ms: number, highlight_color: string, center_label: string }

examples.narration: Every skinned knee teaches kids that they can heal. Every failed test teaches them failure isn't permanent
examples.why: Scenes 57-59 are explicitly describing a psychological cycle of experience-building — a looping wheel diagram communicates the compounding, self-reinforcing nature of resilience in a way static images fundamentally cannot

age_regression_timeline.description: A horizontal timeline of a human lifespan with a burnout-marker icon that animates sliding leftward — from age 45 back to age 14 — showing how the onset age has collapsed over generations
age_regression_timeline.schema_sketch: { lifespan_range: [0, 80], historical_onset_age: number, current_onset_age: number, marker_label: string, animation_duration_ms: number, era_labels: [{ age: number, label: string }] }

examples.narration: By age fourteen already showing signs of burnout that used to only appear in mid-career professionals
examples.why: The entire impact of this data point is temporal regression — burnout arriving decades too early — and an animated marker visibly sliding backward on a lifespan bar makes that regression shocking in a way a stat overlay cannot

population_dot_matrix.description: Two grids of human figure icons side by side — one for kids with self-efficacy, one without — with affected figures animating to a highlighted color to show proportional mental health outcomes
population_dot_matrix.schema_sketch: { group_a_label: string, group_b_label: string, total_figures: number, group_a_affected_ratio: number, group_b_affected_ratio: number, affected_color: string, outcome_label: string }

examples.narration: Kids who don't develop anxiety and depression at twice the rate of their peers
examples.why: A 'twice the rate' comparison between populations is the canonical use case for a dot matrix / unit chart — it converts an abstract multiplier into countable human figures the viewer can actually see, making the scale emotionally real rather than numerically abstract

examples.narration: cortisol levels thirty-seven percent higher — equivalent to someone with PTSD
examples.why: The statistic lands hardest when you can see the sheer share of people affected relative to a reference group; a gauge needle is abstract and scalar, whereas a dot matrix makes the 37% gap feel like real humans, which matches the video's empathy-driven tone

expanding_signal_wave.description: An animated concentric ring pulse radiating outward from a central point on a star-map backdrop, with a labeled radius ring showing current light-year distance
