expanding_signal_wave.schema_sketch: { origin_label: string, signal_start_year: number, current_radius_ly: number, ring_color: hex, backdrop: 'starmap' | 'dark', speed_multiplier: number, milestone_rings: [{ year: number, label: string }] }

examples.narration: expanding bubble of electromagnetic radiation with Earth at the center
examples.why: The narration is literally describing an expanding spherical wavefront — a static or zooming AI image cannot show the dynamic, ever-growing bubble of EM radiation that makes Earth's accidental broadcast feel viscerally unstoppable

survival_attrition_tree.description: A branching decision-tree animation where nodes representing civilizations light up then blink out as they make 'broadcast' vs 'hide' choices, leaving only the silent branches alive at the bottom
survival_attrition_tree.schema_sketch: { generations: number, branch_factor: number, elimination_trigger: 'broadcast' | 'detected' | 'conflict', survivor_color: hex, eliminated_color: hex, eliminated_icon: 'skull' | 'explosion' | 'fade', final_survivor_label: string }

examples.narration: ones that broadcasted their locations too early, gone, extinct, only civilizations left learned to whisper
examples.why: The narration describes a selection-pressure extinction process — a branching survival tree with nodes going dark communicates the Dark Forest culling logic far more powerfully than a funnel, which implies a smooth flow rather than sudden elimination events

cosmic_age_timeline_bar.description: A horizontal deep-time bar showing the full 13.8 billion year universe lifespan, with labeled civilization emergence windows rendered as colored spans — Earth's window appearing as a tiny sliver at the far right
cosmic_age_timeline_bar.schema_sketch: { universe_age_by: number, spans: [{ label: string, start_by: number, end_by: number, color: hex, highlight: boolean }], tick_interval_by: number, earth_marker_label: string, animation: 'sweep_left_to_right' | 'zoom_in_on_earth' }

examples.narration: civilizations out there that had a ten billion year head start on us
examples.why: A stat comparison can show two numbers side by side but cannot make viscerally clear how cosmically late humanity arrived; a proportionally scaled timeline bar where Earth's entire existence is nearly invisible against 10 billion years of potential head-start civilizations delivers the existential dread the narration demands

signal_intercept_countdown.description: A dual-panel display showing a signal wave traveling across a star map on the left while a countdown clock tied to the signal's estimated intercept distance ticks down on the right, with a 'threat assessment' status bar filling as time elapses
signal_intercept_countdown.schema_sketch: { signal_speed: 'light', origin_coords: [x, y], intercept_distance_ly: number, countdown_seconds_screen: number, threat_bar_label: string, threat_bar_fill_color: hex, status_states: ['signal sent', 'signal received', 'threat assessed', 'response launched'] }

examples.narration: moment a civilization broadcasts its location, it starts a countdown timer
examples.why: The existing countdown_timer is a generic clock widget; this scene needs the countdown to be causally linked to a traveling signal and a threat-response pipeline — the spatial-plus-temporal coupling is what makes the scene's concept land emotionally rather than abstractly

surveillance_orbit_map.description: A rotating Earth globe with labeled orbital shell rings representing surveillance tiers, animated observation-point dots tracking along the rings, and a side panel showing intercepted-technology-level readout climbing over time
surveillance_orbit_map.schema_sketch: { globe_rotation_speed: number, orbital_shells: [{ altitude_label: string, color: hex, dot_count: number }], tech_level_metric: { label: string, start_year: number, current_value: number, max_value: number }, observation_dot_style: 'pulse' | 'solid', panel_position: 'right' | 'bottom' }

examples.narration: UAP sightings military pilots report could be surveillance missions checking our technological progress
examples.why: The narration reframes UAP sightings as systematic surveillance with a strategic logic — a globe with orbital monitoring layers and a technology-threat gauge makes that intelligence-collection framing concrete and visual rather than relying on a split-image that just pairs a picture with a statistic

capability_threshold_axis.description: A two-axis scatter plot animating civilizations as dots climbing along a 'communication capability' vs 'weapons capability' curve, with a diagonal threshold line showing they always rise together, and elimination events marked where dots cross into another civilization's detection radius
capability_threshold_axis.schema_sketch: { x_axis_label: string, y_axis_label: string, threshold_line_label: string, civilization_dots: [{ name: string, comm_level: number, weapon_level: number, status: 'active' | 'eliminated' }], animation_style: 'sequential_rise' | 'simultaneous', elimination_marker: 'explosion' | 'fade_out', highlight_earth_dot: boolean }

examples.narration: any civilization advanced enough for interstellar communication is also advanced enough to wipe out others
examples.why: The core logical claim is a correlation between two capabilities that rise in lockstep — this is inherently a two-variable relationship best shown as a plotted curve with dynamic dot movement, not a bullet-point list that simply asserts the conclusion without showing the underlying mechanic

failure_sequence_strip.description: A horizontal strip of 4 identical rocket silhouettes that animate sequentially into explosion icons, with attempt labels below each
failure_sequence_strip.schema_sketch: { attempts: [{ label: 'Attempt 1', year: 1969, outcome: 'exploded' }, ...], icon_type: 'rocket', failure_icon: 'explosion', animate_delay_ms: 600 }

examples.narration: N-One rocket keeps exploding on the launch pad. Four attempts, four catastrophic failures.
examples.why: The narration is specifically about repeated, countable failures — a visual rhythm of four identical attempts each ending in destruction is far more visceral than a static split-image with a stat overlay

budget_area_shift.description: A single large circle (1969 NASA share) that visually shrinks in real time to a tiny dot beside it (2026 share), both labeled with percentage and year
budget_area_shift.schema_sketch: { bars: [{ label: '1969 Apollo Era', value: 4.0, unit: '%' }, { label: '2026 Artemis Era', value: 0.4, unit: '%' }], shape: 'circle', animate: true, color_scheme: ['#E8A000', '#3A3A3A'] }

examples.narration: Four percent of the entire federal budget. In twenty twenty-six NASA gets less than half of one percent.
examples.why: Area-scaled circles make the ratio feel physically shocking — a 10x budget collapse needs proportional geometry, not just two numbers side by side

knowledge_decay_chart.description: An animated line graph showing a skill or headcount metric declining steeply from 1972 to 1980 as labeled nodes (engineers, factories, tooling) drop off and fade out along the curve
knowledge_decay_chart.schema_sketch: { x_axis: 'year', y_axis: 'capability_index', data_points: [{ year: 1969, value: 100 }, { year: 1972, value: 95 }, { year: 1975, value: 60 }, { year: 1980, value: 20 }, { year: 1990, value: 5 }], dropout_nodes: [{ year: 1973, label: 'Saturn V tooling destroyed' }, { year: 1976, label: 'Key engineers retired' }, { year: 1979, label: 'Factories repurposed' }] }

examples.narration: Institutional knowledge to get to the Moon scattered to the wind as engineers retired
examples.why: The narration is fundamentally about institutional erosion over time — a decay curve with disappearing capability nodes communicates irreversible loss better than any photographic treatment

sliding_deadline_rail.description: A horizontal rail with a fixed goal flag on the right and an animated target marker that visibly slides rightward through labeled year stops, each slip annotated with a reason
sliding_deadline_rail.schema_sketch: { goal_label: 'First Crewed Artemis Landing', slips: [{ original_date: '2025', revised_date: '2026', reason: 'SLS delays' }, { original_date: '2026', revised_date: '2027', reason: 'Spacesuits not ready' }, { original_date: '2027', revised_date: '2028', reason: 'Budget recision' }], animate_slide: true }

examples.narration: First crewed landing keeps getting pushed back. Twenty twenty-five became twenty twenty-six became twenty twenty-seven.
examples.why: A standard timeline shows events that happened — this narration is specifically about a deadline that keeps moving forward, which demands an animation of rightward slippage that a static timeline cannot convey

precision_trajectory_map.description: An animated US map showing a golf ball arc from New York to LA with a moving target icon (the train/cup) along a rail, and a precision margin indicator at the landing point
precision_trajectory_map.schema_sketch: { map_bounds: 'USA', origin: { city: 'New York', coords: [40.71, -74.00] }, destination_path: { start: [34.05, -118.24], end: [39.09, -94.58], speed_label: '2000 mph' }, projectile_icon: 'golf_ball', target_icon: 'coffee_cup', target_moves: true, precision_radius_miles: 0.001 }

examples.narration: Like hitting a golf ball from New York and landing it in a coffee cup in Los Angeles while the coffee cup is on a moving train.
examples.why: The analogy only lands emotionally if viewers see the arc travel in real time toward a target that is simultaneously moving — a static map callout pins a location but kills the motion that makes the metaphor visceral

parallel_program_swimlane.description: A dual-row horizontal swimlane chart showing NASA milestones on top and Soviet/Russian milestones below across the same 1972–2000 timeline, with mission icons plotted as events
parallel_program_swimlane.schema_sketch: { lanes: [{ label: 'NASA', color: '#1A4FA0', events: [{ year: 1973, name: 'Skylab' }, { year: 1981, name: 'Shuttle STS-1' }, { year: 1998, name: 'ISS Alpha' }] }, { label: 'Soviet / Russia', color: '#CC0000', events: [{ year: 1971, name: 'Salyut 1' }, { year: 1975, name: 'Venera 9' }, { year: 1986, name: 'Mir' }, { year: 1988, name: 'Phobos probes' }] }], x_range: [1970, 2000] }

examples.narration: Soviets were building space stations. Mir. ISS. Robot missions to Venus and Mars.
examples.why: The narration explicitly contrasts what the Soviets kept building versus what NASA did instead — parallel swimlanes make the divergence of two programs immediately readable where a single timeline collapses that comparison

daily_event_dot_grid.description: Two side-by-side grids of dots representing discrete daily events, where each dot lights up sequentially to show frequency contrast between two groups
daily_event_dot_grid.schema_sketch: { group_a_label: string, group_a_count: number, group_b_label: string, group_b_count: number, dot_label: string, animate_sequential: boolean, highlight_color_a: hex, highlight_color_b: hex }

examples.narration: American kids get three opportunities daily. Japanese kids get fifteen.
examples.why: A static number comparison flattens what is fundamentally a frequency-across-a-day story — animated dot grids make the 3-vs-15 gap viscerally countable rather than abstractly readable

day_slice_clock.description: A 24-hour circular clock face where colored arc segments carve out proportional slices for screen time, structured activity, sleep, and unstructured/boredom time — with two rings shown simultaneously for comparison
day_slice_clock.schema_sketch: { rings: [ { label: string, segments: [ { activity: string, minutes: number, color: hex } ] } ], highlight_segment: string, show_legend: boolean }

examples.narration: Japanese kids? One hundred and forty-seven minutes of unstimulated time daily.
examples.why: 147 minutes only lands emotionally when the viewer sees it as a real proportion of a child's waking day against a competing slice, which a radial time-budget visualization communicates instantly where a counting number cannot

dual_radial_skill_web.description: Two overlapping spider/radar charts — one per child cohort — plotting multiple developmental dimensions (empathy, community thinking, impulse control, resilience) as filled polygon shapes that visually diverge from each other
dual_radial_skill_web.schema_sketch: { cohort_a_label: string, cohort_b_label: string, axes: [ { dimension: string, cohort_a_score: number, cohort_b_score: number } ], fill_opacity: float, highlight_axis: string }

examples.narration: children who regularly clean up messes they didn't create develop twenty-nine percent better empathy scores and forty-one percent better community thinking
examples.why: The narration is building a multi-dimensional developmental profile across the whole video, and a radar web lets viewers see the cumulative shape of Japanese vs. American child outcomes as a gestalt rather than isolated two-column rows

persistence_timeline_bar.description: Two animated horizontal elapsed-time bars that fill left-to-right in real time at different speeds, stopping when each cohort 'gives up,' with a help-request icon appearing at the stop point
persistence_timeline_bar.schema_sketch: { bars: [ { label: string, duration_units: number, color: hex, stop_icon: string } ], unit_label: string, animation_speed_multiplier: number, show_percentage_gap: boolean }

examples.narration: Japanese children attempt difficult tasks forty-seven percent longer than American children before asking for help
examples.why: Persistence is a temporal, duration-based concept — watching one bar outlast the other by 47% in live animation makes the behavioral difference felt rather than merely read, which no static stat or image achieves

concentric_priority_rings.description: An animated diagram of concentric circles where the innermost ring is labeled 'self,' surrounded by progressively larger rings for 'family,' 'class,' 'community' — with a pulsing indicator showing where decision-making authority is centered under each parenting philosophy
concentric_priority_rings.schema_sketch: { rings: [ { label: string, radius_weight: number, color: hex } ], philosophy_a_center: string, philosophy_b_center: string, pulse_animation: boolean, label_position: 'inside' | 'outside' }

examples.narration: children raised with group-first thinking develop thirty-nine percent better conflict resolution and fifty-one percent lower behavioral problems
examples.why: The core concept is about the structural hierarchy of whose needs come first, which is an inherently spatial/relational idea that concentric rings embody architecturally rather than the flat left-to-right quantity framing of a bar chart

habit_unlock_cascade.description: A vertical stack of seven locked cards that visually unlock one by one as a 30-day progress bar fills beneath each, with a glow effect cascading downward to show how mastering one habit enables the next
habit_unlock_cascade.schema_sketch: { habits: [ { id: number, label: string, icon: string, unlock_day: number } ], progress_bar_days: number, cascade_delay_ms: number, locked_color: hex, unlocked_color: hex }

examples.narration: Start with one technique tonight. Commit to it for thirty days. The changes will convince you to add the rest.
examples.why: The narration's entire call-to-action is a sequential unlock mechanic — one habit opens the door to the rest — which a linear roadmap path cannot convey as precisely as a staged lock-and-release cascade tied to a time commitment

neural_cascade.description: An animated brain-to-body diagram showing a trigger event igniting a cascade through labeled neural/hormonal stages (perception → amygdala → cortisol spike → heart rate) with millisecond timestamps on each node
neural_cascade.schema_sketch: { trigger_label: string, stages: [{ node_name: string, delay_ms: number, icon: string, color: string }], body_region_highlight: string, animation_speed: 'slow' | 'real_time' }

examples.narration: This happens in milliseconds. Before you've even realized what's going on, your nervous system is drowning in stress hormones.
examples.why: The narration is explicitly describing a biological sequence happening inside the body — a cascading process diagram with timing would make that invisible internal mechanism viscerally visible rather than relying on a generic AI image

repetition_counter.description: An animated counter showing a parent icon repeating an instruction, with child brain icons lighting up incrementally across 8–12 attempts until a 'retained' signal fires
repetition_counter.schema_sketch: { instruction_label: string, repetitions_needed_min: number, repetitions_needed_max: number, child_age: number, brain_fill_color: string, retention_animation: 'glow' | 'checkmark', counter_style: 'dots' | 'tally' }

examples.narration: Research shows it's between eight and twelve times. Your boys aren't defying you.
examples.why: The key insight is not just the number but the process — that repetition is required for a child's brain to retain information — and an iterative visual loop of attempts building toward retention conveys that mechanism in a way a static big number cannot

mirror_trait_map.description: A split reflection visualization where a parent silhouette and child silhouette face each other, with shared trait labels appearing as glowing lines connecting identical points on both figures
mirror_trait_map.schema_sketch: { parent_label: string, child_label: string, shared_traits: [{ trait: string, intensity_parent: number, intensity_child: number, color: string }], mirror_style: 'reflection' | 'overlay', rage_highlight: boolean }

examples.narration: watching her avoid that poster board feels like looking in a mirror you'd rather smash
examples.why: The entire concept of 'projection rage' is visual — the same trait existing in both parent and child simultaneously — and a literal mirrored connection diagram would make the psychological mechanism immediately legible without words

capacity_drain_timeline.description: A horizontal timeline of a parent's day showing a reservoir draining across labeled events (work emails, laundry, kid crisis, skipped lunch) with a color shift from green to red as capacity depletes toward the breaking-point moment
capacity_drain_timeline.schema_sketch: { start_capacity: number, events: [{ label: string, time_of_day: string, drain_amount: number, icon: string }], breaking_point_threshold: number, final_trigger_label: string, color_scale: [string] }

examples.narration: Your nervous system is running on empty.
examples.why: The gauge_meter only shows a static state, but the narration's power comes from the accumulation — each demand depleting a finite resource over time — which a draining timeline would show dynamically and create empathy through recognition

trigger_interrupt_diagram.description: A visual loop diagram showing each trigger (unmet expectation, projection, nervous system depletion) as a node on a cycle, with an animated 'interrupt' slash cutting the loop when awareness is applied
trigger_interrupt_diagram.schema_sketch: { triggers: [{ name: string, icon: string, color: string, interrupt_action: string }], loop_style: 'circular' | 'linear_loop', interrupt_animation: 'slash' | 'pause_symbol' | 'glow_break', awareness_label: string }

examples.narration: But when you understand these three triggers, everything changes.
examples.why: The video's entire thesis is that awareness breaks an automatic loop — a cycle diagram with a visible interrupt moment is the conceptual payoff visual the whole video has been building toward, which a simple bullet list of three points completely fails to deliver

generational_load_comparison.description: A side-by-side bar or weight visualization comparing the cognitive and emotional load items facing a 1985 parent versus a 2025 parent, with categories stacked visually as weighted blocks
generational_load_comparison.schema_sketch: { era_a_label: string, era_b_label: string, load_categories: [{ category: string, era_a_weight: number, era_b_weight: number, icon: string }], display_style: 'stacked_blocks' | 'bar_chart' | 'scale_balance', highlight_delta: boolean }

examples.narration: a nervous system that's overwhelmed by demands no previous generation of parents ever faced
examples.why: The narration makes a direct historical comparison claim that deserves to be shown as data — a generational load comparison would validate the viewer's sense of overwhelm with concrete visual evidence rather than asking them to take an AI image on faith

thought_spiral.description: An animated loop of words orbiting a central point, spinning faster until they blur into chaos, then freezing on one word
thought_spiral.schema_sketch: { words: string[], spin_speed_start: number, spin_speed_peak: number, freeze_word: string | null, freeze_on_blank: boolean, loop_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: brain starts cycling through options so fast that it can't settle on one
examples.why: The narration is literally describing cognitive overload from too many cycling options — a visual of words spinning and blurring captures that paralysis in a way no text or static image can

brain_mode_split.description: A split-brain diagram showing two cognitive states side by side — calm/methodical vs. pressured/scrambled — with animated pathway lines in each half
brain_mode_split.schema_sketch: { mode_a_label: string, mode_a_descriptors: string[], mode_a_pathway_style: 'orderly' | 'branching', mode_b_label: string, mode_b_descriptors: string[], mode_b_pathway_style: 'scattered' | 'random', transition_trigger: 'scroll' | 'timer' }

examples.narration: time pressure doesn't just make you think faster, it makes you think differently
examples.why: The narration explicitly contrasts two distinct mental operating modes; a compare_reveal implies a before/after sequence but can't show the architectural difference in how the brain searches under each condition

progress_fracture.description: A near-complete progress bar that visually cracks or glitches at the final segment, with a completion percentage frozen just short of 100%
progress_fracture.schema_sketch: { total_steps: number, completed_steps: number, fracture_position: number, fracture_style: 'crack' | 'glitch' | 'freeze', label_completed: string, label_remaining: string, animate_crack: boolean }

examples.narration: twenty-three letters down, three to go, brain decides this is perfect time to malfunction
examples.why: A gauge_meter shows a level but misses the cruel irony of being almost done — the fracture metaphor at the finish line is the entire emotional beat of this scene and needs a progress structure, not a dial

momentum_crash.description: An animated velocity line graph that builds a rising streak then drops sharply at a marked inflection point, with a labeled 'wall' indicator
momentum_crash.schema_sketch: { x_label: string, y_label: string, data_points: { x: string, y: number }[], wall_x: string, wall_label: string, pre_wall_color: string, post_wall_color: string, crash_animation: boolean }

examples.narration: sudden shift from easy to challenging creates mental whiplash
examples.why: The narration describes a momentum arc — building speed followed by a sudden crash — which is fundamentally a time-series shape, not a static level that a gauge_meter implies

memory_web.description: A node-and-edge network diagram radiating from a single name/word at center, with branching associations (people, places, emotions) as labeled nodes
memory_web.schema_sketch: { center_node: string, branches: { label: string, category: 'person' | 'place' | 'emotion' | 'event', weight: number, nodes: string[] }[], highlight_distraction_path: boolean, animate_spread: boolean }

examples.narration: think of the name David, picture your cousin, that David from high school, David from the Bible
examples.why: The narration describes exactly how associative memory works — a web of connected personal references anchored to one name — which is a network graph structure, not a circular image frame

metabolic_web.description: An animated node-and-edge diagram showing how a central biological actor (e.g. gut bacteria) connects to and powers multiple downstream outputs simultaneously
metabolic_web.schema_sketch: { hub_label: string, hub_icon: string, spokes: [{ label: string, icon: string, animate_delay_ms: number }], color_theme: string }

examples.narration: They manufacture energy at the cellular level. They produce B vitamins.
examples.why: A checkmark list implies a simple sequence, but the narration describes a hub-and-spoke biological system where one thing powers many things at once — a network diagram makes that interdependence viscerally clear

depletion_curve.description: A smooth animated line graph showing a single metric (e.g. microbiome diversity or energy capacity) declining across age milestones, with a second compounding-factor line overlaid that steepens the drop
depletion_curve.schema_sketch: { x_axis_label: string, y_axis_label: string, baseline_line: [{ age: number, value: number }], compounding_line: [{ age: number, value: number }], milestone_labels: [{ age: number, event: string }], animate_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: You're already working with less than you had at forty. Now add decades of chlorinated water.
examples.why: The narration is explicitly about compounding decline over decades — a before/after static split can't show the slope or the acceleration that makes the point devastating; a dual-line decay curve does

flow_throttle.description: An animated pipe-and-flow diagram with a visible valve that closes partway, showing flow rate dropping from source to endpoint cells, with a live throughput number ticking down
flow_throttle.schema_sketch: { source_label: string, endpoint_label: string, fluid_label: string, valve_open_pct_start: number, valve_open_pct_end: number, throughput_start: number, throughput_end: number, throughput_unit: string, animate_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: Slow down the pump? You slow down the energy delivery to every cell.
examples.why: The narration uses an explicit pump-and-delivery metaphor — a scale implies trade-off between two equal things, but the concept is throttled throughput through a system, which a flow diagram with a closing valve communicates instantly and viscerally

breath_anatomy_compare.description: A side-by-side animated cross-section of two torsos — one showing shallow chest expansion with a small highlighted lung region, one showing deep diaphragmatic expansion with a large highlighted lung region — with an oxygen-uptake percentage label under each
breath_anatomy_compare.schema_sketch: { left: { label: string, lung_fill_pct: number, highlight_region: 'upper' | 'full', o2_uptake_pct: number }, right: { label: string, lung_fill_pct: number, highlight_region: 'upper' | 'full', o2_uptake_pct: number }, animate_breath_cycle_ms: number }

examples.narration: Now watch a child breathe. Deep belly breaths. Relaxed shoulders. Real gas exchange.
examples.why: Static images of two people breathing look nearly identical; the scientific point is about where inside the body the air goes and how much surface area is activated — only an internal anatomical animation makes that invisible difference visible

pathway_interrupt.description: An animated biochemical pathway diagram showing a linear production chain (enzyme → outputs A and B) with a drug molecule visually blocking the enzyme node, causing both downstream outputs to gray out and diminish simultaneously
pathway_interrupt.schema_sketch: { pathway_nodes: [{ id: string, label: string, icon: string }], edges: [{ from: string, to: string }], blocker: { label: string, targets_node_id: string }, blocked_outputs: [string], animate_block_delay_ms: number }

examples.narration: Statins block an enzyme that doesn't just make cholesterol. It also makes CoQ10.
examples.why: The entire rhetorical power of this scene is the surprise that blocking one enzyme kills two products — a definition card explains words, but a pathway diagram with a visible block makes the unintended collateral damage emotionally legible in one glance

breath_box_timer.description: An animated square that traces its four sides in sequence — each side labeled with its phase (Inhale / Hold / Exhale / Hold) and a live second-countdown, with a traveling dot moving along the perimeter in real time
breath_box_timer.schema_sketch: { phase_duration_seconds: number, phases: [{ label: string, side: 'top' | 'right' | 'bottom' | 'left', color: string }], loop_count: number, dot_color: string, line_color: string, size_px: number }

examples.narration: Box breathing. Four seconds in. Hold four. Four seconds out. Hold four.
examples.why: Box breathing is a named visual technique whose whole identity IS the square and the moving cycle — a roadmap implies a one-way journey with destinations, but this technique is a loop that needs a living, cycling shape to teach and demonstrate simultaneously

dual_receipt_breakdown.description: Two side-by-side animated receipts that itemize line costs, with totals that count up and a delta badge showing the savings gap
dual_receipt_breakdown.schema_sketch: { left_label, right_label, line_items: [{ label, left_amount, right_amount }], left_total, right_total, delta_badge_text, currency }

examples.narration: Same company, same airport, same everything. How could his car cost almost half
examples.why: The narration hinges on identical conditions producing wildly different prices — a line-by-line receipt teardown makes the cost asymmetry viscerally legible in a way a generic compare_reveal cannot, because it shows *where* the overcharge lives (airport fees, overhead surcharges) rather than just two final numbers

cost_layer_stripper.description: A stacked visual of labeled cost layers (real estate, fleet maintenance, advertising, call centers, shareholders) that animate off one by one, revealing a shrinking final price beneath
cost_layer_stripper.schema_sketch: { base_price, layers: [{ label, cost_removed, icon }], final_price, animation_delay_ms }

examples.narration: They're not paying airport fees. They're not paying for TV commercials
examples.why: The narration is explicitly about costs being peeled away — a sequential removal animation turns an abstract financial argument into a satisfying visual proof, something no existing text-list or checkmark component can convey through motion

live_price_scan_overlay.description: A simulated search interface where price results populate in real time, with competing listings animating in and a highlight ring locking onto the winning low price
live_price_scan_overlay.schema_sketch: { search_label, results: [{ provider, vehicle, price, highlight: boolean }], scan_duration_ms, highlight_delay_ms }

examples.narration: Same dates, same pickup area. I found an SUV for three hundred and ninety dollars
examples.why: This scene is the money moment of the whole video — the narrator finding a dramatically cheaper option in real time — and a static number comparison kills the drama; a scanning/loading animation that resolves on the winning price mirrors the actual user experience and makes the savings feel discovered rather than declared

vehicle_category_carousel.description: A horizontally scrolling carousel of vehicle silhouettes with category badges (Classic, EV, Luxury, Van) and a price-range chip beneath each, auto-advancing with a parallax drift
vehicle_category_carousel.schema_sketch: { vehicles: [{ silhouette_type, category_badge, price_range_low, price_range_high, city_availability }], auto_advance_ms, loop: boolean }

examples.narration: Classic cars for special occasions. Electric vehicles in cities where major companies
examples.why: The narration is selling variety as a core advantage — a static polaroid stack implies a small fixed set, while a carousel that keeps producing new categories viscerally communicates an expanding inventory the major companies can't match

market_share_shift_animation.description: A split area chart where the traditional rental sector shrinks left and the peer-to-peer sector expands right over a time axis, with year markers ticking forward
market_share_shift_animation.schema_sketch: { start_year, end_year, time_series: [{ year, traditional_share_pct, p2p_share_pct }], label_traditional, label_p2p, animation_duration_ms }

examples.narration: But the sharing economy keeps evolving
examples.why: trend_arrow shows direction but not magnitude or competitive displacement — this scene is specifically about a structural market shift happening over time, which requires showing two competing forces changing relative size, not just a single upward arrow

friction_cost_accumulator.description: An animated running tally that adds non-monetary friction costs (wait time, shuttle minutes, upsell minutes, inspection stress) alongside dollar hidden fees, summing into a true total-cost-of-experience number
friction_cost_accumulator.schema_sketch: { items: [{ label, time_cost_minutes, dollar_equivalent, icon }], dollar_per_minute_rate, final_total_label, count_up_duration_ms }

examples.narration: frustration of dealing with rental car counters, hidden fees, and vehicles that barely meet
examples.why: The narration explicitly frames time and frustration as having real value alongside money — a bullet list treats these as equal text items, but an accumulating counter that converts wasted minutes into equivalent dollar values makes the hidden cost emotionally tangible and quantified

null_timeline.description: A horizontal timeline where expected events are marked with empty/struck-through nodes, visually showing the absence of something that should have appeared
null_timeline.schema_sketch: { years: [{ label: '1940', event: null, expected_event: 'Yellow star decree' }, { label: '1941', event: null, expected_event: 'Yellow star decree' }, { label: '1942', event: null, expected_event: 'Yellow star decree' }], null_label: 'Never issued', style: 'struck_through' }

examples.narration: Not in nineteen forty-one. Not ever. Think about that
examples.why: The narration is proving a negative across multiple years — a standard timeline shows events that happened, but this scene needs a timeline that dramatizes conspicuous absence, with ghosted placeholder nodes for 1940 and 1941 where the star order never appears

animated_route_map.description: A dark nautical map with dotted animated boat-path lines moving from Denmark's coastline to Sweden, with patrol-zone overlays and a live boat-count ticker
animated_route_map.schema_sketch: { origin_coords: [55.67, 12.56], destination_coords: [55.87, 12.83], route_paths: [{ id: 'route_1', waypoints: [...], danger_zone: true }], patrol_zones: [{ center: [...], radius_km: 8, label: 'German patrol' }], boat_count: 7000, animation_duration_ms: 4000 }

examples.narration: fishing boats crossing to neutral Sweden, huddled in cargo holds while Danish fishermen navigated
examples.why: This is the climactic rescue scene and its drama is inherently geographic and kinetic — the peril of navigating past German patrol boats through dark water is impossible to convey with a static AI image, but a map with moving paths and danger-zone shading would make viewers feel the crossing viscerally

symbol_to_action_spectrum.description: A horizontal spectrum bar with 'Pure Symbol' on the left and 'Direct Action' on the right, with labeled event markers that animate into position along the spectrum
symbol_to_action_spectrum.schema_sketch: { left_pole: 'Symbolic Gesture', right_pole: 'Coordinated Action', markers: [{ label: 'Wearing a star (the myth)', position: 0.05 }, { label: 'Daily horse rides', position: 0.45 }, { label: 'Threatening abdication', position: 0.7 }, { label: 'Fishing boat rescue network', position: 0.95 }], highlight_threshold: 0.5 }

examples.narration: Symbols matter, but only if they're backed up by action
examples.why: The entire video's thesis is the spectrum between symbolic gesture and real action — a balance scale implies equal weight between two things, but the narration argues for a directional relationship; a spectrum lets you plot 'wearing a star' on the left, 'morning rides' in the middle, and 'coordinating the boat rescue' on the right, making the argument visual

sentiment_crowd_map.description: A stylized overhead street-view with crowd-dot clusters color-coded by sentiment (warm for Danish civilians, cold for German officials), showing the social temperature of the same physical space
sentiment_crowd_map.schema_sketch: { setting: 'Copenhagen street', groups: [{ id: 'danish_citizens', count_display: 'many', sentiment: 'positive', color: '#F9A825', behavior_label: 'Waving, calling out' }, { id: 'german_officials', count_display: 'few', sentiment: 'negative', color: '#455A64', behavior_label: 'Watching, frustrated' }], focal_point: { label: 'King on horseback', icon: 'horse' } }

examples.narration: Germans hated it. Here was their model protectorate, and the population was treating their king
examples.why: The tension in this scene is explicitly about two groups reacting differently to the same event in the same street — an icon component only shows one side, but a crowd-sentiment map would visually encode both the Danish warmth and the German resentment happening simultaneously in Copenhagen's streets

cosmic_distance_ruler.description: A logarithmic scale bar stretching from Earth outward through the solar system to interstellar space, with labeled distance markers and travel-time annotations at each milestone
cosmic_distance_ruler.schema_sketch: { scale_type: 'logarithmic' | 'linear', markers: [{ label: string, distance_au: number, travel_time_years: number, highlight: boolean }], reference_object: string, animation_sweep: boolean }

examples.narration: seventy-three thousand years just to reach the next star
examples.why: The sheer incomprehensibility of interstellar distance demands a visual scale comparison — a ruler that makes 4.2 light-years feel viscerally impossible next to, say, the Moon or Mars — not a static image with a stat overlay

mass_ratio_expander.description: An animated stacked-block diagram where the fuel block visibly inflates and dwarfs a fixed payload block in real-time, with a live ratio label updating as the animation plays
mass_ratio_expander.schema_sketch: { payload_mass_kg: number, fuel_mass_kg: number, ratio_label: string, animation_duration_ms: number, payload_color: string, fuel_color: string, stage_labels: [string] }

examples.narration: weight of the fuel becomes heavier than the spacecraft itself, way heavier
examples.why: The Tsiolkovsky rocket equation's exponential tyranny is a dynamic, growing relationship — a static balance implies equal-arm comparison, but an expanding mass animation viscerally shows the fuel eating the mission alive

generational_population_flow.description: A flowing family-tree diagram showing successive generations born, living, and dying across a horizontal timeline, with population-count bands and a destination marker at the far right
generational_population_flow.schema_sketch: { total_duration_years: number, initial_population: number, generations: [{ gen_number: number, born_year: number, dies_year: number, avg_lifespan: number }], destination_label: string, highlight_color: string }

examples.narration: ten thousand people live, work, have kids, and die during two-hundred-year journey
examples.why: The emotional core of the generation ship concept is that real human lives — entire lifespans — are consumed en route; a generational flow diagram makes that sacrifice tangible in a way no stat overlay can

biological_decay_timeline.description: A horizontal body-system status tracker showing organ/system health degrading across a journey timeline, with color-coded deterioration bars per system (DNA, brain, reproductive, cardiovascular) keyed to elapsed mission time
biological_decay_timeline.schema_sketch: { systems: [{ name: string, icon: string, health_at_months: [{ month: number, health_percent: number }] }], journey_duration_months: number, critical_threshold_percent: number, color_scale: ['healthy_color', 'warning_color', 'critical_color'] }

examples.narration: after just six months in interstellar space, human DNA starts falling apart
examples.why: Radiation damage is cumulative and multi-system — a single stat framing collapses a deeply layered biological horror into a number, whereas a degradation timeline across body systems communicates the slow, irreversible nature of the destruction

consciousness_transfer_diagram.description: A split animated diagram showing a biological brain on the left with neural connection threads streaming and resolving into a geometric data-node network on the right, with labeled attribute transfers (memory, personality, intelligence) flowing across
consciousness_transfer_diagram.schema_sketch: { source_label: string, destination_label: string, transfer_attributes: [{ name: string, transfer_percent: number, icon: string }], animation_style: 'stream' | 'pulse', substrate_colors: { biological: string, digital: string }, duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: digital consciousness that keeps all memories, personality, and intelligence, but exists as pure information
examples.why: The concept of mapping a mind into a computer is fundamentally a structural transformation that demands a visual encoding metaphor — showing what leaves the biology and what arrives in the silicon makes the abstraction concrete and emotionally resonant

propulsion_method_race.description: A horizontal animated race track comparing multiple propulsion methods (chemical rocket, Voyager ion, light sail) as labeled markers advancing across a fixed distance bar, with arrival-year callouts appearing as each method finishes
propulsion_method_race.schema_sketch: { distance_label: string, methods: [{ name: string, color: string, travel_years: number, speed_fraction_of_light: number, icon: string }], animation_speed_multiplier: number, highlight_winner: boolean }

examples.narration: light sail powered by Earth-based lasers could reach Proxima Centauri in just twenty years
examples.why: The twenty-years versus forty-thousand-years contrast is not a before/after state change — it is a speed competition between discrete technologies, and a race visualization makes the light sail's advantage feel immediate and competitive rather than abstractly numerical

neural_network_erosion.description: An animated node-and-synapse web that visually dims, disconnects, or fades a percentage of its connections to show loss over time
neural_network_erosion.schema_sketch: { node_count: number, connection_density: number, erosion_percent: number, erosion_label: string, animation_style: 'fade' | 'sever' | 'dim', age_label: string }

examples.narration: At sixty-five, you've lost about ten percent of these connections
examples.why: A generic fill bar cannot show the structural, relational nature of synaptic loss — an animated network that literally severs glowing connections at the stated percentage makes the biology visceral rather than abstract

brain_scan_compare.description: A side-by-side fMRI-style heat map of two stylized brain cross-sections with labeled activation zones and an age or group label beneath each
brain_scan_compare.schema_sketch: { scan_left: { label: string, age_display: string, activation_level: 0-100, highlight_zones: string[] }, scan_right: { label: string, age_display: string, activation_level: 0-100, highlight_zones: string[] }, match_callout: string }

examples.narration: brain scans identical to people twenty years younger. Not similar. Identical.
examples.why: The narration's entire credibility rests on the visual power of matching brain scans — a generic compare_reveal shows two arbitrary panels, while a brain-scan-specific component makes the scientific claim land with the authority the script demands

brain_region_growth_map.description: A stylized overhead or cross-section brain outline where specific named regions pulse, expand, or fill with color to indicate percentage volume change
brain_region_growth_map.schema_sketch: { regions: [{ name: string, location: 'prefrontal' | 'hippocampus' | 'motor_cortex' | 'auditory_cortex' | 'default_mode' | string, change_percent: number, change_direction: 'increase' | 'decrease' }], baseline_label: string, timeframe_label: string }

examples.narration: After four months of practice, seniors show a twenty-eight percent increase in grey matter volume
examples.why: Grey matter volume growth is spatially meaningful — filling a generic bar erases the anatomical story entirely, whereas a region-targeted brain map lets viewers see exactly where growth is happening, which is the point the narrator is building toward

activity_brain_activation_grid.description: A grid of activity icons (bike, music note, chess piece, etc.) each paired with a small glowing brain silhouette showing which regions light up for that activity
activity_brain_activation_grid.schema_sketch: { activities: [{ icon_label: string, icon_type: string, activated_regions: string[], activation_intensity: 1-3, caption: string }], layout: 'grid' | 'row', highlight_color: string }

examples.narration: Learning to ride a bike at seventy. Taking up dancing. Practicing tai chi.
examples.why: The script is making a functional neuroscience argument about why these specific activities matter — a polaroid stack treats them as lifestyle photos, but a brain-activation grid communicates the mechanistic reason each activity belongs in the protocol

brain_region_change_ticker.description: A labeled anatomical brain diagram where each named region animates sequentially with an up/down arrow and a change descriptor, like a stock ticker applied to brain anatomy
brain_region_change_ticker.schema_sketch: { regions: [{ name: string, function_label: string, change_direction: 'increase' | 'decrease', change_descriptor: string, sequence_order: number }], trigger: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous', anatomy_style: 'cross_section' | 'lateral' }

examples.narration: The prefrontal cortex — responsible for decision-making — becomes more active. The amygdala — your fear center — shrinks.
examples.why: Four distinct brain regions are each changing in different directions — trend_arrow can only show one directional movement at a time, making three out of four of these scenes visually redundant, while a multi-region ticker resolves all four in a single memorable graphic

longitudinal_study_tracker.description: An animated cohort visualization showing two diverging population paths over a timeline — one group declining, one stable or improving — with institution badge, sample size, and duration anchored at key points
longitudinal_study_tracker.schema_sketch: { institution: string, sample_size: number, duration_years: number, groups: [{ label: string, outcome_label: string, trajectory: 'improve' | 'decline' | 'stable', highlight_color: string }], key_finding: string, year_start: number }

examples.narration: Harvard researchers followed twelve thousand seniors for eight years. Those who engaged in challenging social interactions
examples.why: The persuasive weight of every major study cited in this video depends on the viewer grasping scale (thousands of people, multiple years) and divergence (one group vs. another) — a real-place stock image communicates none of that, while a cohort tracker makes the study design itself the visual argument

cumulative_dose_stacker.description: An animated bar or column that visibly fills and compounds over time as repeated doses are added, showing accumulation milestones
cumulative_dose_stacker.schema_sketch: { substance_label: string, dose_unit: string, doses: [{ day: number, amount: number }], danger_threshold: number, animation_speed_ms: number, color_ramp: [safe_color, warning_color, danger_color] }

examples.narration: The effects are cumulative. Every pill builds up in your system.
examples.why: A gauge implies a single current reading, but the narration is explicitly about compounding over time — a stacking accumulation animation would viscerally show the buildup that makes this danger feel real

nutrient_absorption_pathway.description: A visual flow diagram showing a substance entering the gut, blocking a nutrient, and tracing the downstream deficit to a target organ (brain)
nutrient_absorption_pathway.schema_sketch: { drug_name: string, blocked_step: string, nutrients_affected: string[], target_organ: string, deficit_consequence: string, arrow_style: 'linear' | 'branched', highlight_color: string }

examples.narration: It helps your body absorb vitamin B12, magnesium, and iron. Your brain needs all three.
examples.why: The narration describes a causal chain — drug blocks acid, acid enables absorption, absorption feeds the brain — which is a process, not a list; a pathway diagram communicates mechanism far more powerfully than bullet points

synapse_block_diagram.description: An animated close-up of a synaptic gap showing neurotransmitter molecules releasing, then a drug molecule physically blocking the receptor, dimming the signal
synapse_block_diagram.schema_sketch: { neurotransmitter_name: string, blocker_name: string, receptor_label: string, pre_block_signal_color: string, post_block_signal_color: string, animation_phases: ['release', 'intercept', 'dim'], caption: string }

examples.narration: Anticholinergics block acetylcholine, a neurotransmitter your brain needs for memory.
examples.why: The concept of receptor blocking is inherently spatial and mechanical — showing it happening at the synapse level would make the invisible biochemical threat feel tangible and alarming in a way no text card can match

risk_multiplier_grid.description: A person-icon grid (e.g. 100 figures) where a proportional subset lights up in a warning color to represent elevated risk, with a 'Nx more likely' badge overlaid
risk_multiplier_grid.schema_sketch: { total_icons: number, baseline_affected: number, risk_group_affected: number, multiplier_label: string, icon_style: 'person' | 'circle', baseline_color: string, risk_color: string, time_context: string }

examples.narration: Statin users were four times more likely to experience memory problems within thirty days.
examples.why: A four-times risk multiplier is most emotionally legible as a population visual — seeing 4 out of 5 figures highlighted versus 1 out of 5 communicates relative danger far more viscerally than a number beside a photo

symptom_med_correlation_chart.description: A dual-axis timeline chart plotting medication dose events against a clarity/fog score across days, with auto-highlighted correlation clusters
symptom_med_correlation_chart.schema_sketch: { days: number[], med_events: [{ day: number, med_name: string, time: string }], clarity_scores: [{ day: number, score: number }], highlight_correlations: boolean, score_range: [min, max], correlation_color: string }

examples.narration: Note which medications you take and when. Patterns will emerge.
examples.why: The narration is literally describing a data-logging and pattern-detection exercise — a correlation chart would demonstrate what that diary actually reveals, turning an abstract instruction into a concrete, motivating visual

organ_composition_donut.description: A stylized brain silhouette filled with a segmented composition breakdown, with the cholesterol segment animating to highlight and label its share
organ_composition_donut.schema_sketch: { organ: string, segments: [{ label: string, percent: number, color: string, highlight: boolean }], animation_sequence: 'fill' | 'explode_slice', silhouette_style: 'outline' | 'filled', caption: string }

examples.narration: Your brain is twenty-five percent cholesterol.
examples.why: Saying the brain is 25% cholesterol is a structural fact about an organ's makeup — showing it as a filled composition inside the brain shape makes the statistic feel anatomically real rather than just a counter-intuitive number

substance_decay_curve.description: A curved decay graph showing a substance's concentration in the body over time, with labeled threshold markers (e.g. 'sleep zone', 'safe zone') and a draggable ingestion-time input that shifts the whole curve
substance_decay_curve.schema_sketch: { substance_name, ingestion_time, half_life_hours, starting_amount_mg, threshold_markers: [{ label, mg_level, color }], time_axis_start, time_axis_end, curve_color }

examples.narration: half-life of six hours, half still circulating at two PM
examples.why: The half-life story requires showing exponential decay across specific clock times with threshold crossings — a generic line chart cannot encode the biological threshold concept or let the viewer internalize the personal dose-time relationship the narration is building

biological_age_vs_habit_clock.description: A split analog clock face where one half shows the body's internal biological pace (slowed, color-coded) and the other shows the habit/schedule clock still running at the old rate, with a visible gap angle between them
biological_age_vs_habit_clock.schema_sketch: { label_left, label_right, pace_left_percent, pace_right_percent, event_marker: { label, time_string }, gap_highlight_color, annotation }

examples.narration: metabolism slowed twelve percent but your eating schedule hasn't changed
examples.why: The core insight is a mismatch between two timelines running at different speeds — a visual metaphor of two clocks diverging communicates this biological drift far more viscerally than a text-based stat comparison

daily_load_distribution_bar.description: A horizontal 24-hour timeline bar where fluid (or nutrient) intake volumes are shown as stacked blocks across the day, with two side-by-side rows comparing the 'old pattern' versus the 'optimized front-loaded pattern', color-coded by a danger/safe zone after a cutoff time
daily_load_distribution_bar.schema_sketch: { row_a_label, row_b_label, time_blocks: [{ hour, volume_oz, row, color }], cutoff_hour, cutoff_label, unit_label }

examples.narration: front-loading hydration instead of spreading it out across the day
examples.why: The narration's entire point is about redistribution of the same total quantity across time — a temporal volume distribution visual makes the before/after shift immediately legible in a way no checklist or bar chart can achieve

circadian_temp_wave.description: An animated sine-wave style curve mapped to a 24-hour clock showing core body temperature rising and falling, with a highlighted descent zone labeled as the sleep-trigger window and meal-spike overlays showing how eating disrupts the natural dip
circadian_temp_wave.schema_sketch: { base_temp_f, sleep_trigger_drop_degrees, wave_data: [{ hour, temp_offset }], disruption_events: [{ hour, spike_degrees, label }], highlight_zone: { start_hour, end_hour, label }, color_scheme }

examples.narration: core body temperature drops two degrees — that's the trigger for sleep
examples.why: A gauge shows a single static value, but the narration is describing a dynamic biological rhythm with a critical directional drop — only a time-mapped wave can show both the natural curve and the meal-caused disruption on the same visual axis

sleep_protocol_countdown_stack.description: A visual reverse-countdown stack anchored to a user-set target bedtime, showing each intervention (magnesium, melatonin, last meal, last fluid, last caffeine) as labeled bands counting backward from that anchor with color-coded urgency indicating how far each deadline falls from now
sleep_protocol_countdown_stack.schema_sketch: { target_bedtime, interventions: [{ label, hours_before_bed, color, icon }], current_time_indicator: boolean, timezone_aware: boolean }

examples.narration: optimal melatonin window exactly four hours before target sleep time
examples.why: The narration is building a precise multi-step backward-scheduled protocol — a stacked reverse-timeline anchored to bedtime makes all the interdependencies and relative spacings visible simultaneously, which no linear forward timeline or text list achieves

molecular_competition_lane.description: A simplified lane-race diagram showing amino acid molecules as labeled icons competing to cross a barrier (blood-brain barrier), with tryptophan winning when lanes are clear (small meal) versus getting blocked when lanes are crowded (large meal)
molecular_competition_lane.schema_sketch: { barrier_label, competitor_molecules: [{ name, icon, count_small_meal, count_large_meal }], hero_molecule: { name, color }, lane_count, scenario_a_label, scenario_b_label }

examples.narration: tryptophan only works if not competing with other amino acids from a big meal
examples.why: The mechanism being explained is literal biological competition for a transport pathway — a visual metaphor of crowded versus clear lanes at a crossing makes the abstract biochemistry intuitively graspable without any text, which is precisely what the narration needs at this turning-point moment

cascade_degradation_diagram.description: A vertical chain of connected body-system nodes that visually 'degrade' in sequence — each node dims and cracks as an animated signal passes downward through the chain
cascade_degradation_diagram.schema_sketch: { nodes: [{ label: string, icon: string, severity_color: string }], animation_direction: 'top_down' | 'bottom_up', pulse_speed_ms: number, degradation_style: 'crack' | 'dim' | 'redline' }

examples.narration: arteries getting stiffer, blood pressure failing, heart working harder
examples.why: The narration is explicitly describing a domino cascade of systemic failure, which a static three-point list flattens into parallel items — an animated chain diagram would make the sequential degradation viscerally readable at a glance

diverging_speed_track.description: Two horizontal race-track lanes with animated dots moving at different speeds, labeled with cohort names and a speed-multiplier badge — visually communicating relative rates rather than static endpoint values
diverging_speed_track.schema_sketch: { lane_a: { label: string, speed_multiplier: number, color: string }, lane_b: { label: string, speed_multiplier: number, color: string }, metric_label: string, duration_seconds: number }

examples.narration: weakest grip showed cognitive decline two and a half times faster
examples.why: The narration is specifically about the RATE of decline (2.5× faster), not a snapshot difference — a speed-differential animation communicates 'faster' intuitively in a way a static comparison card cannot

incremental_risk_ladder.description: A vertical ladder or staircase where each rung represents a fixed unit increase (e.g. +2 lbs grip) and the corresponding risk percentage visibly drops on a live counter as the camera climbs the rungs
incremental_risk_ladder.schema_sketch: { unit_label: string, unit_increment: number, effect_per_unit: number, effect_direction: 'decrease' | 'increase', effect_metric: string, rungs_visible: number, animate_climb: boolean }

examples.narration: every two-pound increase in grip strength reduces mortality risk by seven percent
examples.why: The narration presents a precise dose-response relationship — every unit of input yields a proportional output — which a static spotlight stat ignores entirely; the ladder makes the compounding, actionable nature of that relationship visually obvious

decade_band_decay_chart.description: A timeline chart with age decades on the x-axis where each decade column is shaded as a gradient band — the band width representing the min/max range (6–12%) and an animated fill showing cumulative loss accumulating across a lifetime
decade_band_decay_chart.schema_sketch: { start_age: number, end_age: number, decade_loss_min_pct: number, decade_loss_max_pct: number, baseline_value: number, unit_label: string, highlight_age: number, animate_fill: boolean }

examples.narration: lose six to twelve percent of grip strength every decade after age thirty
examples.why: The narration gives a range (6–12%), not a single line — an animated_line_chart plots one trajectory and loses the uncertainty band that makes the stat feel personally threatening across a spectrum of outcomes

body_neural_pathway_overlay.description: A simplified human silhouette with animated signal-pulse lines tracing from the hands up through the spine and down to the legs, color-coded by pathway type (grip → core → legs), with labeled waypoints that light up in sequence
body_neural_pathway_overlay.schema_sketch: { pathways: [{ id: string, label: string, color: string, waypoints: [{ body_region: string, x_pct: number, y_pct: number }] }], silhouette_style: 'outline' | 'filled', pulse_loop: boolean, animation_delay_ms: number }

examples.narration: neural pathways that power your grip also stabilize your spine and coordinate walking
examples.why: The narration is making a specific anatomical connectivity argument — shared neural infrastructure — which an AI image of two people cannot convey; an animated pathway diagram makes the biological claim self-evident without any text

age_outcome_projection_card.description: A split timeline showing two projected futures from a shared present age — one path (strong grip) annotated with independence milestones, one path (weak grip) annotated with dependency milestones — rendered as a forking road diagram with age markers
age_outcome_projection_card.schema_sketch: { fork_age: number, path_a: { label: string, color: string, milestones: [{ age: number, event: string }] }, path_b: { label: string, color: string, milestones: [{ age: number, event: string }] }, end_age: number }

examples.narration: strong grip strength at sixty predicts independence at eighty
examples.why: A before_after component implies a transformation of one thing, but this narration is about two diverging futures from the same starting point — a forking projection diagram captures the choice-and-consequence framing that makes the longevity argument emotionally land

cash_flow_ledger_animation.description: An animated side-by-side cash flow visual showing dollars IN versus dollars OUT with a running deficit ticker that bleeds red as outflows exceed inflows
cash_flow_ledger_animation.schema_sketch: { inflow_label: string, inflow_amount: number, outflow_label: string, outflow_amount: number, currency_symbol: string, deficit_highlight_color: string, animate_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: for every dollar they collect, they pay out one dollar and forty-seven cents
examples.why: A static balance metaphor misses the visceral arithmetic here — animated cash physically flowing in one column and hemorrhaging out a larger column turns an abstract ratio into a felt financial loss

repair_timeline_gantt.description: A horizontal Gantt-style bar broken into labeled phases — diagnosis, parts order, shipping, repair, QA — each phase filling in sequentially with a running total-days counter and a rental cost accumulator ticking alongside it
repair_timeline_gantt.schema_sketch: { phases: [{ label: string, duration_days: number, color: string }], daily_rental_cost: number, currency_symbol: string, show_cost_accumulator: boolean }

examples.narration: You wait for Tesla to ship them. Average wait time? Fourteen weeks.
examples.why: The narration is fundamentally about compounding waiting costs across distinct phases, not a single rising number — a phased timeline makes the insurance company's pain tangible in a way count_up cannot

sparse_density_map.description: A filled US map where authorized service locations appear as glowing dots — the vast empty geography between dots auto-shading darker to visualize coverage deserts, with a tow-radius ring around each dot
sparse_density_map.schema_sketch: { locations: [{ lat: number, lng: number, label: string }], coverage_radius_miles: number, empty_zone_color: string, dot_color: string, show_tow_radius: boolean, country_bounds: string }

examples.narration: There are forty-seven McLaren-authorized service centers in the entire United States
examples.why: map_callout highlights a single location; the actual story is the terrifying emptiness between 47 dots across a continent, which only a density-aware choropleth map communicates viscerally

depreciation_vs_payout_crossover.description: A dual-line animated chart plotting vehicle market value decay against outstanding loan balance over time, with a highlighted crossover zone where payout exceeds debt — the fraud incentive window — shaded in red
depreciation_vs_payout_crossover.schema_sketch: { vehicle_start_value: number, loan_start_balance: number, depreciation_curve: [{ month: number, value: number }], loan_balance_curve: [{ month: number, balance: number }], highlight_crossover: boolean, fraud_zone_color: string, x_axis_label: string }

examples.narration: owners total them on purpose because the insurance payout exceeds what they owe on financing
examples.why: The fraud motive is entirely a crossover-point story between two financial curves; no existing chart component visualizes the intersection of depreciation and loan balance that creates the incentive window

loss_ratio_threshold_gauge.description: A segmented radial gauge with color-coded threshold bands — green for healthy, yellow for nervous, red for blacklist territory — where an animated needle sweeps to a specific loss ratio value with threshold labels at 90% and 120%
loss_ratio_threshold_gauge.schema_sketch: { current_value: number, bands: [{ min: number, max: number, color: string, label: string }], needle_animate: boolean, show_threshold_labels: boolean, unit_suffix: string }

examples.narration: Claims paid out divided by premiums collected. Anything above ninety percent makes executives nervous.
examples.why: percent_fill shows a single fill level with no concept of danger thresholds; the narration explicitly defines multiple meaningful breakpoints that a banded gauge communicates in one glance

theft_rate_model_comparison_bar.description: A horizontal ranked bar chart comparing theft rates across BMW model lineup, with the M8 Competition bar animating last and overshooting all others, its bar rendered in alarm red with a theft-ring icon and absolute theft-per-10k-vehicles figure
theft_rate_model_comparison_bar.schema_sketch: { models: [{ name: string, theft_rate_per_10k: number, highlight: boolean }], bar_color_default: string, bar_color_highlight: string, animate_sequence: string, icon: string, source_label: string }

examples.narration: The M8 Competition has the highest theft rate of any BMW model
examples.why: A static image with a stat gives one data point in isolation; the claim that it has the HIGHEST rate among a lineup is inherently a comparative story that only lands when you see all the other bars dwarfed beside it

crowd_density_visualizer.description: An animated dot-grid where each dot represents a person, transitioning between two crowd states (e.g. 2500 dots collapsing to 30) with duration labels beneath each state
crowd_density_visualizer.schema_sketch: { crowd_a: { count: 2500, label: 'Traditional cruise', duration: '6 hours' }, crowd_b: { count: 30, label: 'Immersion itinerary', duration: '3 days' }, dot_color_a: string, dot_color_b: string, animation_style: 'collapse' | 'scatter' }

examples.narration: thirty passengers arrive and stay for three days instead of twenty-five hundred
examples.why: The sheer visual impact of 2,500 dots dissolving into 30 makes the scale contrast viscerally felt in a way no number comparison can match — this is the single most dramatic data point in the entire video

social_connection_map.description: A node-graph animation showing one central passenger node with connection lines attempting to reach a field of 4,000 faded nodes, with only 3 lines fully illuminating
social_connection_map.schema_sketch: { total_nodes: 4000, active_connections: 3, center_label: 'You', connected_label: 'Talked to', unconnected_opacity: 0.08, animation: 'pulse_active_nodes', layout: 'radial' }

examples.narration: average person talks to three strangers during their entire week-long cruise
examples.why: The isolation of '3 out of 4,000' is a social geometry story, not a photograph story — a network graph makes the loneliness of mass cruising instantly readable without a word of text

itinerary_map_compare.description: A split animated map showing two cruise routes simultaneously — left side traces rapid multi-stop pins across many countries, right side shows fewer pins with expanding radius circles indicating depth of stay
itinerary_map_compare.schema_sketch: { route_a: { label: 'Traditional', stops: [{ location, country, days: 1 }], line_style: 'dashed-fast' }, route_b: { label: 'Immersion', stops: [{ location, country, days: 3 }], depth_radius: number, line_style: 'solid-slow' }, map_region: string, split_mode: 'side-by-side' | 'overlay-toggle' }

examples.narration: seven countries in seven days versus three destinations three to four days each
examples.why: This is fundamentally a geographic and temporal argument that lives on a map; a before/after static frame cannot show the frantic breadth vs. rooted depth contrast the way animated route overlays on actual geography can

health_intake_dashboard.description: A stylized pre-boarding health profile card that animates in biometric data rings (sleep, genetics, fitness, nutrition) filling progressively, mimicking a real medical onboarding UI
health_intake_dashboard.schema_sketch: { metrics: [{ label: 'Blood work', icon: string, fill_percent: number, color: string }], card_style: 'medical' | 'premium', animation: 'sequential_fill', passenger_name_placeholder: boolean, subtitle: string }

examples.narration: blood work, genetic testing, fitness evaluations, sleep studies before you board
examples.why: A checkmark list makes this feel like a hotel amenity menu; an animated biometric dashboard makes it feel like the premium medical product it actually is, selling the credibility of wellness cruising visually

fleet_transition_timeline.description: An animated horizontal timeline showing ship silhouettes shrinking in size across years, with fleet count and average passenger capacity displayed as live-updating numbers below each year marker
fleet_transition_timeline.schema_sketch: { years: [{ year: number, avg_ship_capacity: number, fleet_size: number, silhouette_scale: number }], investment_label: '$20B', highlight_year: number, animation: 'scrub' | 'auto-play', color_scheme: 'brand' | 'neutral' }

examples.narration: Royal Caribbean twenty-billion-dollar decision selling off floating cities building floating neighborhoods
examples.why: A $20B strategic fleet pivot is a multi-year physical transformation story — silhouettes of ships visibly downsizing across a timeline communicates industrial scale shift in a way no single spotlight statistic can

before_after_biometric_chart.description: A dual-panel animated line chart showing the same passenger's biometric curves (weight, cardiovascular score, sleep quality) at embarkation vs. six months post-cruise, with delta callouts
before_after_biometric_chart.schema_sketch: { metrics: [{ name: string, unit: string, before_value: number, after_value: number, delta_label: string }], time_labels: { t0: 'Embarkation', t1: '6 months later' }, chart_style: 'line' | 'area', highlight_color: string, annotation: string }

examples.narration: improved cardiovascular health by measurable metrics better sleep patterns six months later
examples.why: Health outcomes are longitudinal data — they need a before/after chart with time on the x-axis to show permanence, not a static stat split, which is precisely what makes the 'not vacation weight loss' claim believable

cruise_route_animator.description: An animated dotted ship-path tracing port stops sequentially across a geographic map, with port name labels pulsing on arrival and a running day counter
cruise_route_animator.schema_sketch: { ports: [{ name, lat, lng, day_number, country }], ship_icon, path_color, stop_pulse_color, day_counter_visible: boolean, loop: boolean }

examples.narration: ships dock in twelve different countries across seventeen days
examples.why: map_highlight statically shades regions, but the narration is about movement and accumulation across a multi-stop journey — an animated sequential route makes the 'twelve countries, seventeen days' feel viscerally large and operationally complex, which is what makes it threatening

incident_timeline_room.description: A stylized overhead floor-plan of a single ship cabin with dated incident markers appearing one by one as pins, each pin expanding to a one-line fate label
incident_timeline_room.schema_sketch: { room_label, incidents: [{ date, victim_name_or_anon, fate_label, pin_color }], reveal_mode: 'sequential' | 'all_at_once', room_outline_style: 'blueprint' | 'realistic' }

examples.narration: six passengers assigned to that specific cabin have disappeared or died
examples.why: a number statistic alone abstracts the horror — anchoring each incident to the physical space of one room makes the pattern feel concrete and inescapable, which is the entire narrative point of the 'suicide room' section

passenger_drain_grid.description: A grid of uniform human-silhouette icons representing total disembarked passengers, with silhouettes animating to 'gone' state (fading to red outlines) one cluster at a time until thirty-one remain hollow
passenger_drain_grid.schema_sketch: { total_disembarked, missing_count, icon_style: 'silhouette' | 'person', missing_color, present_color, animation_speed_ms, grid_columns }

examples.narration: thirty-one passengers haven't returned by seven thirty PM departure
examples.why: big_number renders 31 as an abstraction, but a silhouette grid makes viewers feel the mass of individual people vanishing — it visualizes the proportion and the humanity simultaneously, which directly serves the 'they can't come back' reveal

budget_split_bar.description: A single horizontal bar that animates splitting into two labeled segments — settlements vs. safety spend — with dollar amounts counting up inside each segment as they grow
budget_split_bar.schema_sketch: { segments: [{ label, amount, color }], bar_height, currency_symbol, animation_duration_ms, show_percentage: boolean, emphasis_segment_index }

examples.narration: spend more money on legal settlements than they do on passenger safety
examples.why: scale_balance is iconographic and static; this narration is specifically about proportional budget allocation across two competing line items, which demands a quantified proportional visual that makes the imbalance legible at a glance rather than metaphorical

cctv_ghost_frame.description: A simulated CCTV monitor UI showing a timestamped corridor feed that cuts to static mid-playback, with a blinking 'SIGNAL LOST' overlay and a redacted incident log beneath
cctv_ghost_frame.schema_sketch: { camera_id, location_label, timestamp_start, timestamp_cutout, static_duration_ms, signal_lost_text, incident_log_lines: [{ time, entry, redacted: boolean }] }

examples.narration: keycards inactive, last known locations captured on malfunctioning security cameras
examples.why: evidence_board shows collected documents pinned together, but this moment is specifically about surveillance footage failing at a critical instant — a CCTV simulation that visually 'loses' the subject creates the exact dread of the cover-up in motion rather than its aftermath

passenger_profile_scanner.description: An animated passenger manifest row that scrolls through profile attributes — age, gender, travel-party size, payment method — with a targeting reticle locking onto rows that match a hidden filter criteria, flagging them red
passenger_profile_scanner.schema_sketch: { manifest_rows: [{ passenger_id, age, gender, party_size, payment_method, flagged: boolean }], filter_criteria_visible: boolean, reticle_color, flag_color, scan_speed_ms, redact_names: boolean }

examples.narration: passengers who fit specific profiles get marked before they even disembark
examples.why: evidence_board is static and document-like, but the narration describes an active selection process — a scanning/filtering animation makes the algorithmic, predatory nature of 'profiling before disembarkation' viscerally legible rather than merely alleged

crowd_fill_ratio.description: A silhouette crowd grid that fills proportionally to show what fraction of a total population fits a condition — like 200 of 3000 people highlighted
crowd_fill_ratio.schema_sketch: { total_count: number, highlighted_count: number, highlight_label: string, total_label: string, icon_shape: 'person' | 'dot', highlight_color: string, grid_columns: number }

examples.narration: The ship carries three thousand. Do the math.
examples.why: The gut-punch of 200 vs 3000 is visceral and spatial — you need to see the tiny lit cluster drowning in a sea of grey figures, which no existing ratio or stat component renders as a human-scale crowd

supply_chain_loop.description: An animated geographic loop map tracing a product's multi-stop journey with labeled waypoints, directional arrows, and irony-highlighting at the origin/end match
supply_chain_loop.schema_sketch: { stops: [{ location: string, lat: number, lng: number, action_label: string }], loop_closes: boolean, highlight_stop_index: number, line_color: string, animation_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: caught in Alaska, frozen, shipped to Seattle, processed, then shipped back
examples.why: The absurdity of salmon traveling Alaska→Seattle→Alaska is inherently a looping geographic animation — roadmap_journey is linear and non-geographic, so it cannot show the circular waste or actual map distance that makes this narration land

ship_scale_overlay.description: A top-down or cross-section diagram placing a to-scale ship silhouette inside a waterway outline, showing clearance margins with labeled widths for both vessel and passage
ship_scale_overlay.schema_sketch: { waterway_name: string, waterway_width_meters: number, ship_name: string, ship_beam_meters: number, clearance_label: string, view_mode: 'top_down' | 'cross_section', highlight_color: string }

examples.narration: Misty Fjords. Tracy Arm. Places where the water's so narrow you can reach out and touch
examples.why: The entire point is physical scale — how narrow the waterway is relative to the ship — which is a spatial comparison that a photograph cannot convey but a dimensioned diagram communicates immediately and memorably

port_time_bar_race.description: A horizontal bar chart where each row is a port, and color-coded bars extend to show hours docked per cruise line, animating in line by line for dramatic comparison
port_time_bar_race.schema_sketch: { ports: [{ name: string, lines: [{ cruise_line: string, hours_docked: number, includes_overnight: boolean, color: string }] }], max_hours_axis: number, animate_stagger_ms: number }

examples.narration: Ketchikan gets fourteen hours instead of eight. Juneau gets overnight stays.
examples.why: Shore time is the entire competitive differentiator being argued here — a table renders it as text, but an animated duration bar makes the extra hours feel tangible and lets viewers instantly see which line wins each port at a glance

dual_axis_rating.description: A split scorecard that rates two opposing dimensions simultaneously with independent scales, visual icons per axis, and a tension-gap indicator between the two scores
