dual_axis_rating.schema_sketch: { line_name: string, axes: [{ label: string, score: number, max: number, icon: string, color: string }], tension_label: string, show_gap_indicator: boolean }

examples.narration: Ten out of ten for service. Five out of ten for authenticity.
examples.why: Every cruise line in this video earns two separate verdicts on different axes — comfort vs wilderness, service vs authenticity — but review_stars collapses them into a single number, losing the exact tension that is the video's central thesis

glacier_recession_timeline.description: A side-scrolling timeline with a glacier silhouette that visually shrinks at labeled year markers, ending with a projected outline versus current outline overlaid
glacier_recession_timeline.schema_sketch: { glacier_name: string, waypoints: [{ year: number, volume_percent_remaining: number, label: string }], projection_year: number, current_year: number, recession_color: string, baseline_color: string }

examples.narration: The Alaska you can visit today won't exist in twenty years.
examples.why: This is the emotional climax and the only data-backed urgency claim in the entire video — rendering it as a text spotlight wastes the one moment that deserves a visceral animated visual showing actual disappearance over time

territory_size_comparison.description: Side-by-side silhouette map overlays scaling one region against another with labeled area figures beneath each shape
territory_size_comparison.schema_sketch: { primary_region: { label, silhouette_svg, area_acres }, comparison_regions: [{ label, silhouette_svg, area_acres }], highlight_color, annotation_text }

examples.narration: Alaska has more federal land than most countries have total territory
examples.why: A raw number like '600 million acres' is cognitively abstract, but seeing Alaska's federal land mass rendered as a silhouette dwarfing recognizable countries makes the scale viscerally obvious in a way a big_number cannot.

ownership_layer_map.description: An interactive-style map of Alaska that animates through stacked land-ownership layers — federal, state, Native corporation, private — each appearing as a distinct color wash with a legend
ownership_layer_map.schema_sketch: { base_map_region, layers: [{ label, color, opacity, area_label }], animation_sequence: 'stack' | 'reveal', legend_position }

examples.narration: land that looks public on maps might be owned by one of more than two hundred village corporations
examples.why: The entire point of this narration is that invisible ownership boundaries exist beneath a deceptively uniform landscape; a layered map visualization makes that invisible complexity literally visible in a way a full-screen AI image cannot.

temperature_range_bar.description: A vertical thermometer-style bar spanning the full recorded temperature range for a location, with labeled markers at key thresholds and a sliding highlight showing the human-survivable zone
temperature_range_bar.schema_sketch: { location_label, temp_min_f, temp_max_f, highlight_zones: [{ label, min, max, color }], unit: 'F' | 'C', animate_sweep: boolean }

examples.narration: summer temperatures can hit ninety degrees while winter drops to negative sixty
examples.why: The 150-degree swing is the dramatic hook, and a thermometer range bar communicates that extreme span as a single continuous visual object — the sheer distance between the two endpoints lands harder than two static numbers side by side.

road_network_density_map.description: Two scaled map outlines side by side — Alaska and Rhode Island — with their actual paved road networks drawn as white lines on dark fill, making the density contrast immediately legible
road_network_density_map.schema_sketch: { regions: [{ label, outline_svg, road_network_svg, area_sq_miles, paved_road_miles }], background_color, road_color, scale_to: 'equal_area' | 'equal_size' }

examples.narration: Alaska has more than three million square miles — fewer paved roads than Rhode Island
examples.why: This is a pure visual-density argument; rendering the actual sparseness of Alaska's road lines against Rhode Island's dense web communicates the absurdity of the comparison in one glance rather than requiring the viewer to process a text reveal.

cultural_dot_map.description: A map of Alaska with each of the 229 tribal nations represented as a color-coded dot or region polygon, grouped by language family, with a language-family legend and count badges
cultural_dot_map.schema_sketch: { map_region, tribes: [{ name, lat, lon, language_family, corporation_affiliation }], color_by: 'language_family' | 'corporation', show_legend: boolean, dot_size_px }

examples.narration: two hundred and twenty-nine federally recognized tribes speaking twenty different languages
examples.why: The narration is making a diversity-of-jurisdiction argument that is spatial at its core; showing 229 distinct entities distributed across the map makes the 'cultural minefield' metaphor concrete rather than leaving it as an abstract statistic.

budget_waterfall_chart.description: A horizontal waterfall/cascade bar chart that builds a total production cost by stacking labeled cost segments — permits, flights, gear shipping, accommodation — with a running total that climbs to a final figure
budget_waterfall_chart.schema_sketch: { currency, segments: [{ label, amount, color }], animate: boolean, show_running_total: boolean, highlight_segment: string }

examples.narration: eight hundred dollars per flight each way — transportation costs alone can hit five thousand dollars
examples.why: The narrative through scenes 66–67 is fundamentally about cumulative cost shock; a waterfall chart that animates each cost segment snapping into place and visibly growing the total drives that 'costs compound fast in Alaska' message far better than isolated price tags.

body_system_diagram.description: A semi-transparent human silhouette with animated overlays highlighting specific organs, vessels, or systems with labeled callouts
body_system_diagram.schema_sketch: { body_region: string, highlight_zones: [{ label: string, color: string, pulse_animation: boolean }], overlay_mode: 'skeletal' | 'organs' | 'vascular', caption: string }

examples.narration: No air bubbles. No nitrogen absorption. No decompression sickness.
examples.why: The narration is mechanically about what happens inside the lungs and blood during liquid breathing — a body diagram animating oxygen flow through lung tissue would make the physiology viscerally clear in a way a simple checkmark list cannot

depth_gauge_descent.description: A vertical animated depth ruler showing ocean zones with a diver marker descending in real time, labeled with depth milestones and danger thresholds
depth_gauge_descent.schema_sketch: { max_depth_ft: number, milestones: [{ depth_ft: number, label: string, event: string, danger_level: 'safe' | 'warning' | 'lethal' }], animated_marker_label: string, ocean_zone_bands: boolean }

examples.narration: He's about to descend to eight hundred feet.
examples.why: Scenes 33–39 are entirely structured around depth milestones — 100ft, 300ft, 600ft, 800ft — and a single animated descent ruler would unify all of them into a coherent visual journey that a static stat number completely fails to convey

neural_upload_viz.description: A split graphic showing a human brain on one side and a circuit/processor schematic on the other, with animated data-stream particles flowing between them
neural_upload_viz.schema_sketch: { source_label: string, destination_label: string, transfer_items: [{ name: string, icon: string }], particle_speed: 'slow' | 'fast', color_theme: string }

examples.narration: memories, her training, her instincts — all uploaded and enhanced with processing power
examples.why: The core concept here is the transfer of human consciousness into a machine substrate — a static stat block communicates nothing about that transformation, while an animated brain-to-chip transfer graphic makes the conceptual leap tangible

underwater_city_cross_section.description: An animated architectural cross-section cutaway of a multi-level underwater structure showing labeled zones at different depths, with ambient marine life and light rays
underwater_city_cross_section.schema_sketch: { city_name: string, depth_zones: [{ depth_ft: number, zone_label: string, purpose: string, color_band: string }], connectors: [{ from_zone: string, to_zone: string, type: 'tunnel' | 'shaft' }], ambient_elements: ['fish' | 'vents' | 'light_rays'] }

examples.narration: Massive transparent domes create neighborhoods connected by pressurized tunnels.
examples.why: Scenes 74–90 repeatedly reference layered depth zones, dome structures, and tunnels — a cross-section cutaway is the canonical visual language for explaining layered architecture and would serve five consecutive scenes that AI imagery cannot anchor precisely

global_build_status_map.description: A 3D-tilted globe or flat world map with animated location pins showing construction status, depth, and projected completion date per site
global_build_status_map.schema_sketch: { projection: 'globe' | 'mercator', sites: [{ name: string, lat: number, lon: number, status: 'planned' | 'under_construction' | 'operational', depth_ft: number, capacity: number, completion_year: number }], animate_pins: boolean }

examples.narration: Similar cities are under construction off the coasts of Australia, Chile, and Iceland.
examples.why: map_highlight is a static region shader — this scene needs multiple discrete point-locations with per-site metadata (status, depth, population target) conveying a global rollout, which is a fundamentally different spatial-data problem

volume_comparison_3d.description: Two side-by-side 3D volumetric blocks — land-habitable space vs. ocean volume — scaled accurately to each other with animated fill and labeled percentages
volume_comparison_3d.schema_sketch: { items: [{ label: string, volume_relative: number, color: string, icon: string }], animate_fill: boolean, display_mode: '3d_blocks' | 'spheres', highlight_winner: boolean }

examples.narration: The ocean contains ninety-nine percent of Earth's livable space.
examples.why: The 99% claim is about three-dimensional livable volume, not a simple proportion — a percent_fill bar flattens this into 2D and loses the entire conceptual punch; volumetric blocks make the ocean's spatial dominance immediately shocking

bucket_fill_diagram.description: Three side-by-side animated bucket or jar illustrations that visually fill with color as dollar amounts are assigned to each category
bucket_fill_diagram.schema_sketch: { buckets: [{ label: string, color: string, fill_percent: number, amount: number }], animation_sequence: 'simultaneous' | 'staggered' }

examples.narration: Fixed bills. Variable spending. Savings.
examples.why: The narration is literally introducing three money containers — an animated filling metaphor makes the categorization visceral and memorable in a way a text list never can

animated_budget_split.description: A single horizontal bar that animates left-to-right, carving itself into three labeled color segments with percentage callouts that pop in as each segment completes
animated_budget_split.schema_sketch: { segments: [{ label: string, percent: number, color: string, dollar_amount: number }], animation_style: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous', show_dollar_labels: boolean }

examples.narration: Fifty percent needs. Thirty percent wants. Twenty percent savings.
examples.why: A donut chart shows static proportions, but an animated sequential split communicates the rule as a decision process — need first, then want, then save — matching the instructional cadence of the narration

live_spreadsheet_build.description: A screen-recorded-style animated spreadsheet that types in rows, column headers, and values one step at a time in sync with narration cues
live_spreadsheet_build.schema_sketch: { rows: [{ row_number: number, label: string, value: string | number, style: 'header' | 'item' | 'total', reveal_delay_ms: number }], highlight_color: string, animate_typing: boolean }

examples.narration: Open Google Sheets and create a new document. Title it My Budget 2026.
examples.why: Scenes 51–64 walk through building a real spreadsheet row by row — google_docs is a static screenshot, but the entire instructional value of this section depends on watching the budget assemble itself live

income_scenario_slider.description: An interactive-looking slider UI showing a take-home income amount, with the three budget category bars below it dynamically rescaling as the income value changes across displayed scenarios
income_scenario_slider.schema_sketch: { scenarios: [{ income: number, fixed: number, variable: number, savings: number }], active_scenario_index: number, bar_colors: { fixed: string, variable: string, savings: string }, animate_transition: boolean }

examples.narration: Tight, but doable if you're intentional about where the money goes.
examples.why: Scenes 67–73 show multiple income levels to prove the system is universal — a slider visualization communicates scalability far more powerfully than a static side-by-side table

habit_streak_calendar.description: A six-month mini calendar grid where each month cell fills in or checks off progressively, with an accumulating streak counter, visualizing compounding consistency over time
habit_streak_calendar.schema_sketch: { months: [{ label: string, status: 'complete' | 'partial' | 'empty', savings_amount?: number }], streak_count: number, highlight_color: string, show_amounts: boolean }

examples.narration: Every month you stick to the system, even imperfectly, builds the muscle.
examples.why: Scenes 82–90 are entirely about the value of imperfect repetition over time — a streak calendar makes the compounding nature of monthly consistency visible in a way a single gauge reading cannot

paycheck_waterfall_chart.description: A waterfall-style bar that starts at gross income and shows sequential downward deduction blocks — taxes, health insurance, retirement — landing on the net take-home amount at the bottom
paycheck_waterfall_chart.schema_sketch: { gross_income: number, deductions: [{ label: string, amount: number, color: string }], net_income: number, animate_sequential: boolean, currency_symbol: string }

examples.narration: You make sixty thousand but take home around forty-two thousand after taxes.
examples.why: The narration's entire point is showing money disappearing through deductions layer by layer — a waterfall decomposition makes each deduction a visible subtraction, which before_after's binary framing completely misses

memory_decay_curve.description: An animated curve showing retention percentage dropping over time, with labeled drop-off points at key intervals (24h, 48h, 1 week)
memory_decay_curve.schema_sketch: { x_axis_label: string, y_axis_label: string, data_points: [{ time_label: string, retention_percent: number, highlight: boolean }], curve_color: string, annotation_labels: string[] }

examples.narration: after just forty-eight hours, people forget thirty-seven percent of their purchases
examples.why: The narration describes a time-based decay process with two specific data points — a curve over time communicates the forgetting slope far more viscerally than a static fill bar that only shows a single percentage

budget_overflow_bar.description: A horizontal budget bar showing the planned limit, a fill representing actual spending that visually bursts past the limit line with an overflow amount labeled in red
budget_overflow_bar.schema_sketch: { category_label: string, budget_amount: number, actual_amount: number, currency_symbol: string, overflow_color: string, limit_line_label: string }

examples.narration: you've already blown past your dining out category by a hundred and twelve dollars
examples.why: The narration's emotional punch is the overage — spending going beyond a boundary — which requires a single bar with a hard limit line and a visible overflow zone, something a before/after two-panel layout cannot show as a continuous transgression

time_projection_multiplier.description: An animated split display that shows a monthly figure on the left transforming into annual, 3-year, and 5-year totals on the right with a rolling ticker, optionally labeling each total with a real-world equivalent (e.g., 'a vacation')
time_projection_multiplier.schema_sketch: { base_amount: number, base_label: string, projections: [{ period_label: string, total: number, real_world_equivalent: string }], currency_symbol: string, animation_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: two hundred and thirty-seven dollars a month is two thousand eight hundred and forty-four dollars a year
examples.why: The narration's entire power is the compounding revelation across time horizons — a single rolling money counter only lands one number, while this component would animate the multiplication across multiple timeframes to make the scale feel progressively more shocking

subscription_audit_grid.description: A grid of app/service logo tiles that animate in one by one, with a running total cost ticker at the bottom and tiles color-coded between 'known' (e.g., white) and 'forgotten' (e.g., red)
subscription_audit_grid.schema_sketch: { services: [{ name: string, logo_url: string, monthly_cost: number, was_forgotten: boolean }], total_label: string, known_color: string, forgotten_color: string, animate_in_sequence: boolean }

examples.narration: Marcus found out he was paying for six different streaming services. Six. He thought he was paying for two.
examples.why: The visual shock of six logos appearing when you expected two — especially with forgotten ones highlighted differently — is a far stronger concrete reveal than a stat beside an image, and directly mirrors the discovery experience being described

budget_runway_meter.description: A dual-axis visual showing remaining budget dollars on one axis and remaining days in the month on the other, with a diagonal 'safe zone' band and a plotted current position dot indicating whether spending pace is sustainable
budget_runway_meter.schema_sketch: { budget_remaining: number, days_remaining: number, total_budget: number, total_days: number, safe_zone_color: string, danger_zone_color: string, current_dot_label: string, daily_burn_rate: number }

examples.narration: you've got a hundred dollars left, and there are still ten days in the month
examples.why: The narration explicitly juxtaposes two dimensions — money remaining vs. time remaining — which is a spatial tension that no single-axis meter or alert notification can express; this component would make the collision between those two countdowns immediately readable

receipt_category_breakdown.description: A stylized receipt visual where line items are color-coded by category, with a live-tallying footer showing each category subtotal as items animate in, making misclassification visually obvious
receipt_category_breakdown.schema_sketch: { merchant_name: string, items: [{ description: string, amount: number, category: string, category_color: string }], category_totals: [{ category: string, total: number, percent_of_receipt: number }], animate_sequential: boolean }

examples.narration: sixty percent of what she bought at Target wasn't food
examples.why: The narration is specifically about hidden category mixing inside a single merchant transaction — a receipt metaphor with color-coded line items makes the 'groceries vs. not groceries' split tangible in a way a percent fill bar completely loses

monthly_fill_accumulator.description: A jar or container that visually fills month-by-month as fixed contributions stack toward a labeled target amount, with each month's deposit animating in as a distinct colored layer
monthly_fill_accumulator.schema_sketch: { monthly_contribution: number, target_amount: number, months: number, container_label: string, currency_symbol: string, fill_color: string, target_label: string }

examples.narration: put fifty-four dollars aside every month — by December, six hundred and fifty dollars waiting
examples.why: A line chart implies volatility and trend — this narration is about predictable, mechanical accumulation toward a fixed goal, which a filling vessel communicates instantly and emotionally without axes or gridlines

calendar_expense_heatmap.description: A six-month calendar grid where each month cell is color-coded by total irregular expense load — cool for light months, hot/red for heavy months — with dollar totals displayed inside each cell
calendar_expense_heatmap.schema_sketch: { months: [{ label: string, total_irregular: number, expenses: [{ name: string, amount: number }] }], low_color: string, high_color: string, currency_symbol: string }

examples.narration: months that look totally normal and months that look terrifying
examples.why: The narration is explicitly about scanning across a six-month horizon to spot dangerous months at a glance; a heatmap calendar makes that danger visible spatially in a way no split-image or stat card can replicate

reverse_countdown_budget.description: A goal amount at the top with an animated backward-calculation cascade showing deadline → months remaining → required monthly amount → required weekly amount, each value revealing in sequence
reverse_countdown_budget.schema_sketch: { goal_amount: number, deadline_label: string, months_remaining: number, monthly_required: number, weekly_required: number, currency_symbol: string, accent_color: string }

examples.narration: five thousand dollars by December thirty-first — four hundred and seventeen dollars monthly starting now
examples.why: The entire point of this segment is the backwards calculation logic — the cognitive 'working backwards' move — and no existing component visualizes a multi-step reverse derivation; a static stat split only shows the answer, not the method that makes the strategy credible

savings_threshold_scatter.description: A two-cluster dot plot showing households plotted by savings level on the X-axis against percent of income lost to fees and emergencies on the Y-axis, with a visible cliff-edge threshold line at the five-thousand-dollar savings mark
savings_threshold_scatter.schema_sketch: { x_label: string, y_label: string, threshold_value: number, threshold_label: string, clusters: [{ label: string, savings_range: string, avg_pct_lost: number, dot_color: string, dot_count: number }], currency_symbol: string }

examples.narration: less than one thousand saved spends eight percent — more than five thousand saved spends less than two percent
examples.why: The narration is making a threshold argument — that crossing a specific savings level produces a dramatic, non-linear drop in financial friction costs — which a scatter with a threshold line communicates as a structural insight rather than just two numbers side by side

compound_milestone_ladder.description: A vertical ladder or staircase where each rung represents a year, with the rung height scaling non-linearly to reflect compounding momentum — early rungs close together, later rungs visibly further apart — and each step labeled with its milestone and a net-worth delta indicator
compound_milestone_ladder.schema_sketch: { steps: [{ year: number, milestone_label: string, delta_label: string, rung_weight: number }], accent_color: string, show_compound_curve: boolean, currency_symbol: string }

examples.narration: Year one stop overdrafting. Year two real emergency fund. Year three investing. Year five making money on money
examples.why: A standard timeline treats all years as equal intervals, but the narration's power is the compounding acceleration — later years are qualitatively larger leaps — and a ladder with non-linear spacing visually encodes that the payoff is back-weighted, which is the emotional core of the argument

labeled_money_buckets.description: A row of visual containers (buckets or envelopes) each with a label and fill level, showing how the same total balance looks either as one unlabeled pool versus multiple purpose-assigned buckets that cover known expenses
labeled_money_buckets.schema_sketch: { total_balance: number, currency_symbol: string, views: [{ view_label: string, buckets: [{ label: string, amount: number, color: string, is_deficit: boolean }] }] }

examples.narration: three thousand in checking but none labeled for car repairs — feels like you can't afford it
examples.why: The narration is making a purely visual argument about money organization — same dollars, different psychological reality depending on whether they are partitioned — and no existing component shows parallel allocation states of an identical balance

cycle_phase_wheel.description: An animated circular dial divided into four named arc segments that highlights the current active phase and rotates to show progression through a repeating cycle
cycle_phase_wheel.schema_sketch: { phases: [{ label, duration_months, color }], current_phase_index: number, cycle_count: number, animation: 'clockwise_sweep' }

examples.narration: Accumulation phase. Markup phase. Distribution phase. Markdown phase. Repeat.
examples.why: The narration is explicitly about a repeating circular structure — a linear roadmap metaphor fights the geometry of the idea, while a rotating wheel makes the 'repeat' nature viscerally obvious

investment_growth_replay.description: A side-by-side animated panel showing a starting dollar amount on the left inflating in real time into a final value on the right, anchored to a dated price chart underneath both figures
investment_growth_replay.schema_sketch: { asset: string, start_date: string, end_date: string, start_value_usd: number, end_value_usd: number, price_series: [{ date, price }], animation_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: ten thousand dollars worth of Solana would've had four point two million dollars
examples.why: A simple counter ticking up loses the causal story — pairing the growing dollar figure directly above the actual Solana price curve for that 12-month window makes the gain feel earned rather than abstract

transfer_race_animation.description: A dual-lane animated race graphic where two labeled transaction bars travel from a source city to a destination city at visually proportional speeds, with cost and time stamps appearing on arrival
transfer_race_animation.schema_sketch: { route: { from: string, to: string }, options: [{ label, transfer_time_seconds, cost_usd, color }], animation: 'simultaneous_race' }

examples.narration: Bank of America can send ten million dollars to Tokyo in three seconds for twelve cents
examples.why: The comparison_table presents this as static rows, but the entire emotional punch of the XRP argument is kinetic — one transfer is instantaneous and cheap, the other is glacially slow and expensive, which only lands if the viewer watches the contrast happen

allocation_builder.description: An interactive-style animated allocation bar that fills segment by segment as each asset is named, with risk-level color coding and a live percentage label snapping into place per segment
allocation_builder.schema_sketch: { segments: [{ asset_name, allocation_percent, risk_level: 'low'|'medium'|'high'|'speculative', color }], reveal_mode: 'sequential', show_risk_label: boolean }

examples.narration: Fifty percent of your crypto allocation goes to Bitcoin and Ethereum
examples.why: A static donut renders all allocations simultaneously, collapsing the narrative build across scenes 98–107 into a single glance; an allocation bar that constructs itself one asset at a time as the voiceover names each coin holds attention across the full sequence

cycle_countdown_bar.description: A horizontal progress bar representing the full 48-month cycle with a scrubber marker at the current month, elapsed portion filled, remaining portion color-coded by phase, and a ticking months-remaining counter in the corner
cycle_countdown_bar.schema_sketch: { total_months: number, current_month: number, phases: [{ label, start_month, end_month, color }], show_countdown: boolean, urgency_threshold_months: number }

examples.narration: we're in month thirty-seven of the current cycle. Thirteen months left before the next markdown phase
examples.why: A gauge meter implies a pressure or intensity reading, but the narration is about elapsed vs. remaining time within a fixed-length window — a segmented timeline bar with a live countdown communicates both position and urgency simultaneously

phase_wave_timeline.description: A dual-layer animated timeline showing two actor groups moving through the same phases at offset intervals, with a visible lag gap between them
phase_wave_timeline.schema_sketch: { phases: [{ label, duration_weeks }], actors: [{ name, color, start_phase_offset }], gap_highlight_label, x_axis_unit }

examples.narration: spend six to eighteen months building positions, then retail investors notice
examples.why: The core visual message is the temporal gap between institutional and retail entry — a standard roadmap shows sequence but cannot show two parallel actors moving through identical phases with a deliberate delay, which is the entire point of the narration

market_share_particle_field.description: A field of animated dots where each dot represents a unit of trading volume, dynamically clustering into two color-coded groups to show proportional control
market_share_particle_field.schema_sketch: { total_particles: number, segments: [{ label, percent, color }], animation_style: 'cluster' | 'sweep', duration_ms }

examples.narration: Institutions control roughly eighty-seven percent of all trading volume
examples.why: An 87/13 split needs visceral scale to land emotionally — a percent_fill bar is abstract, but watching hundreds of particles magnetically pull toward the institutional cluster makes the dominance feel overwhelming and real in a way a single fill bar cannot

divergence_chart.description: A split animated chart where two opposing trend lines move in real time in opposite vertical directions from a shared center axis, with magnitude labels on each arm
divergence_chart.schema_sketch: { event_label, axis_zero_label, series: [{ name, direction: 'up' | 'down', magnitude_percent, color }], time_period }

examples.narration: institutional buying increased by two hundred and thirty percent while retail selling hit record highs
examples.why: The narration describes simultaneous opposite behaviors — one group aggressively buying while the other panic-sells — which is a divergence story that needs two lines moving away from each other, not a side-by-side static stat card

org_flow_allocator.description: An animated org-chart style visual showing a single entity at the top distributing flows downward into labeled destination buckets with proportional flow-width pipes
org_flow_allocator.schema_sketch: { source: { name, logo_slug }, destinations: [{ label, allocation_percent, color }], animation: 'flow' | 'reveal', currency_unit }

examples.narration: buying it for their private wealth clients, their institutional accounts, and their own balance sheet
examples.why: The narration reveals a hidden multi-channel accumulation structure — one buyer, three distinct destinations — which is an allocation flow diagram, not a bullet list; the visual hierarchy and pipe widths would communicate scale and strategy that three_points completely flattens

volatility_band_chart.description: A price chart overlaid with a dynamically shrinking shaded band representing volatility range, showing the envelope visibly compressing over time as a key second data layer
volatility_band_chart.schema_sketch: { asset_name, data_points: [{ date, price, volatility_band_width }], milestone_marker: { date, label }, band_color, reduction_callout_percent }

examples.narration: Bitcoin's volatility has dropped forty-three percent since institutional ETFs launched
examples.why: A plain line chart shows price but cannot show volatility compression — the entire argument depends on visualizing the narrowing range around price movement, which requires a dedicated band/envelope layer that animated_line_chart does not support

wallet_pulse_heatmap.description: A calendar-grid heatmap of on-chain wallet activity where each cell represents one day, color intensity encodes transaction volume from large wallets, and clusters of intensity are circled and labeled
wallet_pulse_heatmap.schema_sketch: { asset, weeks_shown, cells: [{ date, large_wallet_volume, intensity_score }], threshold_label, highlight_clusters: [{ start_date, end_date, annotation }] }

examples.narration: consistent buying pressure from large wallets over multiple weeks, that's institutional accumulation
examples.why: The narration is literally teaching the viewer to read accumulation patterns over time from on-chain data — this is a temporal pattern-recognition visual that cannot be conveyed by a static AI image, and no existing chart type in the library handles the calendar-grid heat encoding needed

tax_layer_stack.description: A stacked bar or layered block diagram where each tax type visually consumes a slice of a single income dollar, with labels and percentages animating in layer by layer
tax_layer_stack.schema_sketch: { total_income: number, layers: [{ label: string, percent: number, color: string }], final_total_percent: number, animation_style: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous' }

examples.narration: average American pays 42% of their income in taxes
examples.why: The narration's entire power comes from the accumulation of hidden tax layers — a single big number flattens that discovery arc, while a layered visual builds the dread incrementally as each tax type lands on top of the last

compound_growth_split_path.description: A single starting dollar amount that visually forks into two animated growth curves — one exponential (invested) and one flat (savings account) — with a dramatic gap that widens over a timeline marked in decades
compound_growth_split_path.schema_sketch: { start_amount: number, start_age: number, end_age: number, path_a: { label: string, annual_rate: number, color: string }, path_b: { label: string, annual_rate: number, color: string }, milestone_ages: number[] }

examples.narration: invested that money and earned 10% annually, worth $155,000 by 65
examples.why: The narration is explicitly comparing two diverging futures from one identical starting point; a standard line chart lacks the visual drama of the fork moment and the yawning gap that makes the opportunity cost feel visceral

tax_rate_face_off.description: A side-by-side portrait comparison panel — one labeled 'You' and one labeled a named public figure — each with a fill-meter animating up to their respective effective tax rate, making the disparity feel personal and immediate
tax_rate_face_off.schema_sketch: { entity_a: { label: string, avatar_type: 'generic_silhouette' | 'icon', effective_rate: number, color: string }, entity_b: { label: string, avatar_type: 'generic_silhouette' | 'icon', effective_rate: number, color: string }, annotation: string }

examples.narration: Warren Buffett pays effective tax rate of 17.4%, less than half what you pay
examples.why: The stat comparison component shows numbers side by side but lacks the identity contrast the narration is weaponizing; the face-off format makes the viewer feel the injustice against themselves rather than between two abstract percentages

fee_erosion_waterfall.description: A waterfall chart showing a projected portfolio balance being stepped down by recurring fee extractions year by year, with the cumulative amount lost to fees rendered as a filled red zone beneath the 'what you kept' bar
fee_erosion_waterfall.schema_sketch: { starting_balance: number, annual_return_gross: number, annual_fee_percent: number, years: number, bar_color_kept: string, bar_color_lost: string, final_loss_label: string }

examples.narration: average managed mutual fund charges 1.3% annually, reduces your lifetime returns
examples.why: The narration is making a compounding-loss argument — fees aren't a one-time cut but a continuous drain that compounds in reverse — and a static before/after hides that temporal erosion, while a waterfall makes the bleeding visible across time

lifetime_wealth_drain_clock.description: A real-time animated counter that ticks upward showing 'wealth lost so far today' based on a lifetime loss figure divided into a per-second rate, with a secondary display showing the cumulative loss since age 25
lifetime_wealth_drain_clock.schema_sketch: { lifetime_loss: number, working_years: number, per_second_rate: number, viewer_assumed_age: number, display_modes: ['per_second', 'cumulative_from_age'], accent_color: string }

examples.narration: average person loses two to three million dollars in potential wealth over their lifetime
examples.why: A money_counter shows a static or simple count-up to a number, but the narration's emotional hook is that the loss is happening right now, constantly, in real time — a clock framing converts an abstract lifetime stat into an immediate, personal urgency

tax_code_reward_matrix.description: A 2x2 grid visual where axes are 'income source' (earned vs. capital) and 'tax treatment' (punished vs. rewarded), with icons populating each quadrant and color-coded penalty/reward indicators
tax_code_reward_matrix.schema_sketch: { quadrants: [{ x_label: string, y_label: string, examples: string[], tax_rate_range: string, sentiment: 'punished' | 'rewarded', icon: string, color: string }], axis_x_label: string, axis_y_label: string }

examples.narration: trading time for money like employee you get punished, building assets like owner you get rewarded
examples.why: The narration is describing a structural rule — a coded system with two distinct behavioral categories and two distinct outcomes — which is a matrix logic that a tilting balance scale cannot convey; the matrix makes the system's design feel deliberate and learnable

cascade_degradation_chart.description: A three-row visual cascade where each row shows a labeled input dissolving into a progressively blurrier or smaller output, illustrating compounding vagueness
cascade_degradation_chart.schema_sketch: { rows: [{ label: string, clarity_level: 0-100, output_label: string }], animation: 'sequential_fade', arrow_style: 'degrading_dotted' }

examples.narration: Vague desire creates vague effort. Vague effort creates vague results.
examples.why: The narration is explicitly about a chain reaction of diminishing specificity — a static bullet list cannot show the compounding nature of that degradation the way a visual cascade can.

dual_velocity_timeline.description: Two parallel horizontal timelines side by side — one showing a fast decision point followed by a long stable hold, the other showing a slow crawling decision followed by rapid reversals — with labeled inflection markers
dual_velocity_timeline.schema_sketch: { tracks: [{ label: string, decision_speed_ms: number, reversal_count: number, color: string, events: [{ t: number, type: 'decide'|'reverse', label: string }] }] }

examples.narration: The wealthy made decisions fast and changed them slowly. Everyone else made decisions slowly and changed them fast.
examples.why: This is a speed-and-pattern contrast that lives in time, not in static values — two timelines with distinct velocity signatures would make the behavioral gap viscerally obvious where compare_reveal only handles a before/after snapshot.

progress_interruption_bar.description: A horizontal savings/progress bar filling over a timeline with two visible break points — each marked with a cause label and a restart marker — ending at goal completion
progress_interruption_bar.schema_sketch: { goal_label: string, total_duration_months: number, segments: [{ start: number, end: number, status: 'active'|'quit'|'restart' }], break_labels: [{ month: number, reason: string }], goal_reached_month: number }

examples.narration: The steel worker saving for his machine shop quit twice.
examples.why: The story is structurally about sustained progress punctuated by two specific collapses and restarts; a progress bar with labeled interruptions makes the emotional arc of persistence legible at a glance in a way no stat or image can.

time_allocation_donut_split.description: Two side-by-side donut charts — one labeled 'Most People' showing dominant slices for thinking/planning/researching with a tiny action slice, one labeled 'Hill's Wealthy' showing the inverse — with animated slice reveals
time_allocation_donut_split.schema_sketch: { panels: [{ label: string, slices: [{ activity: string, percent: number, color: string }] }], animation: 'sequential_arc_draw', highlight_slice: string }

examples.narration: Most people love the thinking part. They'll spend hours visualizing success. Days planning. Weeks researching.
examples.why: The narration is explicitly about proportional time allocation, which is the native language of a donut chart; a fullscreen image leaves the quantitative contrast invisible and forces the viewer to infer what the visual could directly show.

chasm_gap_diagram.description: A split-platform diagram showing two cliffs labeled 'KNOWING' and 'DOING' with a visible gap between them, an animated figure standing at the edge of the first cliff, and a bridge rendered as a dotted outline labeled 'Application'
chasm_gap_diagram.schema_sketch: { left_label: string, right_label: string, gap_label: string, bridge_label: string, bridge_state: 'missing'|'partial'|'complete', figure_position: 'left_edge'|'mid_bridge'|'right_edge', color_scheme: string }

examples.narration: The problem isn't access to information. It's the gap between knowing and doing.
examples.why: The word 'gap' is doing heavy lifting in this narration — the behavioral chasm between knowing and doing is the entire thesis of the video, and a literal visual chasm with a missing bridge externalizes the abstract concept far more powerfully than a quote card.

convergence_trait_map.description: Multiple labeled nodes representing different wealthy individuals radiating inward with animated arrows converging on a single central trait node, visualizing 'one thing in common' across diverse people
convergence_trait_map.schema_sketch: { central_trait: string, nodes: [{ name: string, descriptor: string, icon_style: 'portrait'|'initials' }], animation: 'staggered_arrow_draw', color_scheme: string, center_emphasis: 'pulse'|'glow' }

examples.narration: The wealthy people Hill studied had one thing in common.
examples.why: The narration is structurally a convergence story — many different people, one shared behavior — and a radial convergence diagram makes that pattern legible instantly, whereas a vintage film image simply illustrates the era rather than the analytical finding.

orbital_diagram.description: An animated top-down diagram of two or more bodies tracing elliptical orbits around a shared barycenter, with distance markers and period labels
orbital_diagram.schema_sketch: { bodies: [{ name, size_relative, color }], orbit_type: 'elliptical' | 'circular', period_years, min_distance: { value, unit, reference_body }, max_distance: { value, unit, reference_body }, animate: boolean }

examples.narration: orbit each other every eighty years, sometimes coming as close as Saturn
examples.why: The narration is fundamentally about orbital geometry and the range of separation between two stars — a spatial, animated diagram conveys the eccentric ellipse and distance extremes far more precisely than a static image with a stat overlay

travel_time_comparison_bar.description: A horizontal bar chart where each bar represents a propulsion method, scaled to show journey duration from years to millennia, with a glowing highlight on the shortest option
travel_time_comparison_bar.schema_sketch: { entries: [{ label, duration_years, category: 'current' | 'near_future' | 'theoretical', highlight: boolean }], scale: 'linear' | 'log', unit_display: 'years' | 'auto' }

examples.narration: cut the journey time to Alpha Centauri down to centuries instead of millennia
examples.why: The narration is explicitly comparing multiple propulsion technologies by travel time across orders of magnitude — a logarithmic duration bar gives that compression visceral impact in a way a bullet list completely fails to

speed_of_light_gauge.description: A radial or linear gauge that fills from 0% to a marked percentage of the speed of light, with reference tick marks for notable human-made speeds like Voyager and New Horizons
speed_of_light_gauge.schema_sketch: { target_percent_c: number, reference_marks: [{ label, percent_c }], animate_fill: boolean, display_mph: boolean, color_ramp: [string] }

examples.narration: accelerate these micro-probes to twenty percent the speed of light
examples.why: Twenty percent of light speed is meaningless without context — a gauge anchored to real reference speeds (Voyager at 0.006%, New Horizons at 0.005%) transforms an abstract number into a viscerally comprehensible leap

dual_planet_sightline.description: A split-space visual showing two planets facing each other across a starfield with a faint dotted sightline beam connecting them, and a light-travel-time label on the beam
dual_planet_sightline.schema_sketch: { planet_a: { name, appearance_hint }, planet_b: { name, appearance_hint }, distance_light_years: number, beam_label: string, animate_pulse: boolean }

examples.narration: right now, something on Proxima b is looking back at us
examples.why: The narration's entire emotional payload is the mutual, symmetrical act of two civilizations looking at each other across an impossible gap — a sightline diagram with a labeled delay makes that reciprocal longing concrete rather than abstract

cosmic_abundance_distance_matrix.description: A two-axis scatter plot where X is distance from Earth and Y is estimated habitability likelihood, with named exoplanets plotted as labeled dots and a shaded unreachable zone
cosmic_abundance_distance_matrix.schema_sketch: { planets: [{ name, distance_ly, habitability_score, highlight: boolean }], unreachable_threshold_ly: number, axes: { x_label, y_label }, shaded_zone_label: string }

examples.narration: how common life might be, and how utterly impossible it is to reach it
examples.why: The scene crystallizes the video's central tension — proximity of hope versus impossibility of contact — and a scatter plot with an explicit unreachable zone boundary makes that structural irony visually undeniable rather than philosophical

mission_window_timeline.description: A horizontal mission arc timeline showing launch, transit years, arrival window, and data-return delay as distinct labeled phases, with a tiny highlighted sliver representing the active window
mission_window_timeline.schema_sketch: { phases: [{ label, duration_value, duration_unit, color, highlight: boolean }], total_mission_years: number, show_proportional: boolean, active_window_label: string }

examples.narration: four hours to snap photos and beam data back to Earth before disappearing
examples.why: The emotional gut-punch is the grotesque ratio of a 20-year journey to a 4-hour window followed by a 4-year return signal delay — a proportional timeline makes that absurdity visible in a single glance where a stat overlay cannot

civilization_spread_map.description: A top-down galaxy or grid map where a glowing origin point pulses outward in concentric waves, each ring labeled with a time epoch (1M yrs, 10M yrs, etc.), visually filling the space
civilization_spread_map.schema_sketch: { origin_label: string, wave_intervals: [{ years: number, label: string, color: string }], galaxy_background: boolean, animation_duration_seconds: number }

examples.narration: expanding civilization should fill the entire galaxy over time
examples.why: The narration is explicitly spatial — a civilization radiating outward across star systems — which a line chart cannot convey; a radial expansion map makes the inevitability of galactic colonization viscerally obvious

multi_axis_radar.description: A radar/spider chart with 5–7 labeled axes (Technology, Social Cohesion, Emotional Maturity, Environmental Stewardship, etc.) showing humanity's current profile as a partially-filled polygon against a 'threshold' outline
multi_axis_radar.schema_sketch: { axes: [{ label: string, current_value: number, threshold_value: number }], current_label: string, threshold_label: string, color_current: string, color_threshold: string }

examples.narration: Not just technological, but social. Emotional. Spiritual.
examples.why: The narration explicitly frames civilizational readiness as a multi-dimensional scorecard, not a list — a radar chart visualizes the gap between where humanity is and where the threshold lies across all dimensions simultaneously

sensor_glitch_feed.description: A simulated radar or sensor-screen display showing a blip or anomalous contact appearing briefly, with scan lines, signal-strength bars, and a classified-style timestamp overlay — then fading out
sensor_glitch_feed.schema_sketch: { screen_type: 'radar' | 'spectrograph' | 'thermal', blip_label: string, coordinates: { x: number, y: number }, signal_strength: number, timestamp: string, fade_after_seconds: number }

examples.narration: A probe might malfunction and become visible. A monitoring device might be detected.
examples.why: The narration describes instruments detecting something fleeting and anomalous; a radar/sensor aesthetic communicates the accidental detection narrative far more viscerally than a static evidence board

global_broadcast_cascade.description: A world map where simultaneous transmission rings emanate from a single orbital point, with small device icons (TV, radio, phone, monitor) lighting up across continents in real time as the signal reaches them
global_broadcast_cascade.schema_sketch: { origin: 'orbit', targets: [{ region: string, device_icons: string[], delay_ms: number }], wave_color: string, map_style: 'dark' | 'satellite', animation_duration_seconds: number }

examples.narration: a message broadcast simultaneously to every radio, television, and internet connection
examples.why: The narration's power is in the word 'simultaneously' — a cascade animation across a globe showing every medium lighting up at once captures the overwhelming, inescapable nature of the contact moment in a way a notification stack cannot

observation_dome_diagram.description: A cutaway schematic diagram showing Earth at center inside a transparent containment sphere, with observer nodes positioned around the perimeter at various distances, connected by dotted sight-lines to the planet
observation_dome_diagram.schema_sketch: { subject_label: string, subject_icon: 'earth' | 'custom', containment_layers: [{ radius: number, label: string, opacity: number }], observer_nodes: [{ angle_degrees: number, label: string, sight_line: boolean }], style: 'blueprint' | 'clean' }

examples.narration: Earth sits in the middle of a cosmic zoo where exhibits don't know they're being watched
examples.why: The Zoo Hypothesis is fundamentally a structural/spatial concept — something is enclosing and observing something else — and a schematic diagram externalizes that containment geometry in a way no photographic or text component can

species_dossier_card.description: A classified-file-style data card displaying a species profile with redacted fields, behavioral metrics shown as small bar indicators, observation duration counter, and a case-file number — styled as alien bureaucratic documentation
species_dossier_card.schema_sketch: { case_id: string, subject_name: string, observation_start: string, metrics: [{ label: string, value: number, max: number }], redacted_fields: string[], classification_level: string, status: 'active' | 'pending' | 'concluded' }

examples.narration: They've studied our psychology, our social structures, our capacity for change
examples.why: The narration frames humanity as a documented research subject with accumulated behavioral data; a dossier card concretizes the chilling idea that we are already categorized and filed by an external intelligence

orbital_distance_scale.description: An interactive scale visualization showing two objects in space with a dynamic distance readout between them, animated to illustrate how debris dispersal increases exponentially over distance
orbital_distance_scale.schema_sketch: { object_a_label: string, object_b_label: string, distance_value: number, distance_unit: string, dispersion_rate_km_per_hour: number, formation_broken_threshold: number, animate_divergence: boolean }

examples.narration: Debris from their own rocket couldn't maintain formation at that range
examples.why: The narration is making a precise spatial-physics argument about why debris cannot maintain formation at millions of miles — a scaled distance diagram with labeled separation vectors would make that argument visually self-evident rather than leaving it abstract

radiation_exposure_timeline.description: A mission-duration timeline bar with a radiation dose accumulation overlay that pulses brighter at each flash-event marker, showing the compounding neurological exposure over days in deep space
radiation_exposure_timeline.schema_sketch: { mission_name: string, duration_days: number, flash_events: [{ day: number, intensity: number, description: string }], exposure_unit: string, danger_threshold: number, body_region_highlight: string }

examples.narration: The longer humans spend in deep space, the more these flashes intensify
examples.why: animated_line_chart renders generic x/y data; this narration needs a human-body-in-time visualization where the dose accumulates against a silhouette of a mission arc, making the biological threat visceral rather than statistical

orbital_track_comparison.description: A side-by-side overhead orbital path diagram showing two trajectory lines — the station's and the unknown object's — with timestamps marking where the paths stay parallel versus where debris would have diverged
orbital_track_comparison.schema_sketch: { track_a: { label: string, color: string, path_points: [{ time_s: number, altitude_km: number }] }, track_b: { label: string, color: string, path_points: [{ time_s: number, altitude_km: number }] }, ghost_track: { label: string, diverge_at_second: number }, duration_seconds: number, anomaly_window: { start: number, end: number } }

examples.narration: Keeping pace with the station. For seven minutes and thirty-three seconds
examples.why: The entire evidentiary weight of this scene rests on the claim that the object's path was anomalously stable; a dual-trajectory diagram with a 'where debris should have been' ghost line makes the physics argument visually undeniable rather than asking the viewer to take it on faith

mission_log_artifact.description: A aged paper mission-log page rendered with handwritten grid coordinates, a date stamp, and a central area where a geometric sketch animates into existence stroke by stroke, as if being drawn in real time
mission_log_artifact.schema_sketch: { mission_id: string, date_stamp: string, astronaut_initials: string, sketch_svg_path: string, annotation_text: string, draw_duration_ms: number, paper_texture: 'aged' | 'clean', stamp_label: string }

examples.narration: Some reported seeing geometric patterns so complex they sketched them in their mission logs
examples.why: handwritten_note exists but is generic; this narration needs a specifically space-mission-document aesthetic — graph-paper grid, mission date header, and the sketch appearing progressively — to sell the authenticity of astronauts documenting impossible visions

station_exterior_silhouette.description: A schematic side-elevation diagram of a space station hull with a small human silhouette figure positioned on the exterior surface, no suit indicator, with a pulsing 'vacuum pressure' environmental readout showing conditions incompatible with survival
station_exterior_silhouette.schema_sketch: { station_model: string, figure_position: { x_percent: number, y_percent: number }, figure_label: string, environmental_readouts: [{ metric: string, value: string, status: 'lethal' | 'warning' | 'normal' }], highlight_module: string, animate_figure_disappear: boolean, disappear_after_ms: number }

examples.narration: A figure. Humanoid. Walking on the outside of the station
examples.why: An AI image will render this as a dramatic visual scene, but what makes the moment horrifying is the physics impossibility — a clinical schematic showing exactly WHERE on the hull the figure appeared alongside lethal environmental data creates dread through information rather than through imagery

humanity_dot_grid.description: A grid of six hundred small human-figure icons representing every person ever sent to space, with the grid dwarfed beside a scale representation of Earth's total population, animating in one dot at a time before zooming out to show the overwhelming ratio
humanity_dot_grid.schema_sketch: { space_travelers_count: number, total_population_reference: number, icon_shape: 'human' | 'dot', build_animation: boolean, build_duration_ms: number, zoom_out_to_population: boolean, highlight_color: string, background_color: string, label_space: string, label_population: string }

examples.narration: We've sent maybe six hundred people beyond Earth's atmosphere in the entire history
examples.why: spotlight_stat shows a number; this narration is making a profound argument about how vanishingly small our observational sample is — a dot-grid that builds person by person and then zooms out against 8 billion makes the epistemological humility visceral and is a genuinely missing isotype-style component

thermal_gradient_sphere.description: A rotating planet graphic split into two visible hemispheres with a continuous color-temperature gradient from deep red/white on one side to deep blue/black on the other, with temperature labels anchored to each pole.
thermal_gradient_sphere.schema_sketch: { hot_side_label: string, hot_side_temp: string, cold_side_label: string, cold_side_temp: string, gradient_colors: [string, string], planet_name: string, rotation_speed_ms: number }

examples.narration: day side reaches two thousand degrees. Night side drops to nearly absolute zero.
examples.why: The narration is fundamentally about a spatial temperature contrast across a single object — a gradient-mapped sphere communicates that extreme divide in one glance where two stacked text numbers cannot.

mass_drain_gauge.description: An animated planet silhouette with a visible stream of particles bleeding off one edge into a star glow, alongside a real-time depleting fill-bar representing remaining planetary mass with a projected extinction timestamp.
mass_drain_gauge.schema_sketch: { planet_name: string, loss_rate_label: string, loss_rate_unit: string, current_mass_percent: number, years_remaining: number, particle_color: string, star_color: string }

examples.narration: planet is hemorrhaging mass at a rate of ten billion tons per second.
examples.why: The horror of WASP-12b's story is the relentless, ongoing loss — an animated drain visualization makes that continuous hemorrhaging visceral in a way a static large number cannot.

albedo_comparator.description: A horizontal row of sphere icons representing different objects (Earth, Jupiter, coal, asphalt, TrES-2b) each rendered with its actual reflectivity as a visible brightness level, with albedo percentage labels beneath each.
albedo_comparator.schema_sketch: { items: [{ label: string, albedo_percent: number, icon: string }], highlight_item: string, background_color: string, label_color: string }

examples.narration: reflects less than one percent of the light that hits it.
examples.why: The narration's power comes from the ranked comparison of reflectivities across familiar objects — a brightness-scaled lineup makes the darkness of TrES-2b immediately and viscerally legible where a single percent-fill bar loses all context.

planetary_layer_peel.description: A cross-section sphere animation that sequentially strips away labeled concentric layers (atmosphere, ocean, crust, mantle) one by one until only a glowing core remains, with each removed layer fading outward.
planetary_layer_peel.schema_sketch: { layers: [{ name: string, color: string, thickness_relative: number }], strip_interval_ms: number, core_color: string, core_label: string, final_hold_ms: number }

examples.narration: strip away Earth's entire atmosphere, then its oceans, then its crust, leaving only the molten iron core.
examples.why: The narration is explicitly a sequential stripping metaphor — an animated layer-removal diagram maps directly onto that logic and turns an abstract description into a concrete, memorable visual process.

phase_cycle_diagram.description: A looping animated diagram showing a substance cycling through labeled phase states (liquid → vapor → solid) as it travels between two temperature zones, with arrows indicating directional flow and phase-change labels at transition points.
phase_cycle_diagram.schema_sketch: { zone_a: { label: string, temp: string, color: string }, zone_b: { label: string, temp: string, color: string }, substance_name: string, phases: [{ zone: string, state: string, label: string }], arrow_color: string, loop_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: winds carry clouds of vaporized silicate from day side to night side, where temperature drop turns vapor back into glass.
examples.why: The narration describes a planetary-scale chemical phase cycle — a directional flow diagram between two named temperature zones with phase-change callouts captures the scientific mechanism far better than an atmospheric image alone.

particle_flood.description: A field of thousands of tiny glowing dots that floods the screen in waves, with a live counter, conveying quantity so large individual units become texture
particle_flood.schema_sketch: { particle_count_target: number, particle_color: hex, wave_count: number, counter_label: string, overflow_behavior: 'blur' | 'compress' | 'fade', duration_seconds: number }

examples.narration: somewhere between two hundred billion and four hundred billion stars
examples.why: A count_up gives you a number, but the narrative center demands the previous frame of reference become meaningless — watching discrete particles lose individuality as they overwhelm the screen physically enacts that collapse in a way a counter cannot.

density_void_grid.description: A vast grid of empty space with two labeled objects placed so far apart that the emptiness is the overwhelming visual — objects occupy maybe 0.001% of visible area
density_void_grid.schema_sketch: { grid_width_units: number, grid_height_units: number, objects: [{ label: string, size_px: number, position: { x: number, y: number } }], background_color: hex, label_probability: string }

examples.narration: chances of any two individual stars actually hitting each other is virtually zero
examples.why: percent_fill communicates a statistic, but this moment requires the viewer to viscerally feel the absurdity of emptiness — the component needs to make the void the protagonist, not a labeled bar.

nested_scale_ruler.description: A horizontal ruler that zooms through nested scale ticks — Earth, Solar System, Milky Way, Local Group, Observable Universe, Great Wall — each prior label shrinking to invisibility as the next is revealed
nested_scale_ruler.schema_sketch: { scales: [{ label: string, size_ly: number, color: hex }], animation: 'sequential_zoom' | 'simultaneous', highlight_index: number, unit_label: string, duration_seconds: number }

examples.narration: largest structure we've ever discovered is called the Hercules-Corona Borealis Great Wall
examples.why: The narrative center is explicitly about each new scale making the previous one meaningless — a definition card names the structure but a nested scale ruler performs that annihilation visually in real time.

recession_horizon_diagram.description: An animated radial diagram with an observer at center, concentric rings showing light travel zones, and an outer boundary ring visibly expanding faster than an inward-moving light pulse can close the gap
recession_horizon_diagram.schema_sketch: { observer_label: string, rings: [{ label: string, radius_relative: number, color: hex }], light_pulse_speed: number, expansion_speed: number, horizon_ring_index: number, show_speed_labels: boolean, duration_seconds: number }

examples.narration: cosmic horizon — regions of space are moving away from us faster than light can travel
examples.why: This is one of the hardest concepts in the video to grasp — the horizon is not a place but a dynamic relationship between expansion speed and light speed — a definition card gives words where a diagram showing the light pulse perpetually falling behind gives genuine intuition.

observable_dot_universe_map.description: A full-bleed field representing the total universe at some scale, with a single near-invisible labeled dot marking the entire observable universe — the dot is the point, not the field
observable_dot_universe_map.schema_sketch: { total_field_label: string, dot_label: string, dot_size_px: number, dot_color: hex, dot_position: 'center' | 'random', field_color: hex, scale_ratio_display: string, pulse_animation: boolean }

examples.narration: total universe could be at least ten to the twenty-three times larger than what we can observe
examples.why: big_number renders 10²³ as typography, which the brain instantly discards as incomprehensible — rendering the observable universe as a literally invisible dot inside a vast field forces the eye to do the cognitive work the narration is demanding.

bubble_nucleation_field.description: An infinite dark field where labeled iridescent bubbles continuously nucleate, expand, and occasionally overlap at edges, with one bubble highlighted as 'our universe'
bubble_nucleation_field.schema_sketch: { bubble_spawn_rate_per_second: number, bubble_growth_speed: number, highlight_bubble_label: string, highlight_bubble_color: hex, default_bubble_color: hex, field_color: hex, physics_label_display: string[], loop: boolean }

examples.narration: one bubble in an endless multiverse where new universes are constantly being born
examples.why: An AI image can render something beautiful but static — the multiverse claim is inherently about ongoing, endless process, and the continuous nucleation animation makes the 'constantly being born' language land as direct observation rather than metaphor.

universe_composition_orb.description: An animated 3D-style sphere that fractures and reveals proportional interior layers for dark energy, dark matter, and visible matter with glowing labels
universe_composition_orb.schema_sketch: { segments: [{ label: string, percent: number, color: string, glow_color: string }], reveal_order: 'outside_in' | 'inside_out', animation_style: 'fracture' | 'peel' | 'expand' }

examples.narration: dark matter makes up eighty-five percent of all matter in the universe
examples.why: The three-way cosmic composition (68% dark energy, 27% dark matter, 5% visible matter) is a nested relationship across three invisible categories that a flat percent_fill bar fundamentally misrepresents — a layered orb makes the 'invisible wrapping the visible' concept viscerally clear

spacetime_expansion_animator.description: An animated grid of glowing dots representing galaxies that visibly accelerates its outward drift in real-time, with a live velocity counter climbing in the corner
spacetime_expansion_animator.schema_sketch: { dot_count: number, initial_velocity: number, acceleration_factor: number, duration_seconds: number, show_velocity_counter: boolean, background_color: string, dot_color: string }

examples.narration: expansion isn't slowing down at all — it's speeding up, every second faster
examples.why: A static trend arrow cannot convey accelerating expansion — the horror of dark energy is that the rate itself is increasing, which requires an animation where the dots not only move apart but visibly lurch faster as you watch, something no existing component delivers

galaxy_particle_scale.description: A top-down spiral galaxy renders from a single dot outward, with a zoom-to-highlight that isolates one tiny marked region and labels it 'You Are Here' alongside a particle count readout
galaxy_particle_scale.schema_sketch: { star_count_label: string, particle_density: number, highlight_position: { arm: number, radius_percent: number }, highlight_label: string, zoom_sequence: boolean, arm_count: number }

examples.narration: our galaxy alone contains four hundred billion stars, many of them have planets
examples.why: The Fermi Paradox gut-punch depends on the viewer viscerally feeling their own insignificance inside 400 billion stars — a big_number is just typography, while a rendered galaxy that zooms to show how cosmically lost Earth is makes the silence feel genuinely terrifying

signal_blip_scope.description: An oscilloscope-style dark screen with a flat baseline that suddenly erupts in a sharp spike of light, then returns to silence — repeatable and configurable for single-burst vs. repeating-pattern variants
signal_blip_scope.schema_sketch: { burst_count: number, burst_interval_ms: number, burst_duration_ms: number, spike_height: number, baseline_noise_level: number, color_scheme: 'radio_green' | 'plasma_white' | 'warning_red', show_energy_label: boolean }

examples.narration: they arrive without warning and disappear just as quickly, some happen only once
examples.why: Fast Radio Bursts are defined by their millisecond brevity and the contrast between silence and violent energy — an oscilloscope blip that fires and dies is the only visual that literally enacts the phenomenon rather than describing it, and nothing in the library animates raw signal data

subsurface_cross_section.description: An animated layered cross-section of a rogue planet cutting from frozen surface crust through ice mantle down to a glowing liquid ocean, with depth and temperature labels at each layer boundary
subsurface_cross_section.schema_sketch: { layers: [{ name: string, depth_km: number, temperature_celsius: number, color: string, texture: 'solid' | 'liquid' | 'gas' }], planet_radius_km: number, show_depth_labels: boolean, animate_heat_glow: boolean, glow_source: 'core' | 'radioactive_decay' }

examples.narration: a planet floating in darkness between stars, surface covered in ice kilometers thick
examples.why: The rogue planet's entire conceptual tension — frozen death outside, possible life inside — lives in the vertical layer structure that no AI image can clearly label and annotate; a cross-section diagram makes the hidden ocean's existence feel scientifically concrete rather than speculative

information_throughput_comparator.description: A side-by-side animated comparison of two throughput meters — one representing all human computing history as a finite bar, one representing the black hole's information processing as an overflowing, crashing meter that shatters its scale
information_throughput_comparator.schema_sketch: { left: { label: string, value: number, unit: string, color: string }, right: { label: string, value: number, unit: string, color: string, overflow_behavior: 'shatter' | 'flood' | 'infinite_scroll' }, animation_duration_seconds: number, show_ratio_label: boolean }

examples.narration: supermassive black hole processing more information every second than every computer ever existed
examples.why: The awe of this claim depends on the ratio being incomprehensible — a static stat on a split image doesn't convey overflow and scale-breaking, but a visual meter that literally cannot contain the black hole's value makes the consciousness-emergence argument feel earned rather than asserted

asymptote_curve.description: A smooth curve that visibly rockets upward and flattens toward a vertical asymptote, with a labeled threshold line and axis annotations
asymptote_curve.schema_sketch: { x_label: string, y_label: string, asymptote_value: number, curve_data: [{x, y}], threshold_label: string, highlight_color: string, animation_speed: 'slow'|'fast' }

examples.narration: energy required to push that object faster approaches infinity
examples.why: A generic line chart implies finite growth, but the visual drama of a curve screaming toward infinity and never crossing a vertical wall is the entire physical intuition the narration is building — that distinction is lost without an asymptote-specific component

spacetime_warp_bubble.description: An animated cross-section diagram showing a ship at center, with grid lines compressing ahead and stretching behind, and a labeled bubble boundary ring
spacetime_warp_bubble.schema_sketch: { ship_label: string, front_compression_factor: number, rear_expansion_factor: number, bubble_radius_display: string, grid_color: string, bubble_color: string, animate: boolean, speed_label: string }

examples.narration: contracts space in front of the ship and expands space behind it, creating a bubble
examples.why: This is the single most important conceptual diagram in the entire video — the Alcubierre bubble — and a static AI image cannot animate the directional compression/expansion that makes the physics legible; a dedicated diagram component would make the loophole viscerally understandable

cosmic_scale_comparison.description: A side-by-side proportional size comparison of two or more celestial or physical objects with labeled mass/energy values beneath each, rendered as stylized silhouettes to scale
cosmic_scale_comparison.schema_sketch: { items: [{ label: string, silhouette_type: 'planet'|'star'|'ship'|'human'|'building', relative_scale: number, stat_label: string, stat_value: string }], background_color: string, unit_note: string }

examples.narration: mass-energy equivalent of Jupiter converted entirely into exotic matter just
examples.why: The narration's entire emotional punch is the absurd scale mismatch between Jupiter's mass and a car-sized ship — a split-stat image cannot render that proportional horror the way a true to-scale silhouette comparison could

entanglement_link_diagram.description: Two particle nodes connected by an animated pulsing line across an arbitrary distance, with an instantaneous-signal indicator and a light-travel-time counter shown for contrast
entanglement_link_diagram.schema_sketch: { particle_a_label: string, particle_b_label: string, distance_display: string, signal_type: 'instant'|'light_speed', light_travel_time_label: string, pulse_color: string, animate: boolean }

examples.narration: Quantum entanglement lets particles communicate instantly across any distance
examples.why: Entanglement's key visual concept is the instantaneous correlated link across space — a twin image suggests similarity but not connection, whereas an animated bidirectional link with a simultaneous-flash makes the physics immediately graspable

universe_expansion_rate_map.description: A radial diagram of observable universe boundaries with animated outward arrows whose speed is labeled at different distance bands, showing recession velocity exceeding c beyond a threshold ring
universe_expansion_rate_map.schema_sketch: { center_label: string, rings: [{ distance_label: string, recession_velocity: string, exceeds_c: boolean }], arrow_color: string, threshold_ring_color: string, animate_expansion: boolean, hubble_constant_note: string }

examples.narration: Dark energy is stretching space faster than light on cosmic scales
examples.why: The narration is making a precise and counterintuitive claim — that space itself already exceeds light speed at large distances — which needs a labeled radial velocity map to feel like evidence rather than assertion

time_dilation_clock_split.description: Two synchronized analog or digital clocks side by side — one labeled 'GPS Satellite' and one labeled 'Earth Surface' — drifting apart in real time with a running microseconds-per-day drift counter beneath each
time_dilation_clock_split.schema_sketch: { clock_a_label: string, clock_b_label: string, drift_rate_us_per_day: number, correction_applied: boolean, correction_label: string, clock_style: 'analog'|'digital', animate: boolean }

examples.narration: GPS satellites have to account for time dilation effects predicted by Einstein's theories
examples.why: Time dilation is abstract until you see two clocks visibly diverging; a running drift counter grounds Einstein's theory in a real-world engineering consequence that the audience already carries in their pocket, making it the most relatable proof point in the video

compound_growth_simulator.description: An animated curve that visually separates principal contributed from interest earned over time, with a draggable timeline scrubber showing balance at any year
compound_growth_simulator.schema_sketch: { monthly_contribution: number, annual_return_pct: number, years: number, principal_color: string, growth_color: string, milestone_labels: [{ year: number, label: string }] }

examples.narration: Two thousand dollars a month, invested for thirty years, seven percent annually
examples.why: A generic line chart shows a line going up, but this narration is specifically about the jaw-dropping gap between what you put in versus what compounding returns, which requires a stacked-area fill with two distinct zones to make the exponential divergence viscerally clear

asset_accumulation_ladder.description: A vertically stacking visual where each acquired asset (property, investment account) animates in as a physical block with its acquisition date, value, and monthly cash flow, building a tower over time
asset_accumulation_ladder.schema_sketch: { assets: [{ label: string, date: string, value: number, monthly_cashflow: number, asset_type: 'property' | 'stock' | 'business', icon: string }], currency_symbol: string }

examples.narration: Two years later, she bought another duplex. Then another.
examples.why: The narration is specifically about sequential wealth-building through asset acquisition, and a plain timeline treats each duplex as just a date marker rather than showing the compounding mass of owned assets growing heavier and more powerful with each addition

daily_loop_clock.description: A 24-hour radial clock face that fills each hour segment with color-coded activity blocks (sleep, work, study, repeat), animating through a single day then looping to show the grinding repetition of a monk-mode schedule
daily_loop_clock.schema_sketch: { schedule_blocks: [{ label: string, start_hour: number, end_hour: number, color: string }], loop_count: number, loop_duration_ms: number, center_label: string }

examples.narration: Eight months, David's entire life was work, code, sleep, repeat
examples.why: The narration is explicitly about the cyclical, relentless daily rhythm of sacrifice, and a roadmap implies forward progress through milestones, while what actually needs to land visually is the claustrophobic, repeating loop of a single day stretched across eight months

job_displacement_heatmap.description: A grid of occupation icons where cells dim and fade out one by one in an animated wave, with a running counter of displaced roles and a highlighted survivor cluster showing which job categories are growing
job_displacement_heatmap.schema_sketch: { total_icons: number, displaced_count: number, displaced_category_label: string, survivor_categories: [{ label: string, icon: string, growth_pct: number }], animation_speed_ms: number, accent_color: string }
