examples.narration: Artificial intelligence quietly eliminated two million customer service jobs
examples.why: The word 'quietly' and the scale of two million jobs demands a visual that shows mass simultaneous disappearance across an entire occupational landscape, not a single statistic beside an AI image, so that the viewer feels the breadth of disruption rather than reading a number

perceived_vs_actual_wealth_split.description: A dual vertical bar pair where the left bar shows visible lifestyle signals (car, clothes, apartment tier) rendered as branded icons stacked up, and the right bar shows net worth as a number, with the two bars intentionally inverse in height to expose the illusion
perceived_vs_actual_wealth_split.schema_sketch: { persona_label: string, lifestyle_signals: [{ label: string, icon: string, monthly_cost: number }], net_worth: number, lifestyle_bar_color: string, networth_bar_color: string, lifestyle_bar_label: string, networth_bar_label: string }

examples.narration: They'll choose looking successful over being successful. Every single time.
examples.why: The narration's core tension is the inversion between appearance and reality, which a stat_comparison renders as two equal-weight numbers side by side, but the insight only lands when the visual encoding itself is inverted — tall appearance bar, tiny net worth bar — making the trap geometric rather than textual

economy_flow_diagram.description: A Sankey-style animated flow where money visibly streams from a large consumer pool through platforms and products, pooling into a small creator node that grows disproportionately large, making the wealth concentration dynamic visible as fluid motion
economy_flow_diagram.schema_sketch: { consumer_node_label: string, consumer_count: number, platform_nodes: [{ label: string }], creator_node_label: string, creator_count: number, flow_color: string, animation_duration_ms: number, money_per_second: number }

examples.narration: The economy of 2026 rewards creators, not consumers
examples.why: This narration is about a structural economic mechanism, not a binary comparison, and a compare_reveal shows two static states side by side, while the actual insight is the directional flow of money from many to few, which requires showing the transfer in motion to make the opportunity feel real and exploitable

decay_curve.description: An animated physiological decline curve plotted over age, with a marked inflection point where decline steepens and annotated rate labels at key age milestones
decay_curve.schema_sketch: { metric_label: string, x_axis_unit: 'age', x_range: [number, number], data_points: [{ age: number, value: number }], inflection_age: number, rate_annotations: [{ age: number, label: string }], baseline_reference_age: number }

examples.narration: leg muscle power drops by three point five percent every year after age sixty-five
examples.why: The narration describes a continuous, accelerating biological decline over decades — a trend_arrow implies a single direction but cannot show the steepening curve or the critical inflection point after 65 that makes this statistic alarming

muscle_fiber_recruitment_viz.description: A side-by-side grid of muscle fiber units where active fibers are illuminated and inactive ones are dimmed, with two labeled states (Young Adult vs Age 70) and a live percentage counter showing recruitment differential
muscle_fiber_recruitment_viz.schema_sketch: { total_units: number, states: [{ label: string, age: number, active_fraction: number, color_active: string, color_inactive: string }], animation_style: 'sequential_dim' | 'random_dim', show_percentage_delta: boolean }

examples.narration: by age seventy, people activate thirty-two percent fewer motor units when walking
examples.why: Motor unit recruitment is an inherently visual, countable concept — seeing a grid of lit vs dark fibers makes the 32% gap viscerally understandable in a way a static stat overlay on an image cannot achieve

sensory_system_drain.description: A three-panel body-system dashboard showing vestibular, visual, and proprioceptive systems each as a signal-strength meter that animates down to its age-degraded level, with percentage remaining labeled per system
sensory_system_drain.schema_sketch: { systems: [{ name: string, icon: string, baseline_age: number, comparison_age: number, degraded_percent: number, color: string }], animation: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous', show_combined_deficit: boolean }

examples.narration: vestibular function decreases by about forty percent compared to age twenty
examples.why: Scenes 52–58 cover three distinct degrading sensory systems in sequence; a unified multi-system drain visualization would show their simultaneous decline and reinforce the cumulative collapse narrative far better than three separate percent_fill instances

predictive_web.description: A radial spoke diagram with walking speed at the center hub and outcome nodes (heart disease, stroke, dementia, etc.) radiating outward, where spoke thickness encodes relative predictive strength and nodes pulse to animate the causal relationship
predictive_web.schema_sketch: { hub_label: string, hub_icon: string, spokes: [{ outcome: string, relative_strength: number, risk_increase_percent: number, icon: string }], animation: 'pulse_outward' | 'draw_in', color_scheme: string }

examples.narration: walking speed predicts heart disease, stroke, diabetes, dementia, and disability better than almost any other single measurement
examples.why: The narration's core claim is that one metric predicts many outcomes — a radial web makes the hub-and-spoke causal structure immediately legible, whereas a bullet list suggests parallel independent items rather than a single powerful predictor

dose_response_slider.description: An interactive-style animated slider showing walking speed on the x-axis with a corresponding risk percentage bar rising or falling as the speed value decrements in real time, pausing at the cited threshold
dose_response_slider.schema_sketch: { x_axis_label: string, x_unit: 'm/s', x_range: [number, number], y_axis_label: string, y_unit: '%', dose_step: number, risk_change_per_step: number, highlight_threshold: number, animation_direction: 'decrement' }

examples.narration: every point one meter per second decrease in walking speed, risk of developing dementia increased by twelve percent
examples.why: This narration describes a dose-response relationship — for every incremental unit of speed lost, risk climbs by a fixed amount — which is a dynamic, proportional relationship that a static stat panel cannot convey but an animated slider sweep makes intuitively graspable

speed_benchmark_gauge.description: A horizontal speed track with labeled population percentile bands (bottom 20%, average, top 20%) and an animated marker that moves from the starting speed to the improved speed, landing relative to age-group benchmark zones
speed_benchmark_gauge.schema_sketch: { unit: 'm/s', range: [number, number], benchmark_bands: [{ label: string, min: number, max: number, color: string }], reference_lines: [{ label: string, value: number }], before_value: number, after_value: number, subject_label: string, animate_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: eighteen months later, she was walking at one point one meters per second. Faster than the average sixty-year-old
examples.why: The power of Margaret's story is not just the raw number change but where she landed relative to population norms — a before_after panel shows two static values while a benchmark gauge shows the journey across a meaningful reference landscape

viscosity_comparator.description: A split-panel animated fluid simulation showing the same tilting vessel with fast-moving vs. slow-moving fluid, labeled with age and fluid metaphor
viscosity_comparator.schema_sketch: { left_label: string, right_label: string, left_fluid_speed: 'fast'|'medium'|'slow', right_fluid_speed: 'fast'|'medium'|'slow', fluid_color: hex, vessel_tilt_angle: number, metaphor_text_left: string, metaphor_text_right: string }

examples.narration: At twenty, it moves like water. At seventy, it's more like honey.
examples.why: before_after implies a static snapshot swap, but the core insight here is a difference in motion and flow rate — an animated fluid viscosity comparison would make the degradation viscerally felt rather than merely described

decay_slope_ticker.description: A single animated line that draws itself across a time axis while a live counter increments the cumulative cost — e.g. milliseconds lost — at each age milestone, with a danger threshold line that the curve visibly crosses
decay_slope_ticker.schema_sketch: { x_axis_label: string, y_axis_label: string, start_age: number, end_age: number, annual_increment: number, danger_threshold_value: number, danger_threshold_label: string, baseline_value: number, accent_color: hex }

examples.narration: reaction time for stepping increases by about twelve milliseconds every year after fifty
examples.why: animated_line_chart exists but shows trends generically; this narration needs the viewer to feel each year's compounding cost accumulating in real time with a clear moment where the body crosses into dangerous territory, which requires a threshold-aware animated counter fused with the curve

body_contribution_map.description: A silhouette of a standing human figure with anatomical regions highlighted and weighted — each region glows proportionally to its contribution percentage to the described function, with a callout label
body_contribution_map.schema_sketch: { body_regions: [{ region_id: 'ankle'|'knee'|'hip'|'core'|'spine', contribution_percent: number, label: string, highlight_color: hex }], silhouette_orientation: 'front'|'side', title: string }

examples.narration: ankle muscles alone responsible for about seventy percent of the tiny adjustments keeping you upright
examples.why: percent_fill shows a number filling up but gives no spatial or anatomical context; the real insight is WHERE on the body the dominant contribution lives, which demands a localized body-map visualization so viewers connect the statistic to their own physical experience

signal_convergence_diagram.description: Three animated signal streams — each labeled and iconified — flowing along distinct paths into a central brain node, with stream thickness and opacity representing signal strength or reliability
signal_convergence_diagram.schema_sketch: { inputs: [{ id: string, label: string, icon: string, signal_strength: 0-100, stream_color: hex, description: string }], center_node_label: string, center_node_icon: string, degradation_mode: boolean }

examples.narration: brain combines information from three different systems: inner ear, vision, and proprioception
examples.why: three_points is a static bullet-style layout; the narration is explicitly about parallel inputs merging into one processing center, and the core metaphor of degrading signal quality cries out for a dynamic convergence diagram where each stream can visually degrade to show what aging does to each channel

environment_risk_heatmap.description: A simple isometric or top-down floor-plan of a home with rooms color-coded by fall-risk intensity, overlaid with a dynamic light-level dimmer that visually shifts the heatmap as ambient light drops
environment_risk_heatmap.schema_sketch: { rooms: [{ name: string, base_risk_score: 1-10, risk_color: hex }], light_level: 'bright'|'dim'|'dark', animate_transition: boolean, overlay_label: string, stat_callout: string }

examples.narration: Ninety percent of fall-related hip fractures in seniors happen in low-light conditions
examples.why: percent_fill conveys the ninety percent statistic but strips away the environmental and situational dimension — the real insight is that ordinary domestic spaces become dangerous under low light, and a spatial heatmap makes that threat feel immediate and personally recognizable

nerve_signal_race.description: A side-by-side dual-lane animated pulse racing down a stylized nerve fiber — one lane labeled age 30 moves visibly faster, one labeled age 70 lags behind — with speed readouts updating in real time
nerve_signal_race.schema_sketch: { lane_a: { label: string, speed_mps: number, color: hex }, lane_b: { label: string, speed_mps: number, color: hex }, track_length_ms: number, unit_label: string, show_gap_callout: boolean }

examples.narration: At thirty, one hundred twenty meters per second. By seventy, it's down to ninety meters per second.
examples.why: trend_arrow reduces a kinetic, race-like comparison to a static directional cue; the narration is specifically about transmission speed and the gap between two ages, which is most powerfully shown as a literal animated race where the viewer can see the lag accumulate over the same distance

neural_burst_counter.description: An animated brain silhouette where glowing nodes and synaptic arcs fire in real-time bursts, with a live connection counter ticking upward beneath it
neural_burst_counter.schema_sketch: { connections_per_second: number, burst_color: string, brain_region_highlights: string[], counter_label: string, duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: seven hundred to one thousand new connections per second
examples.why: A plain count_up shows the number but loses the spatial, biological drama of synaptic firing — a neural burst visualization makes the rate feel viscerally real rather than abstractly numerical

diverging_outcome_paths.description: Two animated timeline paths that start at the same origin point (birth) and visibly diverge over months, with outcome labels anchored at the split endpoints
diverging_outcome_paths.schema_sketch: { path_a: { label: string, daily_input: string, outcome_label: string, outcome_value: string }, path_b: { label: string, daily_input: string, outcome_label: string, outcome_value: string }, x_axis_label: string, fork_point_label: string }

examples.narration: thirty thousand words per day versus three thousand words per day IQ twelve points higher
examples.why: The core insight is a trajectory divergence over time, not a static side-by-side stat — a forking-path animation communicates compounding consequence in a way a flat comparison card cannot

waking_day_heatmap.description: A 24-hour day broken into color-coded blocks showing engaged eye contact time versus phone/distracted time versus sleep, like a visual time audit of a parent's day
waking_day_heatmap.schema_sketch: { total_hours: number, segments: [{ label: string, hours: number, color: string, icon?: string }], highlight_segment: string, annotation: string }

examples.narration: average parent makes eye contact only eighteen percent of their waking hours
examples.why: 18% is abstract until you see exactly how small a sliver of the day it occupies laid against all the hours of distraction — a temporal block map makes the deficit emotionally undeniable rather than just numerically stated

stress_hormone_gauge_animated.description: A dual-needle gauge styled like a medical monitor showing cortisol level dropping in real time from a high-stress baseline to a calm baseline as a sequence animation plays beside it
stress_hormone_gauge_animated.schema_sketch: { hormone_label: string, baseline_value: number, reduced_value: number, reduction_percent: number, trigger_label: string, gauge_color_high: string, gauge_color_low: string, animation_duration_ms: number }

examples.narration: cortisol levels drop by thirty-two percent when prediction system works well
examples.why: A big_number freezes the statistic; an animated gauge that visibly falls as the routine sequence completes ties the biological cause directly to the effect, making the mechanism legible not just the outcome

stimulus_overload_grid.description: An animated icon grid that starts sparsely populated and rapidly fills with flashing, overlapping stimuli icons (toy, screen, sound wave, notification) until the grid visually overloads and glitches
stimulus_overload_grid.schema_sketch: { stimulus_icons: [{ icon_id: string, label: string, flash: boolean }], fill_speed_ms: number, overload_threshold: number, glitch_effect: boolean, caption: string }

examples.narration: toys that light up, apps that play music, videos designed for infant brains
examples.why: The narration is about sensory overload crowding out processing space — a grid that visually saturates and breaks down communicates cognitive overwhelm in a way a static photo stack of toys cannot

sequence_loop_animator.description: A circular or chain loop of 3–6 illustrated ritual steps (song → diaper → high chair) that animate in repeating order, with a 'prediction confidence' indicator that visibly strengthens on each loop cycle
sequence_loop_animator.schema_sketch: { steps: [{ icon: string, label: string }], loop_count: number, confidence_bar: { label: string, increments_per_loop: number }, loop_speed_ms: number, color_scheme: string }

examples.narration: follow the same sequence of events and their prediction system gets stronger
examples.why: A roadmap implies linear progress toward a destination, but the neuroscience point is about repetitive looping sequences building a predictive model — a looping chain animation captures the cyclical, reinforcing nature that a journey metaphor misrepresents

recovery_time_bar.description: A horizontal duration comparison bar showing two labeled groups with animated fill representing time-to-regulate, with a shared baseline and labeled range brackets
recovery_time_bar.schema_sketch: { group_a_label, group_a_min_minutes, group_a_max_minutes, group_b_label, group_b_min_minutes, group_b_max_minutes, bar_color_a, bar_color_b, unit_label, headline }

examples.narration: autistic children can take anywhere from two to six hours to regulate
examples.why: The narration contrasts a precise range (2–6 hours vs 15–20 minutes) between two populations — a single big number loses the comparative drama and the range dimension that makes this statistic viscerally meaningful

attention_clock_dial.description: Two side-by-side circular clock-face dials that animate a sweep arc to visually fill the proportion of time each group sustains focus, with minute labels at key positions
attention_clock_dial.schema_sketch: { dial_a_label, dial_a_minutes, dial_b_label, dial_b_minutes, max_scale_minutes, color_a, color_b, activity_label_a, activity_label_b }

examples.narration: two hundred and thirty-seven minutes versus forty-two minutes on most engaging tasks
examples.why: Duration data lives intuitively on a clock face — viewers instantly feel how much longer 237 minutes is versus 42 minutes when expressed as a sweep arc, which a generic stat_comparison card cannot convey viscerally

social_cue_miss_map.description: A stylized two-figure interaction scene with annotated callout pins positioned at specific social-behavior hotspots (personal space bubble, eye contact zone, turn-taking indicator) that pulse in sequence
social_cue_miss_map.schema_sketch: { scene_description, callout_pins: [{ position_x, position_y, label, icon_type }], animation_sequence_order, background_style }

examples.narration: stand too close, interrupt at wrong time, miss social cues obvious to everyone else
examples.why: The narration describes spatially and behaviorally specific social missteps — a bullet list reads them as abstract text while an annotated interaction diagram makes each missed cue feel concrete and locatable in real behavior

sensory_channel_grid.description: A grid of labeled sensory channels (sound, touch, sight, smell, proprioception) shown as signal-strength bars for two profiles side by side, with some bars over-amplified and some under-amplified relative to a neutral baseline
sensory_channel_grid.schema_sketch: { channels: [{ name, icon, neurotypical_level, autistic_level, direction: 'over'|'under' }], baseline_value, color_over, color_under, profile_a_label, profile_b_label }

examples.narration: autistic children process sensory information eighteen to twenty-three percent differently
examples.why: The narration explicitly names multiple sensory modalities processed differently — a single percentage stat collapses this into one number, while a per-channel signal grid shows the uneven, modality-specific nature of sensory processing difference

examples.narration: can't wear certain fabrics, need sunglasses indoors, hear electrical buzzing others can't
examples.why: The narration lists specific sensory channels at abnormal intensities, which is inherently a multi-axis data display; a checkmark_build reduces rich sensory-intensity information to a binary list, whereas a channel grid with intensity bars shows both which senses are affected and how severely

intervention_decay_curve.description: An animated curve chart plotting intervention outcome percentage against age of diagnosis, with a steep drop-off zone highlighted between ages five and eight and labeled outcome markers at key ages
intervention_decay_curve.schema_sketch: { x_axis_label, y_axis_label, data_points: [{ age, outcome_percent }], highlight_zone_start_age, highlight_zone_end_age, marker_labels: [{ age, percent, annotation }], curve_color, highlight_color }

examples.narration: before age five ninety-one percent improvement, after age eight drops to forty-two percent
examples.why: This is the emotional climax statistic of the video — it describes a continuous decay over time, not just two isolated numbers, and an animated curve makes the urgency of the narrowing window far more visceral than two static percentages

routine_ritual_sequence.description: An animated horizontal timeline of icon-based ritual steps that build left-to-right with a visual 'break' disruption state that triggers a red fracture animation when any single step is altered
routine_ritual_sequence.schema_sketch: { steps: [{ label, icon }], disruption_step_index, normal_color, disrupted_color, disruption_animation_type, sequence_label, caption }

examples.narration: shoes tied exact same way, backpack packed same order, bedtime story same inflection
examples.why: The narration is about sequential rigidity and the emotional consequence of any break — a checkmark build shows completion but cannot show the sequential dependency or the disruption consequence that makes routine needs visually distinct from mere preference

receipt_unroll.description: An animated receipt that unrolls vertically, revealing line items one by one with a running total that climbs to the shocking final price
receipt_unroll.schema_sketch: { header_label: string, line_items: [{ label: string, amount: number, highlight: boolean }], subtotal_label: string, taxes_fees_items: [{ label: string, amount: number }], final_total: number, currency: string, animation_speed_ms: number }

examples.narration: website said two hundred dollars, paper in front of you says six hundred and seventy-three
examples.why: A before/after shows two static states, but the horror of rental car fees is experiential and cumulative — watching a receipt grow line by line makes the viewer feel the trap closing, which is the entire emotional point of this scene

gas_pump_fill_race.description: Two side-by-side animated gas pump nozzles filling identical tanks — one labeled 'Rental Company Prepay' and one labeled 'You Fill It Yourself' — with dollar amounts ticking up as each tank fills, stopping at different totals
gas_pump_fill_race.schema_sketch: { pump_a: { label: string, price_per_gallon: number, gallons: number, color: string }, pump_b: { label: string, price_per_gallon: number, gallons: number, color: string }, tank_capacity_gallons: number, fill_duration_ms: number, show_waste_callout: boolean }

examples.narration: fifty-eight dollars to fill a tank that costs fifty-three dollars at any gas station
examples.why: The stat_comparison shows two numbers statically, but the fuel scam is viscerally about watching money pour away for the same physical outcome — the pumping animation makes the waste tangible in a way raw numbers cannot

fee_waterfall.description: A cascading waterfall chart where a base price bar drops through labeled negative layers — concession fee, facility charge, transportation cost, customer service fee — each visually eating into a budget bar before revealing the inflated final price
fee_waterfall.schema_sketch: { base_price: number, fee_layers: [{ label: string, amount: number, color: string, tooltip: string }], final_price: number, currency: string, orientation: 'descending' | 'ascending', animate_sequential: boolean }

examples.narration: airport locations charge a concession recovery fee, passing the cost of renting space directly to you
examples.why: A notification stack lists fees as parallel items but hides their compounding nature; a waterfall chart shows exactly how each fee layer drags the price downward from the traveler's perspective, making the extraction mechanism instantly legible

city_distance_tradeoff.description: A simple top-down city map card showing the airport pin and the off-airport rental pin connected by a dotted rideshare route, with the ride cost badge on the route and the savings badge anchored to the destination pin
city_distance_tradeoff.schema_sketch: { map_style: 'minimal' | 'satellite', origin: { label: string, icon: 'airport' | 'pin' }, destination: { label: string, icon: 'car' | 'pin' }, route_distance_miles: number, ride_cost: number, savings_amount: number, currency: string, show_travel_time_minutes: boolean }

examples.narration: Lisa took an Uber from the airport to the downtown location. Twelve dollars
examples.why: A scale_balance is abstract; the actual insight here is spatial and tactical — the viewer needs to see that the off-airport location is a short, cheap hop away, not a heroic detour, and a map makes that distance feel negligible versus the savings

brand_logo_grid_reveal.description: A grid of major rental brand logos that animate in one by one, each accompanied by a small 'advertised from' teaser price, then simultaneously flip to reveal the true average all-in price beneath each logo
brand_logo_grid_reveal.schema_sketch: { brands: [{ name: string, logo_url: string, advertised_price: number, actual_avg_price: number, currency: string }], flip_animation: 'sequential' | 'simultaneous', front_bg_color: string, back_bg_color: string, price_label_advertised: string, price_label_actual: string }

examples.narration: Hertz, Budget, Enterprise, Avis — gleaming counters, thirty-nine dollars a day advertised
examples.why: A polaroid stack suggests personal travel photos; this scene is making a systemic argument about an entire industry's pricing theater — a synchronized logo flip creates a visual 'gotcha' moment that brands the deception as industry-wide rather than anecdotal

search_results_race.description: A mock search results interface where two result cards animate in side by side — the airport incumbent on the left with its fee-stacked total, the local operator on the right with its clean total — with a savings badge that snaps into place between them
search_results_race.schema_sketch: { result_a: { provider_name: string, car_class: string, daily_rate: number, fees_breakdown: [{ label: string, amount: number }], total: number, badge_label: string }, result_b: { provider_name: string, car_class: string, daily_rate: number, fees_breakdown: [{ label: string, amount: number }], total: number, badge_label: string }, savings_amount: number, currency: string, highlight_winner: 'a' | 'b' }

examples.narration: exact same car class through a local Phoenix rental company for eighty-seven dollars a day
examples.why: Before/after works for transformations but this is a discovery moment, not a transformation — the viewer needs to see it as a search result comparison the way they actually experience it on screen, making the workflow immediately replicable rather than abstract

rhythm_pulse_timer.description: A looping visual metronome showing alternating hold/release intervals as expanding rings or heartbeat pulses with labeled durations
rhythm_pulse_timer.schema_sketch: { intervals: [{ label: 'HOLD', duration_seconds: 3, color: string }, { label: 'BREAK', duration_seconds: 1, color: string }], loop: boolean, animation_style: 'pulse' | 'ring_expand' | 'bar_fill', caption: string }

examples.narration: hold eye contact for exactly three seconds, then look away for one second
examples.why: The narration is teaching a precise repeating rhythm — hold 3s, break 1s, repeat — which is inherently cyclical and pattern-based, not a one-shot countdown; a looping pulse visualization would teach the cadence viscerally instead of just showing a clock ticking down.

body_position_diagram.description: A clean illustrated silhouette or skeletal overlay of a human figure demonstrating a specific posture or hand position with labeled callout arrows
body_position_diagram.schema_sketch: { body_region: 'full' | 'upper' | 'hands' | 'face', highlight_zones: [{ zone_id: string, label: string, arrow_direction: string }], posture_label: string, silhouette_style: 'outline' | 'solid' | 'skeleton', background_color: string }

examples.narration: place your hands in front of you with fingertips touching, forming a triangle
examples.why: The narration is instructing a precise physical configuration of the body — fingertip triangle, lean angle, head level — which demands a spatial anatomical diagram, not a text card; a definition card can only tell viewers what to do, while a body diagram can show them exactly how it looks.

lean_angle_visualizer.description: A side-profile silhouette of a seated figure with an animated arc showing the precise degree of forward lean, with a danger zone marked for over-leaning
lean_angle_visualizer.schema_sketch: { baseline_angle: 0, target_angle: 4, danger_threshold_angle: 15, labels: { target: string, danger: string, neutral: string }, animate_to_target: boolean, silhouette_style: 'outline' | 'solid' }

examples.narration: Not aggressively. Just three to five degrees.
examples.why: A gauge meter abstracts the concept into a generic dial with no spatial meaning; the narration is specifically about a physical angle of the human body, and showing an actual leaning figure with a measured arc and a labeled 'too much' threshold would communicate the precision and the risk simultaneously.

mirror_behavior_sync.description: A split-panel animation showing two silhouetted figures where the right figure's posture animates to match the left figure's posture with a brief delay, visualizing unconscious mirroring
mirror_behavior_sync.schema_sketch: { figure_a: { label: string, posture_sequence: string[] }, figure_b: { label: string, mirror_delay_ms: number }, highlight_color: string, sync_indicator: boolean, caption: string }

examples.narration: Boss leans back, they lean back. Leader crosses their arms, they cross their arms.
examples.why: The core concept here is dynamic behavioral synchronization — one person causes another to move — which is inherently animated and relational; a static split diagonal image cannot show the causal chain or the delay that makes mirroring psychologically real.

negotiation_outcome_matrix.description: A two-column visual scorecard comparing 'talks to fill silence' vs 'holds silence' across multiple outcome metrics (win rate, concession rate, information revealed) with bar or icon fills
negotiation_outcome_matrix.schema_sketch: { columns: [{ id: 'talks', label: string }, { id: 'silent', label: string }], metrics: [{ label: string, talks_value: number, silent_value: number, unit: string, higher_is_better: boolean }], highlight_winner: boolean, source_label: string }

examples.narration: side that used strategic silence achieved better outcomes eighty-two percent of the time
examples.why: The narration positions silence as a multi-dimensional strategic tool across thousands of negotiations, not just one statistic; a single big number flattens this into one data point, while a comparative outcome matrix would let the cumulative strategic advantage of silence land with full weight.

skill_unlock_progression.description: A vertical or horizontal chain of eight numbered technique nodes that visually 'unlock' in sequence — locked nodes are grayed out, the active node glows, completed nodes show a check — like a skill tree
skill_unlock_progression.schema_sketch: { nodes: [{ id: number, label: string, state: 'locked' | 'active' | 'complete' }], active_node_id: number, layout: 'horizontal' | 'vertical' | 'tree', unlock_animation: boolean, connector_style: 'line' | 'arrow' | 'chain' }

examples.narration: Start with one technique. Master it completely before moving to the next.
examples.why: The narration is explicitly prescribing a sequential mastery path through all eight techniques, implying dependency and progression; a generic roadmap journey shows steps but not the lock/unlock logic that makes the viewer feel the stakes of doing it in order.

suction_force_comparator.description: A side-by-side animated pressure visualization showing three devices as vertical liquid columns or piston-force bars, with a highlighted 'safe zone' band overlaid and real-world analogy icons (shop vac, dishwasher, etc.) anchored to their respective pressure levels
suction_force_comparator.schema_sketch: { devices: [{ label, value_kpa, color }], safe_zone: { min_kpa, max_kpa, label }, analogy_anchors: [{ value_kpa, icon, label }], animation: 'fill_up' | 'piston' }

examples.narration: Grownsy produces forty-five kilopascals — equivalent to a small shop vacuum
examples.why: A single gauge only shows one device at a time, but the narrative payoff here is the simultaneous visual relationship between all three pressure values AND the pediatric safe-zone band — that three-column pressure comparison with a shaded optimal range is fundamentally a different component than a lone radial gauge

age_suction_matrix.description: An animated heat-map grid with baby age bands on the X-axis and suction level numbers on the Y-axis, where each cell fills with a color (green = optimal, yellow = acceptable, red = too intense) for each of the three devices shown as separate grid panels
age_suction_matrix.schema_sketch: { devices: [{ label, grid: [{ age_band, suction_level, rating: 'optimal'|'acceptable'|'too_strong'|'too_weak' }] }], age_bands: string[], suction_levels: number[], color_map: { optimal, acceptable, too_strong, too_weak } }

examples.narration: Level three worked best for newborns to three months; level four for three to twelve months
examples.why: The narration is explicitly a two-dimensional recommendation space (age × suction level × device), which a linear journey metaphor cannot encode — a matrix grid communicates 'which cell is safe for your baby' instantly without requiring the viewer to mentally decode a path

decibel_waveform_stack.description: Three stacked animated sound-wave bars (one per device) whose waveform amplitude and color intensity scale with measured dB value, with a labeled threshold line for 'typical infant startle response' and real-world environment callouts pinned at matching dB points along a shared axis
decibel_waveform_stack.schema_sketch: { devices: [{ label, db_value, color }], startle_threshold_db: number, environment_anchors: [{ db_value, label, icon }], animation: 'oscillate' | 'pulse', waveform_style: 'sine' | 'jagged' }

examples.narration: Momcozy hits fifty-one decibels — noticeably louder and more startling for infants
examples.why: Sound is inherently a waveform concept — static numbers in a comparison block don't viscerally communicate 'this will scare your baby' the way an amplitude-animated waveform does, and the shared environmental-reference axis (dishwasher, vacuum) gives parents an immediate sensory anchor that no text-based stat component can replicate

battery_drain_race.description: Three animated battery icons draining in real-time at proportional rates side by side, with a 'sessions remaining' counter ticking down beneath each and a finish-line marker showing when each device dies relative to a simulated 24-hour sick-baby use scenario
battery_drain_race.schema_sketch: { devices: [{ label, runtime_minutes, charge_time_minutes, cable_type: 'universal'|'proprietary', color }], scenario: { sessions_per_day: number, minutes_per_session: number }, animation_speed_multiplier: number, show_recharge_phase: boolean }

examples.narration: NoseFrida electric gets eighteen minutes per charge — maybe twelve clearing sessions before dead
examples.why: Battery life is a dynamic, time-comparative story — a static ranked list conveys the numbers but loses the visceral 'the NoseFrida dies first' drama that an animated simultaneous drain race communicates, especially when tied to a relatable scenario counter (sessions, not just minutes)

device_cross_section_callout.description: A cutaway diagram of a nasal aspirator showing its internal airflow path as an animated dotted line, with hotspot pins highlighting trap zones where mucus accumulates, color-coded by hygiene risk level and togglable between the three device designs
device_cross_section_callout.schema_sketch: { devices: [{ label, svg_cross_section_asset, airflow_path_nodes: [{ x, y }], trap_zones: [{ x, y, risk_level: 'low'|'medium'|'high', label }] }], active_device_index: number, animation: 'trace_airflow' }

examples.narration: dried mucus weeks later — not just gross, that's bacteria heaven
examples.why: The narration is making a structural argument — that physical bends in the suction pathway cause bacterial buildup — which is an architectural/spatial claim that only a cross-section diagram with trap-zone callouts can make visually; a warning badge just restates the text without showing the mechanism

cumulative_time_calendar_burn.description: A calendar grid representing a 90-day cold-and-flu season where each day's cell fills with a colored time-cost block per device, animating cumulatively so the viewer watches hours of wasted time stack up visually for the slower-cleaning devices versus the Grownsy staying nearly empty
cumulative_time_calendar_burn.schema_sketch: { devices: [{ label, seconds_per_clean, color }], season_days: number, avg_cleans_per_day: number, cell_height_scale: 'linear' | 'sqrt', animation: 'day_by_day' | 'instant_fill', show_total_hours_label: boolean }

examples.narration: Grownsy saves roughly three minutes per cleaning session — over a season, that's hours back
examples.why: A count_up shows a single number ticking upward, but the emotional power of this claim is the accumulation across many individual days — a calendar burn visualization makes the parent feel the season-long drag of inefficient cleaning in a way a lone counter cannot, and the three-device color overlay makes the comparison immediate

fee_accumulation_stack.description: An animated stacked-block or pill chart where individual fee items visibly pile up over time across milestone years, each block labeled with a fee name
fee_accumulation_stack.schema_sketch: { milestones: [{ year: number, fees: [{ name: string, category: 'base' | 'airport' | 'insurance' | 'fuel' | 'misc' }] }], animation_speed_ms: number, color_by_category: boolean }

examples.narration: By 1995, seventeen fees. By 2010, thirty-one. Today, forty-two distinct charges.
examples.why: A line chart shows a rising number but loses the visceral sense of individual fees multiplying — a stacking accumulation visual makes each new charge feel like a physical weight added to the pile, which is the emotional core of the scene.

fuel_gauge_receipt.description: A split visual pairing an analog fuel gauge needle frozen at a specific fill level alongside an itemized receipt line that calculates the implied per-gallon cost in real time
fuel_gauge_receipt.schema_sketch: { gauge_level_percent: number, charged_amount: number, claimed_gallons_short: number, implied_price_per_gallon: number, market_price_per_gallon: number, highlight_multiplier: boolean }

examples.narration: Seventy-three dollars for fuel. Two gallons short. Thirty-six dollars per gallon.
examples.why: The absurdity of $36/gallon is a data punchline that needs a visual calculator, not a quote card — seeing the gauge position physically connected to the dollar math makes the exploitation undeniable at a glance.

contract_xray.description: A document overlay animation that scans a dense rental contract page and progressively highlights, labels, and floats out hidden fee clauses as color-coded callout bubbles
contract_xray.schema_sketch: { document_bg_image: string, highlighted_clauses: [{ position: { x: number, y: number }, label: string, fee_amount: number, category: string, reveal_order: number }], total_hidden_fees: number, animation_sweep_direction: 'top_down' | 'left_right' }

examples.narration: Every fee, every surcharge, every fine print — then compared them side by side.
examples.why: A before/after toggle only shows two static states, but the narrative is about the act of excavation — revealing buried fees one by one from inside a real-looking contract document is far more dramatic and credible than a side-by-side price flip.

savings_распределение_histogram.description: A bell-curve histogram showing the distribution of individual user savings amounts, with the mean line labeled and a shaded region highlighting what most travelers actually saved
savings_распределение_histogram.schema_sketch: { buckets: [{ range_label: string, count: number }], mean_value: number, median_value: number, highlight_range: { min: number, max: number }, total_sample_size: number, x_axis_label: string, y_axis_label: string }

examples.narration: Average savings per rental: one hundred and thirty-seven dollars.
examples.why: A single price tag reduces a rich dataset to one number and feels like marketing copy; a distribution chart makes the savings real and statistically credible, showing that the $137 average represents millions of real transactions spread across a believable range.

revenue_cliff_chart.description: A 40-year area chart with a long ascending plateau that visibly breaks and curves downward at a marked inflection point year, with the drop zone shaded in a contrasting color and annotated with cause labels
revenue_cliff_chart.schema_sketch: { data_points: [{ year: number, revenue_index: number }], inflection_year: number, pre_inflection_color: string, post_inflection_color: string, annotations: [{ year: number, label: string }], y_axis_label: string, baseline_year: number }

examples.narration: Airport rental revenue drops for the first time in forty years.
examples.why: A generic line chart doesn't convey the historic weight of 'first time in forty years' — an area chart with the full four-decade climb rendered as context makes the downward break feel like a genuine structural rupture rather than a routine data point.

power_structure_map.description: An animated node-link diagram showing rental corporations connected by labeled edges to lobbying groups, law firms, airport authorities, and regulatory agencies, with edge thickness representing financial flow
power_structure_map.schema_sketch: { nodes: [{ id: string, label: string, type: 'corporation' | 'lobbying' | 'legal' | 'regulatory' | 'airport', size: number }], edges: [{ source: string, target: string, label: string, weight: number, type: 'financial' | 'legal' | 'political' }], animate_in_sequence: boolean, highlight_center_node: string }

examples.narration: Major brands fought back with lawsuits, lobbying, and predatory pricing.
examples.why: An evidence board is a static collage metaphor, but the narration describes a coordinated multi-front counterattack involving distinct institutional actors — a network graph makes the structural power and its interconnections immediately legible without requiring text.

fee_stack_builder.description: An animated bar that visually stacks individual fee blocks one by one onto a base price, with each block labeled and color-coded, culminating in a final total
fee_stack_builder.schema_sketch: { base_price: number, currency: string, fees: [{ label: string, amount: number, color: string }], final_total: number, animation_speed_ms: number }

examples.narration: fifty-nine-dollar economy car has magically become a hundred and twenty-nine dollars per day
examples.why: The narration is specifically about fees accumulating sequentially on top of a base price — a stacking animation with labeled blocks (base rate, insurance, GPS, airport surcharge, etc.) visualizes this deceptive layering far more viscerally than a static before/after split

examples.narration: forty-nine dollar rental became two hundred and thirty-seven dollars
examples.why: before_after shows only two states, but this scene's entire dramatic power comes from watching each hidden fee accumulate one by one — the animation of the stack building IS the story, not just the start and end price

dual_receipt_compare.description: Two side-by-side receipt-style visuals with itemized line entries, subtotals, and a highlighted final price — one corporate, one peer-to-peer — designed to look like actual printed receipts
dual_receipt_compare.schema_sketch: { receipt_a: { label: string, items: [{ description: string, amount: number }], total: number, accent_color: string }, receipt_b: { label: string, items: [{ description: string, amount: number }], total: number, accent_color: string }, highlight_winner: 'a' | 'b' }

examples.narration: Marcus paid sixty-two dollars total for three days with a BMW
examples.why: The entire emotional punch of this scene is the absurd dollar contrast between two people on the same trip — a receipt-format comparison makes the disparity feel tangible and real-world rather than just an abstracted number on a spotlight card

examples.narration: same car for five days in Los Angeles for three hundred and twenty dollars
examples.why: before_after is a single split reveal, but a receipt format carries the specific cultural weight of the 'surprise at the counter' narrative thread running through the entire video, making the savings feel concrete and transactional rather than abstract

wait_time_snake_queue.description: An animated top-down floor-plan view of a snaking queue line that fills in person-by-person, with a live-style wait timer incrementing in the corner
wait_time_snake_queue.schema_sketch: { queue_length: number, wait_time_seconds: number, animate_fill: boolean, location_label: string, timer_label: string, highlight_color: string }

examples.narration: joins a line that snakes around two corners
examples.why: The narration explicitly calls out the physical experience of a winding queue — an animated overhead queue map with a ticking wait clock turns an abstract frustration into a concrete, relatable visual that static imagery cannot replicate

lifetime_overpay_calculator.description: An animated running total that multiplies trips-per-year by average overpay amount, showing cumulative money lost over N years as a dollar figure that counts upward with a 'years wasted' axis below it
lifetime_overpay_calculator.schema_sketch: { trips_per_year: number, overpay_per_trip: number, years: number, currency: string, animation_duration_ms: number, highlight_total_color: string }

examples.narration: Sarah calculated she'd been getting robbed by an average of sixty percent for years
examples.why: The insight here is the compounding personal loss over time, not a single percentage — a temporal counter that shows total dollars drained across a travel history reframes the stakes from one bad trip to a years-long pattern of being exploited

industry_disruption_timeline.description: A horizontal timeline showing an incumbent industry's dominance curve being intersected and overtaken by a challenger curve, with labeled inflection points and market-share shading between the two lines
industry_disruption_timeline.schema_sketch: { incumbent: { label: string, data_points: [{ year: number, share: number }], color: string }, challenger: { label: string, data_points: [{ year: number, share: number }], color: string }, crossover_label: string, x_axis_label: string, y_axis_label: string }

examples.narration: same way rideshare apps obliterated the taxi monopoly
examples.why: The narration is making a structural analogy about market disruption arcs — an intersecting S-curve timeline with labeled industry names and a visible crossover point communicates the disruption thesis visually in a way no text reveal or split-screen can match

step_flow_connector.description: Three or more horizontally connected process steps rendered as icon-anchored nodes joined by animated flowing lines, each node revealing its label and a micro-icon as the animation progresses
step_flow_connector.schema_sketch: { steps: [{ icon: string, label: string, sublabel: string }], connector_style: 'arrow' | 'line' | 'dotted', animation_delay_ms: number, accent_color: string, direction: 'horizontal' | 'vertical' }

examples.narration: Compare prices. Message owners directly. Meet at a convenient location.
examples.why: The narration is describing a literal user workflow with discrete sequential steps — an animated node-connector flow diagram with iconography per step (search, message, handshake, drive) communicates process clarity and simplicity far better than a static journey roadmap built for narrative arcs

brain_mode_duel.description: A split-brain animation showing two distinct behavioral loops — one pulsing outward seeking novelty, one spiraling inward deepening into a single node — with a labeled axis between them
brain_mode_duel.schema_sketch: { left_label: string, left_mode: 'outward_pulse' | 'scatter_radiate', right_label: string, right_mode: 'inward_spiral' | 'depth_focus', axis_label: string, accent_color_left: hex, accent_color_right: hex }

examples.narration: ADHD brains hunt for stimulation. Autistic brains hunt for patterns.
examples.why: The core contrast here is a directional, dynamic cognitive tendency, not a number, so a stat_comparison wastes the moment — an animated loop diagram would make the hunter-vs-pattern distinction viscerally intuitive without any text

physiological_cascade.description: A body-silhouette diagram that sequentially highlights biological stress responses — heart, hands, brain — with animated intensity meters next to each organ as the trigger event appears above
physiological_cascade.schema_sketch: { trigger_label: string, response_nodes: [{ body_region: string, label: string, intensity: 0-100, delay_ms: number }], silhouette_style: 'outline' | 'filled', color_ramp: [hex, hex] }

examples.narration: heart rate spikes, palms sweat, mind races — coworker suggested different restaurant
examples.why: The narration is explicitly listing physical body reactions in sequence, which is a perfect data-driven anatomical animation, but no body-map or cascading-highlight component exists in the library to make those internal reactions visible

overlap_venn_stat.description: An animated Venn diagram where two labeled circles partially overlap, with percentage figures anchored inside each exclusive region and the shared region, all values counting up on entry
overlap_venn_stat.schema_sketch: { circle_a_label: string, circle_b_label: string, circle_a_exclusive_stat: string, circle_b_exclusive_stat: string, overlap_stat: string, overlap_label: string, color_a: hex, color_b: hex, animate_on_entry: boolean }

examples.narration: ninety-six percent of autistic people have sensory differences; for ADHD, sixty percent
examples.why: The entire video's thesis is about two conditions that share symptoms but differ in degree — a Venn diagram with live percentage anchors makes the overlap-vs-distinction relationship spatially legible in a way before_after (which implies sequential change) fundamentally cannot

timeline_bar_compare.description: Two horizontal timeline bars stacked vertically — one per demographic group — that animate from left to right to show duration, with milestone markers and a final year-count callout
timeline_bar_compare.schema_sketch: { bars: [{ group_label: string, duration_years: number, accent_color: hex, milestone_labels: [{ year: number, label: string }] }], animate_sequentially: boolean, end_callout_style: 'bold_number' | 'flag' }

examples.narration: For women, that number jumps to eight years. Eight years.
examples.why: The narration is a direct duration comparison between two groups (general vs. women), which is a horizontal-time-span problem; before_after implies a state transformation rather than elapsed time, so the emotional weight of 'eight years' is lost without a bar that visually stretches to show how long that wait actually is

market_dominance_pie.description: An animated pie or treemap chart showing market share by company with color-coded ownership group highlighting which brands share a parent
market_dominance_pie.schema_sketch: { total_market_label: string, dominant_group_percent: number, segments: [{ brand: string, parent_company: string, share_percent: number, color: string }], highlight_threshold: number }

examples.narration: six companies that control ninety percent of the rental car market
examples.why: A static percent_fill can only show one number, but this scene needs to visually communicate both the 90% concentration AND the ownership clustering simultaneously — two nested ideas that require a grouped visual hierarchy

brand_ownership_tree.description: A visual org-chart style tree where parent company nodes branch down to subsidiary brand logos, with a 'looks like competitors' overlay that collapses into shared roots
brand_ownership_tree.schema_sketch: { parent_companies: [{ name: string, color: string, logo_url: string, subsidiaries: [{ brand: string, logo_url: string }] }], reveal_mode: 'collapse' | 'expand' }

examples.narration: Enterprise owns National and Alamo. Avis owns Budget and Zipcar.
examples.why: A comparison_table implies rivalry between columns, which is the opposite of the point — the scene needs a branching hierarchy that visually proves the 'competing' brands are actually the same entity underneath

asset_depreciation_curve.description: An animated curve graph showing a vehicle's value dropping sharply from purchase price over time, with labeled inflection points for key depreciation events
asset_depreciation_curve.schema_sketch: { asset_label: string, start_value: number, currency: string, time_unit: 'months' | 'years', data_points: [{ time: number, value: number, event_label?: string }], highlight_zone: { start: number, end: number, label: string } }

examples.narration: huge fleets that depreciate the moment they drive off the lot
examples.why: trend_arrow is a single directional indicator, but depreciation is a non-linear curve with a dramatic initial cliff — that specific shape (steep drop then plateau) is the visual metaphor that makes the overhead argument visceral

supply_network_scatter.description: An animated dot map where dots representing car owners appear one by one across a city grid, density building outward from a center point to show network supply growth versus a single fixed rental lot marker
supply_network_scatter.schema_sketch: { city_label: string, traditional_lot_coords: [{ lat: number, lng: number, label: string }], owner_nodes: [{ lat: number, lng: number, car_type?: string }], animation_style: 'burst' | 'sequential', density_radius_km: number }

examples.narration: more individual owners means more options
examples.why: trend_arrow communicates direction but not scale or distribution — the peer-to-peer advantage is fundamentally geographic and volumetric, and watching a network of owner dots bloom across a city grid makes the 'more options everywhere' idea immediately spatial and intuitive
